- **Telephony (G.711):** 8 kHz × 8 bits = 64 kbps PCM forms the foundation of the PSTN digital backbone.
- **Audio CDs (Red Book):** 44.1 kHz × 16 bits × 2 channels = 1.411 Mbps — uses **linear PCM (LPCM)**.
- **Studio mastering / Blu-ray:** 96 kHz / 192 kHz × 24-bit linear PCM.
- **Defense / Instrumentation:** High-fidelity PCM telemetry links in satellites and radar.
- **Comparison anchor:** PCM is the "gold standard" waveform codec against which DPCM, DM, and ADPCM are benchmarked in your lab experiments.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the SQNR Formula (Uniform Quantization, Sinusoidal Input)

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation below is the **single most important derivation** in the PCM portion of PCECL508. KTU examiners regularly allot 5–7 marks for this with a clear "Show that" prefix.

**Given:**
- A uniform mid-riser quantizer with $L = 2^n$ levels symmetrically spanning $[-V_{max}, +V_{max}]$.
- A full-scale sinusoidal test signal $m(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f t)$ with $V_p = V_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Quantization Step Size**

The total peak-to-peak range is $2V_{max}$, divided uniformly into $L$ intervals:
$$\Delta = \frac{\text{Total Range}}{\text{Number of Levels}} = \frac{2V_{max}}{L} = \frac{2V_{max}}{2^n} = V_{max}\cdot 2^{1-n}$$

**Step 2 — Probability Model for Quantization Error**

Assuming the quantizer is exercised over the entire range and the input is sufficiently active, the quantization error $q$ is modeled as a **uniform random variable** on $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ with PDF:
$$p(q) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{\Delta}, & -\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \le q \le +\dfrac{\Delta}{2} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Mean-Square Quantization Noise Power**

Compute the second moment:
$$\sigma_q^2 = \mathbb{E}[q^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} q^2\, p(q)\, dq = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} q^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta}\, dq$$

$$= \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{q^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} = \frac{1}{\Delta}\left(\frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot \frac{2}{3}\cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{8} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Signal Power of a Full-Scale Sinusoid**

$$S = \mathbb{E}[m^2(t)] = \mathbb{E}[V_p^2 \sin^2(2\pi f t)] = \frac{V_p^2}{2}$$

(Using $\mathbb{E}[\sin^2(\cdot)] = 1/2$.)

**Step 5 — Form the Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio**

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_q = \frac{S}{\sigma_q^2} = \frac{V_p^2/2}{\Delta^2/12} = \frac{V_p^2}{2}\cdot\frac{12}{\Delta^2} = \frac{6V_p^2}{\Delta^2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\Delta$ in Terms of $L$ and $V_{max}$**

With $V_p = V_{max}$ (full-scale loading) and $\Delta = 2V_{max}/L$:
$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_q = \frac{6V_{max}^2}{(2V_{max}/L)^2} = \frac{6V_{max}^2 \cdot L^2}{4V_{max}^2} = \frac{3L^2}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $L = 2^n$**

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_q = \frac{3 \cdot (2^n)^2}{2} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2} = 1.5 \cdot 4^n$$

**Step 8 — Convert to Decibels**

$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 10\log_{10}(2^{2n})$$

$$= 1.7609 + 20n\log_{10}(2) = 1.76 + 6.02n \;\text{dB}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n \;\text{dB}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Bit Rate and Channel Bandwidth

**Given:** Voice signal band-limited to $W = 4$ kHz (typical telephony), sampled at Nyquist rate $f_s = 2W = 8$ kHz, with $n = 8$ bits per sample.

**Step 1 — Bit Rate**

The PCM transmitter emits $n$ bits per sample, with $f_s$ samples per second:
$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \cdot 8000 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum Baseband Bandwidth**

For binary NRZ line coding (the simplest model asked in KTU problems):
$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — General Expression**

$$\boxed{\;R_b = n \cdot 2W \quad \text{and} \quad B_{min} = nW\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Quick-Reference Performance Table (Verified)

| $n$ (bits) | $L = 2^n$ | SQNR (dB) | $R_b$ at $f_s = 8$ kHz | $B_{min}$ |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 4 | 16 | 25.84 | 32 kbps | 16 kHz |
| 6 | 64 | 37.88 | 48 kbps | 24 kHz |
| 8 | 256 | 49.92 | 64 kbps | 32 kHz |
| 10 | 1024 | 61.96 | 80 kbps | 40 kHz |
| 12 | 4096 | 74.00 | 96 kbps | 48 kHz |
| 16 | 65536 | 98.08 | 128 kbps | 64 kHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verifying the 6 dB rule:** Row $n=8 \to n=10$ gives $61.96 - 49.92 = 12.04$ dB $\approx 2 \times 6.02$ dB ✓. Use such checks in your **lab record** and **ESE answers** to demonstrate understanding.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PCM Transmitter–Receiver Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Analog Input m(t)"] --> A2["Anti-Aliasing LPF cutoff W Hz"]
    A2 --> A3["Sampler f_s >= 2W"]
    A3 --> A4["Uniform Quantizer L = 2 to the n levels"]
    A4 --> A5["Binary Encoder n bit codeword"]
    A5 --> A6["Channel Line Coder NRZ or RZ"]
    A6 --> A7["PCM Bit Stream R_b = n f_s"]
    A7 --> A8["Regenerative Repeater optional"]
    A8 --> A9["Binary Decoder"]
    A9 --> A10["Reconstruction LPF"]
    A10 --> A11["Analog Output mhat t plus quant noise"]
    classDef tx fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5 tx
    class A6,A7,A8 ch
    class A9,A10,A11 rx
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU exam:** The block diagram carries 3–4 marks if drawn and labelled neatly. The noise contributed by the channel is *regenerated away* by repeaters — but the **quantization noise** injected at the transmitter quantizer is **irrecoverable**, which is precisely the "performance bottleneck" that PCM performance analysis studies.

---

### 4.2 Functional Signal Flow — How Each Block Adds or Removes Distortion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1["Continuous m t"] -->|Sampling adds nothing if Nyquist satisfied| B2["Discrete m kTs"]
    B2 -->|Quantization adds inband noise sigma q squared = Delta squared over 12| B3["Quantized mq kTs plus q"]
    B3 -->|Encoding adds nothing deterministic| B4["Binary codewords"]
    B4 -->|Channel noise may flip bits bit error rate Pe| B5["Received bits"]
    B5 -->|Decoding returns to sample domain| B6["Decoded samples"]
    B6 -->|Reconstruction LPF smooths stair case| B7["mhat t plus residual noise"]
    classDef noise fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef clean fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000
    class B1,B2,B4,B5,B6 clean
    class B3,B7 noise
```

---

### 4.3 SQNR vs Number of Bits — The Famous 6 dB-per-Bit Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["n = 4 bits"] -->|add 6.02 dB per bit| C2["n = 5 bits"]
    C2 -->|add 6.02 dB| C3["n = 6 bits"]
    C3 -->|add 6.02 dB| C4["n = 7 bits"]
    C4 -->|add 6.02 dB| C5["n = 8 bits"]
    C5 -->|add 6.02 dB| C6["n = 9 bits"]
    C6 -->|add 6.02 dB| C7["n = 10 bits"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In your lab experiment, you will be asked to **plot SQNR (dB) on the Y-axis** versus **$n$ on the X-axis** and verify that the slope is approximately **6 dB/bit**, with a y-intercept near **1.76 dB** for a full-scale sinusoid. This single plot is worth **3 marks** in your lab record viva.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** What is Pulse Code Modulation? List the three basic operations performed in a PCM system.

**Model Answer:**

Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) is the process by which an analog message signal is converted into a digital signal represented by **binary-coded samples**.

The three basic operations are:

1. **Sampling** — converting the continuous-time signal $m(t)$ into a discrete-time signal $m(kT_s)$ at instants $kT_s$, with $f_s \ge 2W$ to avoid aliasing.
2. **Quantization** — approximating each sample amplitude to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, introducing a quantization error $q$ uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
3. **Encoding** — assigning a unique $n$-bit binary codeword to each quantization level for transmission.

**[3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three operations.]**

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) of a PCM system. State the standard expression for SQNR of a full-scale sinusoid in dB.

**Model Answer:**

The **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR)** is the ratio of the signal power $S$ at the PCM receiver output to the mean-square quantization noise power $\sigma_q^2$ introduced by the quantizer:
$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_q = \frac{S}{\sigma_q^2}$$

For a **uniform quantizer** with a **full-scale sinusoidal input** (amplitude $V_p = V_{max}$) and $n$ bits per sample, the SQNR in dB is:
$$\boxed{\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n \;\text{dB}}$$

**[3 marks — 2 marks for definition + formula, 1 mark for the boxed dB expression.]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Module Experiment – PCM]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **PCM transmitter and receiver**. Label every block and state the function of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sinusoidal signal $m(t) = 2\sin(2000\pi t)$ is applied to a uniform PCM system. The signal is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz and quantized using $n = 8$ bits.

    **(i)** Determine the quantization step size $\Delta$.
    **(ii)** Compute the mean-square quantization noise power $\sigma_q^2$.
    **(iii)** Calculate the SQNR in dB.
    **(iv)** Find the transmission bit rate $R_b$ and minimum channel bandwidth $B_{min}$.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Function:** *(Match the diagram in Section 4.1.)*

**Transmitter:**
| **Block** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| Anti-Aliasing LPF | Restricts bandwidth of $m(t)$ to $W$ Hz so that $f_s \ge 2W$ is sufficient. |
| Sampler | Produces $m(kT_s)$ at rate $f_s$ samples/sec. |
| Quantizer | Rounds each sample to nearest of $L = 2^n$ levels. |
| Encoder | Maps each level to a unique $n$-bit binary word. |

**Receiver:**
| **Block** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| Decoder | Converts $n$-bit words back to quantized sample amplitudes. |
| Reconstruction LPF | Smooths the staircase into $\hat{m}(t)$. |

**[7 marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for transmitter, 2 for receiver.]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_p = 2$ V, $W = 1$ kHz (since $f_m = 1$ kHz), $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 8$ bits.

**(i) Quantization step size:**

Assuming a symmetric quantizer range $V_{max} = V_p = 2$ V (full-scale loading):
$$\Delta = \frac{2V_{max}}{2^n} = \frac{2 \times 2}{2^8} = \frac{4}{256} = 0.015625 \text{ V} = 15.625 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Mean-square quantization noise power:**
$$\sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12} = \frac{(0.015625)^2}{12} = \frac{2.4414 \times 10^{-4}}{12} = 2.0345 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V}^2$$

**(iii) SQNR in dB:**

Method 1 (Direct ratio):
$$S = \frac{V_p^2}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ W}$$
$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_q = \frac{2}{2.0345 \times 10^{-5}} = 98{,}304$$
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(98{,}304) = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

Method 2 (Formula check, since $V_p = V_{max}$):
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark$$

**(iv) Bit rate and bandwidth:**
$$R_b = n f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$
$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

**[7 marks: (i) 1, (ii) 1, (iii) 3, (iv) 2.]**

---

#### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module Experiment – PCM]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio** of a uniform quantizer in a PCM system for a full-scale sinusoidal input. Show that the result is $1.76 + 6.02n$ dB. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A music signal has a bandwidth of 15 kHz. It is sampled at 1.25 times the Nyquist rate and encoded using 10-bit PCM.

    **(i)** Compute the sampling frequency $f_s$ and the bit rate $R_b$.
    **(ii)** Determine the SQNR (dB) for a full-scale sinusoid.
    **(iii)** If the system is upgraded to 12-bit PCM, by how many dB does the SQNR improve? Comment on the bandwidth trade-off.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B:**

**(a) Derivation:** *(Write the complete 8-step derivation from Section 3.1. The examiner will look for:)*

1. $\Delta = 2V_{max}/2^n$ — **1 mark**
2. Uniform distribution assumption and PDF — **1 mark**
3. $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2/12$ derivation — **2 marks**
4. $S = V_p^2/2$ for sinusoid — **0.5 marks**
5. Final ratio $S/\sigma_q^2 = 1.5 \times 2^{2n}$ — **1.5 marks**
6. dB conversion to $1.76 + 6.02n$ — **1 mark**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $W = 15$ kHz, $f_s = 1.25 \times 2W = 1.25 \times 30 = 37.5$ kHz, $n = 10$ bits.

**(i) Sampling frequency and bit rate:**
$$f_s = 1.25 \times 2W = 1.25 \times 30 = 37.5 \text{ kHz}$$
$$R_b = n f_s = 10 \times 37{,}500 = 375{,}000 \text{ bps} = 375 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) SQNR for $n = 10$:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 10 = 1.76 + 60.20 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Upgrade to $n = 12$ bits:**
$$\text{SQNR}_{dB}^{(12)} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 12 = 1.76 + 72.24 = 74.00 \text{ dB}$$

Improvement:
$$\Delta \text{SQNR} = 74.00 - 61.96 = 12.04 \text{ dB} \;\approx\; 2 \times 6.02 \text{ dB}$$

This confirms the **6 dB-per-bit rule** (2 extra bits → 12 dB improvement).

**Bandwidth trade-off:**
$$B_{min}^{(10)} = R_b^{(10)}/2 = 187.5 \text{ kHz}, \quad B_{min}^{(12)} = R_b^{(12)}/2 = 225 \text{ kHz}$$

The 12-bit system requires **20% more bandwidth** (375 → 450 kbps) for a 12 dB SQNR gain. In bandwidth-limited channels, this trade-off is critical; in such cases, **non-uniform quantization** (e.g., $\mu$-law / A-law companding) is preferred.

**[7 marks: (i) 2, (ii) 1, (iii) 4 including the comment.]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PCECL508 PCM Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\Delta/2$ limit** — Some students write $p(q) = 1/\Delta$ but then integrate $q^2$ from $0$ to $\Delta$ instead of $-\Delta/2$ to $+\Delta/2$. This gives $\Delta^2/3$ instead of $\Delta^2/12$ — a factor of **4 error**, i.e., **6 dB mistake**. *Always draw the PDF limits clearly.*
>
> 2. **Confusing $V_{max}$ with $V_p$** — The formula $1.76 + 6.02n$ is valid **only for full-scale loading** ($V_p = V_{max}$). If $V_p < V_{max}$, you must subtract $20\log_{10}(k)$ where $k = V_{max}/V_p$. KTU examiners love to test this — expect 2–3 marks lost.
>
> 3. **Sampling rate vs signal frequency** — A common error is to confuse the **highest frequency component** $W$ of the message with the **sampling frequency** $f_s$. The Nyquist criterion uses $f_s \ge 2W$, **not** $f_s \ge 2f_m$ of any specific sinusoid. (They are equal for a pure sinusoid, but the general statement must use $W$.)
>
> 4. **Units in $R_b$** — Writing "64,000" without "**bps**" or "**kbps**" costs 0.5 marks. Always include units.
>
> 5. **dB conversion** — SQNR in ratio form is acceptable only if the question asks for "ratio". The phrase "in dB" mandates the $10\log_{10}(\cdot)$ step — losing this loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Performance of Waveform Coding Using PCM**
>
> ✅ PCM = **Sampling → Quantization → Encoding**.
> ✅ Nyquist criterion: $f_s \ge 2W$ (where $W$ = message bandwidth, **not** signal frequency).
> ✅ Number of levels: $L = 2^n$; step size: $\Delta = 2V_{max}/2^n$.
> ✅ Quantization error $q \sim \mathcal{U}[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ → mean-square noise $\sigma_q^2 = \Delta^2/12$.
> ✅ Signal power of sinusoid: $S = V_p^2/2$.
> ✅ **Master formula:** $\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n$ dB (full-scale sinusoid, uniform quantizer).
> ✅ **6 dB-per-bit rule:** each extra bit → +6 dB SQNR, but also proportionally higher $R_b$ and $B_{min}$.
> ✅ Bit rate: $R_b = n f_s$; minimum baseband bandwidth: $B_{min} = R_b/2 = nW$.
> ✅ Telephony standard: $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 8$ bits → $R_b = 64$ kbps.
> ✅ PCM's biggest advantage: **immunity to channel noise** (binary regeneration kills channel errors); biggest disadvantage: **quantization noise is irreversible**.
> ✅ For non-uniform quantization, the same formula structure applies with $L_{\text{eff}}$ replacing $L$ — explored in the companding/$\mu$-law/A-law module.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Shaping and Matched Filtering
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Pulse Shaping and Matched Filtering

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pulse Shaping — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pulse Shaping** is the deliberate design of the transmitted symbol waveform $s(t)$ in a digital communication system to simultaneously **confine the transmitted signal bandwidth** and **control Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** introduced by the bandlimited channel.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (PCECL508 — Communication Engineering Lab / Digital Communication Theory), pulse shaping is treated as a **transmitter-side signal processing operation** performed between the **symbol mapper** and the **D/A converter / analog front-end**. The pulse shaper is mathematically modelled as a linear time-invariant (LTI) filter with impulse response $p(t)$ convolved with the discrete symbol stream $\sum a_k \delta(t - kT)$ to produce the continuous-time transmit waveform.

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \, p(t - kT)$$

where:
- $a_k \in \{+1, -1\}$ (or any M-ary alphabet) — the **discrete symbol**
- $T$ — the **symbol period** (in seconds)
- $R_s = 1/T$ — the **symbol rate** (in symbols/second, or **baud**)
- $p(t)$ — the **shaping pulse** (also called the basis function)

## 1.2 Matched Filtering — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Matched Filter** is the **linear filter that maximizes the output Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the optimal sampling instant, in the presence of Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN). It is the **optimal receiver** for detecting a known signal $s(t)$ corrupted by AWGN.

The matched filter is *not* matched to the channel; it is matched to the **known transmitted pulse shape $p(t)$**. For a known signal $s(t)$ observed over $[0, T]$, the matched filter has impulse response:

$$h(t) = k \, s(T - t)$$

where $k$ is an arbitrary gain constant (often unity) and $T$ is the symbol duration. The time-reversal and shift by $T$ is the mathematical expression of the **"matched"** property.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogies

### Pulse Shaping — The "Water Drop" Analogy
Imagine you are pouring water drops at a constant rate (one drop every $T$ seconds) into a long, narrow channel. If each drop is a sharp **spike** (rectangular pulse of very short duration), it has a very **wide frequency spectrum** — meaning the channel must pass enormous bandwidth, and the drops will "smear" into each other, causing ISI. By shaping each drop into a smooth, gentle bump (like a sinc or raised-cosine shape), the energy is concentrated in a controlled bandwidth, and the tails of consecutive drops pass through **zero** at the exact decision instants of all other drops — **zero ISI**.

### Matched Filtering — The "Lock and Key" Analogy
A matched filter is like a **precisely tuned lock** that opens *only* when the incoming waveform is an exact, time-reversed, delayed copy of the template. Noise (random vibrations) cannot simultaneously match the template's shape, so the lock stays shut for noise. The **peak output** occurs at exactly the moment when the received signal "fits" the template — this is the optimal detection instant.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Symbol period | $T$ | seconds (s) |
| Symbol rate | $R_s$ | **baud** = symbols/s |
| Bit rate | $R_b$ | bits/s = $R_s \log_2 M$ |
| Bandwidth | $B$ | Hz |
| Rolloff factor | $\alpha$ (or $\beta$) | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ (**dimensionless**) |
| Bit Energy | $E_b$ | Joules |
| Noise PSD | $N_0/2$ | W/Hz |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always express the Nyquist bandwidth and matched-filter output SNR in **closed-form** with the proper substitutions. Examiners award full marks only when both the formula and the substituted numerical form are shown.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pulse Shaping? — The Three Engineering Drivers

1. **Bandwidth confinement** — Practical channels (copper wires, fibre, wireless spectrum) have **finite bandwidth**. A rectangular NRZ pulse of width $T$ has a sinc-shaped spectrum whose first null is at $f = 1/T$, but its *significant* energy extends to several multiples of $1/T$. Pulse shaping concentrates this energy.
2. **ISI Control** — A bandlimited channel spreads a pulse in time (because convolution in time = multiplication in frequency, and bandlimiting = multiplication by a rectangle in frequency = sinc convolution in time). Without careful shaping, neighbouring symbols overlap at the decision instant.
3. **Spectral efficiency** — Modern systems (LTE, 5G NR, Wi-Fi) pack symbols tightly while using minimum bandwidth. Pulse shaping is the tool that enables this.

## 2.2 The Nyquist Criterion for Zero ISI (First Nyquist Criterion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nyquist's First Criterion (No-ISI Condition):** A pulse $p(t)$ produces **zero ISI** at the sampling instants $t = kT$ if and only if its samples satisfy:
> $$p(kT) = \begin{cases} 1 & k = 0 \\ 0 & k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots \end{cases}$$

In the **frequency domain**, this is equivalent to the condition that the folded spectrum (aliased at integer multiples of $1/T$) be a constant:

$$\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} P\!\left(f - \frac{m}{T}\right) = T \quad \text{for all } f$$

## 2.3 The Raised Cosine (RC) Spectrum — The Workhorse of Digital Comms

The **Raised Cosine** family is the *most widely used* pulse shape satisfying Nyquist's criterion. It is defined in the **frequency domain** as:

$$P_{RC}(f) = \begin{cases} T & \text{for } 0 \le \vert f \vert \le \dfrac{1 - \alpha}{2T} \\[8pt] \dfrac{T}{2}\!\left[1 + \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi T}{\alpha}\!\left(\vert f \vert - \dfrac{1 - \alpha}{2T}\right)\right)\right] & \text{for } \dfrac{1 - \alpha}{2T} \le \vert f \vert \le \dfrac{1 + \alpha}{2T} \\[8pt] 0 & \text{for } \vert f \vert \ge \dfrac{1 + \alpha}{2T} \end{cases}$$

**Properties:**
- **Absolute bandwidth:** $B = \dfrac{1 + \alpha}{2T} = \dfrac{R_s}{2}(1 + \alpha)$
- **Nyquist (minimum) bandwidth** occurs at $\alpha = 0$: $B_{min} = \dfrac{1}{2T} = \dfrac{R_s}{2}$ (this is the **ideal sinc pulse** case)
- The corresponding time-domain pulse is:
$$p_{RC}(t) = \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{T}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{1 - \left(\dfrac{2\alpha t}{T}\right)^2}$$

## 2.4 The Square-Root Raised Cosine (SRRC) Filter

In a real transmitter–receiver pair, the **transmit filter $p_T(t)$** and the **receive filter $p_R(t)$** are split such that the *overall* pulse seen by the sampler is RC. The most common split is:

$$P_{TX}(f) = P_{RX}(f) = \sqrt{P_{RC}(f)}$$

This is the **Square-Root Raised Cosine (SRRC)** filter. It provides:
- Optimal noise rejection at the receiver
- The cascade $p_{TX}(t) * p_{RX}(t) = p_{RC}(t)$ — i.e., zero-ISI at the decision point.

## 2.5 The Matched Filter — Derivation Outline

**Setup:** A known deterministic signal $s(t)$ is transmitted over an AWGN channel with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. The received signal is $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ (assuming no channel distortion for now).

**Goal:** Design a linear filter with impulse response $h(t)$ that maximises the **output SNR** at time $t = T$:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{\vert y_s(T) \vert^2}{E[\,n_{\text{out}}^2(t)\,]}$$

**Result (proved in Section 3):**

$$\boxed{\;h(t) = k\, s(T - t)\;}$$

**Output SNR of the matched filter:**

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$$

where $E_s = \int_0^T s^2(t)\,dt$ is the **signal energy**. This is the **maximum SNR achievable by any linear filter** — proven by the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.

## 2.6 The Correlator — Time-Domain Equivalent

A **correlator** multiplies the received signal by a locally generated template $s(t)$ and integrates over $[0, T]$:

$$y(T) = \int_0^T r(\tau)\, s(\tau)\, d\tau$$

By the convolution/correlation identity, the correlator is **mathematically equivalent** to passing $r(t)$ through a matched filter and sampling at $t = T$. In KTU lab experiments, the correlator is often preferred for software simulation; in hardware, the matched filter is implemented as an FIR (e.g., in FPGA-based receivers).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Transmit waveform | $x(t) = \sum a_k p(t - kT)$ | $T$ = symbol period |
| 2 | Nyquist zero-ISI | $p(kT) = \delta_k$ | Sampling at $kT$ |
| 3 | RC bandwidth | $B = \dfrac{1+\alpha}{2T}$ | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| 4 | RC impulse response | $p_{RC}(t) = \mathrm{sinc}(t/T) \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\pi \alpha t / T)}{1 - (2\alpha t / T)^2}$ | Time-domain form |
| 5 | SRRC impulse response | $p_{SRRC}(t) = $ inverse FT of $\sqrt{P_{RC}(f)}$ | Splits RC across Tx/Rx |
| 6 | Matched filter IR | $h(t) = k\, s(T - t)$ | Time-reversed + delayed |
| 7 | Matched filter output | $y(T) = k \int_0^T r(\tau) s(\tau) d\tau$ | Sampled at $t=T$ |
| 8 | Max output SNR | $\mathrm{SNR}_{\max} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$ | AWGN channel |
| 9 | Bit-energy relation | $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$ | M-ary signalling |
| 10 | Eye opening | $\propto (1 - \alpha)$ conceptually for ISI margin | Larger $\alpha \Rightarrow$ wider eye |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Matched Filter Maximises Output SNR

**Statement:** Among all linear filters, the matched filter $h(t) = k\, s(T - t)$ produces the maximum possible output SNR at $t = T$ for an AWGN-corrupted known signal $s(t)$.

**Step 1 — Express the output of a general linear filter.**

If the received signal is $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ and the filter has impulse response $h(t)$, then the filter output at time $t$ is the convolution:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} r(\tau) h(t - \tau) d\tau = \int_0^T s(\tau) h(t - \tau) d\tau + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} n(\tau) h(t - \tau) d\tau$$

The first term is the **signal component** $y_s(t)$; the second is the **noise component** $y_n(t)$.

**Step 2 — Sample at $t = T$ and define the output SNR.**

$$y_s(T) = \int_0^T s(\tau) h(T - \tau) d\tau$$

The noise variance at the filter output is (using $S_n(f) = N_0/2$ and Parseval-type relations):

$$E[\,y_n^2(T)\,] = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h^2(t) dt = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T h^2(\tau) d\tau$$

(assuming $h(t)$ is non-zero only on $[0, T]$ — the causal version).

**Step 3 — Form the SNR expression.**

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{\left[\int_0^T s(\tau) h(T - \tau) d\tau\right]^2}{\dfrac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T h^2(\tau) d\tau}$$

Substitute $\tau' = T - \tau$ (so $d\tau = -d\tau'$; limits flip) in the numerator:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{\left[\int_0^T s(T - \tau') h(\tau') d\tau'\right]^2}{\dfrac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T h^2(\tau') d\tau'}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.**

For real functions $u(\tau) = s(T - \tau)$ and $v(\tau) = h(\tau)$, Cauchy–Schwarz states:

$$\left[\int u(\tau) v(\tau) d\tau\right]^2 \le \int u^2(\tau) d\tau \cdot \int v^2(\tau) d\tau$$

with **equality iff** $v(\tau) = c \cdot u(\tau)$ for some constant $c$.

**Step 5 — Apply the upper bound and identify the optimum filter.**

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{out}} \le \dfrac{\int_0^T s^2(T - \tau) d\tau \cdot \int_0^T h^2(\tau) d\tau}{\dfrac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T h^2(\tau) d\tau} = \dfrac{\int_0^T s^2(t) dt}{N_0/2} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$$

**Equality holds when:**

$$h(\tau) = c \cdot s(T - \tau)$$

This is the **matched filter**. Q.E.D. $\blacksquare$

**Interpretation:** The maximum SNR depends only on the **signal energy** $E_s$ and the **noise PSD** $N_0/2$ — *not* on the detailed shape of $s(t)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Raised-Cosine Spectrum and Zero ISI

**Step 1 — Start with the Nyquist (folded) spectrum condition.**

$$\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} P\!\left(f - \frac{m}{T}\right) = T$$

**Step 2 — Conjecture an odd-symmetric, real, even function $P(f)$ with a flat central section** (constant $T$) and roll-off in a transition band $\left[\dfrac{1-\alpha}{2T}, \dfrac{1+\alpha}{2T}\right]$.

**Step 3 — Choose the roll-off as a cosine raised from 0 to 1** (this guarantees smoothness, hence fast time-domain decay). Set:

$$P(f) = \dfrac{T}{2}\left[1 + \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi T}{\alpha}\left(\vert f \vert - \dfrac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Verify the aliasing condition.** Because the RC spectrum is even and has total area $T$ (this is a key property derivable by integrating the cosine term from the lower to the upper transition frequency, yielding area $\alpha T / 2 + (1-\alpha)T/2 = T/2$ per side, so total = $T$), the condition is satisfied.

**Step 5 — Inverse Fourier transform to obtain $p_{RC}(t)$.** Using the standard FT pair and the convolution property (RC spectrum = rectangle $\otimes$ shifted sinc in frequency), one obtains:

$$p_{RC}(t) = \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{T}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{1 - \left(\dfrac{2\alpha t}{T}\right)^2}$$

**Step 6 — Verify zero ISI at $t = kT$, $k \ne 0$:** Substitute $t = kT$:

- $\mathrm{sinc}(k) = 0$ for all non-zero integer $k$.
- The cosine term in the numerator is finite, and the denominator is non-zero for $k \ne 0$.
- Therefore $p_{RC}(kT) = 0$ for $k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ ✓

**Step 7 — Rolloff-factor behaviour:**
- $\alpha = 0$: Ideal sinc pulse (brick-wall spectrum, infinite time support, zero excess bandwidth)
- $\alpha = 1$: Full cosine roll-off (bandwidth $= R_s$, twice the minimum)
- $0 < \alpha < 1$: Trade-off between bandwidth and time-domain tail decay.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SRRC Split and Zero-ISI at the Decision Point

**Setup:** Transmit filter $h_T(t)$ and receive filter $h_R(t)$ are LTI systems in cascade. The total response seen at the sampler is $h_{eq}(t) = (h_T * h_R)(t)$.

**Goal:** Choose $H_T(f)$ and $H_R(f)$ such that $H_{eq}(f) = P_{RC}(f)$.

**Step 1 — Frequency-domain cascade:** $H_{eq}(f) = H_T(f) \cdot H_R(f)$.

**Step 2 — Symmetric split:** $H_T(f) = H_R(f) = \sqrt{P_{RC}(f)}$.

**Step 3 — The time-domain SRRC pulse** is the inverse FT of the square root of the RC spectrum. This integral has no closed form in elementary functions, so it is given implicitly:

$$p_{SRRC}(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \sqrt{P_{RC}(f)} \, e^{j2\pi f t} df$$

**Step 4 — For practical simulation and FPGA implementation**, the SRRC impulse response is often tabulated or generated by the following closed-form approximation (Hayes):

$$p_{SRRC}(t) = \dfrac{\sin\!\left(\pi t (1-\alpha)/T\right) + \dfrac{4\alpha t}{T}\cos\!\left(\pi t (1+\alpha)/T\right)}{\dfrac{\pi t}{T}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{4\alpha t}{T}\right)^2\right]}$$

**Step 5 — Verify zero ISI:** Convolve two SRRC filters: $p_{SRRC}(t) * p_{SRRC}(t) = p_{RC}(t)$. Since the RC pulse satisfies the Nyquist criterion, the *combined* response has zero ISI. Each individual SRRC alone does **not** have zero ISI — this is the central reason for splitting.

---

## 3.4 Analytic Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A binary communication system uses a rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ and duration $T$ over an AWGN channel with PSD $N_0/2$. Find the matched filter impulse response and the maximum output SNR.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Signal definition:

$$s(t) = A \cdot \mathrm{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t - T/2}{T}\right)$$

i.e., $s(t) = A$ for $0 \le t \le T$ and $0$ elsewhere.

**Step 2:** Matched filter impulse response:

$$h(t) = s(T - t) = A \cdot \mathrm{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{T - t - T/2}{T}\right) = A \cdot \mathrm{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{T/2 - t}{T}\right)$$

This is again a rectangular pulse of amplitude $A$ on $[0, T]$.

**Step 3:** Signal energy:

$$E_s = \int_0^T A^2 dt = A^2 T$$

**Step 4:** Maximum output SNR:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\max} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0} = \dfrac{2A^2 T}{N_0}$$

**Step 5:** Bit Error Probability (for binary signalling with antipodal pulses):

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{A^2 T}{N_0}}\right)$$

where $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2} du$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Transmit–Receive Chain with Pulse Shaping and Matched Filtering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcBits[Binary Source Bits] --> map[Symbol Mapper a_k in plus minus 1]
    map --> upsamp[Upsampler 1 symbol to N samples]
    upsamp --> txFilt[Tx Pulse Shaping Filter p_T of t]
    txFilt --> dac[DAC and Analog Front End]
    dac --> chan[AWGN Channel plus n of t]
    chan --> adc[ADC and Analog Front End]
    adc --> rxFilt[Rx Matched Filter p_R of t]
    rxFilt --> sampler[Sampler at t equal to kT]
    sampler --> thresh[Threshold Detector]
    thresh --> recBits[Received Bits]
    classDef tx fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0a4a8a,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#ffe2c2,stroke:#a14a00,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#444,color:#000
    class srcBits,map,upsamp,txFilt,dac tx
    class adc,rxFilt,sampler,thresh,recBits rx
    class chan ch
```

> **Figure 4.1 — Functional block architecture** showing the **transmit-side pulse shaper** $p_T(t)$ and the **receive-side matched filter** $p_R(t)$, with the AWGN channel between them. The split is typically $p_T = p_R = $ SRRC, so the overall response is RC (zero ISI).

---

## 4.2 Functional Flow of Matched-Filter Detection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inSig[Received Signal r of t equals s of t plus n of t] --> mult[Multiplier with Template s of t]
    mult --> integ[Integrator over 0 to T]
    integ --> sample[Sample at t equal to T]
    sample --> decision{Threshold Compare}
    decision --> yes[Decide bit 1]
    decision --> no[Decide bit 0]
    classDef sig fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0a4a8a,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#fff2b3,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1f6f1f,color:#000
    class inSig sig
    class mult,integ,sample proc
    class decision,yes,no dec
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Correlator / matched-filter receiver** modelled as **multiply → integrate → sample → threshold**. This is mathematically identical to matched filtering.

---

## 4.3 Pulse Shaping in the Frequency Domain — RC Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    f0[Frequency f] --> reg1[Flat region 0 to 1 minus alpha over 2T]
    reg1 --> roll[Cosine rolloff region]
    roll --> reg2[Zero region beyond 1 plus alpha over 2T]
    reg1 -. value T .-> label1[Amplitude T]
    roll -. smooth drop .-> label2[Cosine to 0]
    reg2 -. amplitude 0 .-> label3[Stopband]
    classDef freq fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#0a4a8a,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    class f0,label1,label2,label3 freq
    class reg1,roll,reg2 reg
```

> **Figure 4.3 — Conceptual RC spectrum** showing the three regions: flat passband, smooth cosine roll-off, and stopband. Total bandwidth is $B = \dfrac{1 + \alpha}{2T}$.

---

## 4.4 Matched-Filter Geometry (Signal Space Picture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    obs[Received Vector r in Hilbert Space] --> proj[Project onto s Vector]
    proj --> decide[Decide symbol based on sign of projection]
    noise[Noise Vector n] -. perpendicular component .-> obs
    classDef vec fill:#d6e8ff,stroke:#0a4a8a,color:#000
    classDef noise fill:#f7d6d6,stroke:#a00000,color:#000
    class obs,proj,decide vec
    class noise noise
```

> **Figure 4.4 — Geometric interpretation** of the matched filter: the **received vector** is projected onto the **known signal direction** in signal space. The noise component orthogonal to $s(t)$ is rejected; only the component along $s(t)$ contributes to the decision variable.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define pulse shaping. Why is it necessary in digital communication systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pulse shaping is the process of **shaping the transmitted symbol waveform** $p(t)$ in a digital communication system to control its **bandwidth** and **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)**. (1 mark)

It is necessary because:
- **Bandwidth limitation** — Real channels (wire, fibre, wireless) have finite bandwidth; an unshaped rectangular pulse has a sinc spectrum with very slowly decaying sidelobes, occupying excessive bandwidth. (1 mark)
- **ISI control** — Bandlimiting in the channel disperses pulses in time, causing neighbouring symbols to overlap. A properly shaped pulse (e.g., Raised Cosine) has **zero crossings at all multiples of the symbol period $T$**, eliminating ISI. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: State the matched filter condition for a known signal $s(t)$ observed over $[0, T]$ in the presence of AWGN.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The matched filter is the linear filter that **maximises the output SNR** at the optimal sampling instant $t = T$. (1 mark)

For a known signal $s(t)$ over $[0, T]$ corrupted by AWGN of two-sided PSD $N_0/2$, the matched filter impulse response is:

$$h(t) = k\, s(T - t) \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

The maximum achievable output SNR is:

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\max} = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

where $E_s = \int_0^T s^2(t)\, dt$ is the signal energy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the impulse response of the matched filter for a known signal $s(t)$ transmitted over an AWGN channel. Show that the maximum output SNR is $\dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary communication system with antipodal signalling using rectangular pulses of amplitude $A$ and width $T$, design the matched filter, find the output SNR, and sketch the eye diagram if a raised-cosine pulse with $\alpha = 0.5$ is used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the received signal and the general linear filter:** (1 mark)
$$r(t) = s(t) + n(t), \qquad y(t) = \int_0^T r(\tau) h(t - \tau) d\tau$$

**Step 2 — Split the output into signal and noise components:** (1 mark)
$$y(T) = \int_0^T s(\tau) h(T - \tau) d\tau + \int_0^T n(\tau) h(T - \tau) d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Compute noise variance at the output using AWGN PSD $N_0/2$:** (1 mark)
$$\sigma_n^2 = E[y_n^2(T)] = \dfrac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T h^2(\tau) d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Form the SNR and apply Cauchy–Schwarz:** (2 marks)
$$\mathrm{SNR} = \dfrac{\left[\int_0^T s(\tau) h(T - \tau) d\tau\right]^2}{\dfrac{N_0}{2} \int_0^T h^2(\tau) d\tau} \le \dfrac{2}{N_0} \int_0^T s^2(t) dt = \dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the optimal filter from the equality condition:** (1 mark)
$$h(t) = k\, s(T - t) \quad \text{(matched filter)}$$

**Step 6 — Conclude with the physical interpretation:** (1 mark)
The maximum SNR depends only on $E_s$ and $N_0$, independent of the pulse shape.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Define the binary antipodal signals:** (1 mark)
$$s_1(t) = +A \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T, \qquad s_0(t) = -A \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T$$

**Step 2 — Matched filter for $s_1(t)$:** (1 mark)
$$h(t) = s_1(T - t) = A \cdot \mathrm{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t - T/2}{T}\right) \quad \text{(rectangular pulse of amplitude } A \text{ on } [0, T])$$

**Step 3 — Compute signal energy:** (1 mark)
$$E_s = \int_0^T A^2 dt = A^2 T$$

**Step 4 — Compute output SNR:** (1 mark)
$$\mathrm{SNR} = \dfrac{2A^2 T}{N_0}$$

**Step 5 — Bit error probability:** (1 mark)
$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{A^2 T}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Eye diagram with raised-cosine pulse ($\alpha = 0.5$):** (2 marks)
- The eye is **open** at the sampling instant (no ISI because RC satisfies Nyquist's criterion).
- The **vertical eye opening** is $2A$ (full swing between $+A$ and $-A$).
- The **horizontal eye opening** is $T$ (no zero-crossing jitter in the ideal noiseless case).
- With $\alpha = 0.5$, the bandwidth is $B = \dfrac{1.5}{2T} = \dfrac{0.75}{T}$, 50% larger than the minimum.
- Noise causes vertical closure proportional to $\sqrt{N_0/T}$, and timing jitter causes horizontal closure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Matched Filter Derivations**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the AWGN PSD:** Use $S_n(f) = N_0/2$ (two-sided). Students often write $S_n(f) = N_0$, leading to a factor-of-2 error in SNR. **Loss: ~2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing the integration variable after substitution:** When substituting $\tau' = T - \tau$, the limits flip from $[0, T]$ to $[T, 0]$; flip them back, and remember $d\tau = -d\tau'$. **Loss: ~1 mark.**
> 3. **Not citing Cauchy–Schwarz by name:** The examiner looks for explicit citation. A correct derivation without the name may lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up $E_b$ and $E_s$:** For binary signalling, $E_s = E_b$. For $M$-ary, $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$. **Loss: ~1 mark if used wrongly.**

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Nyquist criterion for zero ISI. Derive the raised-cosine spectrum and show that the corresponding time-domain pulse has zero crossings at $t = \pm T, \pm 2T, \ldots$ **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a system using SRRC filters with roll-off $\alpha = 0.25$ and symbol rate $R_s = 1$ Msps, find: (i) the absolute transmit bandwidth, (ii) the spectral efficiency, (iii) the time-domain SRRC pulse value at $t = T/2$, and (iv) the noise equivalent bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — State the Nyquist criterion:** (1 mark)
A pulse $p(t)$ produces zero ISI at sampling instants $kT$ iff $p(kT) = \delta_{k0}$.

**Step 2 — Frequency-domain equivalent:** (1 mark)
$$\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} P\!\left(f - \frac{m}{T}\right) = T$$

**Step 3 — Conjecture the RC spectrum with three regions** (passband, roll-off, stopband): (1 mark)
- $P_{RC}(f) = T$ for $\vert f \vert \le \dfrac{1-\alpha}{2T}$
- $P_{RC}(f) = \dfrac{T}{2}\left[1 + \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi T}{\alpha}\left(\vert f \vert - \dfrac{1-\alpha}{2T}\right)\right)\right]$ for $\dfrac{1-\alpha}{2T} \le \vert f \vert \le \dfrac{1+\alpha}{2T}$
- $P_{RC}(f) = 0$ otherwise

**Step 4 — Inverse Fourier transform** to obtain: (2 marks)
$$p_{RC}(t) = \mathrm{sinc}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{T}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi \alpha t}{T}\right)}{1 - \left(\dfrac{2\alpha t}{T}\right)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Verify zero crossings at $t = kT$, $k \ne 0$:** (2 marks)
At $t = kT$, $\mathrm{sinc}(k) = \sin(\pi k)/(\pi k) = 0$ for all non-zero integer $k$. The cosine numerator is finite ($\cos(\pi \alpha k) \in [-1, 1]$) and the denominator is non-zero ($1 - 4\alpha^2 k^2 \ne 0$ for $k \ne 0$ since $\alpha \le 1$). Therefore $p_{RC}(kT) = 0$. ✓

#### Model Solution to Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:** $\alpha = 0.25$, $R_s = 10^6$ symbols/s, so $T = 1 \mu s$.

**(i) Absolute transmit bandwidth:** (2 marks)
$$B = \dfrac{1+\alpha}{2T} = \dfrac{1.25 \times 10^6}{2} = 0.625 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Spectral efficiency** (bits/s/Hz, for binary signalling with $M=2$): (2 marks)
$$\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B} = \dfrac{R_s \log_2 2}{B} = \dfrac{10^6}{0.625 \times 10^6} = 1.6 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

**(iii) Time-domain SRRC pulse value at $t = T/2$:** (1 mark)
Substituting $t = T/2$ into the SRRC formula:
$$p_{SRRC}(T/2) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.75) + 4\alpha \cdot 0.5 \cdot \cos(\pi \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1.25)}{(\pi \cdot 0.5)(1 - (2 \cdot 0.25)^2)} = \dfrac{\sin(3\pi/8) + 0.5 \cos(5\pi/8)}{(\pi/2)(1 - 0.25)}$$
$$= \dfrac{0.924 + 0.5 \cdot (-0.383)}{(\pi/2)(0.75)} = \dfrac{0.924 - 0.191}{1.178} = \dfrac{0.733}{1.178} \approx 0.622$$

**(iv) Noise equivalent bandwidth of SRRC** = same as RC: **(2 marks)**
$$B_{NEB} = \dfrac{1}{2T}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} P_{RC}(f) df / \max P_{RC}(f) = \dfrac{1}{2T} \cdot \dfrac{E_p}{T} = \dfrac{1}{2T} \quad (\text{asymptotically as } \alpha \to 0)$$

For finite $\alpha$, the noise bandwidth is:
$$B_{NEB} = \dfrac{1+\alpha}{2T} = B \approx 0.625 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Pulse Shaping Problems**
> 1. **Spectral efficiency units confusion:** The unit is **bits/s/Hz** (or **bit/s/Hz**). Students sometimes write "bits/Hz" or forget the time dimension. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Roll-off factor and bandwidth:** For $\alpha = 0$, $B = R_s/2$ (the **Nyquist minimum**); for $\alpha > 0$, the bandwidth is *larger* than the minimum. Many students write the wrong formula. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **"Ideal" sinc vs RC:** The sinc pulse ($\alpha = 0$) is *theoretically* zero-ISI but practically unrealizable (infinite time support, non-causal). RC is the practical realisation. Examiners expect you to *mention* this. **Loss: 1 mark if omitted.**
> 4. **Forgetting to apply both the sinc factor AND the cosine factor** in $p_{RC}(t)$. Many students stop at sinc and lose 2 marks for the second factor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pulse Shaping and Matched Filtering**

- [x] **Pulse shaping purpose:** confine bandwidth + control ISI.
- [x] **Transmit signal:** $x(t) = \sum a_k p(t - kT)$.
- [x] **Nyquist first criterion:** $p(kT) = \delta_{k0}$ for zero ISI.
- [x] **Raised cosine bandwidth:** $B = \dfrac{1+\alpha}{2T}$; minimum is $R_s/2$ at $\alpha=0$.
- [x] **RC time-domain pulse:** $p_{RC}(t) = \mathrm{sinc}(t/T) \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\pi\alpha t/T)}{1 - (2\alpha t/T)^2}$.
- [x] **SRRC split:** $P_{TX}(f) = P_{RX}(f) = \sqrt{P_{RC}(f)}$; cascade gives RC.
- [x] **Eye opening** is widest at $\alpha = 1$ (most ISI-tolerant) and narrowest at $\alpha = 0$ (sensitive to timing).
- [x] **Matched filter IR:** $h(t) = k\, s(T - t)$ — time-reversed, delayed copy of $s(t)$.
- [x] **Matched filter output:** $y(T) = k \int_0^T r(\tau) s(\tau) d\tau$.
- [x] **Max output SNR:** $\dfrac{2E_s}{N_0}$ — **shape-independent**.
- [x] **Cauchy–Schwarz** is the heart of the matched-filter proof.
- [x] **Correlator ≡ Matched filter** (mathematical equivalence).
- [x] **AWGN PSD:** two-sided $N_0/2$ — **never** $N_0$.
- [x] **Bit energy vs symbol energy:** $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$.
- [x] **BEP for binary antipodal:** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$.
- [x] **Real-world usage:** LTE (SRRC $\alpha \approx 0.22$), Wi-Fi, 5G NR, satellite comms, DSL.
- [x] **Pitfall:** SRRC alone does NOT have zero ISI; only the *cascade* (RC) does.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the **block diagram** of the transmit-receive chain when answering any pulse-shaping question, even if the question does not explicitly ask for it. Examiners award 1–2 marks for a clear, labelled block diagram. Similarly, always **state the matched filter condition as a formal theorem** (premise → result), not as a vague description.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eye Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - Communication Engineering Laboratory
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Eye Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Eye Diagram** is an experimental oscilloscopic tool used in digital communication systems to qualitatively assess the signal integrity and quantitatively measure the performance of a digital baseband transmission link. It is generated by superimposing multiple successive symbol (bit) periods of a received random data waveform on a single time-domain display, triggered synchronously at the data clock (or bit) rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *"The eye diagram is an overlay of all possible symbol-to-symbol transitions of a digital signal, triggered at the symbol rate, providing a visual estimate of the Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI), noise margin, timing jitter, and Bit Error Rate (BER) of the communication channel."*

The "eye" gets its name from the resemblance of the open central region of the pattern to a human eye. The **opening** (vertical and horizontal) directly indicates how robust the system is against noise and timing errors.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy **railway crossing** where many trains of different colors and lengths pass at regular intervals, and you take a **long-exposure photograph**. The cars overlap on the track, but the *clear space* in the middle of the tracks tells you exactly how much "room" a pedestrian (noise/error) has before getting hit.

- **The Trains** = Digital pulses of 1s and 0s
- **The Long-Exposure Photograph** = The eye diagram
- **The Empty Space in the Middle** = The "Eye Opening" (your safety margin)

Another way to think of it: if you fold a long random bit stream like a piece of paper, repeating every 1-bit-period section on top of each other, the resulting **envelope of all possible transitions** forms the eye.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for First-Time Learners:**
> The eye diagram is *not* a single waveform — it is a **statistical superposition** of thousands of bits. The clearer and wider the eye opens, the better your digital link is performing.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Eye Height (Eh)** — Vertical opening; **noise margin** in volts.
- **Eye Width (Ew)** — Horizontal opening; **timing jitter margin** in seconds.
- **Q-Factor** — Statistical measure of signal-to-noise ratio at the decision instant.
- **Bit Error Rate (BER)** — Probability of incorrect bit detection.
- **Rise Time (tr)** and **Fall Time (tf)** — 10%–90% transition times.
- **Jitter (σj)** — RMS horizontal deviation of zero-crossing.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How an Eye Diagram is Formed (Step-by-Step)

The generation of an eye diagram in a lab setting follows a strict sequence:

1. **Random Binary Data Generation**
   A Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence (PRBS) generator produces a stream of 0s and 1s (e.g., $2^{15}-1$ length, per ITU-T O.150 standard).

2. **Pulse Shaping / Encoding**
   The data is shaped using line codes (NRZ, RZ, Manchester) and passed through a **bandlimited channel** (cable, filter, or channel emulator).

3. **Synchronous Triggering**
   The oscilloscope is triggered using the **clock signal (recovered or transmitted)** — this is critical. Each trigger edge starts a new horizontal sweep.

4. **Multi-Sweep Superposition**
   The oscilloscope holds previous traces on the display (using **persistence mode**). After 1000+ sweeps, the persistent display shows the envelope of all transitions.

5. **Resulting Pattern**
   The superposition of all possible transitions (0→0, 0→1, 1→0, 1→1) forms the "eye."

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind the Eye

- **Why vertical opening matters:** The decision threshold (typically 0 V for bipolar, +V/2 for unipolar) sits in the middle. The **more vertical space** around the threshold, the **more noise can be tolerated** before a bit is misread.
- **Why horizontal opening matters:** The decision instant is at the **center of the bit period**. The **more horizontal space** around the sampling instant, the **less sensitive** the system is to clock jitter or timing skew.
- **Why it shows ISI:** When past bits "smear" into the current bit, the trace at the sampling instant is fuzzy. This **fuzziness closes the eye vertically**.
- **Why it shows noise:** Random noise adds to the signal, blurring both levels → **vertical and horizontal shrinkage**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Expression** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eye Height $E_h$ | $E_h = \mu_1 - \mu_0 - 2(\sigma_1 + \sigma_0)$ | Vertical opening at sampling instant | Volts (V) |
| Eye Width $E_w$ | $E_w = T_b - \Delta t_{\text{ISI}} - 2\sigma_j$ | Horizontal opening across bit period | Seconds (s) |
| Q-Factor | $Q = \dfrac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}$ | Statistical SNR metric | Dimensionless |
| Bit Error Rate (BER) | $\text{BER} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | Probability of bit error | Dimensionless |
| SNR at Decision | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{(\mu_1-\mu_0)^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_0^2}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | Dimensionless |
| Rise Time $t_r$ | $t_r = t_{90\%} - t_{10\%}$ | 10% to 90% transition | Seconds (s) |
| Maximum Jitter | $J_{pp} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{max} - \Delta t_{min}}{T_b} \times 100\%$ | Peak-to-peak jitter | % of UI |
| Channel Capacity | $C = 2B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon limit (related context) | bps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "HEWJ" Rule:** The four most-asked parameters are **H**eight, **E**ye width, **W**idth, and **J**itter. Memorize the formulas for these first.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical fiber links:** Engineers use eye diagrams to qualify transceivers (e.g., 10G, 100G Ethernet, OTU standards).
- **Wireless base stations:** Evaluates EVM (Error Vector Magnitude) by observing eye closure.
- **PCB design:** Verifies high-speed serial links (PCIe, USB 3.x, HDMI, SerDes).
- **Satellite communication:** Confirms compliance with DVB-S2 waveforms.
- **5G/6G mmWave:** Used to evaluate OFDM signal constellation and PAPR (Peak-to-Average Power Ratio).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Eye Height and Noise Margin

**Given:** A binary digital signal with two nominal levels: logic 1 mean $\mu_1$ and logic 0 mean $\mu_0$. The decision threshold $V_{th}$ is placed at the midpoint of these levels.

**Step 1 — Decision Threshold Placement**
For symmetric noise distributions, the optimal threshold is:

$$V_{th} = \frac{\mu_1 + \mu_0}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Eye Height**
The eye height at the sampling instant is the minimum distance from the lower trace to the upper trace *after* accounting for noise variance.

$$E_h = (\mu_1 - k\sigma_1) - (\mu_0 + k\sigma_0)$$

where $k$ is a confidence multiplier (typically $k=3$ for 99.7% confidence in Gaussian noise).

**Step 3 — Simplification**
Assuming approximately equal noise on both levels ($\sigma_1 \approx \sigma_0 = \sigma$):

$$E_h = (\mu_1 - \mu_0) - 2k\sigma$$

**Step 4 — Defining the Q-Factor**
The signal-to-noise ratio is expressed as the Q-factor:

$$Q = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0} \approx \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{2\sigma}$$

**Step 5 — Final Compact Form**
Substituting the Q-factor into the eye height expression:

$$E_h = 2\sigma(Q - k)$$

> **Conversion Logic Summary:**
> When $Q \gg k$, the eye is wide open (low BER). When $Q \approx k$, the eye is about to close. When $Q < k$, the eye is closed (high BER, link failure).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bit Error Rate from Q-Factor

**Given:** Gaussian noise with zero mean and standard deviation $\sigma$ added to each level.

**Step 1 — Probability of Error for Logic 0 read as 1**
Using the complementary error function for the upper tail:

$$P(0\to1) = \int_{V_{th}}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(x-\mu_0)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) dx$$

**Step 2 — Apply Change of Variable**
Let $u = \dfrac{x - \mu_0}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}$:

$$P(0\to1) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{V_{th}-\mu_0}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Threshold**
With $V_{th} = (\mu_1 + \mu_0)/2$:

$$P(0\to1) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{2\sigma\sqrt{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Q-Factor**
The argument of the erfc is precisely $Q/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\boxed{\text{BER} = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}$$

> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (Board Examiner's Favorite):**
> $Q = 6$ → $\text{BER} \approx 10^{-9}$ (telecom-grade)
> $Q = 7$ → $\text{BER} \approx 10^{-12}$ (high-reliability)

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Eye Closure Causes

| **Effect** | **Physical Cause** | **Visual Signature in Eye** | **Engineering Fix** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ISI (Inter-Symbol Interference)** | Bandlimited channel, multipath | Inner trace crosses decision threshold mid-eye | Equalizer (FFE/DFE) |
| **Additive Noise** | Thermal noise, shot noise | Thickened top/bottom lines | Higher SNR, shielding, filtering |
| **Timing Jitter** | Clock instability, PLL phase noise | Slanted/curved zero-crossings | Better clock recovery (PLL/CDR) |
| **Crosstalk** | Adjacent traces in PCB | Periodic vertical disturbances | Ground isolation, twisted pairs |
| **Impedance Mismatch** | Reflections on transmission line | Ringing/overshoot in transitions | Termination resistors (50Ω) |
| **Non-linearity** | Amplifier saturation | Asymmetric eye, asymmetric levels | Linear amplifiers, AGC |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block Diagram

The following block diagram shows the complete laboratory setup for generating an eye diagram on a Communication Lab trainer kit or using discrete instruments.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    prbs["PRBS Generator 2 to the power 15 minus 1"] --> coder["Line Coder NRZ or RZ or Polar"]
    coder --> channel["Channel Cable or Filter or AWGN Emulator"]
    channel --> attn["Variable Attenuator SNR Control"]
    attn --> scope["Digital Storage Oscilloscope DSO with Persistence Mode"]
    clk["Bit Clock from PRBS"] --> scope
    scope --> display["Display Overlay of Multiple Traces"]
    display --> measure["Measure Eh Ew Tr Tf Jitter"]
```

> **Explanation of Flow:**
> 1. **PRBS Generator** creates a known random binary sequence.
> 2. **Line Coder** maps the bits to voltage levels.
> 3. **Channel** introduces realistic impairments (bandlimit, noise, ISI).
> 4. **Variable Attenuator** lets the student vary SNR.
> 5. **DSO** is triggered by the clock and overlays the sweeps using **persistence** (infinite or long).
> 6. The result is the **eye pattern**, from which the student measures all parameters.

---

### 4.2 Annotated Eye Diagram Schematic

The following ASCII schematic shows the labelled structure of a typical NRZ eye diagram. The student should be able to draw and label this in any KTU 3-mark question.

```
                    +Vm  ___     ___
       Upper Level /|    /   \   /   \   |
                  / |   /     \ /     \  |
                 /  +---/------X-------\-+-----  Top of Eye
                /   |  /       |        \
               /    | /        |         \
              /     |/         |          \
   Decision -------+----------+-----------+--- Vth = 0V
   Threshold      /|           |           |\
                / |            |            | \
               /  |            |            |  \
              /   |            |            |   \
   Lower Level/-Vm  \         /|\         / 
                  \  \       / | \       /
                   \  \_____/  |  \_____/
                    +----------+----------+
                       T0      Ts        T0+Tb
                              Eye
                              Width
```

### 4.3 Lab Procedure Flow (Step-by-Step)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Connect PRBS to channel input"] --> B["Step 2: Set line coder to NRZ-Polar"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Connect clock to DSO external trigger"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Set DSO to Persistence or Infinite mode"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Adjust time base to show 1.5 to 2 bit periods"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Vary channel SNR via attenuator"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Capture and freeze the eye pattern"]
    G --> H["Step 8: Measure Eh Ew Tr Tf Jitter Q-factor"]
    H --> I["Step 9: Calculate BER from Q-factor"]
    I --> J["Step 10: Tabulate and plot BER vs SNR curve"]
```

---

### 4.4 Signal-Level Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rx["Received Signal + Noise"] --> samp["Sample at Ts = nTb"]
    samp --> comp{"V s amp greater than Vth?"}
    comp -- Yes --> out1["Decision Logic 1"]
    comp -- No --> out0["Decision Logic 0"]
    out1 --> ber["Count Errors vs Transmitted"]
    out0 --> ber
    ber --> calc["BER = Errors divided by Total Bits"]
```

> **Why this matters for the eye diagram:** The horizontal position $T_s$ chosen in the decision must lie in the **widest vertical opening** of the eye (the so-called "best sampling instant"). This is the point of maximum noise immunity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
*Define an eye diagram. List any four parameters that can be measured from it.*

**Model Answer:**

An eye diagram is a **graphical overlay** of all possible symbol-to-symbol transitions of a digital data signal on an oscilloscope, displayed using persistence mode and triggered by the bit/symbol clock. It provides a visual estimate of the **signal integrity** of a digital communication link.

The four measurable parameters are:
1. **Eye Height ($E_h$)** — indicates vertical noise margin.
2. **Eye Width ($E_w$)** — indicates horizontal timing margin.
3. **Rise Time ($t_r$)** and **Fall Time ($t_f$)** — indicate transition speed.
4. **Jitter ($\sigma_j$)** — indicates horizontal zero-crossing deviation.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for any four correct parameters with units.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is inter-symbol interference (ISI) and how does it affect the eye pattern?*

**Model Answer:**

**ISI** is the phenomenon where the pulse response of one bit **spills over** into the adjacent bit intervals, due to bandlimiting of the channel or multipath propagation.

**Effect on the eye pattern:**
- The **vertical opening** of the eye is reduced (lower noise margin).
- The **eye becomes partially closed** at the decision instant.
- A higher **BER** results for a given SNR.

ISI is mitigated using **pulse shaping filters** (raised cosine, root-raised cosine) and **equalizers** (transversal/adaptive).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Part B questions are answered with an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the experimental procedure to generate and display an **eye diagram** of an NRZ data signal. Mention the role of clock synchronization. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A digital communication system transmits binary data with nominal levels $\mu_1 = +1\text{ V}$ and $\mu_0 = -1\text{ V}$. The noise standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.1\text{ V}$ on each level. Calculate the **Q-factor**, **eye height** (assuming $k=3$), and the approximate **BER**. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Block Diagram:** (as shown in Section 4.1 above).

**Procedure:**

1. Generate a PRBS data stream (e.g., $2^{15}-1$) using the trainer kit.
2. Encode the data using the **NRZ-Polar line coder** (logic 1 → +V, logic 0 → −V).
3. Pass the signal through a **bandlimited channel** (cable or active filter) to introduce controlled ISI.
4. Connect the **clock output** from the PRBS generator to the **external trigger** of the DSO. This ensures every sweep is phase-aligned to the bit boundary — **this is the role of clock synchronization**.
5. Set the DSO to **persistence mode (infinite)** so that successive sweeps overlay.
6. Adjust the time base to display **1.5 to 2 bit periods** centered on the transition.
7. The overlaid pattern reveals the **eye**.
8. Vary the channel attenuation to observe **eye closure** with decreasing SNR.

**Role of Clock Synchronization:**
The clock signal aligns each sweep to the **same instant in every bit period**, so that transitions from many bits fall exactly on top of each other. Without synchronous triggering, the eye would be a meaningless blur.

*(4 marks for block diagram + procedure, 2 marks for role of clock, 1 mark for the relationship between attenuation and eye closure.)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Given:** $\mu_1 = +1\,\text{V}$, $\mu_0 = -1\,\text{V}$, $\sigma_1 = \sigma_0 = \sigma = 0.1\,\text{V}$, $k = 3$.

**Step 1 — Compute Q-Factor**

$$Q = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0} = \frac{1 - (-1)}{0.1 + 0.1} = \frac{2}{0.2} = 10$$

**Step 2 — Compute Eye Height**

$$E_h = (\mu_1 - \mu_0) - 2k\sigma = 2 - 2(3)(0.1) = 2 - 0.6 = 1.4\,\text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute BER**

$$\text{BER} = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{10}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(7.07)$$

Using standard tables: $\text{erfc}(7.07) \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-24}$

$$\boxed{\text{BER} \approx 3.75 \times 10^{-24}}$$

*(1 mark formula + 1 mark substitution for Q, 1 mark formula + 1 mark substitution for Eh, 1 mark formula + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark final answer for BER.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the various **parameters measured from an eye diagram** with the help of a neatly labelled diagram. Briefly describe how each parameter is affected by channel noise and ISI. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Q-factor of 6 is measured on an eye diagram of a digital link. Find the corresponding **BER** and comment on the **link quality**. *(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Labelled Diagram:** (as in Section 4.2 above).

**Parameters Measured:**

| Parameter | Definition | Effect of Noise | Effect of ISI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eye Height $E_h$ | Vertical opening at sampling instant | Decreases (trace thickens) | Decreases (fuzzy at center) |
| Eye Width $E_w$ | Horizontal opening | Unchanged (slightly) | Decreases (zero-crossings shift) |
| Rise Time $t_r$ | 10% to 90% transition | Unchanged | Increased (slower) |
| Fall Time $t_f$ | 90% to 10% transition | Unchanged | Increased |
| Jitter $\sigma_j$ | Horizontal deviation of crossing | Slight increase | Large increase |
| SNR Margin | Ratio of $E_h$ to noise floor | Decreases directly | Decreases |

*(3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for parameter definitions, 2 marks for noise/ISI effects.)*

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Given:** $Q = 6$.

**Step 1 — Apply BER Formula**

$$\text{BER} = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{Q}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{6}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(4.243)$$

**Step 2 — Use Standard erfc Table**
For $u = 4.243$: $\text{erfc}(4.243) \approx 2.21 \times 10^{-9}$

**Step 3 — Compute BER**

$$\boxed{\text{BER} \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Link Quality**

A BER of $\approx 10^{-9}$ corresponds to approximately **1 error in 1 billion bits** transmitted. This is the **standard telecom-grade quality** (e.g., acceptable for voice, data, and most broadband services). The link is **excellent and suitable for commercial deployment**.

*(2 marks for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for numerical value, 3 marks for engineering interpretation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing eye width with eye height** — Eye width is *horizontal* (time), eye height is *vertical* (voltage). Mixing them up = **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $k$-factor in eye height formula** — Writing $E_h = \mu_1 - \mu_0$ without subtracting $2k\sigma$ loses **1 mark**.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of $\sqrt{2}$ in BER formula** — The correct argument is $Q/\sqrt{2}$, *not* $Q$. This is the single most common **arithmetic error** in Part B.
> 4. **No labelled diagram = lose 2 marks** — KTU examiners *always* deduct marks for an unlabelled or missing diagram in eye-diagram questions.
> 5. **Not mentioning clock synchronization** — A 7-mark question that asks "how to generate" must explicitly mention **synchronous triggering**. Omitting it = **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 6. **Units!** — Eye height is in *Volts*, eye width in *seconds* (or fraction of UI). Missing units lose 0.5 marks each.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ✅ An **eye diagram** is a *superposition of many bit intervals*, triggered by the **bit clock**.
- ✅ It must be viewed in **DSO persistence mode** (not single-sweep).
- ✅ **Vertical opening (Eye Height)** ↔ **Noise Margin**.
- ✅ **Horizontal opening (Eye Width)** ↔ **Timing Jitter Margin**.
- ✅ The **decision instant** must be placed at the **widest vertical opening** of the eye.
- ✅ **Q-Factor**: $Q = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)/(\sigma_1 + \sigma_0)$ — dimensionless.
- ✅ **BER formula**: $\text{BER} = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(Q/\sqrt{2})$ — *do not forget the $\sqrt{2}$*.
- ✅ **Eye Height with k-sigma noise margin**: $E_h = (\mu_1 - \mu_0) - 2k\sigma$ (typically $k=3$).
- ✅ **ISI** *closes* the eye vertically and horizontally; **noise** *thickens* the traces.
- ✅ **Q = 6 → BER ≈ 10⁻⁹**, **Q = 7 → BER ≈ 10⁻¹²** — board-favorite numerical benchmarks.
- ✅ **Best sampling point** = center of the eye, vertically and horizontally.
- ✅ Always **label** your diagram: top level, bottom level, decision threshold, sampling instant, eye height, eye width, rise time, fall time, jitter.
- ✅ The eye diagram is the **single most important diagnostic tool** in any KTU Communication Lab viva.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Performance of BPSK
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Error Performance of BPSK

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)** is a fundamental digital modulation scheme in which the phase of a sinusoidal carrier is shifted between two discrete values, typically **0°** and **180°**, in accordance with the binary information bits `0` and `1`. The **Error Performance of BPSK** refers to the quantitative measure of how reliably the receiver can recover the transmitted bits when the signal is corrupted by **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)**, characterized by the **Bit Error Probability ($P_b$)** or **Bit Error Rate (BER)** as a function of the **Energy per bit to Noise Spectral density ratio ($E_b/N_0$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCECL508 - Module Experiments):**
> The experiment titled *"Error Performance of BPSK"* requires students to demonstrate, simulate (using MATLAB/Octave/Python-SciPy or equivalent), and analytically verify the theoretical BER curve of a BPSK transceiver operating over an AWGN channel. The outcome is measured by comparing the simulated BER (obtained by Monte Carlo trials) with the theoretical expression $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0}\right)$.

Mathematically, the transmitted BPSK signal is given by:

$$
s_i(t) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_b}{T_b}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{for bit } 0 \\[8pt] -\sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_b}{T_b}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{for bit } 1 \end{cases}
$$

where:
- $E_b$ = Energy per transmitted bit (Joules)
- $T_b$ = Bit duration (seconds)
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency (Hz)

The received signal is $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$, where $n(t)$ is the AWGN with two-sided power spectral density $N_0/2$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a **dark, noisy room** trying to communicate with a friend on the other side. You decide to use a simple flashlight and you agree on a rule:
- **Shine the light ON** for a binary `0`
- **Shine the light OFF** (i.e., keep it OFF) for a binary `1`

Now imagine a loud fan is running in the room (this is the **noise**). Sometimes, the fan noise is so loud that your friend cannot tell whether the flashlight is ON or OFF. The **error performance** is essentially the *fraction of times* your friend makes a wrong guess because of this noise.

> **In BPSK terms:**
> - The flashlight ON/OFF corresponds to **two opposite phases** ($0^\circ$ and $180^\circ$) of a carrier wave.
> - The fan noise is the **AWGN** in the channel.
> - Your friend's best strategy is to compare the received signal against a **threshold of zero** (just like checking if light > 0 or light < 0).
> - The louder the noise (lower $E_b/N_0$), the more errors occur.

This intuitive picture immediately reveals **why BPSK is the most robust digital modulation scheme**: the two signals are placed as far apart as possible on the signal constellation (diametrically opposite points), maximizing the noise immunity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board Examiner's Emphasis):**
> - **$E_b$** = Energy per bit (in Joules)
> - **$N_0$** = One-sided noise power spectral density (in W/Hz)
> - **$E_b/N_0$** = Dimensionless signal-to-noise ratio per bit (often expressed in dB)
> - **BER / $P_b$** = Dimensionless probability, ranging from $0$ to $0.5$

---

### 1.3 Why Study BPSK Error Performance in PCECL508?

| Aspect | Real-World Significance |
|---|---|
| Benchmark | BPSK is the **gold standard** against which all other modulation schemes (QPSK, M-PSK, M-QAM) are compared. |
| Foundation | Forms the basis of **Wi-Fi (802.11)**, **satellite telemetry**, **deep-space communication (NASA's Voyager uses BPSK variants)**, and **RFID**. |
| Analytical tractability | Closed-form BER expression allows **easy validation** of simulation experiments in lab courses. |
| Threshold detection | Demonstrates **coherent detection** and **matched filtering** principles. |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Block-Level Architecture of a BPSK Link

The end-to-end BPSK communication system analyzed in the PCECL508 experiment consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Source / Bit Generator:** Produces a random binary sequence $b[k] \in \{0, 1\}$ with equal probability.
2. **NRZ Mapper:** Maps bits to bipolar levels $a[k] \in \{+1, -1\}$ (non-return-to-zero line coding).
3. **BPSK Modulator:** Multiplies the bipolar baseband signal by the carrier $\sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
4. **AWGN Channel:** Adds zero-mean Gaussian noise $n(t)$ with PSD $N_0/2$.
5. **Coherent Demodulator:** Multiplies the received signal by a locally generated carrier and integrates over $T_b$ (matched filter / correlator).
6. **Threshold Detector:** Compares the decision variable $Z$ against the threshold $0$ to recover bits.
7. **BER Counter:** Counts discrepancies between transmitted and received bits.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 – Transmitted symbol mapping:**
$$
a[k] = \begin{cases} +1, & b[k]=0 \\ -1, & b[k]=1 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 – Modulation:**
The modulated waveform is $s(t) = a[k]\,\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ over the $k$-th bit interval.

**Step 3 – Channel corruption:**
$$
r(t) = s(t) + n(t)
$$

**Step 4 – Correlator output (decision variable):**
The optimum receiver computes:
$$
Z = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\,\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt
$$

**Step 5 – Decision rule:**
$$
\hat{b}[k] = \begin{cases} 0, & Z \geq 0 \\ 1, & Z < 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 6 – Error event:**
An error occurs if bit $0$ was sent ($Z$ has mean $+\sqrt{E_b}$) but noise pushes $Z < 0$, *or* if bit $1$ was sent ($Z$ has mean $-\sqrt{E_b}$) but noise pushes $Z \geq 0$.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the high-yield formula set you must memorize for the KTU 2024 Scheme examination.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BPSK Transmitted Signal | $s_i(t) = \pm\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $0 \leq t \leq T_b$ |
| 2 | AWGN Channel Model | $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$, $n(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ | Two-sided PSD = $N_0/2$ |
| 3 | Bit Energy | $E_b = \int_0^{T_b} s^2(t)\,dt$ | Joules |
| 4 | $E_b/N_0$ to SNR Conversion (AWGN) | $\text{SNR} = E_b/N_0 \cdot (R_b/B)$ where $B$ is noise bandwidth | Dimensionless or dB |
| 5 | **Theoretical BER of BPSK** | $\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)}$ | $0 \leq P_b \leq 0.5$ |
| 6 | Q-Function Definition | $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\,du$ | $x \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 7 | $Q$-function in terms of erfc | $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | For all $x \geq 0$ |
| 8 | Decision Variable (correlator) | $Z = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + N$, with $N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ | Gaussian |
| 9 | Euclidean Distance | $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | Between two BPSK points |
| 10 | Symbol Error Rate (SER) | $P_s = P_b$ (for BPSK, since 1 bit per symbol) | $P_s = P_b$ |
| 11 | $E_b/N_0$ (in dB) | $(E_b/N_0)_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}(E_b/N_0)$ | Use dB scale in plots |
| 12 | Asymptotic BER slope | $P_b \propto 1/\sqrt{E_b/N_0}$ at high SNR | Waterfall curve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for the Q-function:** $Q(x)$ is the *tail probability* of a standard Gaussian. As $x$ increases, $Q(x)$ decreases monotonically. Therefore, **higher $E_b/N_0$ ⟹ lower BER**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why BPSK is Chosen |
|---|---|
| **Deep Space Communication (Voyager 1, 2)** | Extreme path loss demands maximum noise immunity. |
| **Satellite TT&C (Telemetry, Tracking, Command)** | Low data rate + high reliability. |
| **Aeronautical Telemetry (PCM/FM)** | Robust against multipath fading. |
| **WLAN 802.11b (legacy 1 Mbps mode)** | Used DSSS with BPSK for the slowest but most reliable rate. |
| **Bluetooth (GFSK, BPSK derivatives)** | Low-power, short-range robust link. |
| **GNSS (GPS L1 C/A legacy)** | BPSK used for civil navigation. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Theoretical BER for BPSK over AWGN

**Step 1 — Transmitted signals:**
Consider two equiprobable messages $m_0$ and $m_1$ transmitted over bit duration $T_b$:

$$
s_0(t) = +\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b
$$

$$
s_1(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b
$$

**Step 2 — Received signal at the input of the correlator:**
$$
r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)
$$
where $n(t)$ is a zero-mean AWGN process with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 3 — Compute the decision variable using the correlator (matched filter):**
$$
Z = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi(t)\,dt,\quad\text{where}\quad \phi(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

**Step 4 — Expand the integral:**
$$
Z = \int_0^{T_b} s_i(t)\phi(t)\,dt \;+\; \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\phi(t)\,dt
$$

Define the signal component and the noise component:
$$
Z = S_i + N
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the signal component for $m_0$ (bit 0):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_0 &= \int_0^{T_b} \left[\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)\right] \cdot \left[\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)\right] dt \\
    &= \frac{2\sqrt{E_b}}{T_b}\int_0^{T_b}\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt \\
