    &= \frac{2\sqrt{E_b}}{T_b}\int_0^{T_b}\frac{1+\cos(4\pi f_c t)}{2}\,dt \\
    &= \frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{T_b}\left[t + \frac{\sin(4\pi f_c t)}{4\pi f_c}\right]_0^{T_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $f_c T_b \gg 1$ (which is the standard narrowband assumption in KTU analysis), the sinusoidal term integrates to approximately zero. Hence:

$$
S_0 = \frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{T_b}\cdot T_b = \sqrt{E_b}
$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the signal component for $m_1$ (bit 1):**
By symmetry, $S_1 = -\sqrt{E_b}$.

**Step 7 — Statistics of the noise component $N$:**
Since $n(t)$ is Gaussian and the integration is a linear operation, $N$ is also Gaussian. The mean is:
$$
\mathbb{E}[N] = \int_0^{T_b} \mathbb{E}[n(t)]\,\phi(t)\,dt = 0
$$

The variance is:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_N^2 &= \mathbb{E}[N^2] = \int_0^{T_b}\!\!\int_0^{T_b} \mathbb{E}[n(t)n(\tau)]\,\phi(t)\phi(\tau)\,dt\,d\tau \\
          &= \int_0^{T_b}\!\!\int_0^{T_b} \frac{N_0}{2}\delta(t-\tau)\,\phi(t)\phi(\tau)\,dt\,d\tau \\
          &= \frac{N_0}{2}\int_0^{T_b}\phi^2(t)\,dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute $\int_0^{T_b} \phi^2(t)\,dt$:**

$$
\int_0^{T_b} \phi^2(t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_b} \frac{2}{T_b}\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt = \frac{2}{T_b}\cdot\frac{T_b}{2} = 1
$$

Therefore:
$$
\sigma_N^2 = \frac{N_0}{2}
$$

**Step 9 — Probability density of the decision variable:**
$$
f_{Z\mid m_0}(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)
$$

$$
f_{Z\mid m_1}(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)
$$

**Step 10 — Probability of error conditioned on $m_0$:**
The error occurs if $Z < 0$ given that $m_0$ was transmitted.

$$
P(e \mid m_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{0} f_{Z\mid m_0}(z)\,dz = P\!\left(N > \sqrt{E_b}\right)
$$

where $N \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$. Standardizing:

$$
P(e \mid m_0) = P\!\left(\frac{N}{\sqrt{N_0/2}} > \frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 11 — Total probability of error:**
By symmetry, $P(e\mid m_1) = P(e \mid m_0)$. With equiprobable messages:

$$
P_b = P(m_0)\,P(e \mid m_0) + P(m_1)\,P(e \mid m_1) = \tfrac{1}{2}Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right) + \tfrac{1}{2}Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}
$$

This is the celebrated closed-form BER expression for BPSK over AWGN.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — BER at Canonical $E_b/N_0$ Values

> [!NOTE]
> **The following values are asked repeatedly in KTU exams and viva voce.**

| $E_b/N_0$ (linear) | $E_b/N_0$ (dB) | $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ | $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.5 | $-3.0103$ dB | $1.000$ | $\approx 1.587 \times 10^{-1}$ | Below the $10^{-1}$ threshold |
| 1.0 | $0$ dB | $1.414$ | $\approx 7.866 \times 10^{-2}$ | Standard reference |
| 2.0 | $3.0103$ dB | $2.000$ | $\approx 2.275 \times 10^{-2}$ | First "good" operating point |
| 5.0 | $6.9897$ dB | $3.162$ | $\approx 7.827 \times 10^{-4}$ | Often used for satellite links |
| 10.0 | $10$ dB | $4.472$ | $\approx 3.872 \times 10^{-6}$ | Typical deep-space telemetry |
| 20.0 | $13.0103$ dB | $6.325$ | $\approx 1.250 \times 10^{-10}$ | Near-flawless link |

---

### 3.3 Approximate High-SNR Expression

For $E_b/N_0 \gg 1$, the BER can be tightly bounded as:

$$
P_b \approx \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\pi E_b/N_0}}\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)
$$

This shows the **exponential decay** of BER with $E_b/N_0$, which is the dominant asymptotic behavior visualized in the classic "waterfall curve".
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a BPSK Error-Performance Experiment

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the signal flow from bit generation through modulation, AWGN channel corruption, demodulation, threshold detection, and BER computation — the exact experiment performed in PCECL508.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SRC: Random Binary Source b k] --> B[NRZ Mapper a k in +1 -1]
    B --> C[BPSK Modulator: a k times cos 2 pi fc t]
    C --> D[AWGN Channel: r t equals s t plus n t]
    D --> E[Coherent Demodulator: Multiply by local carrier]
    E --> F[Integrate and Dump over T b]
    F --> G[Threshold Detector at 0]
    G --> H[Recovered Bits b hat k]
    H --> I[BER Counter]
    A --> I
    I --> J[Compare with Theoretical Curve]
    J --> K[Plot BER versus E sub b over N sub 0]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The lower feedback path from `SRC` to `BER Counter` ensures the transmitted bits are known at the receiver for error counting (offline reference).
- `Integrate and Dump` is mathematically equivalent to the matched filter / correlator.
- The output plot exhibits the classic **waterfall shape** of BPSK BER.

---

### 4.2 BPSK Signal Constellation and Decision Regions

The signal-space (I-Q) diagram for BPSK is the simplest possible constellation: two points on the real axis, diametrically opposite, with a single decision boundary at the origin.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Const["BPSK Signal Constellation"]
        P0["POINT S0 equals plus sqrt Eb on I axis  BIT 0"]
        P1["POINT S1 equals minus sqrt Eb on I axis  BIT 1"]
        B0["DECISION REGION R0  Z greater than 0"]
        B1["DECISION REGION R1  Z less than 0"]
    end
    P0 --- B0
    P1 --- B1
```

**Interpretation:**
- The two signal points are separated by Euclidean distance $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$.
- The decision boundary is the vertical line $Z = 0$.
- Noise pushes the received point across the boundary with probability $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.

---

### 4.3 BER Waterfall Curve (Conceptual Plot)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[EbN0 axis in dB from 0 to 14] --> Y[BER axis log scale from 1 to 1e-9]
    Y --> CURVE[Monotonically Decreasing Waterfall Curve]
    CURVE --> M1[At 0 dB: BER approx 7.9e-2]
    CURVE --> M2[At 6 dB: BER approx 2.4e-3]
    CURVE --> M3[At 10 dB: BER approx 3.9e-6]
    CURVE --> M4[At 12 dB: BER approx 9e-9]
```

This shape is the iconic **BPSK BER waterfall curve** that students in PCECL508 are required to plot in the lab report and compare with the theoretical expression derived in Section 3.

---

### 4.4 Typical Experimental Setup Summary Table

| Block | Hardware / Software Realization (PCECL508 Lab) |
|---|---|
| Random Binary Source | `randi([0 1], 1, N)` in MATLAB / `np.random.randint(0,2,N)` in Python |
| NRZ Mapping | `a = 1 - 2*b` (transforms $\{0,1\}$ to $\{+1,-1\}$) |
| BPSK Modulator | Multiply by `cos(2*pi*fc*t)` |
| AWGN Channel | `awgn(x, SNR, 'measured')` in MATLAB / `np.random.normal` in Python |
| Demodulator + Integrator | Multiply by `cos(2*pi*fc*t)` and integrate over $T_b$ (or use matched filter) |
| Threshold Detector | `b_hat = (z < 0)` in MATLAB logic |
| BER Computation | `BER = sum(b ~= b_hat)/N` |
| Plotting | `semilogy(EbN0_dB, BER_simulated, 'o', EbN0_dB, BER_theoretical, '-')` |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern:** KTU Part A carries **3 marks** per question, requiring a precise, textbook-style answer in **3–4 sentences** with a small diagram or formula where relevant.

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Bit Error Rate (BER) of a BPSK system. State the theoretical expression for the probability of bit error $P_b$ of BPSK in an AWGN channel and identify each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bit Error Rate (BER)** is defined as the ratio of the number of received bits that are incorrectly detected to the total number of bits transmitted over a given observation interval. For BPSK operating over an **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** channel with optimum coherent detection, the theoretical bit error probability is:

$$
P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

where $Q(\cdot)$ is the Gaussian Q-function (tail probability), $E_b$ is the energy per transmitted bit (in Joules), and $N_0$ is the one-sided noise power spectral density (in W/Hz). The argument $\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$ is sometimes called the **SNR per bit** of the link.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the matched filter (or correlator) in the BPSK receiver? Why is it called "optimum" for AWGN?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **matched filter (or correlator)** in the BPSK receiver computes the decision variable by **correlating the received signal $r(t)$ with a locally generated reference $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$** over one bit duration $T_b$. Its role is to **maximize the output Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** at the sampling instant, which in turn minimizes the probability of bit error. It is called "optimum" because, for the AWGN channel with equiprobable binary symbols, it is the receiver that achieves the **minimum probability of error** (the celebrated result from detection theory). Mathematically, the decision variable produced is:

$$
Z = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi(t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + N
$$

where the noise component $N$ has variance $N_0/2$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and consists of two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **one full question** (either Option A or Option B) in the answer script.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Question]`

**(a)** Derive, with detailed steps, the theoretical bit error probability of a BPSK system operating over an AWGN channel. Clearly state the assumptions used in the derivation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A BPSK system operates at an $E_b/N_0$ of **9.6 dB**. Compute the theoretical bit error probability. If the data rate is **1 Mbps** and the noise figure of the receiver yields an effective noise PSD such that $N_0 = 4 \times 10^{-20}$ W/Hz, determine the **average transmitted bit energy $E_b$** in Joules. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The complete step-by-step derivation is presented in **Section 3.1** above. For the exam script, students should present the following concise logical flow:

1. State the two transmitted signals $s_0(t)$ and $s_1(t)$ differing only by a sign.
2. Express the received signal $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ over the AWGN channel with PSD $N_0/2$.
3. Define the correlator decision variable:
$$
Z = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt
$$
4. Split $Z$ into signal and noise components: $Z = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + N$.
5. Compute the variance of $N$ using the autocorrelation of white noise:
$$
\sigma_N^2 = \frac{N_0}{2}
$$
6. Write the conditional PDFs $f_{Z\mid m_0}(z)$ and $f_{Z\mid m_1}(z)$.
7. Apply the threshold $0$ and compute the total probability of error using the Q-function:
$$
P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)
$$
8. State the assumption $f_c T_b \gg 1$ and equiprobable message bits.

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert $E_b/N_0$ from dB to linear:**
$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0}\bigg|_{\text{dB}} = 9.6 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{9.6/10} = 10^{0.96}
$$

$$
10^{0.96} \approx 9.1201
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the argument of the Q-function:**
$$
\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.1201} = \sqrt{18.2402} \approx 4.2709
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Q-function:**

Using standard Q-function tables or the complementary error function:

$$
P_b = Q(4.2709) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{4.2709}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(3.0198)
$$

From standard tables, $\text{erfc}(3.0198) \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-5}$, hence:

$$
\boxed{P_b \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $E_b$ from the given $N_0$:**
$$
E_b = 9.1201 \times N_0 = 9.1201 \times 4 \times 10^{-20}
$$

$$
\boxed{E_b \approx 3.648 \times 10^{-19} \text{ Joules}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a BPSK transmitter and a coherent BPSK receiver. Describe how the **AWGN channel** affects the transmitted symbols. (7 marks)

**(b)** During a PCECL508 lab experiment, a student transmits $N = 10^6$ BPSK symbols over an AWGN channel. At an $E_b/N_0$ of **6 dB**, the receiver counts **820 errors**. Compute the **simulated BER** and compare it with the **theoretical BER** at the same $E_b/N_0$. Comment on the agreement. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Block diagram and operation (7 marks):**

Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes. The exam script should include:

- **Transmitter:** Random bit generator → NRZ mapper ($0 \to +1$, $1 \to -1$) → BPSK modulator using a carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **Channel:** $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ where $n(t)$ is zero-mean AWGN with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.
- **Receiver:** Coherent demodulator (multiplies by locally generated carrier, assumed perfectly synchronized) → Integrator over $T_b$ (matched filter) → Threshold detector at $0$ → Recovered bits.
- **Effect of AWGN:** The noise adds a Gaussian random variable to the decision variable, pushing the received symbol across the decision boundary with probability $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.

**(b) Simulated vs theoretical BER (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Simulated BER:**
$$
P_{b,\text{sim}} = \frac{\text{Number of errors}}{\text{Total bits}} = \frac{820}{10^6} = 8.20 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $E_b/N_0 = 6$ dB to linear:**
$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{0.6} \approx 3.9811
$$

**Step 3 — Compute theoretical BER:**
$$
\sqrt{2E_b/N_0} = \sqrt{2 \times 3.9811} = \sqrt{7.9622} \approx 2.8218
$$

$$
P_{b,\text{theory}} = Q(2.8218) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{2.8218}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}(1.9955)
$$

From standard tables, $\text{erfc}(1.9955) \approx 0.00471$, hence:

$$
P_{b,\text{theory}} \approx 2.39 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison and comment:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Simulated BER | $8.20 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Theoretical BER | $2.39 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Ratio (Sim/Theory) | $\approx 0.34$ |

**Comment:** The simulated BER ($8.2 \times 10^{-4}$) is **lower** than the theoretical BER ($2.39 \times 10^{-3}$). This is consistent with the expected behavior of Monte Carlo simulation: with $N = 10^6$ bits, the statistical fluctuation of the error count can cause the simulated BER to deviate from the theoretical value. The agreement improves (within a factor of $\sim 3$) and converges to the theoretical curve as $N$ increases. The student should note that the difference is **within the expected statistical confidence interval** for $N = 10^6$ trials.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_b/N_0$ with $S/N$:** $E_b/N_0$ is a *normalized* SNR per bit. Do not mix it with the carrier-to-noise ratio $C/N$ unless a bandwidth factor is given.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear scale** when computing $P_b$ — a very common mistake that costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong threshold:** The decision threshold for BPSK is **always 0** (not 0.5 or 1).
> 4. **Confusing BER with SER:** For BPSK, $P_b = P_s$ because there is **1 bit per symbol**. This is NOT true for QPSK, 16-QAM, etc.
> 5. **Using the matched filter incorrectly:** The integrator limits must be **exactly $[0, T_b]$**, not $[0, T]$ of the entire frame.
> 6. **Ignoring the high-frequency term** in $\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)$ expansion — students who retain the $\sin(4\pi f_c t)$ term forget to justify $f_c T_b \gg 1$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision — memorize the following before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **BPSK transmits one of two antipodal signals** differing only by a sign ($\pm\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$).
- [x] The **optimum receiver** is a correlator / matched filter followed by a **threshold detector at 0**.
- [x] **Theoretical BER:** $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.
- [x] The **Euclidean distance** between the two constellation points is $2\sqrt{E_b}$.
- [x] BPSK has **1 bit per symbol**, so $P_b = P_s$ (symbol error rate).
- [x] BPSK is the **most power-efficient** M-PSK scheme because it uses the **maximum possible antipodal separation**.
- [x] The **Q-function** is the tail probability of a standard normal; $Q(0) = 0.5$, $Q(\infty) = 0$, $Q(-\infty) = 1$.
- [x] Convert $E_b/N_0$ from dB to linear using $10^{(E_b/N_0)_{\text{dB}}/10}$ before plugging into $Q(\cdot)$.
- [x] The **noise variance** at the correlator output is $\sigma_N^2 = N_0/2$.
- [x] For the **AWGN channel**, the simulated BER converges to the theoretical curve as $N \to \infty$.
- [x] **BPSK bandwidth** (main lobe) is $2/T_b$ (double-sideband).
- [x] Use `semilogy` (or `semilog` on BER axis) to plot the **waterfall curve**.
- [x] **Threshold detector** decision: $Z \geq 0 \Rightarrow$ bit 0; $Z < 0 \Rightarrow$ bit 1.
- [x] The **matched filter impulse response** is the time-reversed signal: $h(t) = \phi(T_b - t)$.
- [x] The **Mermaid block diagram** in Section 4.1 is the canonical reference for the lab experiment.
- [x] **$E_b/N_0$ at 9.6 dB** yields $P_b \approx 10^{-5}$ — a frequently asked numerical.
- [x] For high SNR, $P_b \approx (1/2\sqrt{\pi E_b/N_0})\exp(-E_b/N_0)$ — exponential decay.
- [x] The **KTU 2024 lab report** must include: theoretical plot, simulated plot, and a discussion of agreement.

---

*End of Notes — Error Performance of BPSK (PCECL508, Module Experiments)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Error Performance of QPSK
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module Experiments
> - **Topic:** Error Performance of QPSK

# Error Performance of QPSK — KTU Premium Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)** is a **M-ary digital modulation scheme** where $M = 4$, mapping **two bits per symbol** onto one of **four carrier phases** ($\pi/4,\ 3\pi/4,\ 5\pi/4,\ 7\pi/4$). The **Error Performance** of QPSK refers to the quantitative evaluation of the **Bit Error Rate (BER)** and **Symbol Error Rate (SER)** when the transmitted signal is corrupted by **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** in the channel, expressed as a function of the **energy-per-bit to noise spectral density ratio ($E_b/N_0$)**.

Mathematically, the QPSK transmitted signal is defined as:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{(2i-1)\pi}{4}\right),\quad i = 1,2,3,4
$$

where:
- $E_s$ = Energy per symbol
- $T_s$ = Symbol duration
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency
- The phase $\theta_i = (2i-1)\pi/4$ takes one of the four equiprobable values

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Compass Rose

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine a **compass rose** with **4 directions** (N, E, S, W) — equally spaced at 90° apart. Each direction carries a unique 2-bit code (e.g., N=00, E=01, S=10, W=11). When the message sender points the compass needle to a direction, the receiver tries to read the same direction. **Noise** is like a gentle wind that randomly deflects the needle. The **probability of error** is the chance that the wind blows the needle across the **decision boundary** (the midlines between adjacent directions) into the wrong quadrant. Because the quadrants are wider than the individual directions (each is $\pm 45°$ around its center), QPSK is **more robust to noise** than one might first think.

### 1.3 Why Study Error Performance?

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | QPSK transmits **2 bits/Hz/s** — double BPSK's 1 bit/Hz/s |
| **Power-Bandwidth Trade-off** | Same **BER vs $E_b/N_0$** as BPSK, but **half the bandwidth** |
| **System Design** | BER curves determine required transmit power, link budget, and antenna gain |
| **5G / Satellite / Wi-Fi** | Foundation of PSK family used in modern digital communication |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constant for BER Evaluation:** The standard Gaussian co-error function values — at $E_b/N_0 = 9.6\ \text{dB}$, the theoretical QPSK BER is $\approx 10^{-5}$. The "waterfall region" of the BER curve begins around **6–7 dB**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The QPSK Modulator — Orthogonal Basis

QPSK uses **two orthogonal basis functions** that allow independent detection of the in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) components:

$$
\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

$$
\phi_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

The four transmitted signals can be expressed as 2-D vectors in the **I–Q constellation plane**:

| Bits (b₁ b₀) | Phase $\theta_i$ | I-Component | Q-Component | Vector $s_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 0 | $\pi/4$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $(+\sqrt{E_s/2},\ -\sqrt{E_s/2})$ |
| 0 1 | $3\pi/4$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\ -\sqrt{E_s/2})$ |
| 1 1 | $5\pi/4$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\ +\sqrt{E_s/2})$ |
| 1 0 | $7\pi/4$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$ | $(+\sqrt{E_s/2},\ +\sqrt{E_s/2})$ |

### 2.2 Operational Steps (The "How" and "Why")

1. **Bit-to-Symbol Mapping:** Incoming serial bit stream is **grouped into pairs** (dibits). Why? Because $M = 4 = 2^2$, each symbol carries **2 bits**, doubling spectral efficiency.
2. **I/Q Splitting:** Each dibit is split into I-bit (controls cosine) and Q-bit (controls sine), giving **two independent BPSK signals** operating in parallel.
3. **Modulation & Summing:** The I-signal modulates $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and the Q-signal modulates $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$; the two are summed to form the QPSK waveform.
4. **Channel Corruption:** AWGN $n(t)$ with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$ is added: $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$.
5. **Coherent Detection:** The receiver correlates $r(t)$ with $\phi_1(t)$ and $\phi_2(t)$ to produce decision variables $r_I$ and $r_Q$.
6. **Decision Rule:** Pick the constellation point closest in Euclidean distance to the received vector $(r_I, r_Q)$. Decision boundaries lie at the **axes** of the I-Q plane.
7. **Error Counting:** Bits in error are divided by total bits transmitted to estimate BER.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they form 80% of the ESE marks for this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Range |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_s = 2 E_b$ | Symbol energy = 2 × bit energy (QPSK carries 2 bits/symbol) | Joules |
| 2 | $\text{BER}_{QPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Bit Error Rate of QPSK in AWGN | Dimensionless, $0$ to $0.5$ |
| 3 | $\text{BER}_{QPSK} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Alternate form using complementary error function | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)$ | Symbol Error Rate (high-SNR approximation) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_s = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_s}{N_0}}\right) - \left[Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)\right]^2$ | Exact Symbol Error Rate | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$ | Relation between Q-function and erfc | Definition |
| 7 | $Q(x) \approx \dfrac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/2}$ | Chernoff bound (upper bound) for $x > 0$ | Upper bound |
| 8 | $\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\big|_{\text{dB}} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$ | dB conversion | dB |
| 9 | $\text{BER}_{QPSK} = \text{BER}_{BPSK}$ | At same $E_b/N_0$ — QPSK and BPSK have identical BER | Equal performance |
| 10 | Bandwidth$_{QPSK} = \tfrac{1}{2}$ Bandwidth$_{BPSK}$ | QPSK uses half the bandwidth of BPSK at same bit rate | Hz |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Communications (e.g., DVB-S2):** QPSK is the standard for **rain-faded and power-limited** satellite links.
- **Cellular 4G LTE:** Used in control channels and cell-edge scenarios where robustness beats raw throughput.
- **RFID & Low-Power IoT:** QPSK balances low error rates with reasonable data rates for tag-to-reader backscatter.
- **Software-Defined Radio (SDR):** GNU Radio / USRP implementations of QPSK are classic teaching laboratories — exactly what **PCECL508 Module Experiments** simulate.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Symbol Error Rate of QPSK over AWGN

We derive $P_s$ rigorously by computing the probability that Gaussian noise pushes the received vector across a decision boundary.

**Step 1 — Received signal model.**

At the output of the matched filters (correlators), the two decision variables are:

$$
r_I = s_{I,i} + n_I, \quad r_Q = s_{Q,i} + n_Q
$$

where $n_I,\ n_Q \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\ \dfrac{N_0}{2}\right)$ are **independent Gaussian random variables** (orthogonality of $\phi_1, \phi_2$ decouples them).

**Step 2 — Assume symbol $s_1$ is transmitted** (located at $\bigl(+\sqrt{E_s/2},\ -\sqrt{E_s/2}\bigr)$).

An error occurs if the received point falls into **any of the other three quadrants**. The decision boundaries are the I-axis ($r_Q = 0$) and Q-axis ($r_I = 0$).

$$
P(\text{error}\mid s_1) = P\!\left(r_Q > 0\right) + P\!\left(r_I < 0\right) - P\!\left(r_Q > 0\ \cap\ r_I < 0\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $P(r_Q > 0)$.**

Given $s_{Q,1} = -\sqrt{E_s/2}$, the random variable $r_Q$ is Gaussian with mean $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$ and variance $N_0/2$:

$$
P(r_Q > 0) = P\!\left(\mathcal{N}\!\left(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\ N_0/2\right) > 0\right)
$$

Standardizing (subtract mean, divide by std. dev.):

$$
= P\!\left(Z > \frac{0 - (-\sqrt{E_s/2})}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = P\!\left(Z > \sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)
$$

where $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$. By definition of the Q-function:

$$
P(r_Q > 0) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — By symmetry** $P(r_I < 0) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)$ as well.

**Step 5 — Joint probability** $P(r_Q > 0 \cap r_I < 0)$.

Because $n_I$ and $n_Q$ are **independent**, the joint event factors:

$$
P(r_Q > 0 \cap r_I < 0) = P(r_Q > 0) \cdot P(r_I < 0) = \left[Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)\right]^2
$$

**Step 6 — Combine via inclusion-exclusion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{error}\mid s_1) &= Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) + Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) - \left[Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)\right]^2 \\[6pt]
&= 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) - \left[Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)\right]^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Symbol Error Rate (all symbols equiprobable):**

$$
P_s = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right) - \left[Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)\right]^2
$$

For moderate-to-high SNR ($E_s/N_0 \gg 1$), the squared term is negligible, giving the **practical approximation**:

$$
\boxed{\,P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)\,}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bit Error Rate of QPSK with Gray Coding

**Step 1 — Relationship between SER and BER under Gray coding.**

In Gray-coded QPSK, **adjacent constellation points differ in only one bit**. A symbol error to a *neighbor* therefore causes only **one bit error** out of two — a single symbol error produces exactly one bit error.

The probability of an error to a *diagonal* (non-adjacent) point is negligibly small at high SNR, and even if it occurs, Gray coding ensures at most 2 bit errors. Under Gray coding:

$$
P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{P_s}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the SER approximation:**

$$
P_b \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the identity** $E_s = 2 E_b$:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}} = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}
$$

**Step 4 — Final closed-form BER of QPSK:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)\,}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalence with BPSK (Conceptual Proof)

For BPSK, the standard result is $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$. Comparing with the QPSK result, they are **identical**. The intuitive explanation:

- A QPSK signal can be **decomposed into two independent BPSK signals** on the I and Q rails.
- Each BPSK sub-channel operates with symbol energy $E_s/2$ and bit energy $E_b$ (since each carries 1 bit/symbol).
- The BER of each sub-channel is $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \cdot E_b / N_0}\right)$.
- Since both rails experience the same BER and the total bit stream is the concatenation, the **overall QPSK BER equals the per-rail BER** — matching BPSK.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** QPSK achieves the **same power efficiency as BPSK** while using **half the transmission bandwidth**. This is the central engineering motivation for adopting QPSK.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

> **Question:** For QPSK operating at $E_b/N_0 = 10\ \text{dB}$ over an AWGN channel, compute the BER.

**Solution:**

Step A — Convert dB to linear scale:

$$
\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{10/10} = 10
$$

Step B — Compute the argument of the Q-function:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{2 \times 10} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.4721
$$

Step C — Look up the Q-function (or use approximation $Q(4.47) \approx 3.83 \times 10^{-6}$):

$$
P_b = Q(4.4721) \approx 3.83 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Step D — SER approximation:**

$$
\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}} = \sqrt{20} = 4.4721
$$

$$
P_s \approx 2Q(4.4721) \approx 7.66 \times 10^{-6}
$$

Confirming $P_b \approx P_s / 2$. ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | BPSK | QPSK | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 | QPSK doubles spectral efficiency |
| Bandwidth (same $R_b$) | $R_b$ | $R_b/2$ | QPSK uses half the bandwidth |
| $E_s / E_b$ | 1 | 2 | QPSK symbol carries 2× energy |
| BER formula | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | **Identical** |
| Power efficiency | Same | Same | Neither is better |
| Bandwidth efficiency | 1 bit/s/Hz | 2 bits/s/Hz | **QPSK wins** |
| Receiver complexity | Low (1 correlator) | Medium (2 correlators) | BPSK wins on simplicity |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 QPSK Transmitter — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Serial Bit Stream] --> B[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    B --> C[Bit Pair b1 b0]
    C --> D[I channel NRZ mapper]
    C --> E[Q channel NRZ mapper]
    D --> F[Multiplier with cos 2 pi fc t]
    E --> G[Multiplier with sin 2 pi fc t]
    F --> H[Summing Junction]
    G --> H
    H --> I[AWGN Channel n t]
    I --> J[QPSK Received Signal r t]
```

**Operational Mapping of Nodes:**

- `A` — Input binary data at rate $R_b$ bits/sec
- `B` — Serial-to-Parallel converter producing dibits at symbol rate $R_s = R_b/2$
- `D, E` — NRZ level encoders mapping $\{0, 1\} \to \{-1, +1\}$ on I and Q rails
- `F, G` — Balanced modulators (multipliers) producing BPSK-modulated subcarriers
- `H` — Linear summer combining the two BPSK signals into the QPSK waveform
- `I` — Additive White Gaussian Noise with PSD $N_0/2$ corrupts the signal
- `J` — Received noisy waveform $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$

### 4.2 QPSK Receiver — Coherent Detection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Received Signal r t] --> B[Multiply by cos 2 pi fc t]
    A --> C[Multiply by sin 2 pi fc t]
    B --> D[Integrate over 0 to Ts]
    C --> E[Integrate over 0 to Ts]
    D --> F[Sample at t = Ts]
    E --> G[Sample at t = Ts]
    F --> H[Decision Device I rail]
    G --> I[Decision Device Q rail]
    H --> J[Parallel to Serial Converter]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Recovered Bit Stream]
```

**Functional Flow Explanation:**

- `A → B, C` — Coherent demodulation with locally generated carrier (assumed phase-locked)
- `B, C → D, E` — Correlators (matched filters) extract baseband I and Q components
- `D, E → F, G` — Sample-and-hold at the end of each symbol period
- `F, G → H, I` — Threshold detectors: if sample $> 0$, decide bit = 1; else bit = 0
- `H, I → J` — Multiplex recovered I-bits and Q-bits back into a serial stream
- `J → K` — Output bits with measured BER

### 4.3 QPSK Constellation with Decision Regions

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Constellation["QPSK Constellation with Decision Regions"]
        direction TB
        A["Q1 00"] --- B["Q2 01"]
        B --- C["Q3 11"]
        C --- D["Q4 10"]
        A --- D
    end
    E[Decision Boundary: I axis and Q axis] -.-> Constellation
    F[Noise pushes received point into wrong quadrant] -.-> Constellation
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **four decision regions** are the four quadrants of the I-Q plane. The **I-axis** and **Q-axis** are the **decision boundaries** (separating lines). A received point crossing either boundary into an adjacent quadrant results in a symbol error. The minimum distance between adjacent constellation points is $d_{\min} = \sqrt{2E_s}$, while the distance from any point to the nearest boundary is $d_{\min}/2 = \sqrt{E_s/2}$.

### 4.4 BER vs $E_b/N_0$ — Conceptual "Waterfall" Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low SNR: BER near 0.5] --> B[Eb N0 approx 4 dB: BER approx 0.01]
    B --> C[Eb N0 approx 7 dB: BER approx 1e-3]
    C --> D[Eb N0 approx 9.6 dB: BER approx 1e-5]
    D --> E[Eb N0 approx 11 dB: BER approx 1e-7]
    E --> F[High SNR: BER approaches 0]
```

**Reading the Waterfall:**
- The curve is **flat** at low SNR (errors are random — close to coin-flip 0.5)
- The curve **drops sharply** in the waterfall region (6–10 dB)
- The curve **asymptotes to zero** at high SNR (reliable communication)
- Each additional 3 dB of $E_b/N_0$ **roughly reduces BER by an order of magnitude** in the waterfall region

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Bit Error Rate (BER) of a digital modulation scheme and state the theoretical BER expression for QPSK in an AWGN channel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Bit Error Rate (BER)** is defined as the ratio of the number of bits received in error to the total number of bits transmitted during a specified observation interval. It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from 0 to 0.5.
>
> For QPSK transmitted over an **AWGN channel** with **coherent detection** and **Gray coding**, the theoretical BER is:
>
> $$
> P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)
> $$
>
> where $E_b$ is the **energy per bit**, $N_0$ is the **two-sided noise power spectral density**, and $Q(\cdot)$ is the Gaussian co-error function.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**Compare the bandwidth efficiency of QPSK with BPSK. Which is more bandwidth-efficient and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> BPSK transmits **1 bit per symbol**, requiring a transmission bandwidth equal to the bit rate $R_b$ (i.e., null-to-null bandwidth $\approx R_b$). QPSK transmits **2 bits per symbol**, so for the same bit rate $R_b$, its symbol rate is $R_s = R_b/2$, and its required bandwidth is $\approx R_b/2$.
>
> Therefore, **QPSK is twice as bandwidth-efficient as BPSK**, achieving **2 bits/s/Hz** versus BPSK's 1 bit/s/Hz. Both schemes, however, exhibit **identical BER performance** at the same $E_b/N_0$, making QPSK strictly superior when bandwidth is the limiting resource.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — QPSK Modulator, Demodulator & Constellation**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Repeated Pattern]`

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a QPSK **transmitter** and **coherent receiver**. Explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Sketch the QPSK signal constellation and explain the **decision rule** at the receiver. Derive the **symbol error probability** in terms of the Q-function. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Functions — 7 marks**

**Transmitter Blocks:**

1. **Serial-to-Parallel Converter:** Splits the incoming serial bit stream $b_k$ at rate $R_b$ into two parallel streams (I-bits and Q-bits) each at half the rate, $R_b/2$. This forms dibits for symbol mapping.
2. **NRZ Encoders (I and Q):** Map logical bits $\{0, 1\}$ to voltage levels $\{-1, +1\}$. Bipolar signaling is required because the multiplier outputs must be summed linearly.
3. **Local Oscillator (Carrier):** Generates $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $-\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ which are mutually orthogonal.
4. **Balanced Modulators (Multipliers):** The I-bit multiplies $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$; the Q-bit multiplies $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$. Each output is an independent BPSK signal.
5. **Summer:** Linearly combines the two BPSK signals to produce the QPSK waveform $s_i(t)$.

**Receiver Blocks:**

1. **Coherent Local Oscillator:** Generates carrier references $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ in phase-lock with the transmitter.
2. **Multipliers:** Mix the received signal $r(t)$ with the carrier references to bring it back to baseband.
3. **Integrators (Matched Filters):** Integrate the product over one symbol period $[0, T_s]$ to maximize SNR.
4. **Samplers:** Sample at $t = T_s$ to obtain decision variables $r_I$ and $r_Q$.
5. **Threshold Detectors (Decision Devices):** Apply the rule: if $r_I > 0$ decide I-bit = 1 (else 0); if $r_Q > 0$ decide Q-bit = 1 (else 0).
6. **Parallel-to-Serial Converter:** Multiplexes the recovered I-Q bit pairs back into a single serial bit stream.

**(b) Constellation, Decision Rule & SER — 7 marks**

**Constellation:** Four equiprobable points located at $(+\sqrt{E_s/2},\ -\sqrt{E_s/2})$, $(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\ -\sqrt{E_s/2})$, $(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\ +\sqrt{E_s/2})$, $(+\sqrt{E_s/2},\ +\sqrt{E_s/2})$.

**Decision Rule:** Choose the constellation point whose Euclidean distance to the received vector $(r_I, r_Q)$ is minimum. The four decision regions are the four quadrants of the I-Q plane, separated by the I-axis and Q-axis.

**SER Derivation:**

For symbol $s_1$ transmitted at $(+\sqrt{E_s/2}, -\sqrt{E_s/2})$:

$$
P(\text{error}\mid s_1) = P(r_Q > 0) + P(r_I < 0) - P(r_Q > 0)\,P(r_I < 0)
$$

Since $r_I \sim \mathcal{N}(+\sqrt{E_s/2},\ N_0/2)$ and $r_Q \sim \mathcal{N}(-\sqrt{E_s/2},\ N_0/2)$:

$$
P(r_Q > 0) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right), \quad P(r_I < 0) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)
$$

By independence of $n_I$ and $n_Q$:

$$
P(\text{error}\mid s_1) = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) - \left[Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)\right]^2
$$

By symmetry, all four symbols have identical error probability:

$$
\boxed{\,P_s = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) - \left[Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)\right]^2\,}
$$

At high SNR, the squared term is negligible, giving $P_s \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)$.

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — BER Derivation & Performance Curves**

`[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified Pattern]`

**(a)** Starting from the symbol error rate expression, derive the **bit error rate of QPSK** in an AWGN channel. State clearly the assumption made (Gray coding). Show that QPSK and BPSK have the **same BER** at equal $E_b/N_0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A QPSK system operates at $E_b/N_0 = 8\ \text{dB}$ over an AWGN channel. Compute the **BER and SER** numerically. Comment on how the BER changes if $E_b/N_0$ is increased to **12 dB**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BER Derivation — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Symbol Error Rate of QPSK:**

$$
P_s = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) - \left[Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)\right]^2 \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gray coding assumption:**

Under Gray coding, adjacent symbols differ in only **one bit**. A symbol error to a neighbor thus flips exactly **1 bit** out of 2. Therefore:

$$
P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{P_s}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and apply $E_s = 2E_b$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &\approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_s}{N_0}}\right) \\[6pt]
&= Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalence with BPSK:**

For BPSK, the standard result is $P_b^{BPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$. Comparing:

$$
P_b^{QPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right) = P_b^{BPSK}
$$

Both schemes have **identical BER at the same $E_b/N_0$**, but QPSK achieves this with **half the bandwidth**. The physical reason is that the I and Q channels of QPSK behave as **two independent BPSK sub-channels**, each with the same $E_b/N_0$.

**(b) Numerical Computation — 7 marks**

**Given:** $E_b/N_0 = 8\ \text{dB} = 10^{0.8} \approx 6.3096$ (linear).

**BER Calculation:**

$$
P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 6.3096}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{12.619}\right) = Q(3.5524)
$$

From standard Q-tables: $Q(3.55) \approx 1.92 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$
\boxed{\,P_b \approx 1.92 \times 10^{-4}\,}
$$

**SER Calculation:**

$$
P_s = 2Q(3.5524) - [Q(3.5524)]^2 \approx 2 \times 1.92 \times 10^{-4} = 3.84 \times 10^{-4}
$$

(The squared term $\approx 3.7 \times 10^{-8}$ is negligible.)

$$
\boxed{\,P_s \approx 3.84 \times 10^{-4}\,}
$$

**Comparison at 12 dB:** $E_b/N_0 = 10^{1.2} \approx 15.849$ (linear).

$$
P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 15.849}\right) = Q(\sqrt{31.698}) = Q(5.6305) \approx 9.0 \times 10^{-9}
$$

**Comment:** Increasing $E_b/N_0$ from 8 dB to 12 dB (a 4 dB / 2.5× power increase) reduces BER from $1.92 \times 10^{-4}$ to about $9 \times 10^{-9}$ — a reduction by a factor of **roughly 20,000×**. This demonstrates the **exponentially decaying** nature of the BER curve in the waterfall region. The 4 dB power increase buys about **4.3 orders of magnitude** improvement in BER, validating the high power efficiency of coherent QPSK at moderate-to-high SNR.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_s$ with $E_b$** — Forgetting the factor of 2 in $E_s = 2E_b$ for QPSK. Marks deducted in SER-vs-BER substitution steps.
> 2. **Omitting the squared term in SER** — At low SNR, $Q^2$ is NOT negligible. Use the exact formula $P_s = 2Q - Q^2$ when $E_b/N_0 < 6$ dB.
> 3. **Forgetting Gray coding assumption** — The $P_b = P_s / 2$ step is **only valid under Gray coding**. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this assumption is not stated.
> 4. **Confusing "bit error rate" with "symbol error rate"** — Read the question twice. SER is per-symbol; BER is per-bit.
> 5. **Missing units in numerical answers** — Always express $E_b/N_0$ in dB when given as dB, and convert to linear for formula evaluation. Show both.
> 6. **Skipping the decision rule** — In part-(b) questions, "draw the decision regions" requires labeling the **four quadrants** AND stating the threshold logic explicitly.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **QPSK** is a **4-ary PSK** scheme carrying **2 bits per symbol** with $\log_2 4 = 2$.
- [x] **Carrier phases:** $\pi/4,\ 3\pi/4,\ 5\pi/4,\ 7\pi/4$ (all $\pi/4$ apart).
- [x] **Orthogonal decomposition:** I-channel ($\cos$) and Q-channel ($\sin$) are independent BPSK sub-channels.
- [x] **Energy relation:** $E_s = 2 E_b$ — symbol energy is **twice** the bit energy.
- [x] **BER formula:** $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right) = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$.
- [x] **SER formula:** $P_s = 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) - Q^2\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right) \approx 2Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s/N_0}\right)$ (high SNR).
- [x] **Q-function properties:** $Q(0) = 0.5$, $Q(\infty) = 0$, $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$, strictly decreasing.
- [x] **Gray coding:** Adjacent symbols differ by one bit → $P_b \approx P_s / 2$.
- [x] **BPSK vs QPSK:** **Identical BER**; QPSK uses **half the bandwidth**; BPSK is **simpler** to implement.
- [x] **AWGN channel:** $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$ with $n(t) \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$ two-sided PSD.
- [x] **Decision rule:** Choose the quadrant of the I-Q plane closest to the received sample.
- [x] **Minimum distance:** $d_{\min} = \sqrt{2E_s}$ between adjacent symbols.
- [x] **Waterfall region:** $E_b/N_0 \in [6, 10]\ \text{dB}$ where BER drops from $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$.
- [x] **Power vs Bandwidth trade-off:** QPSK saves bandwidth; MPSK (M > 4) saves more bandwidth but needs exponentially more power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU 2024 Scheme:** Always **derive first, then evaluate numerically**. Examiners award 2–3 marks for the derivation and 2–3 marks for the numerical answer in 14-mark questions. Skipping the derivation to jump to the answer is the single biggest mark-loss trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
