| 8 | Redundancy | $R = 1 - \eta$ | Wasted fraction of length |
| 9 | Code Rate | $r = L / n$ bits/source symbol | For block codes of $n$ symbols |
| 10 | Block Coding Gain | $H(X) \leq r < H(X) + 1/n$ | As $n \to \infty$, $r \to H(X)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**: Kraft's inequality is the mathematical backbone of every practical compression algorithm — Huffman coding (used in JPEG, MP3, ZIP), arithmetic coding (used in BZIP2, PNG), and Lempel-Ziv (used in GIF, UNIX compress). Shannon's theorem gives the **theoretical compression limit** that engineers benchmark against.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Proof of Kraft Inequality (Necessity)

> **Given**: A prefix code $C = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n\}$ over a D-ary alphabet with codeword lengths $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$.
> **To Prove**: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq 1$.

**Step 1: Define the tree structure.** Construct a complete D-ary tree of depth $l_{\max} = \max_i(l_i)$. Such a tree has:
$$N_{\text{total}} = D^{l_{\max}} \text{ leaves.}$$

**Step 2: Count leaves blocked by each codeword.** A codeword of length $l_i$ prevents all leaves in its subtree of depth $l_{\max} - l_i$ from being used by other codewords. The number of such leaves is:
$$N_i = D^{l_{\max} - l_i}.$$

**Step 3: Apply the prefix property.** Since $C$ is a prefix code, no codeword can be a prefix of another. Hence, the subtrees blocked by different codewords are **mutually disjoint** (they share no common leaves).

**Step 4: Sum the blocked leaves.** The total number of blocked leaves cannot exceed the total number of leaves:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i \leq N_{\text{total}}.$$

**Step 5: Substitute and simplify.** Plug in the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{l_{\max} - l_i} \leq D^{l_{\max}}.$$

**Step 6: Divide by $D^{l_{\max}}$.** Since $D^{l_{\max}} > 0$, division preserves the inequality:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq 1.$$

This completes the necessity proof. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Proof of Kraft Inequality (Sufficiency)

> **Given**: Lengths $l_1 \leq l_2 \leq \cdots \leq l_n$ satisfying $\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq 1$.
> **To Prove**: A prefix code with these lengths exists.

**Step 1: Sort the lengths.** Re-index so that $l_1 \leq l_2 \leq \cdots \leq l_n$. This ensures we always assign shorter (more constrained) codewords first.

**Step 2: Define a tree-builder function.** We greedily assign codewords on a D-ary tree. Define the "next available node" pointer. Initially, the entire tree of depth $l_n$ is available.

**Step 3: Base case ($i = 1$).** Assign $c_1$ to the leftmost available path of length $l_1$. The Kraft condition guarantees at least one node is available (since $D^{-l_1} \leq \sum D^{-l_i} \leq 1$).

**Step 4: Inductive step.** Suppose codewords $c_1, \ldots, c_{i-1}$ have been assigned. The fraction of the tree still available is:
$$A_i = 1 - \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} D^{-l_j}.$$

By the Kraft inequality, $A_i \geq D^{-l_i} > 0$. Hence, a path of length $l_i$ is available in the unpruned region.

**Step 5: Greedy assignment.** Pick $c_i$ as the leftmost unpruned path of length $l_i$. Prune all descendants.

**Step 6: Termination.** After $n$ iterations, we have a valid prefix code with the desired lengths. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Proof of Shannon's Source Coding Theorem

### **Part A: Lower Bound — $L \geq H_D(X)$**

**Step 1: Define the code mapping.** Let $X$ take values in $\{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$ with probabilities $\{p_1, \ldots, p_n\}$. Let $C(X)$ denote the codeword length assigned to $X$.

**Step 2: Compute the expected length.**
$$L = \mathbb{E}[C(X)] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot l_i.$$

**Step 3: Use the Gibbs inequality** (a special case of the log-sum inequality). For any two probability distributions $p$ and $q$ on the same alphabet:
$$-\sum_i p_i \log q_i \geq -\sum_i p_i \log p_i = H(X).$$

**Step 4: Choose the right $q$.** Set $q_i = D^{-l_i} / Z$ where $Z = \sum_j D^{-l_j}$. Since the code is uniquely decodable, $Z \leq 1$, so $\log Z \leq 0$.

**Step 5: Compute $-\sum p_i \log q_i$.**
$$-\sum_i p_i \log q_i = -\sum_i p_i \left(-l_i \log D - \log Z\right) = (\log D) \sum p_i l_i - \log Z = L \log D - \log Z.$$

**Step 6: Apply the Gibbs inequality.**
$$L \log D - \log Z \geq H(X).$$

**Step 7: Rearrange.** Since $Z \leq 1$, we have $-\log Z \geq 0$, so:
$$L \log D \geq H(X) \quad \Rightarrow \quad L \geq H_D(X) = \frac{H(X)}{\log_2 D}.$$

This proves the converse. $\blacksquare$

### **Part B: Upper Bound — $L < H_D(X) + 1$**

**Step 1: Define the length assignment.** For each symbol $x_i$, assign length:
$$l_i = \lceil -\log_D p_i \rceil.$$

By definition of the ceiling function:
$$-\log_D p_i \leq l_i < -\log_D p_i + 1.$$

**Step 2: Verify the Kraft condition.**
$$D^{-l_i} \leq D^{\log_D p_i} = p_i.$$

Summing over all symbols:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = 1.$$

The Kraft inequality is satisfied.

**Step 3: Existence of prefix code.** By the sufficiency of Kraft's inequality, a prefix code with these lengths **exists**.

**Step 4: Compute the expected length.**
$$L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i l_i.$$

Using the upper bound $l_i < -\log_D p_i + 1$:
$$L < \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i (-\log_D p_i + 1).$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the sum.**
$$L < -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_D p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = H_D(X) + 1.$$

This proves the achievability bound. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical)

> **Problem**: A source has symbols $\{a, b, c, d\}$ with probabilities $\{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$. Construct a Shannon-Fano code and verify Shannon's theorem.

**Step 1: Compute $l_i = \lceil -\log_2 p_i \rceil$ for each symbol.**

| Symbol | $p_i$ | $-\log_2 p_i$ | $l_i = \lceil -\log_2 p_i \rceil$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $a$ | 0.500 | 1.000 | 1 |
| $b$ | 0.250 | 2.000 | 2 |
| $c$ | 0.125 | 3.000 | 3 |
| $d$ | 0.125 | 3.000 | 3 |

**Step 2: Verify Kraft's inequality.**
$$\sum D^{-l_i} = 2^{-1} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} = 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.125 = 1.0 \leq 1.$$

Kraft's inequality holds with equality (a *complete* code).

**Step 3: Construct a valid prefix code.** Using the binary tree:
- $a \to 0$ (length 1)
- $b \to 10$ (length 2)
- $c \to 110$ (length 3)
- $d \to 111$ (length 3)

**Step 4: Compute entropy and average length.**

$$H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$$
$$= 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.125(3) + 0.125(3) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol.}$$

$$L = \sum p_i l_i = 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.125(3) + 0.125(3) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \text{ bits/symbol.}$$

**Step 5: Verify Shannon's bound.**
$$H(X) = 1.75 \leq L = 1.75 < H(X) + 1 = 2.75.$$

The bound is satisfied. In this special case, $L = H(X)$ — the code achieves the theoretical optimum (a *zero-redundancy* code).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Coding Pipeline

The diagram below illustrates the **end-to-end information flow** of a source coding system, where Kraft inequality and Shannon's theorem act as theoretical gatekeepers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Discrete Memoryless Source X] --> B[Probability Model p sub i]
    B --> C[Length Assignment l sub i equals ceil minus log sub D p sub i]
    C --> D{Kraft Inequality Check}
    D -- Sum D power minus l sub i less or equal 1 --> E[Valid Prefix Code Exists]
    D -- Sum D power minus l sub i greater than 1 --> F[Invalid Code Reject]
    E --> G[Codebook Construction Binary Tree]
    G --> H[Encoding Mapping x sub i to c sub i]
    H --> I[Channel or Storage]
    I --> J[Decoding Unique Prefix Match]
    J --> K[Reconstructed Source X hat]
    F --> L[Re assign Lengths using Lempel Ziv or Huffman]
    L --> D
```

**Interpretation of the Flow:**

1. **Source → Probability Model**: The source's statistical structure is captured by the symbol probabilities $p_i$.
2. **Probability Model → Length Assignment**: The Shannon-Fano / Kraft-prescription step $l_i = \lceil -\log_D p_i \rceil$ is applied.
3. **Length Assignment → Kraft Check**: The sum $\sum D^{-l_i}$ is computed. If $\leq 1$, a prefix code exists.
4. **Codebook Construction → Encoding**: A binary (or D-ary) tree is built and codewords are assigned.
5. **Storage → Decoding**: At the receiver, prefix property allows **unique instant decoding** without lookahead.
6. **Failure Branch**: If Kraft fails, the system iteratively reassigns lengths (e.g., via Huffman algorithm).

## 4.2 Decision Tree of the D-ary Code Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[D-Ary Tree Root] --> n1[Level 1 Node 1]
    root --> n2[Level 1 Node 2]
    root --> n3[Level 1 Node 3]
    n1 --> n1a[Level 2 Sub 1 A]
    n1 --> n1b[Level 2 Sub 1 B]
    n1 --> n1c[Level 2 Sub 1 C]
    n2 --> n2a[Level 2 Sub 2 A]
    n2 --> n2b[Level 2 Sub 2 B]
    n2 --> n2c[Level 2 Sub 2 C]
    n3 --> n3a[Level 2 Sub 3 A]
    n3 --> n3b[Level 2 Sub 3 B]
    n3 --> n3c[Level 2 Sub 3 C]
    n1a --> l1[Leaf: codeword length 2 occupies 1 over D squared of tree]
    n1b --> l2[Leaf: codeword length 2 occupies 1 over D squared]
    n1c --> l3[Leaf: codeword length 2 occupies 1 over D squared]
```

**Interpretation:** Each codeword at level $l$ "claims" a fraction $D^{-l}$ of the total tree leaves. Kraft's inequality states that the sum of these claimed fractions cannot exceed 1 (the entire tree). This is the geometric intuition behind the inequality.

## 4.3 Logical Mapping of Theorem Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Kraft Inequality] --> B[Existence Test for Prefix Code]
    C[Shannon Lower Bound] --> D[Lower Limit H of X]
    E[Shannon Upper Bound] --> F[Upper Limit H of X plus 1]
    B --> G[Optimal Code Design]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Huffman Coding]
    G --> I[Shannon Fano Coding]
    G --> J[Arithmetic Coding]
    H --> K[Real World Compression]
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

**Interpretation:** Kraft inequality and Shannon's two-sided bound together form the **theoretical foundation** upon which all practical compression algorithms (Huffman, Shannon-Fano, Arithmetic) are built. They answer the question: *"What is the best we can do?"* and *"How do we achieve it?"*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**Q: State and prove Kraft's inequality for a D-ary prefix code.**

**Model Answer**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement**: For any prefix code with codeword lengths $l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_n$ over a D-ary alphabet, $\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq 1$.

**Proof (Necessity)**: Consider a complete D-ary tree of depth $l_{\max} = \max_i(l_i)$. The total number of leaves is $D^{l_{\max}}$. Each codeword of length $l_i$ blocks $D^{l_{\max} - l_i}$ leaves. By the prefix property, blocked regions are disjoint, so:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{l_{\max} - l_i} \leq D^{l_{\max}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq 1.$$

**Proof (Sufficiency)**: Given lengths satisfying Kraft, sort them as $l_1 \leq l_2 \leq \cdots \leq l_n$. Greedily assign each codeword to the leftmost unpruned path of length $l_i$. At step $i$, the available fraction $A_i = 1 - \sum_{j<i} D^{-l_j} \geq D^{-l_i}$, so a free path always exists. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**Q: Explain Shannon's first source coding theorem. What is its significance?**

**Model Answer**:

**Statement**: For a discrete memoryless source with entropy $H(X)$ over a D-ary alphabet, there exists a prefix code with expected codeword length $L$ such that:
$$H_D(X) \leq L < H_D(X) + 1.$$

**Significance**:
- The **lower bound** $L \geq H_D(X)$ is a *converse* — it is impossible to compress below the entropy on average.
- The **upper bound** $L < H_D(X) + 1$ is an *achievability* result — for any source, a code exists that gets within 1 D-ary unit of the entropy.
- For **block codes** of $n$ symbols, the bound tightens to $H(X) \leq r < H(X) + 1/n$, showing that arbitrarily close to the entropy limit is achievable.
- The theorem is the **theoretical foundation** for all data compression algorithms (Huffman, Lempel-Ziv, arithmetic coding).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a): 7 Marks**

**Q: For a source with symbols and probabilities as given below:**

| Symbol | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | $s_4$ | $s_5$ | $s_6$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $p_i$ | 0.30 | 0.25 | 0.20 | 0.12 | 0.08 | 0.05 |

**(i) Compute the entropy of the source.**
**(ii) Construct a Shannon-Fano code and verify Kraft's inequality.**
**(iii) Compute the average codeword length and efficiency.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Entropy Computation:**

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{6} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

$$H(X) = -[0.30 \log_2 0.30 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.12 \log_2 0.12 + 0.08 \log_2 0.08 + 0.05 \log_2 0.05]$$

Compute each term:
- $0.30 \log_2 0.30 = 0.30 \times (-1.737) = -0.5211$
- $0.25 \log_2 0.25 = 0.25 \times (-2.000) = -0.5000$
- $0.20 \log_2 0.20 = 0.20 \times (-2.322) = -0.4644$
- $0.12 \log_2 0.12 = 0.12 \times (-3.059) = -0.3671$
- $0.08 \log_2 0.08 = 0.08 \times (-3.644) = -0.2915$
- $0.05 \log_2 0.05 = 0.05 \times (-4.322) = -0.2161$

$$H(X) = 0.5211 + 0.5000 + 0.4644 + 0.3671 + 0.2915 + 0.2161 = 2.3602 \text{ bits/symbol.}$$

**(ii) Shannon-Fano Code Construction:**

The Shannon-Fano algorithm sorts probabilities in decreasing order, then recursively divides the list into two halves with nearly equal probability totals, assigning '0' to the upper half and '1' to the lower half.

| Symbol | $p_i$ | Step 1 | Step 2 | Code | Length $l_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $s_1$ | 0.30 | 0 | 0 | **00** | 2 |
| $s_2$ | 0.25 | 0 | 1 | **01** | 2 |
| $s_3$ | 0.20 | 1 | 0 | **10** | 2 |
| $s_4$ | 0.12 | 1 | 1 | 110 | 3 |
| $s_5$ | 0.08 | 1 | 1 | 1110 | 4 |
| $s_6$ | 0.05 | 1 | 1 | 1111 | 4 |

**Kraft's Verification:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{6} 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-2} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-2} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-4} + 2^{-4}$$

$$= 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.0625 + 0.0625 = 1.0$$

Kraft's inequality is satisfied with equality (complete code). ✓

**(iii) Average Length and Efficiency:**

$$L = \sum p_i l_i = 0.30(2) + 0.25(2) + 0.20(2) + 0.12(3) + 0.08(4) + 0.05(4)$$

$$L = 0.60 + 0.50 + 0.40 + 0.36 + 0.32 + 0.20 = 2.38 \text{ bits/symbol.}$$

**Efficiency:**
$$\eta = \frac{H(X)}{L} = \frac{2.3602}{2.38} = 0.9917 = 99.17\%.$$

**Redundancy:** $R = 1 - \eta = 0.0083 = 0.83\%$.

**Shannon's Theorem Verification:**
$$H(X) = 2.3602 \leq L = 2.38 < H(X) + 1 = 3.3602. \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### **Part (b): 7 Marks**

**Q: Prove the converse of Shannon's source coding theorem. Show that for any uniquely decodable code, the average codeword length satisfies $L \geq H_D(X)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Setup.** Let $X$ be a source with symbols $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ having probabilities $p_1, \ldots, p_n$. Let $C(X)$ be the codeword length of $X$, with $\mathbb{E}[C(X)] = L$.

**Step 2: Define an auxiliary distribution.** For a uniquely decodable code, Kraft's inequality gives $\sum_i D^{-l_i} \leq 1$. Define:
$$q_i = \frac{D^{-l_i}}{\sum_j D^{-l_j}}.$$

Note that $q_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i q_i = 1$, so $\{q_i\}$ is a valid probability distribution.

**Step 3: Apply the log-sum inequality.** For any two distributions $p$ and $q$:
$$-\sum_i p_i \log q_i \geq -\sum_i p_i \log p_i = H(X).$$

**Step 4: Compute the LHS explicitly.**
$$-\sum_i p_i \log q_i = -\sum_i p_i \log\left(\frac{D^{-l_i}}{\sum_j D^{-l_j}}\right)$$

$$= -\sum_i p_i \left(-l_i \log D - \log \sum_j D^{-l_j}\right)$$

$$= L \log D + \log \sum_j D^{-l_j}.$$

**Step 5: Combine.** Using the log-sum inequality:
$$L \log D + \log \sum_j D^{-l_j} \geq H(X).$$

**Step 6: Final simplification.** Since $\sum_j D^{-l_j} \leq 1$ (Kraft), we have $\log \sum_j D^{-l_j} \leq 0$, so:
$$L \log D \geq H(X) \quad \Rightarrow \quad L \geq \frac{H(X)}{\log D} = H_D(X).$$

This proves the converse. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b)**:
> 1. **Forgetting the $D$ in the base**: Many students write $L \geq H(X)$ instead of $L \geq H_D(X) = H(X)/\log D$. The base-2 entropy $H(X)$ is in bits; the average length $L$ is in symbols. Always convert.
> 2. **Skipping the definition of $q_i$**: You *must* define a valid probability distribution to apply the log-sum inequality. Don't directly manipulate $\log(D^{-l_i})$.
> 3. **Ignoring the $Z = \sum D^{-l_j}$ term**: The $\log Z$ term is critical. Dropping it loses 2-3 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Alternative Choice]`

#### **Part (a): 7 Marks**

**Q: Consider a ternary (D = 3) source with symbols and probabilities:**

| Symbol | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $p_i$ | 0.40 | 0.25 | 0.15 | 0.12 | 0.08 |

**(i) Design a ternary prefix code using the Shannon-Fano method.**
**(ii) Verify Kraft's inequality.**
**(iii) Compute the entropy, average length, and efficiency. Verify Shannon's bound.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Ternary Code Construction:**

For D = 3, we divide into three groups at each step (assign digits 0, 1, 2).

| Symbol | $p_i$ | Step 1 (group) | Step 2 (group) | Code | $l_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $A$ | 0.40 | 0 | — | **0** | 1 |
| $B$ | 0.25 | 1 | 0 | **10** | 2 |
| $C$ | 0.15 | 1 | 1 | **11** | 2 |
| $D$ | 0.12 | 2 | 0 | **20** | 2 |
| $E$ | 0.08 | 2 | 1 | **21** | 2 |

**Prefix Check**: All codewords are uniquely decodable; no codeword is a prefix of another. ✓

**(ii) Kraft's Verification:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} 3^{-l_i} = 3^{-1} + 3^{-2} + 3^{-2} + 3^{-2} + 3^{-2}$$

$$= \frac{1}{3} + \frac{4}{9} = \frac{3}{9} + \frac{4}{9} = \frac{7}{9} \approx 0.778 \leq 1.$$

Kraft's inequality is satisfied. ✓

**(iii) Entropy, Average Length, Efficiency:**

**Entropy** (in bits/symbol):
$$H(X) = -[0.40 \log_2 0.40 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 + 0.12 \log_2 0.12 + 0.08 \log_2 0.08]$$

$$= 0.5288 + 0.5000 + 0.4105 + 0.3671 + 0.2915 = 2.0979 \text{ bits/symbol.}$$

**Average Length** (in ternary digits):
$$L = 0.40(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.15(2) + 0.12(2) + 0.08(2)$$
$$= 0.40 + 0.50 + 0.30 + 0.24 + 0.16 = 1.60 \text{ ternary digits/symbol.}$$

**Efficiency (in bits):**
$$H_D(X) = \frac{H(X)}{\log_2 3} = \frac{2.0979}{1.5850} = 1.3235 \text{ ternary digits/symbol.}$$

$$\eta = \frac{H_D(X)}{L} = \frac{1.3235}{1.60} = 0.8272 = 82.72\%.$$

**Shannon's Bound Verification (in ternary units):**
$$H_D(X) = 1.3235 \leq L = 1.60 < H_D(X) + 1 = 2.3235. \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### **Part (b): 7 Marks**

**Q: Prove the achievability part of Shannon's source coding theorem: there exists a prefix code with $L < H_D(X) + 1$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Length Assignment.** For each source symbol $x_i$ with probability $p_i$, define the codeword length:
$$l_i = \lceil -\log_D p_i \rceil.$$

By the property of the ceiling function:
$$-\log_D p_i \leq l_i < -\log_D p_i + 1. \quad (\star)$$

**Step 2: Kraft's Condition.** From the left inequality of $(\star)$:
$$D^{-l_i} \leq D^{-(-\log_D p_i)} = D^{\log_D p_i} = p_i.$$

Summing over all $i$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} D^{-l_i} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = 1.$$

The Kraft inequality is satisfied.

**Step 3: Existence of Prefix Code.** By the sufficiency of Kraft's inequality, a prefix code with codeword lengths $\{l_1, \ldots, l_n\}$ **exists**.

**Step 4: Expected Length Bound.** The expected codeword length is:
$$L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i l_i.$$

Using the right inequality of $(\star)$:
$$L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i l_i < \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \left(-\log_D p_i + 1\right) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_D p_i + \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i.$$

**Step 5: Final Bound.**
$$L < H_D(X) + 1. \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Part (b)**:
> 1. **Incorrect ceiling function usage**: The ceiling $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ rounds **up** to the next integer. Many students write $l_i = \lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ (floor), which gives a *lower* bound on length — wrong direction!
> 2. **Forgetting Step 3**: Kraft's *sufficiency* (not just necessity) is required to *construct* the code. Many students stop at Step 2, missing 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing bits and D-ary digits**: The bound $L < H_D(X) + 1$ uses $H_D(X)$ (in D-ary units), not $H(X)$ (in bits). Don't substitute bits directly when $D \neq 2$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Kraft Inequality & Shannon's Source Coding Theorem**

### **Kraft Inequality Essentials**

- ☐ **Statement**: $\sum D^{-l_i} \leq 1$ for any prefix code with lengths $l_1, \ldots, l_n$ over a D-ary alphabet.
- ☐ **Necessary** for any uniquely decodable code; **necessary AND sufficient** for prefix codes.
- ☐ **Tree interpretation**: Each codeword "claims" a fraction $D^{-l_i}$ of the D-ary tree.
- ☐ **Proof technique**: Count blocked leaves; divide by total leaves.
- ☐ **Equality case**: When $\sum D^{-l_i} = 1$, the code is called a *complete* code (no unassigned leaves).

### **Shannon's Source Coding Theorem Essentials**

- ☐ **Lower bound (converse)**: $L \geq H_D(X)$ — proved via the **log-sum inequality**.
- ☐ **Upper bound (achievability)**: $L < H_D(X) + 1$ — proved via the **ceiling function trick** and Kraft sufficiency.
- ☐ **Block coding refinement**: For $n$-symbol blocks, $H(X) \leq L/n < H(X) + 1/n$. As $n \to \infty$, $L/n \to H(X)$ (entropy limit achievable).
- ☐ **Optimal length formula**: $l_i^* = \lceil -\log_D p_i \rceil$ — the Shannon-Fano code.
- ☐ **Efficiency**: $\eta = H(X) / (L \log_2 D)$ for binary codes; generalization to D-ary.
- ☐ **Difference from Shannon's Channel Coding Theorem**: This is the *source* (noiseless) theorem, NOT the channel (noisy) theorem.

### **Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams**

- ☐ Confusing **prefix code** with **uniquely decodable code** (UD codes are more general).
- ☐ Mixing **bits** and **D-ary digits** without explicit base conversion.
- ☐ Forgetting that Kraft's inequality is **necessary but not sufficient** for UD codes.
- ☐ Using **floor** instead of **ceiling** in the Shannon-Fano length formula.
- ☐ Computing entropy with natural log $\ln$ instead of $\log_2$ (gives incorrect units).

### **Real-World Connections**

- ☐ **Huffman Coding**: Practical optimal code achieving $H(X) \leq L < H(X) + 1$.
- ☐ **Lempel-Ziv (ZIP, GIF)**: Asymptotically achieves $H(X)$ for ergodic sources.
- ☐ **Arithmetic Coding**: Approaches the entropy limit with fractional bits per symbol.
- ☐ **JPEG, MP3, PNG**: Use variants of these codes; entropy is the compression benchmark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.id: tModule 1: Information Theory and Source Coding_5
topics.title: Huffman Code and Operational Meaning of Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module Module 1: Information Theory and Source Coding
> - **Topic:** Huffman Code and Operational Meaning of Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Huffman Code — Formal KTU Definition

**Huffman Code** is an optimal, prefix-free, variable-length source coding procedure proposed by **David A. Huffman (1952)**. It assigns shorter binary codewords to symbols of higher probability and longer codewords to symbols of lower probability, such that the **average codeword length (L_avg)** is minimized subject to the **Kraft inequality** constraint:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$$

The procedure is **optimal** in the sense that no other uniquely decodable (prefix) code can produce a smaller average length for a given discrete memoryless source (DMS) with known symbol probabilities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Huffman coding is universally treated as the *gold-standard constructive proof* of the **Noiseless Source Coding Theorem (Shannon's First Theorem)**. KTU examiners expect students to compute the **tree, the codewords, L_avg, entropy H(X), efficiency η, and redundancy R** in a single, integrated question.

## 1.2 Operational Meaning of Entropy

**Entropy H(X)** of a discrete random variable X with pmf p(xᵢ) is:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i) \quad \text{bits/symbol}$$

The **operational meaning of entropy** is the *engineering interpretation* of this abstract quantity: **Entropy is the minimum achievable average number of bits per symbol** required to encode a source in the limit of long block codes. Stated precisely:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{L_n^{*}}{n} = H(X)$$

where $L_n^{*}$ is the minimum average length of a codeword representing $n$ source symbols jointly.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Backpack & Books" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Postal System**
> Imagine you are a postmaster in a town where **A** arrives in 50% of all letters, **B** in 20%, and rare symbols **C, D, E** in tiny amounts.
>
> - A **fixed-length code** (like assigning 3-digit PINs to every letter) is *fair* but *wasteful* — most digits are 0s padding the common letter A.
> - A **Huffman code** is like giving the town a **postal shortcut**: frequent letter **A** gets a *single-character stamp*, while the rare **E** gets a long, awkward code. Over millions of letters, the savings are enormous.
>
> **Entropy** is the *theoretical minimum postage* — the absolute lower bound the postmaster can never beat, no matter how clever the scheme. It is the *physical limit of compression*, like the speed of light is to physics.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Codeword** | Binary string assigned to a source symbol |
| **Codeword length** $l_i$ | Number of bits in codeword for symbol $x_i$ |
| **Average length** $L_{avg}$ | Expected bits per symbol $= \sum p_i l_i$ |
| **Entropy** $H(X)$ | Information content / minimum bits per symbol |
| **Efficiency** $\eta$ | Ratio $H(X) / L_{avg}$ |
| **Redundancy** $R$ | $1 - \eta$ |
| **Prefix code** | No codeword is a prefix of another (uniquely decodable) |
| **DMS** | Discrete Memoryless Source |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Huffman Coding is Optimal — The Logic Chain

The Huffman algorithm builds a **binary tree bottom-up** by repeatedly merging the two lowest-probability nodes. The optimality rests on these logical steps:

- **Step 1 (Greedy merging):** In any optimal tree, the two symbols with the *smallest* probabilities must be siblings at the deepest level. Merging them first can never make the tree worse.
- **Step 2 (Sub-problem reduction):** Once merged, treat the combined node as a *new symbol* with probability equal to the sum, and recurse. This is a textbook **greedy + optimal substructure** strategy.
- **Step 3 (Tree-to-code mapping):** Walking from the root to each leaf, assigning 0 to the left branch and 1 to the right (or vice-versa), generates the codewords.
- **Step 4 (Prefix property):** Since every symbol is a leaf (never an internal node), no codeword is a prefix of another. Hence Huffman codes are **uniquely decodable** without delimiters.

## 2.2 The Noiseless Source Coding Theorem (Operational Meaning)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon's First Theorem (1948):** For a DMS with entropy H(X), there exists a prefix code of block length $n$ whose average codeword length per symbol $L_n$ satisfies:
> $$H(X) \leq L_n < H(X) + \frac{1}{n}$$
> As $n \to \infty$, $L_n \to H(X)$. This is the *operational meaning*: **entropy is the compressibility limit**.

**Engineering Utility:**
- Determines the **channel capacity utilization** in data storage and transmission.
- Forms the **theoretical benchmark** against which any practical coder (Huffman, LZW, arithmetic, BZIP) is compared.
- Used in **JPEG, MP3, ZIP, GZIP, deflate** — every major lossless compressor uses entropy coding as its final stage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorize This!)

| Formula | Expression | Units / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol |
| **Average code length** | $L_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i l_i$ | bits/symbol |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{H(X)}{L_{avg}}$ | dimensionless, $\leq 1$ |
| **Redundancy** | $R = 1 - \eta$ | fraction of wasted bits |
| **Kraft inequality** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ | prefix code feasibility |
| **Source coding bound** | $H(X) \leq L_{avg} < H(X) + 1$ | Shannon's bound |
| **Joint entropy (n-symbol)** | $H(X^n) = n H(X)$ | for DMS only |
| **Variance of code length** | $\sigma^2 = \sum p_i (l_i - L_{avg})^2$ | for buffer analysis |
| **Information per symbol** | $I(x_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ | bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **second law of thermodynamics** with **Shannon entropy**. In information theory, entropy is *purely a measure of uncertainty* — it has *no* physical heat content. Always compute in **log base 2** for bits.

## 2.4 Engineering Application Map

- **Data Storage:** Disk compression (NTFS, ZIP) approaches entropy limit.
- **Communication:** Bandwidth minimization in satellite/telecom links.
- **Image Coding:** Baseline JPEG uses Huffman after DCT/quantization.
- **Cryptographic Steganography:** Huffman tables leak symbol statistics, so custom tables are used.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked-Out Example — Complete Huffman Coding (14-Mark Standard)

**Source Alphabet:** $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ with probabilities $\{0.5, 0.2, 0.15, 0.1, 0.05\}$

### 3.1.1 Huffman Tree Construction (Exhaustive)

**Iteration 1:** Sort probabilities in descending order.
$$p(A) = 0.50, \quad p(B) = 0.20, \quad p(C) = 0.15, \quad p(D) = 0.10, \quad p(E) = 0.05$$

Merge the two smallest: $D(0.10) + E(0.05) \rightarrow (D,E) = 0.15$

**Iteration 2:** New list:
$$A(0.50), \quad B(0.20), \quad C(0.15), \quad (D,E)(0.15)$$

Merge the two smallest (ties broken alphabetically by convention): $C(0.15) + (D,E)(0.15) \rightarrow (C,D,E) = 0.30$

**Iteration 3:** New list:
$$A(0.50), \quad (C,D,E)(0.30), \quad B(0.20)$$

Merge the two smallest: $B(0.20) + (C,D,E)(0.30) \rightarrow (B,C,D,E) = 0.50$

**Iteration 4:** New list:
$$A(0.50), \quad (B,C,D,E)(0.50)$$

Final merge: $A + (B,C,D,E) \rightarrow \text{Root} = 1.00$

### 3.1.2 Codeword Assignment (Assign 0 to upper, 1 to lower branch)

| Symbol | Codeword | Length $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | **0** | 1 |
| B | **10** | 2 |
| C | **110** | 3 |
| D | **1110** | 4 |
| E | **1111** | 4 |

### 3.1.3 Average Code Length $L_{avg}$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i \, l_i \\
&= p(A)\cdot 1 + p(B)\cdot 2 + p(C)\cdot 3 + p(D)\cdot 4 + p(E)\cdot 4 \\
&= 0.50(1) + 0.20(2) + 0.15(3) + 0.10(4) + 0.05(4) \\
&= 0.50 + 0.40 + 0.45 + 0.40 + 0.20 \\
&= 1.95 \;\; \text{bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.4 Entropy $H(X)$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -\sum_{i=1}^{5} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
&= -\bigl[0.50 \log_2 0.50 + 0.20 \log_2 0.20 + 0.15 \log_2 0.15 \\
&\qquad + 0.10 \log_2 0.10 + 0.05 \log_2 0.05\bigr]
\end{aligned}$$

Computing each term:
- $0.50 \log_2 0.50 = 0.50 \times (-1) = -0.5000$
- $0.20 \log_2 0.20 = 0.20 \times (-2.3219) = -0.4644$
- $0.15 \log_2 0.15 = 0.15 \times (-2.7370) = -0.4105$
- $0.10 \log_2 0.10 = 0.10 \times (-3.3219) = -0.3322$
- $0.05 \log_2 0.05 = 0.05 \times (-4.3219) = -0.2161$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -(-0.5000 - 0.4644 - 0.4105 - 0.3322 - 0.2161) \\
&= 1.9232 \;\; \text{bits/symbol}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.5 Efficiency and Redundancy

$$\eta = \frac{H(X)}{L_{avg}} = \frac{1.9232}{1.9500} = 0.9863 = 98.63\%$$

$$R = 1 - \eta = 1 - 0.9863 = 0.0137 = 1.37\%$$

### 3.1.6 Verification of Shannon's Bound

$$H(X) = 1.9232 \leq L_{avg} = 1.9500 < H(X) + 1 = 2.9232 \quad \checkmark$$

The bound is satisfied — the Huffman code is **nearly optimal** for this source.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Second Worked Example

**Source:** $\{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}$ with probabilities $\{0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1\}$

### 3.2.1 Tree Construction

| Step | Sorted List | Merge | New Node |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.4, 0.3, 0.2, 0.1 | $x_3(0.2) + x_4(0.1)$ | $N_1 = 0.3$ |
| 2 | 0.4, 0.3, $N_1(0.3)$ | $x_2(0.3) + N_1(0.3)$ | $N_2 = 0.6$ |
| 3 | 0.4, $N_2(0.6)$ | $x_1(0.4) + N_2(0.6)$ | Root = 1.0 |

### 3.2.2 Codes and Lengths

| Symbol | $p_i$ | Code | $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 0.4 | 0 | 1 |
| $x_2$ | 0.3 | 10 | 2 |
| $x_3$ | 0.2 | 110 | 3 |
| $x_4$ | 0.1 | 111 | 3 |

### 3.2.3 Performance Metrics

$$L_{avg} = 0.4(1) + 0.3(2) + 0.2(3) + 0.1(3) = 0.4 + 0.6 + 0.6 + 0.3 = 1.90 \;\; \text{bits/sym}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= -[0.4 \log_2 0.4 + 0.3 \log_2 0.3 + 0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0.1 \log_2 0.1] \\
&= -[0.4(-1.3219) + 0.3(-1.7370) + 0.2(-2.3219) + 0.1(-3.3219)] \\
&= -[-0.5288 - 0.5211 - 0.4644 - 0.3322] \\
&= 1.8465 \;\; \text{bits/sym}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\eta = \frac{1.8465}{1.9000} = 0.9718 = 97.18\%, \quad R = 2.82\%$$

---

## 3.3 Operational Meaning — Formal Derivation

Consider a DMS emitting $n$-symbol blocks $X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$ with $n^k$ typical sequences (as per the Asymptotic Equipartition Property). Each typical sequence has probability $\approx 2^{-nH(X)}$.

**Number of bits needed to enumerate typical sequences:**
$$n^k \approx 2^{nH(X)} \Rightarrow k \approx nH(X) \;\; \text{bits total}$$

**Per-symbol cost:**
$$\frac{k}{n} = H(X) \;\; \text{bits/symbol}$$

This is the *operational meaning*: $H(X)$ is the **per-symbol length** of the shortest code that handles *almost all* source outputs as $n \to \infty$. This is the foundation of **Shannon's Source Coding Theorem**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Huffman Algorithm — Procedural Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: List symbols with probabilities] --> B[Sort symbols in descending order of probability]
    B --> C{More than 1 node left?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Pick the two smallest probabilities]
    D --> E[Merge them into a new internal node with sum probability]
    E --> F[Insert new node back into sorted list]
    F --> C
    C -->|No| G[Root node complete with probability 1]
    G --> H[Traverse tree assigning 0 to left branch and 1 to right branch]
    H --> I[Read codeword from root to each leaf symbol]
    I --> J[Compute L_avg, H X, efficiency, redundancy]
    J --> K[End]
```

## 4.2 Huffman Tree for the 5-Symbol Example

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR["Root: 1.00"]
    nodeR --> nodeA["A: 0.50  (code: 0)"]
    nodeR --> nodeX["Internal: 0.50"]
    nodeX --> nodeB["B: 0.20  (code: 10)"]
    nodeX --> nodeY["Internal: 0.30"]
    nodeY --> nodeZ["Internal: 0.15"]
    nodeZ --> nodeC["C: 0.15  (code: 110)"]
    nodeY --> nodeDE["Internal: 0.15"]
    nodeDE --> nodeD["D: 0.10  (code: 1110)"]
    nodeDE --> nodeE["E: 0.05  (code: 1111)"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Huffman Coder/Decoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Symbol Stream] --> B[Probability Model / Statistics]
    B --> C[Huffman Tree Builder]
    C --> D[Codebook Table]
    A --> E[Encoder: Symbol to Codeword Mapping]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compressed Bit Stream]
    F --> G[Channel or Storage]
    G --> H[Decoder: Bit Stream to Codeword Matching]
    H --> I[Reconstructed Symbol Stream]
    D --> H
    H --> J[Output Sink]
    I --> J
```

## 4.4 Source Coding Theorem — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Discrete Memoryless Source] --> B[Source Encoder with Avg Length L]
    B --> C{Is L greater than or equal to H X?}
    C -->|Yes, always possible| D[Compressed Bit Stream]
    C -->|No, impossible| E[Violation of Shannon Bound]
    D --> F[Channel / Storage]
    F --> G[Source Decoder]
    G --> H[Reconstructed Source Output]
    H --> I[Verified: Average bits per symbol approaches H X as block length grows]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**State and explain the operational meaning of entropy.**

**Model Answer:**
The operational meaning of entropy, derived from Shannon's Noiseless Source Coding Theorem, states that **the entropy H(X) of a discrete memoryless source represents the minimum possible average number of binary digits (bits) per source symbol required to encode the source output in a uniquely decodable manner, in the asymptotic limit of arbitrarily long source blocks**.

In other words, **H(X) bits/symbol is the fundamental lower bound on the average codeword length** $L_{avg}$ of any lossless source code. Mathematically, $H(X) \leq L_{avg} < H(X) + 1$ for fixed-length encoding, and as block length $n \to \infty$, $L_n/n \to H(X)$. This makes entropy the **engineering benchmark of compressibility** for any DMS. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]`
**Why is Huffman coding considered optimal? Mention two key properties.**

**Model Answer:**
Huffman coding is optimal in the sense that **for a given discrete memoryless source with fixed symbol probabilities, no other uniquely decodable (prefix) code can produce a smaller average codeword length** $L_{avg}$.

Two key properties:
1. **Prefix-free property:** No codeword is a prefix of any other codeword, ensuring **unique decodability** without delimiters.
2. **Greedy optimal substructure:** The algorithm repeatedly merges the two least-probable symbols; the resulting tree provably yields the minimum $L_{avg}$ subject to the Kraft inequality. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** A discrete memoryless source emits five symbols $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ with probabilities $\{0.4, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1, 0.1\}$.
**(i)** Construct the Huffman code for this source. Show all intermediate steps.
**(ii)** Calculate the average code length $L_{avg}$ and verify the Kraft inequality.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same source, compute the entropy $H(X)$, the coding efficiency $\eta$, and the redundancy $R$. State the operational meaning of entropy in this context.
**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain Shannon's Noiseless Source Coding Theorem. Discuss the operational meaning of entropy with a suitable real-world analogy.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A source emits four symbols $\{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}$ with probabilities $\{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$.
**(i)** Construct the Huffman code, find $L_{avg}$, entropy $H(X)$, efficiency $\eta$, and redundancy $R$.
**(ii)** Show that this source can be encoded at the entropy limit.
**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions to Part B

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a)(i) Huffman Tree Construction
- **Step 1:** Probabilities sorted: A(0.4), B(0.2), C(0.2), D(0.1), E(0.1).
- **Step 2:** Merge D(0.1) + E(0.1) → node $N_1$ = 0.2.
- **Step 3:** Merge B(0.2) + $N_1$(0.2) → node $N_2$ = 0.4.
- **Step 4:** Merge A(0.4) + $N_2$(0.4) → Root = 0.8... 

(Correction: Continue merging until Root = 1.0)

- **Step 4 (corrected):** Sorted list: A(0.4), C(0.2), $N_2$(0.4) → Merge C(0.2) + $N_2$(0.4) → $N_3$ = 0.6.
- **Step 5:** Merge A(0.4) + $N_3$(0.6) → Root = 1.0.

**Codewords:**
| Symbol | $p_i$ | Code | $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0.4 | 0 | 1 |
| B | 0.2 | 100 | 3 |
| C | 0.2 | 101 | 3 |
| D | 0.1 | 110 | 3 |
| E | 0.1 | 111 | 3 |

#### Part (a)(ii) Average Length and Kraft Check
$$L_{avg} = 0.4(1) + 0.2(3) + 0.2(3) + 0.1(3) + 0.1(3) = 0.4 + 1.8 = 2.20 \;\; \text{bits/sym}$$

**Kraft inequality:**
$$\sum 2^{-l_i} = 2^{-1} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} + 2^{-3} = 0.5 + 0.125 \times 4 = 1.0 \leq 1 \;\; \checkmark$$

#### Part (b) Entropy, Efficiency, Redundancy
$$H(X) = -[0.4 \log_2 0.4 + 0.2 \log_2 0.2 \times 2 + 0.1 \log_2 0.1 \times 2]$$
$$= -[0.4(-1.3219) + 0.2(-2.3219)(2) + 0.1(-3.3219)(2)]$$
$$= -[-0.5288 - 0.9288 - 0.6644] = 2.1220 \;\; \text{bits/sym}$$

$$\eta = \frac{2.1220}{2.2000} = 0.9645 = 96.45\%, \quad R = 1 - 0.9645 = 3.55\%$$

**Operational meaning:** $H(X) = 2.1220$ bits/symbol is the theoretical minimum average length for this source. The Huffman code achieves 96.45% of this ideal, losing only 3.55% to structural redundancy.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) Noiseless Source Coding Theorem
Shannon's First Theorem states that for a DMS with entropy $H(X)$, there exists a prefix code of block length $n$ with average length $L_n$ satisfying:
$$H(X) \leq L_n < H(X) + \frac{1}{n}$$
As $n \to \infty$, $L_n/n \to H(X)$.

**Analogy:** A library with millions of books can be re-shelved into a denser catalog — the *entropy* is the irreducible "label space" no librarian can shrink below.

#### Part (b) Huffman Code for $\{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$

**Tree Construction:**
- Merge 0.125 + 0.125 = 0.25 → $N_1$
- Merge 0.25 + $N_1$(0.25) = 0.5 → $N_2$
- Merge 0.5 + $N_2$(0.5) = 1.0 → Root

**Codewords:**
| Symbol | $p_i$ | Code | $l_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | 0.5 | 0 | 1 |
| $x_2$ | 0.25 | 10 | 2 |
| $x_3$ | 0.125 | 110 | 3 |
| $x_4$ | 0.125 | 111 | 3 |

**Average length:**
$$L_{avg} = 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.125(3) + 0.125(3) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 \;\; \text{bits/sym}$$

**Entropy:**
$$H(X) = -[0.5(-1) + 0.25(-2) + 0.125(-3) \times 2] = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.75 = 1.75 \;\; \text{bits/sym}$$

**Efficiency and Redundancy:**
$$\eta = \frac{1.75}{1.75} = 1.0000 = 100\%, \quad R = 0\%$$

**Reaches entropy limit** because all $p_i$ are of the form $2^{-l_i}$, making Huffman coding perfectly optimal for this source. $\checkmark$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong tie-breaking:** When two symbols have equal probability, KTU expects a *deterministic* tie-breaking rule (e.g., alphabetical or by index). Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $H(X)$ with $L_{avg}$:** Some students compute only one and present it as both. Always show both explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting Kraft verification:** KTU frequently awards 1–2 marks just for verifying $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$. Skipping it is a free-mark loss.
> 4. **Mislabeling $\eta$ and $R$:** Redundancy is $1 - \eta$ (a fraction), not $L_{avg} - H(X)$. Conflating units costs marks.
> 5. **Skipping operational meaning:** A 14-mark question always carries a 2-mark "definition/operational meaning" component. Omitting it = automatic deduction.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
> - **Huffman Algorithm:** Sort → merge two smallest → repeat → assign 0/1.
> - **Optimality:** No other prefix code achieves lower $L_{avg}$ for the same source.
> - **Prefix property** is the reason Huffman codes are uniquely decodable without delimiters.
> - **Entropy $H(X)$** = minimum achievable bits/symbol in the limit $n \to \infty$.
> - **Source coding bound:** $H(X) \leq L_{avg} < H(X) + 1$ (Shannon's First Theorem).
> - **Efficiency $\eta = H(X) / L_{avg}$**, always $\leq 1$.
> - **Redundancy $R = 1 - \eta$** = fraction of bits that are "wasted."
> - **Kraft inequality** $\sum 2^{-l_i} \leq 1$ is *necessary* and *sufficient* for a prefix code to exist.
> - **Always verify Kraft** in KTU solutions — it is a free 1–2 mark bonus.
> - **Use $\log_2$** (not $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$) for entropy in bits.
> - **Tie-breaking** must be consistent and clearly stated.
> - **Operational meaning of entropy** is the *engineering interpretation* — the compressibility limit.
> - Huffman codes are **used in**: JPEG, MPEG, ZIP, GZIP, MP3, PNG.
> - Huffman is **NOT** arithmetic coding; arithmetic coding can beat Huffman on highly skewed sources because it encodes sequences, not individual symbols.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.id: tModule 1: Information Theory and Source Coding_6
topics.title: Channel Capacity of Discrete Memoryless Channels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508 > - **Module:** Module Module 1: Information Theory and Source Coding > - **Topic:** Channel Capacity of Discrete Memoryless Channels # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Discrete Memoryless Channel (DMC)** is a communication system model characterized by: - A finite input alphabet $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ - A finite output alphabet $\mathcal{Y} = \{y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_m\}$ - A transition probability matrix $P(Y \mid X)$ where each entry $p(y_j \mid x_i)$ represents the probability of receiving output $y_j$ given input $x_i$ - The **memoryless property**: $P(Y_k \mid X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k) = P(Y_k \mid X_k)$ The **Channel Capacity** $C$ of a DMC is defined as the maximum rate at which information can be reliably transmitted over the channel. Mathematically, it is the maximum mutual information between the input $X$ and output $Y$, optimized over all possible input probability distributions: $$C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y) \quad \text{bits per channel use}$$ where $I(X;Y)$ is the mutual information between input and output. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Channel capacity is a fundamental limit imposed by the physical channel. It represents the **highest achievable data rate** with arbitrarily small probability of error. No coding scheme can reliably transmit information at a rate exceeding $C$ (Shannon's Noisy Channel Coding Theorem). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are whispering a secret message through a **noisy, crowded hallway** to your friend: - **Input ($X$)**: The words you whisper - **Output ($Y$)**: What your friend actually hears (some words may be misheard due to noise) - **Transition probability $p(y \mid x)$**: The likelihood that "cat" is heard as "bat" or "cap" - **Channel Capacity ($C$)**: The **fastest rate at which you can whisper** such that your friend can still understand you perfectly, even with some background noise Think of it like a **water pipe with leaks**: - You pump water (information) into one end - Some water leaks out (noise corrupts data) - The **maximum flow rate of USEFUL water** at the output end is the channel capacity - No matter how good your pump (encoder) is, you cannot get more useful water than what the pipe allows > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Channel capacity is a property of the **channel alone**, NOT the encoding scheme. A clever coding scheme can *approach* this limit but never exceed it. ## 1.3 Fundamental Properties - **Capacity is a function of the channel, not the source.** Once the noise statistics are known, capacity is fixed. - Capacity is expressed in **bits per channel use** (or equivalently, bits per symbol). - The maximum is achieved at some specific input distribution $P^*(x)$ called the **capacity-achieving distribution**. - For a channel with $m$ input symbols, the optimization requires searching over an $(m-1)$-dimensional probability simplex. - **Channel capacity is bounded**: $0 \leq C \leq \log_2(\min(n, m))$ where $n = \vert \mathcal{X} \vert$ and $m = \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert$. ## 1.4 Shannon's Noisy Channel Coding Theorem (Informal Statement) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (Shannon, 1948):** For a DMC with capacity $C$: > 1. **Achievability:** For any rate $R < C$, there exists a coding scheme such that the probability of decoding error can be made arbitrarily small. > 2. **Converse:** For any rate $R > C$, the probability of error is bounded away from zero for any coding scheme. Reliable communication is impossible. This is one of the most profound results in all of information theory — it tells us **where the fundamental limit lies** and that the limit is **achievable in principle**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Discrete Memoryless Channel A DMC is fully specified by the triple $(\mathcal{X}, \mathcal{Y}, P(Y \mid X))$. Each column of the channel matrix corresponds to a conditional probability distribution that must sum to unity: $$\sum_{j=1}^{m} p(y_j \mid x_i) = 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$ This means each column of the channel matrix is a valid probability mass function. ## 2.2 Mutual Information Formulation The mutual information $I(X;Y)$ is computed as: $$I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)$$ where: - $H(Y)$ = entropy of output (uncertainty before observation) - $H(Y \mid X)$ = conditional entropy (noise entropy / equivocation) - $H(X \mid Y)$ = conditional entropy of input given output - $H(X,Y)$ = joint entropy Equivalently, the **symmetric (Kullback-Leibler) form**: $$I(X;Y) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} p(x_i, y_j) \log_2 \frac{p(x_i, y_j)}{p(x_i) p(y_j)}$$ The interpretation: $I(X;Y)$ measures how much observing $Y$ reduces uncertainty about $X$. ## 2.3 Capacity as a Concave Maximization **Why is the maximum achieved?** - $I(X;Y)$ is a **concave function** of $P(x)$ (this is a celebrated result of information theory). - A concave function over a compact convex set (the probability simplex) achieves its maximum at either an interior critical point or on the boundary. - This guarantees that a global maximum exists. **Computational Strategy:** 1. Compute output distribution: $p(y_j) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) p(y_j \mid x_i)$ 2. Form the mutual information $I(X;Y)$ as a function of $P(x) = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$. 3. Maximize subject to constraints: $p_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_i p_i = 1$. 4. Use **Lagrange multipliers** or **Blahut-Arimoto algorithm** for general channels. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---------|---------|-------| | Channel Capacity | $C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y)$ | Bits per channel use | | Mutual Information | $I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \vert X)$ | Symmetric in $X$ and $Y$ | | Joint Probability | $p(x_i, y_j) = p(x_i) \cdot p(y_j \vert x_i)$ | Channel law | | Marginal Output | $p(y_j) = \sum_{i} p(x_i) p(y_j \vert x_i)$ | Row sums of joint matrix | | Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC) | $C = 1 - H_2(p)$ | $p$ = crossover probability | | Binary Erasure Channel (BEC) | $C = 1 - \alpha$ | $\alpha$ = erasure probability | | Binary Entropy Function | $H_2(p) = -p \log_2 p - (1-p) \log_2(1-p)$ | Range: $[0, 1]$ bit | | Upper Bound on Capacity | $C \leq \log_2 \min(\vert \mathcal{X} \vert, \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert)$ | Cardinality bound | | Fano's Inequality | $H(X \vert Y) \leq H_2(P_e) + P_e \log_2(\vert \mathcal{X} \vert - 1)$ | Lower bound on error | | Noiseless Channel | $C = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{X} \vert$ | All input distinguishable | | Completely Noisy Channel | $C = 0$ | $Y$ independent of $X$ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Memory Aid:** The expression $1 - H_2(p)$ for BSC is the single most important formula for Part B problems. Memorize the binary entropy function $H_2(p)$ and its behavior: $H_2(0) = 0$, $H_2(0.5) = 1$, $H_2(1) = 0$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Channel capacity concepts drive modern engineering: - **5G/6G Wireless Systems:** Determining maximum spectral efficiency (bits/Hz) of a fading channel guides modulation and coding choices. - **Fiber-Optic Communications:** Shannon limit sets the target for forward error correction (FEC) codes like LDPC and Polar codes. - **Data Storage:** Capacity of magnetic recording channels (PRML, EPRML) defines the maximum density limit. - **Deep Space Communication (NASA/JPL):** Voyager probes operate near the Shannon limit using turbo codes. - **Satellite TV (DVB-S2):** Uses LDPC codes achieving > 0.95 of Shannon capacity. ## 2.6 Symmetric Channels — Closed-Form Capacity A channel is **symmetric** if: - All rows of the channel matrix are permutations of each other, AND - All columns of the channel matrix are permutations of each other. For a symmetric channel, **uniform input distribution** $P(x_i) = 1/n$ is optimal, and: $$C = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert - H(\text{row of } P(Y \mid X))$$ This is a huge simplification that appears frequently in KTU problems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Strongly Symmetric Channel:** If every column contains exactly $k$ non-zero entries, all equal to $1/k$, then **uniform input is provably optimal**. Examples: BSC ($k=2, 1/2$), BEC-like channels. ## 2.7 Properties of Channel Capacity - **$C \geq 0$**: Capacity is non-negative (mutual information is non-negative). - **$C \leq H(X)$**: Capacity is bounded by source entropy. - **$C \leq \log_2 \vert \mathcal{X} \vert$**: Cannot exceed information content of input alphabet. - **$C$ is continuous** in the entries of the channel matrix. - **Data Processing Inequality:** If $X \to Y \to Z$ is a Markov chain, then $I(X;Y) \geq I(X;Z)$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capacity of Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC) ### Problem Setup A BSC has input alphabet $\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1\}$, output alphabet $\mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1\}$, and crossover probability $p$. The channel flips the bit with probability $p$: $$p(y \mid x) = \begin{cases} 1-p & \text{if } y = x \\ p & \text{if } y \neq x \end{cases}$$ Channel matrix: $$P(Y \mid X) = \begin{bmatrix} 1-p & p \\ p & 1-p \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define input distribution. Let $P(X=0) = \pi$ and $P(X=1) = 1 - \pi$. **Step 2:** Compute the joint probabilities $p(x, y) = p(x) p(y \mid x)$. $$p(0,0) = \pi(1-p), \quad p(0,1) = \pi p$$ $$p(1,0) = (1-\pi)p, \quad p(1,1) = (1-\pi)(1-p)$$ **Step 3:** Compute marginal output distribution. $$P(Y=0) = p(0,0) + p(1,0) = \pi(1-p) + (1-\pi)p = \pi - 2\pi p + p$$ $$P(Y=1) = \pi p + (1-\pi)(1-p) = 1 - \pi + 2\pi p - p = 1 - P(Y=0)$$ **Step 4:** Express $I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$. We have $H(Y \mid X) = H_2(p)$ (since the noise is independent of the input — a fundamental property of the BSC). **Step 5:** Compute $H(Y)$. $$H(Y) = -P(Y=0) \log_2 P(Y=0) - P(Y=1) \log_2 P(Y=1)$$ **Step 6:** Maximize $I(X;Y)$ over $\pi$. $$I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H_2(p)$$ We need to maximize $H(Y)$. Since $Y$ is binary, $H(Y) \leq 1$ with equality when $P(Y=0) = P(Y=1) = 1/2$. **Step 7:** Find $\pi$ such that $P(Y=0) = 1/2$. $$\pi(1-p) + (1-\pi)p = \frac{1}{2}$$ $$\pi - 2\pi p + p = \frac{1}{2}$$ $$\pi(1 - 2p) = \frac{1}{2} - p = \frac{1 - 2p}{2}$$ $$\pi = \frac{1}{2}$$ So **uniform input distribution is optimal** (as expected by symmetry). **Step 8:** Final capacity formula. $$\boxed{C_{BSC} = 1 - H_2(p) \text{ bits per channel use}}$$ **Step 9:** Verify boundary behavior. - If $p = 0$ (noiseless): $H_2(0) = 0$, so $C = 1$ bit. ✓ - If $p = 1/2$ (pure noise): $H_2(0.5) = 1$, so $C = 0$ bits. ✓ - If $p = 1$ (inverted): $C = 0$ bits (perfectly correlated but useless since ambiguous). ✓ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacity of Binary Erasure Channel (BEC) ### Problem Setup A BEC has input alphabet $\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1\}$ and output alphabet $\mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1, e\}$ where $e$ denotes erasure. Each bit is erased with probability $\alpha$ and received correctly with probability $1-\alpha$: $$P(Y = x \mid X = x) = 1 - \alpha, \quad P(Y = e \mid X = x) = \alpha$$ Channel matrix: $$P(Y \mid X) = \begin{bmatrix} 1-\alpha & 0 & \alpha \\ 0 & 1-\alpha & \alpha \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Compute the marginal output probabilities. $$P(Y=0) = \pi(1-\alpha)$$ $$P(Y=1) = (1-\pi)(1-\alpha)$$ $$P(Y=e) = \pi \alpha + (1-\pi)\alpha = \alpha$$ **Step 2:** Compute $H(Y)$ from the three-element output distribution. $$H(Y) = -P(Y=0)\log_2 P(Y=0) - P(Y=1)\log_2 P(Y=1) - \alpha \log_2 \alpha$$ Note: $0 \log_2 0 = 0$ by convention. **Step 3:** Compute $H(Y \mid X)$. $$H(Y \mid X) = \sum_x P(x) H(Y \mid X=x) = H_2(\alpha)$$ Since each row of the channel matrix gives the same conditional entropy $H_2(\alpha)$ (one correct outcome with prob $1-\alpha$, one erasure with prob $\alpha$). **Step 4:** Substitute into mutual information. $$I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H_2(\alpha)$$ **Step 5:** Expand $H(Y)$. Let $q = \pi(1-\alpha)$, then $P(Y=0) = q$, $P(Y=1) = (1-\pi)(1-\alpha)$, $P(Y=e) = \alpha$. $$I(X;Y) = -q \log_2 q - (1-\pi)(1-\alpha) \log_2[(1-\pi)(1-\alpha)] - \alpha \log_2 \alpha - H_2(\alpha)$$ Note: $-q \log_2 q = -\pi(1-\alpha)[\log_2 \pi + \log_2(1-\alpha)]$. **Step 6:** Simplify. After expansion, the terms involving $\log_2(1-\alpha)$ cancel between $H(Y)$ and $H_2(\alpha)$, leaving: $$I(X;Y) = -(1-\alpha)[\pi \log_2 \pi + (1-\pi)\log_2(1-\pi)]$$ $$= (1-\alpha) H_2(\pi)$$ **Step 7:** Maximize $I(X;Y)$ over $\pi$. The factor $(1-\alpha)$ is independent of $\pi$, so we maximize $H_2(\pi)$, which is maximized at $\pi = 1/2$. **Step 8:** Final capacity formula. $$\boxed{C_{BEC} = (1-\alpha) \text{ bits per channel use} = 1 - \alpha}$$ This is a beautifully clean result: the capacity is simply the fraction of bits that are **NOT erased**. ## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Numerical BSC Calculation **Problem:** A BSC has crossover probability $p = 0.1$. Compute the channel capacity. **Step 1:** Calculate $H_2(0.1)$. $$H_2(0.1) = -0.1 \log_2(0.1) - 0.9 \log_2(0.9)$$ **Step 2:** Compute each term. $$-0.1 \log_2(0.1) = -0.1 \times (-3.3219) = 0.33219$$ $$-0.9 \log_2(0.9) = -0.9 \times (-0.15200) = 0.13680$$ **Step 3:** Sum the terms. $$H_2(0.1) = 0.33219 + 0.13680 = 0.46899 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 4:** Compute capacity. $$C = 1 - 0.46899 = 0.53101 \text{ bits per channel use}$$ **Interpretation:** For every 100 bits transmitted, only ~53 bits of information can be reliably recovered, even with the best possible code. ## 3.4 Worked Example 2: Numerical BEC Calculation **Problem:** A BEC has erasure probability $\alpha = 0.3$. Compute the capacity. **Step 1:** Apply formula directly. $$C = 1 - \alpha = 1 - 0.3 = 0.7 \text{ bits per channel use}$$ **Interpretation:** For every 10 bits sent, 7 can be reliably decoded; the other 3 are erased (and the decoder must be designed to handle this). ## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Generic Channel Matrix **Problem:** A DMC has the channel matrix: $$P(Y \mid X) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$ Find the channel capacity. **Step 1:** Note that each row is a permutation of $(0.5, 0.5, 0)$ and each column contains exactly two non-zero entries each equal to $0.5$. This is a **strongly symmetric channel** — uniform input is optimal. **Step 2:** With $P(X=0) = P(X=1) = 0.5$, compute joint probabilities. $$p(0,0) = 0.25, \quad p(0,1) = 0.25, \quad p(0,2) = 0$$ $$p(1,0) = 0, \quad p(1,1) = 0.25, \quad p(1,2) = 0.25$$ **Step 3:** Compute marginal output distribution. $$P(Y=0) = 0.25, \quad P(Y=1) = 0.5, \quad P(Y=2) = 0.25$$ **Step 4:** Compute $H(Y)$. $$H(Y) = -0.25 \log_2(0.25) - 0.5 \log_2(0.5) - 0.25 \log_2(0.25)$$ $$= 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 5:** Compute $H(Y \mid X)$ using uniform input. For each input, the conditional entropy is $H(0.5, 0.5, 0) = 0.5$ bits. So: $$H(Y \mid X) = 0.5 \times 0.5 + 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.5 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 6:** Compute capacity. $$C = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X) = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0 \text{ bit per channel use}$$ **Step 7:** Sanity check using the symmetric formula. For a strongly symmetric channel: $C = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert - H(\text{row}) = \log_2(3) - H(0.5, 0.5, 0) = 1.585 - 0.5 = 1.085$ bits. Hmm, the discrepancy indicates that this channel is **symmetric (row)** but the column structure is also symmetric. Let me recompute carefully. Re-examining: column sums are $(0.5, 1.0, 0.5)$ which are NOT all equal. So the channel is **row-symmetric only**, not strongly symmetric. The capacity is the direct computation result: $$C = 1.0 \text{ bit per channel use}$$ ## 3.6 Fano's Inequality — Lower Bound on Error Probability **Statement:** For any estimator $\hat{X}$ of $X$ with error probability $P_e = P(\hat{X} \neq X)$: $$H(X \mid Y) \leq H_2(P_e) + P_e \log_2(\vert \mathcal{X} \vert - 1)$$ **Application to Capacity Converse:** By the chain rule, $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y) \geq H(X) - H_2(P_e) - P_e \log_2(\vert \mathcal{X} \vert - 1)$. For reliable communication, $P_e \to 0$ requires $H(X) \leq I(X;Y) \leq C$. This proves the converse of Shannon's theorem. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Discrete Memoryless Channel Model ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Source X
Input Alphabet] enc[Encoder
Maps to Channel Input] ch[Discrete Memoryless Channel
Transition P of Y given X
Noise Source Z] dec[Decoder
Estimates X hat from Y] dst[Destination
Output X hat] src -->|bits or symbols| enc enc -->|X in alphabet X| ch ch -.->|adds noise| zsym[Noise Z
Independent] zsym -.-> ch ch -->|Y in alphabet Y| dec dec -->|X hat| dst style src fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4 style enc fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E style ch fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#D62728 style dec fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E style dst fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4 style zsym fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#7F7F7F ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Information Flow and Capacity Computation ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Define Channel
Matrix P of Y given X] --> B[Parameterize Input
Distribution p of x] B --> C[Compute Joint
p of x,y = p of x times p of y given x] C --> D[Compute Marginal
p of y by summing over x] D --> E[Compute H of Y
Output Entropy] D --> F[Compute H of Y given X
Conditional Entropy] E --> G[Compute I of X semicolon Y
equals H of Y minus H of Y given X] F --> G G --> H[Maximize I of X semicolon Y
over all valid p of x] H --> I[Capacity C
bits per channel use] style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4 style I fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:3px style G fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E style H fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#D62728 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Diagram: BSC vs BEC ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BSC[Binary Symmetric Channel BSC] BSC1[X equals 0] -->|1 minus p| BSC2[Y equals 0] BSC1 -->|p| BSC3[Y equals 1] BSC4[X equals 1] -->|p| BSC2 BSC4 -->|1 minus p| BSC3 end subgraph BEC[Binary Erasure Channel BEC] BEC1[X equals 0] -->|1 minus alpha| BEC2[Y equals 0] BEC1 -->|alpha| BEC3[Y equals e] BEC4[X equals 1] -->|1 minus alpha| BEC5[Y equals 1] BEC4 -->|alpha| BEC3 end BSCnote[Capacity equals 1 minus H sub 2 of p] BECnote[Capacity equals 1 minus alpha] BSC -.-> BSCnote BEC -.-> BECnote style BSCnote fill:#FFFFCC,stroke:#888800,stroke-width:2px style BECnote fill:#CCFFCC,stroke:#008800,stroke-width:2px style BSC fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#D62728 style BEC fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#1F77B4 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map: Shannon's Noisy Channel Coding Theorem ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Given DMC
with Capacity C] --> B{Compare Rate R
with Capacity C} B -->|R less than C| C[Achievable Region
Reliable Communication
Error Probability to 0] B -->|R greater than C| D[Impossible Region
Error Probability
bounded away from 0] B -->|R equals C| E[Critical Limit
Asymptotic Boundary] C --> F[Design Codes with
Block Length to infinity] D --> G[Fano Inequality
Provides Converse Proof] style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4 style B fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22 style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C style E fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#B8860B ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** **Define a Discrete Memoryless Channel (DMC) and state the expression for its capacity.** **Model Answer:** A Discrete Memoryless Channel (DMC) is a communication channel characterized by a finite input alphabet $\mathcal{X}$, a finite output alphabet $\mathcal{Y}$, and a set of conditional probabilities $P(y \mid x)$ for $x \in \mathcal{X}, y \in \mathcal{Y}$, such that the channel output at time $k$ depends only on the input at time $k$ (memoryless property): $$P(Y_k \mid X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_k) = P(Y_k \mid X_k)$$ The channel capacity is defined as: $$C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y) \quad \text{bits per channel use}$$ where $I(X;Y)$ is the mutual information between input $X$ and output $Y$, and the maximization is over all valid input probability distributions $P(x)$. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** **State Shannon's Noisy Channel Coding Theorem.** **Model Answer:** **Shannon's Noisy Channel Coding Theorem:** For a Discrete Memoryless Channel with capacity $C$ bits per channel use: 1. **(Achievability/Positive Part):** For any information rate $R < C$ and any small $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists a coding scheme of block length $n$ and a decoding rule such that the probability of decoding error $P_e \leq \varepsilon$. 2. **(Converse/Negative Part):** For any rate $R > C$, the probability of decoding error is bounded below by a positive constant for all coding schemes. Reliable communication at rate $R > C$ is impossible. This theorem establishes channel capacity as the **fundamental limit** of reliable communication. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 1 Model]** **(a)** For a Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC) with crossover probability $p = 0.2$, derive the channel capacity expression and compute its numerical value. **(b)** Explain the significance of Shannon's Noisy Channel Coding Theorem. How does it influence the design of modern communication systems? #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Channel Definition (1 mark)** A BSC has input alphabet $\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1\}$, output alphabet $\mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1\}$, and crossover probability $p$: $$P(Y \mid X) = \begin{bmatrix} 1-p & p \\ p & 1-p \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2: Express Mutual Information (2 marks)** Let $P(X=0) = \pi$, $P(X=1) = 1-\pi$. By symmetry of the BSC, $H(Y \mid X) = H_2(p)$ (the noise entropy). Therefore: $$I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H_2(p)$$ **Step 3: Maximize over input distribution (2 marks)** $H(Y) \leq 1$ with equality when $P(Y=0) = P(Y=1) = 1/2$. Setting $P(Y=0) = \pi(1-p) + (1-\pi)p = 1/2$ gives $\pi = 1/2$ (uniform distribution is optimal). **Step 4: Capacity formula (1 mark)** $$\boxed{C_{BSC} = 1 - H_2(p)}$$ **Step 5: Numerical computation (1 mark)** With $p = 0.2$: $$H_2(0.2) = -0.2 \log_2(0.2) - 0.8 \log_2(0.8) = 0.4644 + 0.2575 = 0.7219 \text{ bits}$$ $$C = 1 - 0.7219 = 0.2781 \text{ bits per channel use}$$ #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Significance (4 marks):** 1. **Theoretical Limit:** Shannon's theorem establishes that there exists a **maximum rate** (the capacity $C$) at which information can be transmitted over a noisy channel with arbitrarily low error. This is the **fundamental limit** of communication. 2. **Existence of Good Codes:** The theorem guarantees the existence of codes that approach this limit. It does NOT tell us how to construct them — this is left to code designers. 3. **Surprising Insight:** The theorem proves that noise does NOT impose a hard rate ceiling; it only imposes a *soft* ceiling. Error-free communication is possible at any rate below $C$ by using sufficiently long block codes. 4. **Separation of Source and Channel Coding:** Shannon's theorem allows the separation of source compression and channel protection, simplifying system design. **Influence on Modern Systems (3 marks):** - **5G/4G:** LDPC and Polar codes operate within 0.5–1 dB of the Shannon limit. - **Optical Fiber:** Soft-decision FEC and digital coherent detection push spectral efficiency near the nonlinear Shannon limit. - **Deep Space Communication:** NASA uses concatenated turbo codes achieving ~1 dB from capacity on the Voyager missions. - **Data Storage:** Modern hard drives use signal processing and coding that operate within fractions of a dB of magnetic recording channel capacity. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Module 1 Model]** **(a)** A Binary Erasure Channel (BEC) has erasure probability $\alpha = 0.4$. Derive the channel capacity formula and calculate its value. **(b)** Define the following terms with suitable examples: (i) Channel matrix (ii) Symmetric channel (iii) Strongly symmetric channel. Hence compute the capacity of the channel with matrix $P(Y \mid X) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.25 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & 0.5 & 0.25 \\ 0.25 & 0.25 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$. #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1: BEC Definition (1 mark)** A BEC has input alphabet $\mathcal{X} = \{0,1\}$, output alphabet $\mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1, e\}$, and erasure probability $\alpha$: $$P(Y \mid X) = \begin{bmatrix} 1-\alpha & 0 & \alpha \\ 0 & 1-\alpha & \alpha \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2: Compute Output Marginal (1 mark)** With $P(X=0) = \pi$, $P(X=1) = 1-\pi$: $$P(Y=0) = \pi(1-\alpha), \quad P(Y=1) = (1-\pi)(1-\alpha), \quad P(Y=e) = \alpha$$ **Step 3: Compute Conditional Entropy (1 mark)** Each row has the same entropy: $H(Y \mid X=x) = H_2(\alpha)$. Hence: $$H(Y \mid X) = H_2(\alpha)$$ **Step 4: Compute $I(X;Y)$ (2 marks)** After algebraic expansion (substituting marginals and simplifying the $\log_2(1-\alpha)$ terms): $$I(X;Y) = (1-\alpha) H_2(\pi)$$ **Step 5: Maximize and finalize (1 mark)** $H_2(\pi)$ is maximized at $\pi = 1/2$, giving $H_2(1/2) = 1$. Therefore: $$\boxed{C_{BEC} = 1 - \alpha}$$ **Step 6: Numerical calculation (1 mark)** With $\alpha = 0.4$: $$C = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6 \text{ bits per channel use}$$ #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **(i) Channel Matrix — 2 marks** The **channel matrix** (or transition probability matrix) of a DMC is the matrix $P(Y \mid X)$ whose $(i,j)$-th entry is $p(y_j \mid x_i)$. Each column must sum to 1. Example: BSC with $p = 0.1$ has $P(Y \mid X) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & 0.1 \\ 0.1 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}$. **(ii) Symmetric Channel — 2 marks** A channel is **symmetric** if every row of $P(Y \mid X)$ is a permutation of every other row, AND every column is a permutation of every other column. For symmetric channels, uniform input distribution is optimal and: $$C = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert - H(\text{row})$$ Example: The BSC is symmetric. **(iii) Strongly Symmetric Channel — 1 mark** A channel is **strongly symmetric** if every column contains exactly $k$ non-zero entries, all equal to $1/k$. Example: The matrix given in the problem is strongly symmetric with $k = 2$. **Capacity Calculation — 2 marks** For the given channel, $k = 2$ (each column has two entries of 0.5 and one 0.25... wait, let me check). Re-examining: Column 1 is $(0.5, 0.25, 0.25)^T$ — entries sum to 1 but are not all equal. Therefore it is **not strongly symmetric**. However, it is **symmetric** (rows are permutations, columns are permutations). For symmetric channel, capacity uses uniform input $P(X) = (1/3, 1/3, 1/3)$: $$H(Y) = \log_2(3) = 1.585 \text{ bits}$$ $$H(Y \mid X) = H(0.5, 0.25, 0.25) = -0.5 \log_2(0.5) - 0.25 \log_2(0.25) - 0.25 \log_2(0.25) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ bits}$$ $$C = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X) = 1.585 - 1.5 = 0.085 \text{ bits per channel use}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing $H(Y)$ vs $H(Y \mid X)$:** Many students compute $H(Y \mid X)$ incorrectly by treating the channel matrix rows incorrectly. **Remember:** $H(Y \mid X) = \sum_x p(x) H(Y \mid X=x)$ — average the row entropies, weighted by $p(x)$. > > 2. **Forgetting to Maximize:** Some students compute $I(X;Y)$ at uniform input and stop. The examiner expects you to either: (a) explicitly maximize using calculus/Lagrange multipliers, OR (b) justify why uniform input is optimal (symmetry argument). > > 3. **Wrong Logarithm Base:** KTU requires $\log_2$ for capacity in **bits per channel use**. Using natural log gives **nats**. Mixing units is a guaranteed deduction. > > 4. **$0 \log 0 = 0$:** For erasure channels, $P(Y=0) = 0$ is possible. Use the convention $0 \log_2 0 = 0$ explicitly, or examiners may deduct marks. > > 5. **Missing the "Memoryless" Justification:** For DMC problems, briefly mention the memoryless assumption $P(Y_k \mid X^k) = P(Y_k \mid X_k)$ when defining the channel. > > 6. **Confusing "Erasure" with "Error":** In BEC, erased bits are KNOWN to be erased (output $e$); the decoder knows they are unreliable. In BSC, errors are undetected. This affects capacity significantly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist - [ ] **DMC definition:** Finite input/output alphabets + transition matrix + memoryless property - [ ] **Channel capacity:** $C = \max_{P(x)} I(X;Y)$ — maximum over input distribution - [ ] **Mutual information chain:** $I(X;Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y) = I(Y;X)$ - [ ] **BSC capacity:** $C = 1 - H_2(p)$ where $p$ is crossover probability - [ ] **BEC capacity:** $C = 1 - \alpha$ where $\alpha$ is erasure probability (cleaner formula!) - [ ] **Binary entropy:** $H_2(p) = -p \log_2 p - (1-p) \log_2(1-p)$, range $[0,1]$ bit - [ ] **Symmetric channel:** All rows are permutations, all columns are permutations → uniform input is optimal - [ ] **Strongly symmetric:** Columns have identical sets of non-zero entries - [ ] **Shannon's theorem:** Achievability (rates $< C$ are possible) + Converse (rates $> C$ are impossible) - [ ] **Fano's inequality:** $H(X \mid Y) \leq H_2(P_e) + P_e \log_2(\vert \mathcal{X} \vert - 1)$ — used to prove converse - [ ] **Data Processing Inequality:** $X \to Y \to Z$ Markov chain implies $I(X;Y) \geq I(X;Z)$ - [ ] **Upper bound:** $C \leq \log_2 \min(\vert \mathcal{X} \vert, \vert \mathcal{Y} \vert)$ - [ ] **Noiseless channel:** $C = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{X} \vert$ (all inputs distinguishable) - [ ] **Pure noise channel:** $C = 0$ (input and output are independent) - [ ] **Concave maximization:** $I(X;Y)$ is concave in $P(x)$, so global maximum exists ### 📌 Key Numerical Values to Memorize | $p$ | $H_2(p)$ (bits) | $C_{BSC}$ (bits) | |-----|-----------------|------------------| | 0.0 | 0.000 | 1.000 | | 0.1 | 0.469 | 0.531 | | 0.2 | 0.722 | 0.278 | | 0.25 | 0.811 | 0.189 | | 0.3 | 0.881 | 0.119 | | 0.4 | 0.971 | 0.029 | | 0.5 | 1.000 | 0.000 | ### 🧠 Engineering Intuition Channel capacity answers the question: **"What is the best I can possibly do?"** The answer depends ONLY on the channel noise characteristics — not on the coding scheme, not on the source, not on the modulation. Modern codes (LDPC, Polar, Turbo) approach this limit but never exceed it.

topics.id: tModule 1: Information Theory and Source Coding_7
topics.title: Binary Symmetric Channels (BSC) and Binary Erasure Channels (BEC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module Module 1: Information Theory and Source Coding
> - **Topic:** Binary Symmetric Channels (BSC) and Binary Erasure Channels (BEC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Discrete Memoryless Channel (DMC) Framework

Both the **Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC)** and the **Binary Erasure Channel (BEC)** are canonical examples of a **Discrete Memoryless Channel (DMC)** — a fundamental model in Shannon's Information Theory. A DMC is defined by:

- A finite input alphabet $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$
- A finite output alphabet $\mathcal{Y} = \{y_1, y_2, \dots, y_m\}$
- A set of conditional transition probabilities $P(y_j \mid x_i)$ for all $i, j$
- The "memoryless" property: $P(y_k \mid x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k) = P(y_k \mid x_k)$

The channel is completely characterized by its **channel transition matrix** (also called the channel matrix or probability matrix) $\mathbf{P}$, where the entry $P_{ij} = P(y_j \mid x_i)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless Property (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight):** The output at time $k$ depends *only* on the input at time $k$, not on any past inputs or outputs. This Markov-like property enables tractable capacity calculations.

### 1.2 Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC) — Formal Definition

A **Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC)** is a DMC with the following properties:

- Input alphabet: $\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1\}$
- Output alphabet: $\mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1\}$
- A single parameter $p$, called the **crossover probability** or **bit error probability**, such that:

$$P(Y = 0 \mid X = 1) = P(Y = 1 \mid X = 0) = p$$

$$P(Y = 0 \mid X = 0) = P(Y = 1 \mid X = 1) = 1 - p$$

The "symmetric" in BSC refers to the fact that the probability of a $0 \to 1$ error equals the probability of a $1 \to 0$ error — the channel is symmetric across both inputs.

The **channel transition matrix** for a BSC is:

$$\mathbf{P}_{\text{BSC}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - p & p \\ p & 1 - p \end{bmatrix}$$

where row $i$ corresponds to input $X = i-1$ and column $j$ corresponds to output $Y = j-1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always clearly label the rows of the channel matrix as input symbols ($X$) and columns as output symbols ($Y$). A common error is reversing this convention and losing marks in numerical problems.

### 1.3 Binary Erasure Channel (BEC) — Formal Definition

A **Binary Erasure Channel (BEC)** is a DMC where:

- Input alphabet: $\mathcal{X} = \{0, 1\}$
- Output alphabet: $\mathcal{Y} = \{0, 1, e\}$, where $e$ is the **erasure symbol** (often denoted as `?` or $\perp$)
- A single parameter $\epsilon$, the **erasure probability**, where:

$$P(Y = e \mid X = 0) = P(Y = e \mid X = 1) = \epsilon$$

$$P(Y = 0 \mid X = 0) = P(Y = 1 \mid X = 1) = 1 - \epsilon$$

$$P(Y = 1 \mid X = 0) = P(Y = 0 \mid X = 1) = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The BEC captures scenarios where the receiver *detects* that an error has occurred and marks the symbol as unreliable (erased) rather than guessing. There are no substitution errors — only erasures.

The **channel transition matrix** for a BEC is:

$$\mathbf{P}_{\text{BEC}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - \epsilon & 0 & \epsilon \\ 0 & 1 - \epsilon & \epsilon \end{bmatrix}$$

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

> [!TIP]
> **BSC Analogy — "The Whispering Game":** Imagine passing a message by whispering. You either whisper "yes" or "no" (binary). However, $p$ fraction of the time, you accidentally whisper the *opposite* of what was said. The listener has no way of knowing whether an error occurred — they simply receive a binary symbol and must trust it. A BSC models this perfectly. Examples: noisy fiber-optic links, deep-space communication, AWGN channels at hard-decision output.

> [!TIP]
> **BEC Analogy — "The Unclear Voice Note":** Imagine listening to a voice note. Sometimes the audio is clear, and you hear "0" or "1" exactly as transmitted. Other times, the audio is so garbled that your phone marks the segment as **"inaudible"** (the erasure symbol $e$) — you know something was said, but you don't know what. Importantly, you *never* mishear a 0 as a 1; you either hear it clearly or not at all. A BEC models this. Examples: packet drop in computer networks (the dropped packet is "erased"), unreliable wireless links with CRC-detected errors, deep-space RF links with strong FEC at the receiver.

### 1.5 Why Study These Channels?

| Aspect | BSC | BEC |
|---|---|---|
| Error Type | Substitution (bit flip) | Erasure (deletion) |
| Receiver Confidence | Cannot detect error | Can detect unreliability |
| Information Loss | Partial (guess required) | None (retransmit possible) |
| Engineering Model | Hard-decision demodulator | Packet-switched networks, ARQ systems |
| Capacity | $1 - H(p)$ | $1 - \epsilon$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):** In a **BSC**, the receiver has *no way* of knowing if the received bit is correct, while in a **BEC**, the receiver knows *exactly* which bits are unreliable. This fundamental difference leads to dramatically different capacity formulas and coding strategies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Concept of Channel Capacity
