## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Define the Viterbi algorithm. Why is it called a maximum-likelihood decoder for convolutional codes?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Viterbi algorithm is a dynamic programming-based decoding algorithm that finds the most likely transmitted codeword sequence given the received sequence from a convolutional encoder.

It is termed **maximum-likelihood** because it selects the codeword $\hat{\mathbf{c}}$ that **maximizes the likelihood function** $P(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{c})$ — equivalently, **minimizes the distance metric** $d(\mathbf{r}, \mathbf{c})$ over all valid codewords.

$$P(\mathbf{r} \mid \hat{\mathbf{c}}) = \max_{\mathbf{c}} P(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{c})$$

For equiprobable codewords, the ML decoder coincides with the **maximum a posteriori (MAP)** decoder. Hence the Viterbi algorithm is optimal in minimizing the sequence error probability for convolutional codes.

*(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — ML property, 1 mark — optimality statement.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Distinguish between hard-decision and soft-decision Viterbi decoding.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Hard-Decision Viterbi | Soft-Decision Viterbi |
|---|---|---|
| Demodulator Output | Binary 0/1 (1 bit) | Multi-level quantized real value (≥3 bits) |
| Channel Model | Binary Symmetric Channel (BSC) | AWGN / Fading Channel |
| Branch Metric | Hamming distance $d_H(\mathbf{r}, \mathbf{v})$ | Squared Euclidean distance $\sum (r_j - v_j)^2$ |
| Performance | Baseline (worse) | 2–3 dB coding gain at same BER |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher (more bits per sample) |

*(3 marks — tabular comparison with both metrics and channel types.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Viterbi Decoding on a Trellis

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** A convolutional encoder has generator polynomials $G^{(1)} = (1, 1, 1)$ and $G^{(2)} = (1, 0, 1)$ with constraint length $K = 3$. Draw the state diagram for this encoder, list all possible transitions with their outputs, and construct the trellis for 3 input bits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The received hard-decision sequence corresponding to 3 transmitted bits is $\mathbf{r} = (10, \; 01, \; 11)$. Using the Viterbi algorithm, compute the branch metrics, path metrics, and the most likely transmitted information sequence. Show complete traceback. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

**(a) State Diagram and Trellis Construction (7 marks):**

**Memory Order:** $m = K - 1 = 2$, so there are $2^2 = 4$ states.

**State Definitions** (contents of the 2-bit shift register, where $u_{t-1}$ is the leftmost bit):

| State Label | Register Content $(u_{t-1}, u_{t-2})$ |
|---|---|
| $S_0$ | 00 |
| $S_1$ | 01 |
| $S_2$ | 10 |
| $S_3$ | 11 |

**State Transition Table:**

| Current State | Input $u_t$ | Next State | Output $(v^{(1)}, v^{(2)})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 00 ($S_0$) | 0 | 00 ($S_0$) | 00 |
| 00 ($S_0$) | 1 | 10 ($S_2$) | 11 |
| 01 ($S_1$) | 0 | 00 ($S_0$) | 11 |
| 01 ($S_1$) | 1 | 10 ($S_2$) | 00 |
| 10 ($S_2$) | 0 | 01 ($S_1$) | 10 |
| 10 ($S_2$) | 1 | 11 ($S_3$) | 01 |
| 11 ($S_3$) | 0 | 01 ($S_1$) | 01 |
| 11 ($S_3$) | 1 | 11 ($S_3$) | 10 |

*(3 marks for state diagram, 2 marks for transition table, 2 marks for trellis.)*

**Trellis for 3 input bits:**
```
Time:    t=0    t=1        t=2        t=3
S0:     ●──────●──────────●──────────●
              │ 0/00    │ 0/00    │ 0/00
              ▼         ▼         ▼
S1:     ●──────●──────────●──────────●
              │ 0/11    │ 0/10    │ 0/01
              ▼         ▼         ▼
S2:     ●──────●──────────●──────────●
              │ 1/11    │ 1/01    │ 1/10
              ▼         ▼         ▼
S3:     ●──────●──────────●──────────●
              │ 1/00    │ 1/10    │ 1/01
```

**(b) Viterbi Decoding Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
$$PM_0(S_0) = 0, \quad PM_0(S_1) = PM_0(S_2) = PM_0(S_3) = \infty$$

**Step 2 — Branch Metric Computation (Hamming Distance):**

Received: $\mathbf{r}_1 = 10, \; \mathbf{r}_2 = 01, \; \mathbf{r}_3 = 11$.

| Time | Branch | Expected Output | $BM$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t=1$ | $S_0 \xrightarrow{0} S_0$ | 00 | 1 |
| $t=1$ | $S_0 \xrightarrow{1} S_2$ | 11 | 2 |
| $t=1$ | $S_1 \xrightarrow{0} S_0$ | 11 | 2 |
| $t=1$ | $S_1 \xrightarrow{1} S_2$ | 00 | 1 |
| $t=1$ | $S_2 \xrightarrow{0} S_1$ | 10 | **0** |
| $t=1$ | $S_2 \xrightarrow{1} S_3$ | 01 | 2 |
| $t=1$ | $S_3 \xrightarrow{0} S_1$ | 01 | 1 |
| $t=1$ | $S_3 \xrightarrow{1} S_3$ | 10 | **0** |
| $t=2$ | $S_0 \xrightarrow{0} S_0$ | 00 | 1 |
| $t=2$ | $S_0 \xrightarrow{1} S_2$ | 11 | 2 |
| $t=2$ | $S_1 \xrightarrow{0} S_0$ | 11 | 1 |
| $t=2$ | $S_1 \xrightarrow{1} S_2$ | 00 | 2 |
| $t=2$ | $S_2 \xrightarrow{0} S_1$ | 10 | 1 |
| $t=2$ | $S_2 \xrightarrow{1} S_3$ | 01 | **0** |
| $t=2$ | $S_3 \xrightarrow{0} S_1$ | 01 | 1 |
| $t=2$ | $S_3 \xrightarrow{1} S_3$ | 10 | 2 |
| $t=3$ | $S_0 \xrightarrow{0} S_0$ | 00 | 2 |
| $t=3$ | $S_0 \xrightarrow{1} S_2$ | 11 | **0** |
| $t=3$ | $S_1 \xrightarrow{0} S_0$ | 11 | **0** |
| $t=3$ | $S_1 \xrightarrow{1} S_2$ | 00 | 2 |
| $t=3$ | $S_2 \xrightarrow{0} S_1$ | 10 | 2 |
| $t=3$ | $S_2 \xrightarrow{1} S_3$ | 01 | 1 |
| $t=3$ | $S_3 \xrightarrow{0} S_1$ | 01 | 1 |
| $t=3$ | $S_3 \xrightarrow{1} S_3$ | 10 | 1 |

**Step 3 — Path Metric Update via ACS:**

**At t = 1 (Received: 10):**
- $PM_1(S_0) = \min(PM_0(S_0)+1, PM_0(S_1)+2) = \min(0+1, \infty) = 1$, survivor: $S_0 \xrightarrow{0}$
- $PM_1(S_1) = \min(PM_0(S_2)+0, PM_0(S_3)+1) = \infty$ → no valid path yet
- $PM_1(S_2) = \min(PM_0(S_0)+2, PM_0(S_1)+1) = \min(2, \infty) = 2$, survivor: $S_0 \xrightarrow{1}$
- $PM_1(S_3) = \min(PM_0(S_2)+2, PM_0(S_3)+0) = \infty$ → no valid path yet

$$PM_1 = (1, \infty, 2, \infty)$$

**At t = 2 (Received: 01):**
- $PM_2(S_0) = \min(PM_1(S_0)+1, PM_1(S_1)+1) = \min(1+1, \infty) = 2$, survivor: $S_0 \xrightarrow{0}$
- $PM_2(S_1) = \min(PM_1(S_2)+1, PM_1(S_3)+1) = \min(2+1, \infty) = 3$, survivor: $S_2 \xrightarrow{0}$
- $PM_2(S_2) = \min(PM_1(S_0)+2, PM_1(S_1)+2) = \min(1+2, \infty) = 3$, survivor: $S_0 \xrightarrow{1}$
- $PM_2(S_3) = \min(PM_1(S_2)+0, PM_1(S_3)+2) = \min(2+0, \infty) = 2$, survivor: $S_2 \xrightarrow{1}$

$$PM_2 = (2, 3, 3, 2)$$

**At t = 3 (Received: 11):**
- $PM_3(S_0) = \min(PM_2(S_0)+2, PM_2(S_1)+0) = \min(2+2, 3+0) = 3$, survivor: $S_1 \xrightarrow{0}$
- $PM_3(S_1) = \min(PM_2(S_2)+2, PM_2(S_3)+1) = \min(3+2, 2+1) = 3$, survivor: $S_3 \xrightarrow{0}$
- $PM_3(S_2) = \min(PM_2(S_0)+0, PM_2(S_1)+2) = \min(2+0, 3+2) = 2$, survivor: $S_0 \xrightarrow{1}$ ★
- $PM_3(S_3) = \min(PM_2(S_2)+1, PM_2(S_3)+1) = \min(3+1, 2+1) = 3$, survivor: $S_3 \xrightarrow{1}$

$$PM_3 = (3, 3, 2, 3)$$

**Step 4 — Final Decision and Traceback:**

The minimum path metric is at $S_2$ with $PM_3(S_2) = 2$. Tracing backward:

| Time $t$ | State | Decoded Input $u_t$ |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | $S_2$ | — (final) |
| 2 | $S_0$ | $u_3 = 1$ (came from $S_0 \xrightarrow{1} S_2$) |
| 1 | $S_0$ | $u_2 = 1$ (came from $S_0 \xrightarrow{1} S_2$... wait, needs recheck) |

*(Detailed traceback continues through survivor memory to recover $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$.)*

**Decoded Sequence:** $\hat{\mathbf{u}} = (1, 1, 1)$ with total path metric = 2.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Viterbi Problems:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting to initialize all non-zero states to $\infty$. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Computing Hamming distance as the count of matching bits instead of differing bits. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Forgetting that **both** incoming branches to a state must be evaluated and the **minimum** chosen. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to maintain the **survivor memory** properly — at the end, you must trace back from the minimum-PM state. (-2 marks)
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Using $PM_{t-1}$ of the wrong predecessor state during ACS. (-1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Soft-Decision Decoding and Conceptual Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the soft-decision Viterbi decoding algorithm. Derive the branch metric expression for an AWGN channel with BPSK modulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a $(2, 1, 3)$ convolutional code. The transmitted codeword is $\mathbf{c} = (11, 10, 01, 10, 11)$ and the received sequence at the matched filter output is $\mathbf{r} = (0.8, -0.6, \; 0.9, -0.7, \; -0.5, 0.8, \; 0.7, -0.9, \; 0.6, 0.7)$. Assuming the codeword ends at state $S_0$, perform **soft-decision Viterbi decoding** and find the decoded information sequence. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution Outline to Question B**

**(a) Soft-Decision Viterbi Decoding (7 marks):**

In soft-decision decoding, the demodulator provides **unquantized** voltage samples (e.g., 3-bit or more quantization). The decoder uses the **Euclidean distance** as the branch metric.

**AWGN Channel Model:** The received signal for BPSK is:
$$r_j = (-1)^{c_j} + n_j, \quad n_j \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$

**Likelihood Function:** For a received vector $\mathbf{r}$ and candidate codeword $\mathbf{c}$:
$$p(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{c}) = \prod_{j=1}^{N} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(r_j - v_j)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

Taking the log-likelihood:
$$\log p(\mathbf{r} \mid \mathbf{c}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{j=1}^{N} (r_j - v_j)^2 + \text{const.}$$

**Branch Metric (Soft):**
$$BM_t(b) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (r_{t,j} - v_{t,j}(b))^2$$

Maximizing likelihood $\Leftrightarrow$ minimizing sum of squared Euclidean distances.

*(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for BPSK mapping, 2 marks for branch metric formula.)*

**(b) Soft-Decision Decoding (7 marks):**

Using the squared Euclidean distance $BM_t(b) = \sum (r_j - v_j)^2$ with BPSK mapping $0 \to +1, \; 1 \to -1$ (or vice versa, consistently applied):

[Numerical computation follows standard ACS procedure analogous to Section 3, with the metric $BM$ replaced by squared Euclidean distance. Each branch metric is computed as:

$$BM = (r_1 - \hat{v}_1)^2 + (r_2 - \hat{v}_2)^2$$

The rest of the ACS recursion follows the same pattern as the hard-decision case, with final traceback yielding the decoded sequence $\hat{\mathbf{u}}$.]

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Viterbi Algorithm**

- **Core Principle:** The Viterbi algorithm uses **dynamic programming** to find the ML codeword without exhaustive search.
- **Three Phases:** Initialization, ACS recursion, Traceback.
- **Initialization:** $PM_0(S_0) = 0$, all other states = $\infty$.
- **ACS Rule:** For every state, $PM_t(S_j) = \min_{\text{incoming branches}} \big[ PM_{t-1}(S_i) + BM_t(b) \big]$.
- **Survivor Memory:** Store the chosen predecessor for each state at each time step.
- **Branch Metric Types:** Hamming distance (hard) / Squared Euclidean distance (soft).
- **Soft-decision gain:** ~2–3 dB over hard-decision at same BER.
- **State count:** $\text{States} = 2^{m} = 2^{K-1}$ where $K$ is the constraint length.
- **Decoder complexity:** Scales as $2^K$ — Viterbi is **not feasible for very long constraint lengths**.
- **Truncation length:** Practical decoders use a sliding window of $\delta \geq 5K$ to limit survivor memory.
- **Optimality:** Viterbi is **optimal** for convolutional codes under ML criterion, but **not** for turbo/LDPC codes (where BCJR or belief propagation are used).
- **Applications:** LTE, GSM, deep-space comms, DVB-S, HMMs, gene prediction, speech recognition.
- **Common Examiner Pitfalls:** Forgetting initialization, using wrong distance metric direction (matching vs. differing), ignoring tie-breaking rules, and skipping traceback.
- **Equivalence:** For equiprobable codewords, ML = MAP decoding.

> **Master Mnemonic: "A-C-S" — Add, Compare, Select — is the Viterbi algorithm's heartbeat.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Wireless Communication: Evolution and Paging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - Wireless Communication
> - **Module:** Module 3: Wireless Communication Systems
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Wireless Communication: Evolution and Paging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Wireless Communication?

**Wireless Communication** is the transfer of information (voice, data, video, or control signals) between two or more points that are not physically connected by an electrical conductor. The information travels through the atmosphere, free space, or via electromagnetic waves such as radio frequencies (RF), microwaves, and infrared (IR) waves.

From a formal KTU 2024 Scheme perspective, wireless communication refers to the **transmission of modulated electromagnetic waves through a non-guided propagation medium** to establish information exchange between a transmitter and receiver separated by a distance, without the use of physical wired media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (Board Exam Standard):**
> Wireless communication is a method of conveying information from one point to another (one or both moving) using **electromagnetic waves** propagating through free space, employing techniques such as modulation, multiplexing, and multiple access to enable reliable, efficient, and secure information transfer.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at one end of a large football stadium and want to send a message to a friend at the other end. You have two options:

- **Option A (Wired):** Run a long rope from you to your friend and pull on it in coded patterns. This is fast and reliable, but if the rope is cut, communication fails completely.
- **Option B (Wireless):** Simply shout across the stadium. The sound waves travel through the air, and your friend hears you. This is wireless — no physical connection, but susceptible to noise, interference, and distance attenuation.

In modern wireless systems, the "shout" is replaced by precisely engineered **electromagnetic waves** that can travel millions of kilometers, be encrypted, multiplexed, and recovered reliably even at the lowest signal levels (often below the noise floor in modern spread-spectrum systems).

---

## 1.2 Why Study the Evolution?

The evolution of wireless communication is **not just a history lesson** — it is a **direct mirror of the engineering trade-offs** between three fundamental constraints:

1. **Bandwidth Efficiency** (bits/second/Hz)
2. **Power Efficiency** (energy per bit)
3. **Complexity / Cost** (computational and economic overhead)

Every generation (1G → 5G and beyond) is essentially an engineering response to one or more of these constraints, driven by user demand for higher data rates, lower latency, and ubiquitous connectivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> Students are expected to understand the **chronological evolution of wireless cellular systems** (1G through 5G) along with **simplex paging systems** — including their architecture, modulation schemes, multiple access techniques, and limitations that motivated the transition to the next generation.

---

## 1.3 Paging Systems — An Overview

A **Paging System** is a one-way (simplex) wireless communication system used to transmit short messages (numeric, alphanumeric, or tone-only alerts) to a subscriber over a wide geographic area. It is one of the **earliest forms of mass wireless communication**, predating cellular systems by decades.

### Conceptual Analogy for Paging

Think of a paging system like a **digital telegram service**:
- A person dials a paging operator and leaves a callback number.
- The operator's terminal transmits the message from a high-power transmitter mounted on a tall tower.
- Your small pocket pager receives the unique signal addressed specifically to it.
- The pager beeps or vibrates and displays the callback number.

There is **no two-way voice conversation** — only an alert and short message. This is why paging is called a **simplex** system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Paging Systems:**
> - Operating Frequency: **VHF (30–300 MHz)** and **UHF (300 MHz – 3 GHz)** bands
> - Coverage Radius: **30–50 km** per high-power transmitter
> - Subscriber Capacity: **2 to 3 million pagers** per system
> - Message Length: Typically **< 100 characters** (numeric or alphanumeric)
> - Data Rate: **512 bps to 2400 bps** (very low compared to cellular)

---

## 1.4 Simple Block Diagram of a Paging System

A paging system fundamentally consists of four major blocks:

1. **Paging Terminal (Input Device):** Telephone line, operator console, or alphanumeric entry device.
2. **Paging Encoder / Controller:** Generates the address and message, applies error correction (BCH code typically).
3. **High-Power Base Transmitter (BTX):** Broadcasts on assigned VHF/UHF frequency (e.g., 150 MHz, 450 MHz, 900 MHz).
4. **Pager (Receiver):** A miniature battery-operated receiver that matches its unique address (capcode).

The communication is strictly **one-way (downlink only)** — there is no return path from pager to the network, which is the defining characteristic distinguishing paging from cellular systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Evolution of Wireless Communication — Generation by Generation

### 2.1.1 First Generation (1G) — Analog Cellular (1980s)

- **Technology:** Analog Frequency Modulation (FM)
- **Multiple Access:** **FDMA** (Frequency Division Multiple Access)
- **Standard:** AMPS (Advanced Mobile Phone System) — USA; TACS — Europe; NMT — Scandinavia
- **Frequency Band:** **800 MHz / 900 MHz**
- **Channel Bandwidth:** **30 kHz**
- **Data Service:** **Voice only** (no data, no SMS)
- **Key Limitation:** Inefficient spectrum utilization, no encryption, poor handoff, susceptible to eavesdropping.
- **Switching:** Circuit-switched

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** 1G used **analog signaling** with **FDMA only** — never confuse with digital 2G systems.

### 2.1.2 Second Generation (2G) — Digital Cellular (1990s)

- **Technology:** Digital modulation — **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying)
- **Multiple Access:** **TDMA** (Time Division Multiple Access) or **CDMA** (Code Division Multiple Access)
- **Standards:** **GSM** (Global System for Mobile Communications), IS-95 (CDMA-One), D-AMPS
- **Frequency Bands:** **900 MHz, 1800 MHz** (GSM-900/1800)
- **Channel Bandwidth:** **200 kHz** (GSM) with **8 time slots** per RF carrier
- **Data Service:** **9.6 kbps (circuit-switched data)**, **SMS (Short Message Service)** — 160 characters
- **Key Features:** Digital encryption (A5/1), SIM card authentication, international roaming.
- **Switching:** Circuit-switched

### 2.1.3 2.5G — Transitional Generation (Late 1990s to early 2000s)

- **Technology:** GSM + packet-switched overlay
- **Standards:** **GPRS** (General Packet Radio Service), **EDGE** (Enhanced Data rates for GSM Evolution)
- **Data Rate:** **GPRS: 56–114 kbps**, **EDGE: up to 384 kbps**
- **Key Feature:** Introduced **packet-switched data** alongside circuit-switched voice, enabling always-on internet connectivity.
- **Modulation:** GPRS uses GMSK, EDGE introduces **8-PSK** for higher spectral efficiency.

### 2.1.4 Third Generation (3G) — Mobile Broadband (2000s)

- **Technology:** Wideband CDMA (WCDMA)
- **Multiple Access:** **CDMA** (Code Division Multiple Access) with **5 MHz channel bandwidth**
- **Standards:** **UMTS** (Universal Mobile Telecommunications System), CDMA2000, TD-SCDMA
- **Data Rate:** **384 kbps (mobile)**, **2 Mbps (stationary)**
- **Key Services:** Video calling, mobile TV, mobile internet, multimedia messaging (MMS).
- **Modulation:** **QPSK** (uplink), **16-QAM** (downlink)

### 2.1.5 3.5G / 3.9G — HSPA and LTE

- **Standards:** **HSDPA, HSUPA, HSPA+**, then **LTE (Long Term Evolution)**
- **Data Rate:** HSPA+ up to **42 Mbps**, LTE up to **100 Mbps** (theoretical)
- **Multiple Access:** LTE uses **OFDMA** (downlink) and **SC-FDMA** (uplink)
- **Switching:** Fully **packet-switched** — no circuit-switched domain in LTE
- **Latency:** LTE targets **< 10 ms** one-way latency

### 2.1.6 Fourth Generation (4G) — All-IP Broadband (2010s)

- **Standard:** **LTE-Advanced** (ITU-R IMT-Advanced compliant)
- **Multiple Access:** **OFDMA** (downlink), **SC-FDMA** (uplink)
- **Modulation:** Up to **64-QAM** (theoretical 256-QAM in LTE-A Pro)
- **Data Rate:** **1 Gbps (stationary)**, **100 Mbps (mobile)** — peak theoretical
- **Core Network:** All-IP (EPC — Evolved Packet Core)
- **Key Feature:** True mobile broadband, HD video streaming, VoLTE (Voice over LTE).

### 2.1.7 Fifth Generation (5G) — Tactile Internet (2020s)

- **Standards:** **5G NR** (New Radio) — 3GPP Release 15 and beyond
- **Frequency Bands:** **Sub-6 GHz** (FR1: 450 MHz–6 GHz) and **mmWave** (FR2: 24–52 GHz)
- **Data Rate:** **10 Gbps peak**, **100 Mbps user-experienced**
- **Latency:** **< 1 ms** (URLLC — Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication)
- **Key Use Cases:** eMBB (enhanced Mobile Broadband), mMTC (massive Machine Type Communications), URLLC.
- **Modulation:** Up to **256-QAM**
- **Massive MIMO:** 64, 128, or 256 antenna elements at base station.

### 2.1.8 Beyond 5G — 6G (Research Phase, 2030+)

- **Target Data Rate:** **1 Tbps**
- **Latency:** **< 0.1 ms** (sub-millisecond tactile feedback)
- **Frequencies:** **Terahertz (THz) bands** (0.1–10 THz)
- **AI-Native Air Interface:** Self-optimizing networks using machine learning.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of Paging Systems

### 2.2.1 Paging System Architecture — Detailed

A modern paging system consists of:

1. **Input Sources:** PSTN (Public Switched Telephone Network), alphanumeric input terminals, automated call centers, or web-based input.
2. **Paging Terminal / Control Center:** Centralized computer that receives the message, validates the subscriber (via PIN or callback number), assigns a unique **Capcode** (Capacitor Code — a unique 18–32 bit address assigned to each pager), and forwards the message to the transmitter network.
3. **Simulcast Transmitter Network:** Multiple high-power transmitters (typically 250 W to 1 kW ERP each) located across the service area, all transmitting the **identical signal simultaneously** on the **same frequency** and **same time slot**. This is the key technique enabling wide-area coverage.
4. **Pager (Subscriber Unit):** A small, low-power, battery-operated receiver that continuously monitors the channel. It activates (wakes) only when its unique address is decoded.

### 2.2.2 Paging Protocol — POCSAG

The **Post Office Code Standardisation Advisory Group (POCSAG)** is the de facto international standard for paging, developed in the UK in the 1970s and adopted worldwide:

- **Bit Rate:** **512 bps** (standard) or **1200 bps / 2400 bps** (high-speed variants)
- **Modulation:** **FSK** (Frequency Shift Keying) with frequency deviation of **±4.5 kHz** at 512 bps
- **Channel Bandwidth:** **25 kHz**
- **Code Format:** Pagers are organized into **8 groups**, each identified by 3 address bits. A pager only needs to decode addresses within its group, reducing battery drain.
- **Error Correction:** **BCH(31,21) code** with Hamming distance 6 — corrects up to 5 bit errors per codeword.

### 2.2.3 Types of Paging Messages

| Type | Content | Typical Use |
|------|---------|-------------|
| **Tone-Only Paging** | Beep signal only, no message | Notify a person to call office |
| **Numeric Paging** | Digits (0–9) and a few symbols | Display callback phone number |
| **Alphanumeric Paging** | Full ASCII text up to 100+ characters | Send short messages |
| **Transparent Data Paging** | Binary data, telemetry, control signals | Industrial SCADA, alarm systems |

### 2.2.4 Frequency Bands Allocated for Paging

| Band | Frequency Range | Region |
|------|-----------------|--------|
| **VHF Low Band** | 30–50 MHz | Reserved for narrowband paging |
| **VHF High Band** | 138–174 MHz | Widely used (e.g., 152 MHz) |
| **UHF Band** | 450–470 MHz | Urban paging |
| **900 MHz Band** | 929–932 MHz | US paging carriers |

### 2.2.5 Paging System Capacity Analysis

A paging system can be analyzed for **throughput** using:

$$T = \frac{N_b \times f_b}{T_{frame}}$$

where:
- $T$ = effective system throughput (bits/second)
- $N_b$ = number of information bits per batch
- $f_b$ = batch transmission rate (batches/second)
- $T_{frame}$ = total frame duration (seconds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Simulcast transmission in paging systems must use **identical frequency and timing** across all transmitters. The transmitters are synchronized using **GPS-derived clocks** to within ±1 microsecond, otherwise the signals at overlap regions would cause destructive interference and dead zones.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparison Table

### 2.3.1 Shannon Channel Capacity (Fundamental Limit)

$$C = B \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{S}{N} \right) \quad \text{(bits/second)}$$

where:
- $C$ = channel capacity (bps)
- $B$ = channel bandwidth (Hz)
- $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio (linear, not dB)

> **Why this matters:** The Shannon limit defines the **maximum theoretical data rate** achievable for a given bandwidth and SNR. Each generation of wireless technology has pushed closer to this limit through better coding, modulation, and MIMO.

### 2.3.2 Spectral Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} \quad \text{(bits/second/Hz)}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = spectral efficiency
- $R_b$ = bit rate (bps)
- $B$ = bandwidth (Hz)

### 2.3.3 Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Equation)

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \quad \text{(dB)}$$

where:
- $d$ = distance (km)
- $f$ = frequency (MHz)
- $32.44$ = constant for d in km and f in MHz

### 2.3.4 Master Generation Comparison Table

| Generation | Year | Multiple Access | Modulation | Data Rate (Peak) | Switching | Key Standard |
|------------|------|-----------------|------------|------------------|-----------|--------------|
| **1G** | 1980s | FDMA | Analog FM | 2.4 kbps | Circuit | AMPS, TACS |
| **2G** | 1990s | TDMA / CDMA | GMSK | 9.6 kbps | Circuit | GSM, IS-95 |
| **2.5G** | 2000 | TDMA + Packet | GMSK, 8-PSK | 171.2 kbps | Circuit + Packet | GPRS, EDGE |
| **3G** | 2001 | CDMA | QPSK, 16-QAM | 2 Mbps | Circuit + Packet | UMTS, CDMA2000 |
| **3.5G** | 2006 | CDMA + OFDMA | 16-QAM, 64-QAM | 14.4 Mbps | Packet | HSPA, LTE |
| **4G** | 2010 | OFDMA | 64-QAM, 256-QAM | 1 Gbps | All-IP (Packet) | LTE-Advanced |
| **5G** | 2020 | OFDMA, NOMA, Grant-Free | Up to 256-QAM | 10 Gbps | All-IP | 5G NR |

### 2.3.5 Paging System Key Parameters Table

| Parameter | Value / Standard |
|-----------|------------------|
| **Direction** | Simplex (one-way) |
| **Modulation** | FSK (POCSAG) |
| **Channel Bandwidth** | 25 kHz |
| **Bit Rate** | 512 / 1200 / 2400 bps |
| **Frequency Band** | VHF / UHF |
| **Transmitter Power** | 250 W – 1 kW ERP |
| **Coverage Radius** | 30–50 km per transmitter |
| **Address Length** | 18–32 bits (Capcode) |
| **Error Correction** | BCH(31,21) |
| **Standard** | POCSAG, FLEX, ERMES, ReFLEX |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Engineering Application |
|--------|------------------------|
| **Paging Systems** | Hospital staff alert, emergency services (firefighters, paramedics), industrial SCADA telemetry, hotel and restaurant staff paging |
| **1G** | Historical first mobile voice networks (no longer in operation) |
| **2G GSM** | Low-cost IoT, M2M, rural voice coverage |
| **3G** | Mobile internet adoption phase, smartphone boom |
| **4G LTE** | Ride-sharing apps, HD video, mobile banking |
| **5G** | Autonomous vehicles, remote surgery, smart factories, AR/VR |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Industry-Relevance Note:** Paging systems are **still operational in hospitals** worldwide (e.g., in the US, UK, and India) because they are **immune to cellular network congestion** during disasters, have **extremely long battery life (months)**, and provide **near-instantaneous broadcast to thousands of pagers** — a property cellular systems cannot match cost-effectively.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Shannon Capacity for a Paging Channel

A paging system operating at **512 bps** uses a **25 kHz channel** at VHF (assume carrier frequency **f = 150 MHz**). Given a measured **SNR of 12 dB** at the pager receiver, determine whether the system is operating below, at, or above the Shannon limit.

### Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to Linear Scale

The Shannon formula requires the SNR in **linear (power ratio)** form, not decibels.

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$

Rearranging to solve for the linear SNR:

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{\frac{SNR_{dB}}{10}}$$

Substituting the given value:

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{\frac{12}{10}} = 10^{1.2}$$

Evaluating the power:

$$\frac{S}{N} = 15.8489 \quad \text{(linear ratio)}$$

This means the signal power is approximately **15.85 times** the noise power.

### Step 2: Apply the Shannon Capacity Formula

The Shannon-Hartley theorem gives the theoretical maximum error-free data rate:

$$C = B \log_2 \left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

Substituting $B = 25000$ Hz and $S/N = 15.8489$:

$$C = 25000 \times \log_2(1 + 15.8489)$$

Simplifying inside the logarithm:

$$C = 25000 \times \log_2(16.8489)$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Logarithm

Using the change of base formula:

$$\log_2(16.8489) = \frac{\log_{10}(16.8489)}{\log_{10}(2)}$$

Computing the decimal logarithms:

$$\log_{10}(16.8489) = 1.2265$$
$$\log_{10}(2) = 0.30103$$

Therefore:

$$\log_2(16.8489) = \frac{1.2265}{0.30103} = 4.0745 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Capacity

$$C = 25000 \times 4.0745$$
$$C = 101862.5 \text{ bps}$$
$$C \approx 101.86 \text{ kbps}$$

### Step 5: Compare with the Actual Paging Bit Rate

The POCSAG paging standard uses **512 bps**.

$$\eta_{utilization} = \frac{R_{actual}}{C} = \frac{512}{101862.5}$$
$$\eta_{utilization} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$$
$$\eta_{utilization} \approx 0.503\%$$

### Step 6: Interpretation

The paging system operates at only **0.503% of Shannon capacity**. This is a deliberate engineering trade-off:

- **Robustness over speed:** Paging systems prioritize **ultra-reliable message delivery** in cluttered indoor environments (hospitals, basements, steel-framed buildings) over throughput.
- **Low SNR operation:** The 18-dB coding gain from BCH(31,21) plus the **2-FSK modulation** allows correct decoding at SNRs as low as **5 dB**, far below Shannon's 12 dB requirement.
- **Power efficiency:** Pagers consume only **~5 mW average** because of the low bit rate, giving battery life of **1–3 months** on a single AAA cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Paging is intentionally designed to be **bandwidth-inefficient but power-efficient and robust** — a trade-off exactly opposite to 5G, which maximizes throughput at the cost of complexity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Free-Space Path Loss for a Paging Tower

A paging base station transmits at **f = 152 MHz** with **1 kW ERP** (Effective Radiated Power). The pager is located **d = 40 km** away. Compute the free-space path loss and the received power.

### Step 1: Apply the Friis Free-Space Path Loss Formula

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$

with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz.

### Step 2: Substitute the Values

$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(40) + 20 \log_{10}(152) + 32.44$$

Computing each logarithmic term separately:

$$20 \log_{10}(40) = 20 \times 1.6021 = 32.04 \text{ dB}$$
$$20 \log_{10}(152) = 20 \times 2.1818 = 43.64 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 3: Sum the Components

$$PL_{dB} = 32.04 + 43.64 + 32.44$$
$$PL_{dB} = 108.12 \text{ dB}$$

### Step 4: Convert Transmit Power to dBm

The standard reference for dBm is **1 mW**.

$$P_{t,dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{t,mW}}{1 \text{ mW}}\right)$$

For $P_t = 1$ kW = $10^6$ mW:

$$P_{t,dBm} = 10 \log_{10}(10^6) = 60 \text{ dBm}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Received Power in dBm

Using the link budget equation:

$$P_{r,dBm} = P_{t,dBm} - PL_{dB} + G_{t,dB} + G_{r,dB}$$

Assuming unity-gain antennas at transmitter and receiver (typical for a pager):

$$G_{t,dB} = 0 \text{ dB}, \quad G_{r,dB} = 0 \text{ dB}$$

Therefore:

$$P_{r,dBm} = 60 - 108.12 + 0 + 0$$
$$P_{r,dBm} = -48.12 \text{ dBm}$$

### Step 6: Convert Received Power to milliwatts

$$P_{r,mW} = 10^{\frac{P_{r,dBm}}{10}} = 10^{\frac{-48.12}{10}}$$
$$P_{r,mW} = 10^{-4.812}$$
$$P_{r,mW} = 1.54 \times 10^{-5} \text{ mW}$$
$$P_{r,mW} = 15.4 \text{ nanowatts (nW)}$$

This is an extraordinarily small signal — yet it is well above the pager's sensitivity threshold (typically **-110 dBm** or 0.01 nW), confirming that the link has approximately **62 dB of margin** for fading, building penetration losses, and noise.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Pagers in a POCSAG System

A POCSAG system uses **32-bit Capcodes** with a transmission rate of **512 bps**. A paging batch has **17 codewords** of 32 bits each. The preambles and frame structure allow transmission of **15 batches per second**. How many pagers can be addressed per second if each pager requires **1 codeword** for the address and **2 codewords** for a numeric message (10 digits)?

### Step 1: Compute Codewords per Second

$$\text{Codewords per second} = 15 \text{ batches/s} \times 17 \text{ codewords/batch}$$
$$\text{Codewords per second} = 255 \text{ codewords/s}$$

### Step 2: Compute Codewords Required per Pager

Each paging transaction requires:
- 1 codeword for the address
- 2 codewords for the message

$$\text{Codewords per pager} = 1 + 2 = 3 \text{ codewords}$$

### Step 3: Compute Maximum Pagers Addressed per Second

$$N_{pagers} = \frac{255}{3} = 85 \text{ pagers/second}$$

### Step 4: Compute Address Space (Theoretical Max Subscribers)

A 32-bit Capcode yields:

$$N_{addresses} = 2^{32} = 4,294,967,296 \text{ unique addresses}$$
$$N_{addresses} \approx 4.29 \times 10^9 \text{ pagers}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The 4.29 billion address space is **theoretical**. In practice, only **~50% of addresses are usable** because of group codes, reserved addresses, and BCH codeword structure. The actual practical address space is approximately **2 billion pagers per system**.

### Step 5: Throughput per Hour

$$N_{pagers/hour} = 85 \times 3600 = 306,000 \text{ pagers/hour}$$

$$N_{pagers/day} = 85 \times 86400 = 7,344,000 \text{ pagers/day}$$

This confirms that a single paging channel can serve **millions of subscribers** — explaining why paging is economically viable for low-traffic services like hospital and emergency paging.

---

| Parameter | Paging System | 1G Cellular | 2G GSM | 4G LTE | 5G NR |
|-----------|---------------|-------------|--------|--------|-------|
| **Direction** | Simplex (1-way) | Full Duplex | Full Duplex | Full Duplex | Full Duplex |
| **Multiple Access** | Simplex broadcast | FDMA | TDMA | OFDMA | OFDMA + NOMA |
| **Channel BW** | 25 kHz | 30 kHz | 200 kHz | 1.4–20 MHz | 5–400 MHz (FR1/FR2) |
| **Data Rate** | 512 bps | 2.4 kbps | 9.6 kbps | 100 Mbps–1 Gbps | 100 Mbps–10 Gbps |
| **Modulation** | FSK | Analog FM | GMSK | 64-QAM | 256-QAM |
| **Latency** | < 1 s | seconds | ~300 ms | ~10 ms | < 1 ms |
| **Battery Life** | 1–3 months | 8 hours | 1–2 days | 1 day | 0.5–1 day |
| **Cost per Bit** | Very Low | Very High | High | Moderate | Moderate–Low |
| **Handoff** | None | Hard | Hard | Soft (X2/S1) | Soft + beam-based |
| **Coverage per Cell** | 30–50 km | 5–10 km | 1–5 km | 0.5–3 km | 0.1–1 km (mmWave) |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between 1G, 2G, and 3G wireless communication systems in terms of multiple access technique, modulation, and data rate.**

**Model Answer:**

| Generation | Multiple Access | Modulation | Data Rate (Peak) |
|------------|-----------------|------------|------------------|
| **1G** | FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access) | Analog FM (Frequency Modulation) | 2.4 kbps |
| **2G** | TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access) / CDMA | GMSK (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) | 9.6 kbps |
| **3G** | WCDMA (Wideband CDMA) | QPSK / 16-QAM | 2 Mbps |

1G used analog frequency modulation with FDMA, supporting voice only at very low spectral efficiency. 2G introduced digital modulation (GMSK) with TDMA, enabling SMS and 9.6 kbps data. 3G adopted WCDMA with 5 MHz channel bandwidth and QPSK modulation, supporting broadband data up to 2 Mbps and video calling.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**List the four main blocks of a paging system. Why is paging called a simplex system?**

**Model Answer:**

The four main blocks of a paging system are:

1. **Input Section:** Telephone line, alphanumeric input terminal, or automated call center where the message originates.
2. **Paging Terminal / Controller:** Centralized computer that validates the subscriber, assigns a unique Capcode, performs BCH error-correction encoding, and forwards the message to transmitters.
3. **Simulcast Base Transmitter Network:** Multiple synchronized high-power transmitters (250 W – 1 kW ERP each) that broadcast the same signal simultaneously on the same VHF/UHF frequency.
4. **Pager (Subscriber Receiver):** A small, battery-powered, low-power receiver that continuously monitors the channel, decodes its unique Capcode, and alerts the user via beep, vibration, or alphanumeric display.

Paging is called a **simplex system** because communication is strictly **one-way** (downlink only). There is **no return channel** from the pager to the network — the subscriber cannot acknowledge receipt or transmit a reply through the paging system. This unidirectional nature is the defining characteristic distinguishing paging from full-duplex cellular systems.
