## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, ESE Module 3 Internal Choice A]**

### Question:
**(a)** Explain the evolution of wireless communication systems from 1G to 5G with emphasis on multiple access technique, modulation scheme, data rate, and switching mode for each generation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the architecture of a paging system with a neat block diagram. Explain the function of each block and the role of the Capcode in addressing. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to (a): Evolution of Wireless Communication (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
The evolution of wireless communication is a continuous engineering response to the demand for higher data rates, lower latency, and better spectral efficiency. Each generation represents a paradigm shift in multiple access, modulation, and network architecture.

**1G — First Generation (1 Mark):**
- Launched in the early **1980s**, 1G was the first commercially deployed cellular system.
- **Multiple Access:** FDMA — each user is allocated a dedicated frequency channel of **30 kHz**.
- **Modulation:** Analog Frequency Modulation (FM).
- **Switching:** Circuit-switched.
- **Data Service:** Voice only, **2.4 kbps**; no SMS, no encryption.
- **Example Standards:** AMPS (USA), TACS (UK), NMT (Scandinavia).
- **Limitations:** Poor spectrum efficiency, no security, frequent call drops during handoff, large handset size.

**2G — Second Generation (1 Mark):**
- Launched in the early **1990s**, marking the transition from analog to digital.
- **Multiple Access:** TDMA (8 time slots per 200 kHz carrier in GSM) or CDMA (IS-95).
- **Modulation:** **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) — constant envelope, power-efficient.
- **Switching:** Circuit-switched, with packet-switched overlay added later (2.5G GPRS).
- **Data Service:** **9.6 kbps** voice and circuit data, **SMS** (Short Message Service, 160 characters).
- **Key Standards:** **GSM** (Global System for Mobile Communications), IS-95 (CDMA-One), D-AMPS.
- **Bands:** **900 MHz and 1800 MHz** (GSM-900, GSM-1800).
- **Innovations:** SIM card authentication, A5/1 encryption, international roaming.

**3G — Third Generation (1 Mark):**
- Launched around **2001**, defined by **IMT-2000** standards.
- **Multiple Access:** **WCDMA** (Wideband CDMA) with 5 MHz channel bandwidth.
- **Modulation:** **QPSK** (uplink) and **16-QAM** (downlink).
- **Switching:** Mixed circuit and packet switching.
- **Data Service:** **2 Mbps** (stationary), **384 kbps** (mobile).
- **Key Standards:** **UMTS**, CDMA2000, TD-SCDMA.
- **Services:** Video calling, mobile TV, MMS, mobile internet (WAP, early web).

**3.5G / 3.9G — HSPA and LTE (1 Mark):**
- **HSDPA / HSUPA / HSPA+** enhanced 3G to **42 Mbps** downlink.
- **LTE (Long Term Evolution)** introduced **OFDMA** (downlink) and **SC-FDMA** (uplink) with channel bandwidths up to **20 MHz**.
- **Latency:** LTE targets **< 10 ms** one-way.
- **Switching:** Fully packet-switched — circuit-switched domain eliminated in LTE.

**4G — Fourth Generation (1 Mark):**
- Officially specified by **ITU-R IMT-Advanced**, commercially deployed from **2010**.
- **Multiple Access:** **OFDMA** (downlink), **SC-FDMA** (uplink).
- **Modulation:** Up to **64-QAM** (256-QAM in LTE-A Pro).
- **MIMO:** 2×2, 4×4 antenna configurations.
- **Data Rate:** **1 Gbps** (stationary), **100 Mbps** (mobile).
- **Core Network:** All-IP — **EPC** (Evolved Packet Core).
- **Services:** HD video streaming, VoLTE (Voice over LTE), online gaming, mobile broadband.

**5G — Fifth Generation (1 Mark):**
- Standardized as **5G NR** (New Radio) in **3GPP Release 15** (2018), deployed from **2020**.
- **Frequency Bands:** **Sub-6 GHz** (FR1: 450 MHz – 6 GHz) and **mmWave** (FR2: 24 – 52 GHz).
- **Multiple Access:** **OFDMA** with **NOMA** (Non-Orthogonal Multiple Access) and grant-free access for IoT.
- **Modulation:** Up to **256-QAM**.
- **Massive MIMO:** 64, 128, or **256 antenna elements** at base station.
- **Data Rate:** **10 Gbps** peak, **100 Mbps** user-experienced.
- **Latency:** **< 1 ms** (URLLC).
- **Three Use Cases:** **eMBB** (enhanced Mobile Broadband), **mMTC** (massive Machine Type Communications), **URLLC** (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication).
- **Applications:** Autonomous vehicles, remote surgery, smart factories, AR/VR, IoT.

**Conclusion (Optional):**
The evolution shows a clear trend: from narrowband analog voice → digital narrowband → broadband data → all-IP broadband → massive IoT and tactile internet. Each step is driven by new multiple access techniques and modulation schemes that push the Shannon limit closer.

---

### Model Solution to (b): Paging System Architecture (7 Marks)

**Introduction (0.5 Mark):**
A paging system is a **one-way (simplex) wireless communication system** used to transmit short numeric, alphanumeric, or tone-only messages to subscribers over a wide geographic area. It predates cellular systems and remains in use in hospitals, emergency services, and industrial telemetry.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):**

The block diagram is as shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. The major blocks are:

1. **Input Section (1 Mark):**
   - **PSTN Interface:** Standard telephone line used by anyone to dial a paging number and leave a numeric message.
   - **Alphanumeric Input Terminal:** Computer terminal used by hospital staff or office operators to type text messages.
   - **Automated Call Center:** IVR (Interactive Voice Response) system that accepts touch-tone input from callers.
   - The input is forwarded to the **Paging Terminal / Controller**.

2. **Paging Terminal / Controller (1.5 Marks):**
   - Centralized computer that **validates the subscriber** (PIN or account number), **formats the message**, and assigns a unique **Capcode** (Capacitor Code — typically 18 to 32 bits).
   - Performs **BCH(31,21) error-correction encoding** to combat channel errors.
   - Schedules the message for transmission in the next available **POCSAG batch** (POCSAG = Post Office Code Standardisation Advisory Group).
   - Sends the encoded baseband signal to all simulcast transmitters via landline, microwave link, or satellite.

3. **Simulcast Base Transmitter Network (1 Mark):**
   - Consists of **multiple high-power transmitters** (typically 4 to 16 sites) distributed across the service area.
   - Each transmitter has an **ERP of 250 W to 1 kW** and operates on the **same VHF/UHF frequency** (e.g., 152 MHz, 462 MHz, 930 MHz).
   - All transmitters are **time-synchronized** to within **±1 microsecond** using **GPS-derived clocks** to avoid destructive interference at overlap regions.
   - The **simulcast** technique allows seamless coverage across thousands of square kilometers from a single frequency.

4. **Pager (Subscriber Unit) (1.5 Marks):**
   - A small, pocket-sized, **battery-operated receiver** (typically 1.5 V AAA cell).
   - Continuously monitors the channel but **decodes only its own Capcode** (group-based decoding — pager wakes only when its group address appears).
   - On matching the address, it activates the **buzzer, vibrator, or LCD display**.
   - Typical sensitivity: **-110 dBm** (extremely low threshold for indoor reception).
   - Battery life: **1 to 3 months** due to duty-cycled operation.

**Role of the Capcode (0.5 Mark):**
The **Capcode** (Capacitor Code) is a **unique binary address** assigned to each pager at the time of manufacture or activation. It serves as the **hardware-level identity** of the subscriber. When a paging message is transmitted, every pager in the coverage area receives it, but only the pager whose **Capcode matches** the transmitted address will alert the user. The Capcode is typically **21 bits** in POCSAG, providing up to **2 million unique addresses** per system.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, ESE Module 3 Internal Choice B]**

### Question:
**(a)** Compare FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, and OFDMA multiple access techniques with neat diagrams. Identify which generations of wireless systems use each technique. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the receiver section of a paging system. Discuss the function of the BCH(31,21) error-correcting code in detail. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to (a): Comparison of Multiple Access Techniques (7 Marks)

**Introduction (0.5 Mark):**
Multiple access is the technique that allows **many users to share a common communication channel** simultaneously. The four major multiple access techniques in wireless systems are FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, and OFDMA.

**FDMA — Frequency Division Multiple Access (1.5 Marks):**
- The available spectrum is divided into **non-overlapping frequency bands**, each assigned to one user.
- Each user transmits and receives on a **dedicated frequency** for the entire call duration.
- **Filter requirements:** Sharp bandpass filters at base station.
- **Advantages:** Simple, no synchronization needed.
- **Disadvantages:** Wastage of bandwidth during silence; inflexible.
- **Used in:** **1G** (AMPS, TACS).
- **Diagram:** A spectrum plot showing $N$ non-overlapping frequency slots $f_1, f_2, ..., f_N$.

**TDMA — Time Division Multiple Access (1.5 Marks):**
- The available frequency is divided into **time slots**, and each user is allocated a unique slot within a repeating frame.
- Users transmit in **rapid bursts** at the same frequency.
- **Advantages:** Higher capacity than FDMA, supports digital modulation.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires precise synchronization, guard periods needed.
- **Used in:** **2G** (GSM uses 8 time slots per 200 kHz carrier).
- **Diagram:** A time-frequency grid showing 8 users sharing one frequency in non-overlapping time slots.

**CDMA — Code Division Multiple Access (1.5 Marks):**
- All users transmit **simultaneously on the same frequency** but use **unique spreading codes** (Walsh-Hadamard codes or PN sequences) to spread their signals.
- Receivers use **correlator detectors** with the user's code to extract the desired signal.
- **Advantages:** Soft capacity limit, inherent security, robustness against narrowband interference.
- **Disadvantages:** Near-far problem, requires power control, complex receivers (RAKE).
- **Used in:** **2G** (IS-95), **3G** (WCDMA, CDMA2000).
- **Diagram:** Three users with codes $C_1, C_2, C_3$ all using the same frequency, decoded at the receiver using matched filters.

**OFDMA — Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access (1.5 Marks):**
- The wideband channel is divided into **hundreds or thousands of orthogonal subcarriers**, and each user is assigned a subset of subcarriers in specific time slots.
- Subcarriers are **orthogonal**, allowing them to overlap in frequency without mutual interference.
- Uses **FFT/IFFT** for modulation/demodulation.
- **Advantages:** Excellent performance in multipath fading, flexible resource allocation, high spectral efficiency.
- **Disadvantages:** High peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR), sensitive to Doppler at high mobility.
- **Used in:** **4G LTE** (downlink), **5G NR** (downlink and uplink), Wi-Fi 6/7.
- **Diagram:** A time-frequency grid showing resource blocks (12 subcarriers × 7 OFDM symbols) assigned to different users.

**Summary Table (0.5 Mark):**

| Technique | Generation | User Separation | Key Limitation |
|-----------|------------|------------------|-----------------|
| FDMA | 1G | Frequency | Inefficient spectrum |
| TDMA | 2G | Time slots | Synchronization |
| CDMA | 2G / 3G | Code | Power control |
| OFDMA | 4G / 5G | Subcarriers + time | PAPR, Doppler |

---

### Model Solution to (b): Pager Receiver and BCH Coding (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram of the Pager Receiver (3 Marks):**
The block diagram is as shown in Section 4.4. The detailed function of each block:

1. **Whip Antenna:** A compact quarter-wave monopole tuned to the paging frequency (e.g., 152 MHz, ~50 cm long for λ/4). Captures the weak RF signal.

2. **Low Noise Amplifier (LNA):** A high-gain, low-noise RF amplifier (typically 20 dB gain, 2–3 dB noise figure) that boosts the antenna signal above the receiver's thermal noise floor.

3. **Bandpass Filter (BPF):** A 25 kHz ceramic or SAW filter that selects the desired paging channel and rejects adjacent channel interference. Critical for **co-channel rejection** in dense RF environments.

4. **Mixer + Local Oscillator (LO):** Converts the RF signal to an Intermediate Frequency (IF) of 455 kHz or 10.7 MHz using a superheterodyne or direct-conversion architecture.

5. **IF Amplifier and Limiter:** Provides most of the receiver's gain and amplitude-limits the signal for clean FSK demodulation.

6. **FSK Demodulator:** A frequency discriminator (e.g., quadrature detector) recovers the original 512 bps binary data from the FSK signal (±4.5 kHz deviation).

7. **BCH Decoder:** The decoded bits are passed through a **BCH(31,21)** decoder to correct up to 5 random bit errors in each 31-bit codeword.

8. **Capcode Comparator:** The decoder output is compared with the pager's hard-wired Capcode. On match, an alert signal is generated.

9. **Alert and Display:** A buzzer, LED, vibrator, or LCD display is activated to inform the user.

10. **Battery:** A single 1.5 V AAA cell powers the entire receiver. Average current draw is **2–5 mA**, giving battery life of **1–3 months**.

**BCH(31,21) Error-Correcting Code (4 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A **BCH (Bose-Chaudhuri-Hocquenghem)** code is a class of powerful **cyclic block codes** used for correcting random errors in digital communication. The notation **BCH(31,21)** means:
- **Block length:** $n = 31$ bits per codeword
- **Information bits:** $k = 21$ bits (the actual message)
- **Parity bits:** $n - k = 10$ bits (added for error correction)
- **Code rate:** $R = k/n = 21/31 \approx 0.677$ (67.7% efficiency)
- **Minimum Hamming distance:** $d_{min} = 6$
- **Error-correction capability:** $t = (d_{min} - 1)/2 = (6-1)/2 = 2.5 \Rightarrow t = 2$ bit errors can be **guaranteed corrected**; up to 5 bit errors can be detected.

**Generator Polynomial (1 Mark):**
For BCH(31,21) over GF(2), the generator polynomial is:
$$g(x) = x^{10} + x^9 + x^8 + x^5 + x^4 + x^2 + x + 1$$

This is a primitive-degree-5 polynomial that generates a 10-bit cyclic redundancy sequence appended to every 21-bit information block.

**Encoding Process (1 Mark):**
1. Take the **21-bit information block** $m(x)$.
2. Multiply $m(x)$ by $x^{10}$ (shift left by 10 bits).
3. Divide $x^{10} \cdot m(x)$ by $g(x)$ to obtain the **10-bit remainder** $r(x)$.
4. Transmit the **31-bit codeword** $c(x) = x^{10} m(x) + r(x)$.

**Decoding Process (1 Mark):**
1. Receive the **31-bit codeword** $r(x)$, possibly corrupted by channel noise.
2. Compute the **syndrome** $S(x) = r(x) \mod g(x)$. If $S(x) = 0$, no error detected.
3. If $S(x) \neq 0$, use the **Berlekamp-Massey algorithm** to find the error-locator polynomial $\sigma(x)$.
4. Solve $\sigma(x) = 0$ using the **Chien search** to identify the error positions.
5. Flip the bits at the error positions to recover the corrected codeword.

**Why BCH(31,21) is Used in Paging (0.5 Mark):**
- Paging channels operate in **hostile indoor environments** (hospitals, buildings) with severe multipath fading and burst noise.
- The **2-bit error correction per codeword** provides approximately **8–10 dB of coding gain**, allowing correct reception at SNRs as low as **5 dB**.
- The **67.7% code rate** preserves bandwidth efficiency while providing strong protection — an ideal trade-off for low-data-rate, high-reliability paging.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
BCH(31,21) is a perfect match for paging systems because it provides **strong error correction with low overhead**, low decoding complexity (suitable for battery-powered pagers), and robust performance in noisy indoor channels. The code's elegance lies in its **10-bit parity overhead** yielding a **Hamming distance of 6**, which is the maximum achievable for a (31,21) cyclic code.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and Where They Lose Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing 1G with 2G:** Writing "1G uses digital modulation" — **1G is strictly analog FM.** Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
>
> 2. **Writing "paging is a duplex system":** Paging is **simplex (one-way)**. There is no return channel from the pager. This is the most common conceptual error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Capcode:** Many students describe a paging system but forget to explain the **role of the unique Capcode** in subscriber addressing. Examiners specifically look for this — lose 1–2 marks if omitted.
>
> 4. **Writing "OFDMA = OFDM":** OFDMA is the **multiple access version** of OFDM where users are assigned different subcarriers. OFDM is a single-user modulation; OFDMA is multi-user. This distinction carries 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Confusing BCH(31,21) parameters:** Many students write the block length as 21 or the parity as 31. Memorize: **BCH(n=31, k=21), parity = 10, t=2 errors correctable.**
>
> 6. **Skipping the simulcast concept:** Paging uses **simulcast transmission** from multiple synchronized transmitters. Forgetting this loses marks on the "wide-area coverage" question.
>
> 7. **Wrong modulation for 2G GSM:** 2G uses **GMSK**, not BPSK or QPSK. QPSK is for 3G and beyond.
>
> 8. **Wrong Shannon formula application:** Always use the linear SNR (not dB) inside $\log_2(1 + S/N)$. Many students plug dB values directly and get wrong answers.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 3:**

### Evolution of Wireless Communication:
- ☐ **1G (1980s):** Analog FM, **FDMA**, 30 kHz channel, voice only, AMPS/TACS.
- ☐ **2G (1990s):** Digital, **GMSK**, TDMA/CDMA, 9.6 kbps, GSM-900/1800, SMS, SIM card.
- ☐ **2.5G (2000):** GPRS (56–114 kbps), EDGE (384 kbps), packet-switched data.
- ☐ **3G (2001):** WCDMA, 5 MHz, QPSK/16-QAM, 2 Mbps, UMTS, video calling.
- ☐ **3.5G/3.9G:** HSPA+ (42 Mbps), LTE (OFDMA, <10 ms latency).
- ☐ **4G (2010):** LTE-Advanced, OFDMA, 64-QAM, 1 Gbps, all-IP EPC, VoLTE.
- ☐ **5G (2020):** 5G NR, Sub-6 GHz + mmWave (24–52 GHz), Massive MIMO, 10 Gbps, <1 ms URLLC, eMBB + mMTC + URLLC.

### Multiple Access Techniques:
- ☐ FDMA → 1G (frequency separation).
- ☐ TDMA → 2G (time-slot separation).
- ☐ CDMA → 2G/3G (code separation, spread spectrum).
- ☐ OFDMA → 4G/5G (subcarrier + time separation, orthogonal).

### Paging Systems:
- ☐ Paging is **simplex** (one-way downlink only).
- ☐ Standard: **POCSAG** at **512 bps** (also 1200/2400 bps).
- ☐ Modulation: **FSK** with **±4.5 kHz** deviation.
- ☐ Channel bandwidth: **25 kHz**.
- ☐ Operating bands: **VHF (138–174 MHz)** and **UHF (450–470 MHz, 900 MHz)**.
- ☐ Transmitter power: **250 W – 1 kW ERP**.
- ☐ Coverage radius: **30–50 km per site**.
- ☐ **Simulcast** transmission from multiple synchronized sites (GPS sync ±1 µs).
- ☐ Each pager has a unique **Capcode** (18–32 bit address).
- ☐ **BCH(31,21)** code: 21 info bits + 10 parity bits, corrects up to **2 errors**, detects up to **5 errors** per codeword.
- ☐ Pager sensitivity: **-110 dBm**; battery life: **1–3 months**.
- ☐ Types: tone-only, numeric, alphanumeric, transparent data.
- ☐ Applications: hospital staff paging, emergency services, industrial SCADA telemetry.

### Key Formulas to Memorize:
- ☐ **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.
- ☐ **Spectral Efficiency:** $\eta = R_b / B$.
- ☐ **Friis Path Loss:** $PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (d in km, f in MHz).
- ☐ **SNR Conversion:** $S/N_{linear} = 10^{SNR_{dB}/10}$.
- ☐ **BCH(31,21) Rate:** $R = 21/31 \approx 0.677$.

### Examiner's Hot-Spots (High Probability Questions):
- ☐ Compare **three generations** (any combination) — asked in nearly every KTU ESE.
- ☐ Draw and explain **paging system block diagram** — extremely common.
- ☐ Discuss **POCSAG** protocol — frequently asked 7-mark question.
- ☐ Explain **BCH(31,21)** with parameters and error-correction capability.
- ☐ Compare **FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, OFDMA** — guaranteed question in any Module 3 paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless LAN, Bluetooth, Zig-Bee, and Personal Area Networks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module Module 3: Wireless Communication Systems
> - **Topic:** Wireless LAN, Bluetooth, Zig-Bee, and Personal Area Networks

# Wireless LAN, Bluetooth, Zig-Bee, and Personal Area Networks (PANs)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Wireless LAN (WLAN)** is a local area network that uses **radio frequency (RF)** or **infrared (IR)** waves to transmit data between devices without physical cables, typically covering a small geographic area such as a building, campus, or home. The dominant standard governing WLANs today is the **IEEE 802.11 family**, commonly branded as **Wi-Fi (Wireless Fidelity)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Personal Area Networks (PANs)** are short-range networks (typically within a person's reach — about **10 meters**) used for connecting personal devices such as smartphones, laptops, headphones, and IoT sensors. They are governed by standards like **IEEE 802.15**, which includes technologies such as **Bluetooth** and **ZigBee**.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a busy airport with three zones:
- **The Public Announcement Hall (WLAN/Wi-Fi)**: Everyone in the airport can connect to the public Wi-Fi. It's like a wide umbrella network — many users, moderate range (~30–100 m indoors), and a lot of data flowing.
- **Your Personal Bluetooth Headset (Bluetooth)**: A private bubble around you (~10 m), allowing only your phone to talk to your headset. Short range, low power, low cost.
- **A Smart Home Sensor Mesh (ZigBee)**: Tiny devices in every room (lights, thermostats, door sensors) chattering quietly with each other in a closed mesh, sipping tiny amounts of battery power, lasting for years.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Spectrum Bands Used:**
> - **2.4 GHz ISM Band** — used by Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee (Industrial, Scientific, Medical band; license-free globally).
> - **5 GHz and 6 GHz Bands** — used by modern Wi-Fi (802.11ac/ax).
> - **Sub-1 GHz Bands (868 MHz, 915 MHz)** — used by ZigBee variants for longer range.

### 1.2 IEEE 802 Standard Family Overview

| Standard | Common Name | Frequency | Typical Range | Application |
|----------|-------------|-----------|---------------|-------------|
| IEEE 802.11 | Wi-Fi | 2.4/5/6 GHz | 30–100 m | WLAN |
| IEEE 802.15.1 | Bluetooth | 2.4 GHz | 10 m | PAN |
| IEEE 802.15.4 | ZigBee | 2.4 GHz / Sub-GHz | 10–100 m | WPAN/IoT |
| IEEE 802.15.6 | WBAN | 400 MHz–2.4 GHz | ~1–3 m | Body Area Network |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wireless LAN (WLAN / IEEE 802.11)

The **IEEE 802.11 standard** defines the **Physical (PHY)** and **Medium Access Control (MAC)** layers for wireless local area networking. It uses a **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** protocol because wireless stations cannot detect collisions while transmitting (the **hidden node problem**).

#### 2.1.1 Architecture of WLAN

A WLAN can be organized into two modes:
- **Infrastructure Mode**: All communication goes through a central **Access Point (AP)** connected to a wired Distribution System (DS).
- **Ad-hoc Mode**: Devices communicate directly without an AP, forming an **Independent Basic Service Set (IBSS)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BSS (Basic Service Set)** = the set of stations communicating with one AP.  
> **ESS (Extended Service Set)** = multiple BSSs interconnected by a Distribution System.

#### 2.1.2 Hidden and Exposed Node Problem

- **Hidden Node Problem**: Two stations (A and C) cannot hear each other but both reach station B. They may transmit simultaneously, causing collision at B.
- **Solution**: **RTS/CTS (Request-To-Send / Clear-To-Send)** handshake before data transmission.

#### 2.1.3 Key IEEE 802.11 Amendments

| Amendment | Frequency | Max Data Rate | Modulation |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|------------|
| 802.11b | 2.4 GHz | 11 Mbps | DSSS |
| 802.11a | 5 GHz | 54 Mbps | OFDM |
| 802.11g | 2.4 GHz | 54 Mbps | OFDM |
| 802.11n (Wi-Fi 4) | 2.4/5 GHz | 600 Mbps | MIMO-OFDM |
| 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5) | 5 GHz | 6.93 Gbps | MU-MIMO OFDM |
| 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6) | 2.4/5/6 GHz | 9.6 Gbps | OFDMA + MU-MIMO |

### 2.2 Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1)

Bluetooth is a short-range, low-power, low-cost wireless technology designed for **cable replacement** in personal area networks. It operates in the **2.4 GHz ISM band** using **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** with 79 channels (in classic Bluetooth), hopping at **1600 hops per second**.

#### 2.2.1 Piconet and Scatternet

- **Piconet**: One **master** device controls up to **7 active slave** devices (max 8 total). All communication is master-controlled.
- **Scatternet**: Multiple piconets connected via bridge nodes, expanding network reach.

#### 2.2.2 Bluetooth Protocol Stack

| Layer | Function |
|-------|----------|
| Radio (PHY) | 2.4 GHz, FHSS, GFSK modulation |
| Baseband | Piconet management, hopping, addressing |
| Link Manager (LMP) | Link setup, authentication |
| HCI (Host Controller Interface) | Interface between host and controller |
| L2CAP | Logical Link Control & Adaptation Protocol |
| RFCOMM | Serial port emulation |
| SDP (Service Discovery) | Service discovery |
| Application Profiles | A2DP, HFP, HID, etc. |

#### 2.2.3 Bluetooth Versions

| Version | Year | Max Data Rate | Range |
|---------|------|---------------|-------|
| Bluetooth 1.0 | 1999 | 1 Mbps | 10 m |
| Bluetooth 2.0 + EDR | 2004 | 3 Mbps | 10 m |
| Bluetooth 3.0 + HS | 2009 | 24 Mbps | 10 m |
| Bluetooth 4.0 (BLE) | 2010 | 1 Mbps (LE) | 50 m |
| Bluetooth 5.0 | 2016 | 2 Mbps (LE) | 200 m |

### 2.3 ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)

ZigBee is a low-power, low-data-rate wireless mesh networking standard optimized for **IoT (Internet of Things)** applications such as home automation, industrial control, and sensor networks. It is built on the **IEEE 802.15.4 PHY and MAC layers**, with ZigBee Alliance defining the upper layers.

#### 2.3.1 ZigBee Network Topologies

- **Star Topology**: All nodes connect to a central PAN coordinator.
- **Mesh Topology**: Nodes interconnect redundantly, providing self-healing.
- **Tree Topology**: Hierarchical, with coordinators and routers.

#### 2.3.2 ZigBee Device Types

| Device | Role | Power |
|--------|------|-------|
| **Coordinator (ZC)** | Network initiator, one per network | Mains |
| **Router (ZR)** | Routes data, extends coverage | Mains |
| **End Device (ZED)** | Senses/acts, sleeps most of time | Battery |

#### 2.3.3 Key ZigBee Specifications

- **Frequency Bands**: 868 MHz (Europe, 1 channel), 915 MHz (USA, 10 channels), 2.4 GHz (Global, 16 channels).
- **Data Rate**: 20 kbps (868 MHz), 40 kbps (915 MHz), **250 kbps (2.4 GHz)**.
- **Modulation**: DSSS with BPSK (868/915 MHz) and O-QPSK (2.4 GHz).
- **Range**: 10–100 m.
- **Max Nodes**: 65,000+ per network (16-bit short or 64-bit long addresses).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for KTU 2024 numerical/analytical questions.

| Formula | Description | Application |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | Shannon-Hartley Capacity | Max data rate in channel |
| $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Friis Free-Space Path Loss | Range calculation |
| $L_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$ | Free-Space Loss (dB) | WLAN link budget |
| $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Data Payload}}{\text{Total Frame Time}}$ | Effective throughput | Bluetooth/Wi-Fi |
| $N_{hops} = \frac{BW_{total}}{BW_{channel}}$ | Hop count for FHSS | Bluetooth (79 hops in 1 MHz each) |
| $E_b/N_0 = \text{SNR} \cdot \frac{B}{R_b}$ | Energy per bit to noise density | BER analysis |
| $\text{Packet Loss} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | Probability of loss (n attempts, p probability) | ARQ analysis |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Shannon Capacity for WLAN

Consider a wireless channel with bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio SNR. The maximum achievable data rate (Shannon-Hartley theorem) is:

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \text{ bits/second}
$$

**Derivation Steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define SNR} &\rightarrow \text{SNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{P_s}{N_0 B} \\
\text{Step 2: Express noise power} &\rightarrow N = N_0 \cdot B \\
\text{Step 3: Shannon's theorem states} &\rightarrow C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{P_s}{N_0 B}\right) \\
\text{Step 4: For a Wi-Fi channel of } B = 20 \text{ MHz,} &\rightarrow \text{SNR} = 20 \text{ dB} = 100 \\
\text{Step 5: Substitute values} &\rightarrow C = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 100) \\
\text{Step 6: Compute} &\rightarrow C = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(101) \\
\text{Step 7: Use } \log_2(101) &\approx 6.658 \\
\text{Step 8: Final capacity} &\rightarrow C \approx 133.16 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss for Bluetooth Link

For a Bluetooth link at $f = 2.4$ GHz over distance $d$:

$$
L_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})
$$

**Worked Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given: } f &= 2.4 \text{ GHz} = 2400 \text{ MHz}, \quad d = 10 \text{ m} = 0.01 \text{ km} \\
\text{Step 1: Plug in } f &\rightarrow 20 \log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.3802 = 67.604 \\
\text{Step 2: Plug in } d &\rightarrow 20 \log_{10}(0.01) = 20 \times (-2) = -40 \\
\text{Step 3: Sum all terms} &\rightarrow L_{dB} = 32.45 + 67.604 - 40 \\
\text{Step 4: Result} &\rightarrow L_{dB} = 60.054 \text{ dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This ~60 dB loss is why Bluetooth transmitter power is typically limited to **0 dBm (1 mW)** to within **10 m** — beyond that, SNR drops sharply.

### 3.3 Derivation: Bluetooth Hopping Time and Throughput

Bluetooth uses FHSS with **79 channels** of **1 MHz width** each across 2402–2480 MHz. Hop rate is **1600 hops/second**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hop Duration} &= \frac{1}{1600} = 625 \text{ µs/slot} \\
\text{Time Slot Allocation} &\rightarrow \text{Master transmits on even slots, slave on odd} \\
\text{DH1 Packet} &\rightarrow \text{1-slot packet, payload} = 27 \text{ bytes (asymmetric)} \\
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{27 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits}}{625 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} \\
&= 345.6 \text{ kbps (theoretical DH1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, due to protocol overhead (LMP, L2CAP, baseband acknowledgments), effective throughput is **~50–60% of theoretical maximum**.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix: Wi-Fi vs. Bluetooth vs. ZigBee

| Parameter | Wi-Fi (802.11n) | Bluetooth (BLE 5.0) | ZigBee (802.15.4) |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Frequency | 2.4/5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz / Sub-GHz |
| Max Data Rate | 600 Mbps | 2 Mbps | 250 kbps |
| Range | 30–100 m | 10–200 m | 10–100 m |
| Power Consumption | High | Very Low | Ultra Low |
| Topology | Star/Mesh | Piconet/Scatternet | Star/Mesh/Tree |
| Nodes per network | 32 (typical) | 8 (piconet) | 65,000+ |
| Latency | 1–5 ms | 2.5 ms (BLE) | 15 ms+ |
| Battery Life | Hours | Days to months | Months to years |
| Use Case | Web browsing | Audio, wearables | IoT, sensors |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IEEE 802 Protocol Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["Data Link Layer (DLL)"]
        A1[IEEE 802.2 LLC - Logical Link Control]
    end
    subgraph L2["Network Layer"]
        A2[IP / IPv6]
    end
    subgraph L3["Upper Layer Protocols"]
        A3[TCP / UDP / Application]
    end
    subgraph L4["MAC Sublayer Variants"]
        B1[802.11 MAC - Wi-Fi]
        B2[802.15.1 MAC - Bluetooth]
        B3[802.15.4 MAC - ZigBee]
    end
    subgraph L5["Physical Layer Variants"]
        C1[802.11 PHY - OFDM, DSSS]
        C2[802.15.1 PHY - FHSS, GFSK]
        C3[802.15.4 PHY - DSSS, O-QPSK]
    end
    A3 --> A2 --> A1
    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    style L1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style L2 fill:#fff4e1
    style L3 fill:#e8f5e9
    style L4 fill:#fce4ec
    style L5 fill:#f3e5f5
```

### 4.2 WLAN Infrastructure with Hidden Node Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AP[Access Point / BSS]
    STA1[Station A]
    STA2[Station B]
    STA3[Station C]
    AP --- STA1
    AP --- STA2
    AP --- STA3
    STA1 -.X.-> STA3
    style STA1 fill:#ffcccc
    style STA3 fill:#ffcccc
    style AP fill:#cce5ff
    style STA2 fill:#ccffcc
```

> [!WARNING]
> The dotted red line with `X` between **STA1 and STA3** indicates they **cannot sense each other** (hidden nodes). Both reach the AP, causing potential collision. **RTS/CTS** solves this.

### 4.3 Bluetooth Piconet and Scatternet Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1["Piconet 1"]
        M1[Master M1]
        S1[Slave S1]
        S2[Slave S2]
        S3[Slave S3]
        M1 --- S1
        M1 --- S2
        M1 --- S3
    end
    subgraph P2["Piconet 2"]
        M2[Master M2]
        S4[Slave S4]
        S5[Slave S5]
        M2 --- S4
        M2 --- S5
    end
    S2 -.Bridge.-> M2
    style P1 fill:#e3f2fd
    style P2 fill:#fff3e0
    style S2 fill:#ffe082
    style M2 fill:#ffe082
```

### 4.4 ZigBee Mesh Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ZC[ZigBee Coordinator ZC]
    ZR1[ZigBee Router ZR1]
    ZR2[ZigBee Router ZR2]
    ZR3[ZigBee Router ZR3]
    ZED1[End Device 1 - Sensor]
    ZED2[End Device 2 - Light]
    ZED3[End Device 3 - Door Sensor]
    ZED4[End Device 4 - Thermostat]
    ZC --- ZR1
    ZC --- ZR2
    ZR1 --- ZR3
    ZR2 --- ZR3
    ZR1 --- ZED1
    ZR1 --- ZED2
    ZR2 --- ZED3
    ZR3 --- ZED4
    style ZC fill:#ffcc80
    style ZR1 fill:#a5d6a7
    style ZR2 fill:#a5d6a7
    style ZR3 fill:#a5d6a7
    style ZED1 fill:#ef9a9a
    style ZED2 fill:#ef9a9a
    style ZED3 fill:#ef9a9a
    style ZED4 fill:#ef9a9a
```

### 4.5 CSMA/CA Flow with RTS/CTS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ST1[Station ready to transmit]
    DEC1{Sense channel idle for DIFS?}
    BACK1[Start backoff timer]
    DEC2{Timer = 0?}
    RTS1[Send RTS]
    CTS_WAIT[Wait for CTS]
    DEC3{CTS received?}
    SEND_DATA[Transmit Data Frame]
    ACK_WAIT[Wait for ACK]
    RETRY1[Exponential backoff and retry]
    ST1 --> DEC1
    DEC1 -- Yes --> BACK1
    DEC1 -- No --> BACK1
    BACK1 --> DEC2
    DEC2 -- No --> BACK1
    DEC2 -- Yes --> RTS1
    RTS1 --> CTS_WAIT
    CTS_WAIT --> DEC3
    DEC3 -- Yes --> SEND_DATA
    DEC3 -- No --> RETRY1
    SEND_DATA --> ACK_WAIT
    ACK_WAIT --> DONE[Transmission Complete]
    style DONE fill:#a5d6a7
    style RETRY1 fill:#ef9a9a
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define the term **Personal Area Network (PAN)**. Mention two technologies used in PANs.

**Model Answer:**
A **Personal Area Network (PAN)** is a short-range network that interconnects personal devices (smartphones, laptops, wearables) within a person's reach, typically **10 meters**. Technologies used in PANs include:
1. **Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15.1)** — for cable replacement, audio streaming.
2. **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** — for low-power IoT and sensor networks.
3. **Infrared (IrDA)** — line-of-sight communication.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** What is the **hidden node problem** in WLANs? How is it solved?

**Model Answer:**
The **hidden node problem** occurs when two wireless stations (A and C) are out of each other's range but both reach a common Access Point B. They cannot sense each other's transmissions, leading to **simultaneous transmission** and collision at B.

**Solution:** Use the **RTS/CTS (Request-To-Send / Clear-To-Sent)** handshake. Before sending data, A sends an RTS to B; B replies with a CTS that both A and C can hear, reserving the medium.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of **IEEE 802.11 WLAN** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **BSS, IBSS, and ESS**.

**Model Solution:**

The IEEE 802.11 architecture is composed of:
- **BSS (Basic Service Set)**: A set of stations communicating with each other through a single **Access Point (AP)**. Each BSS has a unique **BSSID** (usually the AP's MAC address).
- **IBSS (Independent BSS)**: An **ad-hoc** network where stations communicate peer-to-peer without an AP. Also called a "mesh ad-hoc network."
- **ESS (Extended Service Set)**: Multiple BSSs connected via a **Distribution System (DS)** — typically a wired Ethernet — allowing roaming across APs.

**Diagram Description:**
```
        ESS
   ┌─────┴─────┐
  BSS1        BSS2
   │            │
   AP1          AP2
  / \          / \
 S1 S2        S3 S4
   \____ DS ____/
```

**Key Differences:**

| Parameter | BSS | IBSS | ESS |
|-----------|-----|------|-----|
| AP Required | Yes | No | Yes (multiple) |
| Topology | Star | Peer-to-peer | Multi-cell |
| Range | Limited to AP | Limited to direct | Wide (roaming) |
| Internet Access | Yes | No | Yes |

---

> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **CSMA/CA protocol** used in WLANs. Mention its key timing parameters.

**Model Solution:**

**CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** is the MAC protocol used in IEEE 802.11 because wireless stations cannot detect collisions while transmitting.

**Working Steps:**
1. **Sensing the medium**: Station senses the channel. If idle for **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space = 34 µs in 802.11b)**, it proceeds.
2. **Backoff timer**: Station chooses a random backoff value in [0, CW], where **CW (Contention Window)** starts at minimum (e.g., 31) and doubles on collision.
3. **Transmission**: When the timer reaches 0, the station transmits.
4. **ACK**: Receiver waits **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space = 10 µs)**, then sends **ACK** to confirm receipt.

**Timing Parameters:**
- **SIFS = 10 µs** (highest priority)
- **PIFS = SIFS + Slot time = 30 µs**
- **DIFS = SIFS + 2 × Slot time = 50 µs**
- **EIFS (Extended IFS)** = SIFS + 8 × ACK time + Preamble + PLCP header (used after error)
- **Slot time = 20 µs** (802.11b at 2.4 GHz)

**RTS/CTS Enhancement:**
Before data, sender sends **RTS**; receiver replies **CTS** after SIFS. All other stations defer. After receiving data, ACK is sent. This reduces hidden node collisions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Bluetooth protocol stack** and describe the **piconet and scatternet** concepts.

**Model Solution:**

The **Bluetooth protocol stack** consists of:

1. **Radio (PHY) Layer**: Operates at **2.4 GHz** with **FHSS** using 79 (or 40 in BLE) channels, **GFSK modulation**, and **1 MHz channel spacing**. Hop rate is **1600 hops/sec**.

2. **Baseband Layer**: Manages **piconet**, defines timing, frequency hopping sequence (derived from master's clock), packet formats, and error correction (**FEC 1/3, 2/3**).

3. **Link Manager Protocol (LMP)**: Handles **link setup, authentication, encryption, and power control**.

4. **Host Controller Interface (HCI)**: Standardized interface between **host (PC/Phone)** and **Bluetooth controller chip**.

5. **Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)**: Multiplexing, segmentation, QoS.

6. **RFCOMM**: Emulates serial RS-232 port (for legacy applications).

7. **Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)**: Discovers services offered by other devices.

8. **Application Profiles**: A2DP (audio), HFP (hands-free), HID (keyboards/mice), OBEX (file transfer).

**Piconet and Scatternet:**

- **Piconet**: One **master** (provides clock and hopping pattern) and up to **7 active slaves**. Master transmits on even slots, slaves on odd slots. **Parked slaves** can exist beyond the 7 active (up to 255 total).
- **Scatternet**: Two or more piconets share a node. That node acts as slave in one piconet and master in another, enabling larger networks.

---

> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **ZigBee protocol architecture** and compare it with **Bluetooth** in terms of power, range, and data rate.

**Model Solution:**

**ZigBee Protocol Architecture** is based on the **OSI model** with custom layers:

- **IEEE 802.15.4 PHY**: Defines 27 channels — 11 in 868 MHz (BPSK, 20 kbps), 10 in 915 MHz (BPSK, 40 kbps), **16 in 2.4 GHz (O-QPSK, 250 kbps)**.
- **IEEE 802.15.4 MAC**: Uses **CSMA/CA**, supports beacon-enabled and non-beacon modes, GTS (Guaranteed Time Slots).
- **ZigBee Network Layer (NWK)**: Mesh routing, addressing, network formation.
- **ZigBee Application Layer (APL)**:
  - **Application Support Sub-Layer (APS)**
  - **Application Framework** (with **ZigBee clusters**)
  - **ZDO (ZigBee Device Objects)** — for device management.

**Comparison: ZigBee vs. Bluetooth**

| Parameter | ZigBee (802.15.4) | Bluetooth (Classic) |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Frequency | 2.4 GHz / Sub-GHz | 2.4 GHz |
| Data Rate | 250 kbps | 1–3 Mbps |
| Range | 10–100 m | 10 m |
| Power | Ultra-low (years on coin cell) | Low (days/weeks) |
| Topology | Star, Mesh, Tree | Piconet, Scatternet |
| Max Devices | 65,000+ | 8 (piconet) |
| Latency | 15–30 ms | 2.5 ms (BLE) |
| Modulation | O-QPSK | GFSK |
| Spectrum | DSSS | FHSS |
| Use Case | Sensors, smart home, industrial | Audio, file transfer |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often confuse **802.11** (WLAN) with **802.15** (WPAN). Remember: **802.11 → Wi-Fi, 802.15 → Bluetooth/ZigBee**.
> - Don't confuse **WLAN's CSMA/CA** with Ethernet's **CSMA/CD**. Wireless cannot **detect** collisions — only **avoid** them.
> - For Bluetooth, distinguish between **Classic Bluetooth** (audio, file transfer, FHSS, 79 channels) and **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)** (IoT, 40 channels, GFSK, advertising).
> - For ZigBee, remember the **three device types**: Coordinator, Router, End Device — each with distinct power/role.
> - The **hidden node problem** is unique to wireless — always associate it with **RTS/CTS** solution.
> - Be careful: **Bluetooth max nodes in piconet = 7 active slaves + 1 master = 8**, but ZigBee supports **65,000+**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **WLAN (IEEE 802.11)** = Wi-Fi; uses **CSMA/CA**; bands 2.4/5/6 GHz; data rate up to **9.6 Gbps** (Wi-Fi 6).
- **BSS** = one AP + stations; **ESS** = multiple BSSs; **IBSS** = ad-hoc.
- **Hidden node problem** is solved by **RTS/CTS handshake**.
- **Timing slots**: SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS.
- **Bluetooth (802.15.1)** = 2.4 GHz, **FHSS**, **79 channels**, **1600 hops/s**, piconet = 1 master + 7 slaves.
- **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)** = 40 channels, advertising-based, ultra-low power.
- **ZigBee (802.15.4)** = mesh, **DSSS**, 250 kbps at 2.4 GHz, 65,000+ nodes, three device types.
- **Wi-Fi** is for **high data + range**, **Bluetooth** for **personal audio/peripherals**, **ZigBee** for **battery-powered IoT sensors**.
- **Shannon Capacity**: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
- **Free-space path loss**: $L_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log(f_{MHz}) + 20\log(d_{km})$.
- **Modulation recap**: Wi-Fi → **OFDM**; Bluetooth → **GFSK/FHSS**; ZigBee → **O-QPSK/DSSS**.
- **Topologies**: Wi-Fi → Star/Ad-hoc; Bluetooth → Piconet/Scatternet; ZigBee → Star/Mesh/Tree.
- **Standard family mnemonic**: "**802.11 = Wide (Wi-Fi), 802.15 = Personal (WPAN)**".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Broadband Wireless Access: WiMax Technology and Wireless Spectrum Allocation Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module 3: Wireless Communication Systems
> - **Topic:** Broadband Wireless Access: WiMax Technology and Wireless Spectrum Allocation Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Broadband Wireless Access (BWA)?

**Broadband Wireless Access (BWA)** refers to the delivery of high-speed internet and multimedia services to end users through wireless (radio) links rather than wired infrastructure like DSL, cable, or fibre. It is designed to provide "last-mile" connectivity with data rates comparable to or exceeding traditional wired broadband, but with the flexibility and rapid deployment advantages of wireless systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *Broadband Wireless Access is a point-to-multipoint (PMP) or mesh wireless system operating in licensed or unlicensed radio spectrum above 1 GHz, providing high-throughput IP-centric data services to fixed, nomadic, or mobile subscribers in a metropolitan area network (MAN) scale coverage zone.*

### 1.2 The WiMax Technology (IEEE 802.16)

**WiMax** stands for **Worldwide Interoperability for Microwave Access**. It is a family of wireless broadband communication standards based on the **IEEE 802.16** series, designed to provide BWA over long distances (up to **50 km** in line-of-sight) at data rates up to **75 Mbps** per channel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The City-Sized Wi-Fi":**
> Think of Wi-Fi as a torch (covers one room, ~30 m). Now imagine a giant lighthouse beam that covers an entire city — that is WiMax. Where Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11) is great for your home/café, WiMax (IEEE 802.16) is built to blanket an entire metropolitan area with broadband, so a user on the move (or in a rural village without cables) can still get internet at car-like speeds. Just as 4G/5G cellular evolved alongside WiMax to deliver similar goals, WiMax remains the textbook benchmark for studying BWA PHY/MAC layer design.

### 1.3 Why a Separate Standard? The Gap WiMax Fills

| Technology | Range | Mobility | Typical Rate | Coverage Scope |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bluetooth (802.15) | < 10 m | Stationary | ~3 Mbps | Personal Area Network |
| Wi-Fi (802.11) | < 100 m | Nomadic | ~600 Mbps | Local Area Network |
| **WiMax (802.16)** | **Up to 50 km** | **Fixed / Mobile** | **Up to 75 Mbps** | **Metropolitan Area Network** |
| Cellular (3G/4G/5G) | Few km/cell | Full Mobile | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | Wide Area Network |

### 1.4 Wireless Spectrum Allocation Standards

Spectrum is the **lifeblood of all wireless communication** — without authorised frequency bands, no radio system can legally transmit. International spectrum governance is performed by:

- **ITU-R (International Telecommunication Union – Radiocommunication Sector):** The global body that allocates frequency bands for specific services (e.g., IMT-2000, IMT-Advanced, IMT-2020, Fixed Service, Mobile Service, Satellite Service) through World Radiocommunication Conferences (WRC).
- **Regional / National Regulators:** For India it is the **WPC (Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing)** under DoT; for the USA it is the **FCC (Federal Communications Commission)**; for Europe the **CEPT/ECC**.
- **Standardisation Bodies:** 3GPP, IEEE, ETSI define *how* a given band is to be used (e.g., 3GPP Band 40 = 2300–2400 MHz for TDD-LTE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Spectrum Concepts for KTU:**
> - **Licensed Spectrum:** Exclusive use, protected from interference (e.g., 2.3 GHz, 2.5 GHz, 3.5 GHz auctions).
> - **Unlicensed Spectrum:** Shared, free-to-use, but no interference guarantee (e.g., 2.4 GHz & 5 GHz ISM, 5.8 GHz UNII).
> - **Primary vs Secondary User:** Primary users have priority; secondary users must vacate when a primary user is detected (Cognitive Radio concept).
> - **Duplexing:** TDD (Time Division Duplex) and FDD (Frequency Division Duplex) dictate how uplink/downlink are separated.

### 1.5 Evolution of the IEEE 802.16 Family

| Standard | Year | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| IEEE 802.16 | 2001 | First 10–66 GHz, line-of-sight, fixed |
| IEEE 802.16a | 2003 | 2–11 GHz, non-line-of-sight, OFDM added |
| IEEE 802.16-2004 (REVd) | 2004 | Consolidated fixed WiMax (256-OFDM) |
| IEEE 802.16e-2005 | 2005 | **Mobile WiMax** (SOFDMA, scalable OFDMA) |
| IEEE 802.16-2009 | 2009 | Roll-up of 16e amendments |
| IEEE 802.16m | 2010 | **WiMax 2 / Advanced Air Interface**, > 100 Mbps mobile, baseline for IMT-Advanced candidate |
| IEEE 802.16.1 | 2012 | Machine-to-machine (M2M) profile |
| IEEE 802.16n / 802.16.1a | 2013 | Relay / high-reliability networks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 WiMax Network Architecture (End-to-End)

The WiMax Forum defines a reference architecture composed of three logical entities:

1. **MS / SS (Mobile Station / Subscriber Station):** The customer premises equipment (CPE) — indoor modem, outdoor antenna, USB dongle, or handheld device.
2. **BS (Base Station):** The central radio access node that serves a sectorised cell. Each BS may have one or more sectors.
3. **ASN (Access Service Network) & CSN (Connectivity Service Network):** ASN contains the BS plus ASN-GW (gateway performing mobility, QoS, security). The CSN provides IP connectivity, AAA (Authentication, Authorisation, Accounting), and inter-networking with the public Internet / PSTN.

> [!NOTE]
> The radio link between MS and BS is the **air interface** governed by IEEE 802.16. Everything behind the BS is a standard IP/Ethernet core network.

### 2.2 WiMax Physical Layer (PHY) — Key Technical Details

WiMax uses **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** for fixed profiles and **SOFDMA (Scalable OFDMA)** for mobile profiles.

**Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) sizes for Scalable OFDMA:**

| Channel Bandwidth (MHz) | FFT Size | Subcarrier Spacing (kHz) | Useful Subcarriers |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.25 | 128 | 10.94 | 72 |
| 2.5 | 256 | 5.47 | 144 |
| 5 | 512 | 5.47 | 360 |
| 7 | 1024 | 7.81 | 480 |
| 8.75 | 1024 | 7.81 | 720 |
| 10 | 1024 | 9.77 | 840 |
| 20 | 2048 | 10.94 | 1728 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scalable OFDMA** keeps the subcarrier spacing approximately constant (~10 kHz) by varying FFT size with bandwidth. This is crucial because mobile channels have a coherence bandwidth that is nearly constant — keeping subcarrier spacing constant ensures consistent Doppler tolerance across all channel widths.

### 2.3 Modulation & Adaptive Coding

WiMax supports **adaptive modulation and coding (AMC)** to trade-off throughput vs robustness:

| Modulation | Code Rate | Spectral Efficiency (bits/s/Hz) | Robustness |
|---|---|---|---|
| BPSK | 1/2 | 0.5 | Highest |
| QPSK | 1/2, 3/4 | 1.0, 1.5 | High |
| 16-QAM | 1/2, 3/4 | 2.0, 3.0 | Medium |
| 64-QAM | 2/3, 3/4 | 4.0, 4.5 | Lowest (needs strong SNR) |

### 2.4 Duplexing Schemes

- **TDD (Time Division Duplex):** Uplink and downlink share the same carrier frequency but use different time slots. Allows asymmetric UL/DL allocation — ideal for **bursty IP traffic**. **Used by Mobile WiMax.**
- **FDD (Frequency Division Duplex):** UL and DL use two separate carrier frequencies simultaneously. Better for symmetric voice/legacy traffic. **Used by Fixed WiMax.**

### 2.5 WiMax MAC Layer Highlights

- **Connection-oriented:** Every MS has a 16-bit Connection ID (CID).
- **Five QoS Service Classes:**
  1. **UGS** (Unsolicited Grant Service) — CBR voice (E1/T1)
  2. **rtPS** (Real-Time Polling Service) — MPEG video
  3. **ErtPS** (Extended rtPS) — VoIP with silence suppression
  4. **nrtPS** (Non-Real-Time Polling Service) — FTP
  5. **BE** (Best Effort) — Web browsing, email

- **ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest)** and **HARQ (Hybrid ARQ)** at the PHY-MAC boundary for error control.
- **MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output)** antenna techniques from 802.16e onwards: Matrix A (2×2), Matrix B (4×4), and collaborative spatial multiplexing.
- **Security:** PKMv2 (Privacy Key Management, version 2), AES-CCM encryption, mutual authentication via X.509 certificates / EAP.

### 2.6 Wireless Spectrum Allocation — International Framework

#### 2.6.1 ITU-R Three-Region Model

The ITU divides the world into three regions:
- **Region 1:** Europe, Africa, Middle East, Russia.
- **Region 2:** The Americas.
- **Region 3:** Asia-Pacific (including India).

Each region has its own **frequency allocation table** in the **Radio Regulations (RR)**, updated every 3–4 years at the **WRC (World Radiocommunication Conference)**.

#### 2.6.2 IMT-2000 (3G) Spectrum (identified by WRC-2000)

| Band (MHz) | Total Spectrum | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 806–960 | 154 MHz | 3G uplink/downlink (paired) |
| 1710–1885 | 175 MHz | 3G uplink/downlink (paired) |
| 1885–2025 | 140 MHz | 3G TDD / satellite component |
| 2110–2200 | 90 MHz | 3G downlink |
| 2500–2690 | 190 MHz | 3G TDD / extension |

#### 2.6.3 IMT-Advanced (4G) Spectrum (identified by WRC-2007)

Additional bands identified globally:
- **450–470 MHz** (digital dividend UHF)
- **698–806 MHz** (digital dividend)
- **2300–2400 MHz** (India's prime BWA band — used for WiMax / TD-LTE)
- **3400–3600 MHz** (C-band extension)

#### 2.6.4 IMT-2020 (5G) Spectrum (WRC-2019)

- Low-band: < 1 GHz (e.g., 600 MHz, 700 MHz)
- Mid-band: 1–6 GHz (e.g., 3.5 GHz, 3.3–4.2 GHz, 4.4–4.9 GHz, 24.25–29.5 GHz)
- High-band / mmWave: 24–52.6 GHz (24.25–27.5, 37–43.5, 45.5–52.6 GHz)

### 2.7 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **core formula toolkit** for solving any WiMax / BWA numerical problem in KTU University Exams.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | OFDM Symbol Duration (CP excluded) | $T_u = N_{FFT} / \Delta f$ | $N_{FFT}$ = FFT size, $\Delta f$ = subcarrier spacing (Hz) | $T_u = 1024/10.94\,\text{kHz} \approx 91.4\,\mu\text{s}$ |
| 2 | Total OFDM Symbol Duration | $T_s = T_u + T_{CP} = T_u (1 + G)$ | $G$ = CP ratio (1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32) | Mandatory for prefix overhead |
| 3 | Number of Useful Subcarriers | $N_{used} = N_{FFT} \times \rho$ | $\rho$ = subcarrier utilisation factor (e.g., 0.82 for 1024-FFT) | Fixed for each profile |
| 4 | OFDM Symbol Rate (per second) | $R_s = 1 / T_s$ | Symbols/sec | CP overhead reduces it |
| 5 | Raw PHY Bit Rate (peak) | $R_b = N_{used} \times \log_2 M \times R_c \times R_s$ | $M$ = M-ary, $R_c$ = code rate | Theoretical max throughput |
| 6 | Spectral Efficiency (Shannon) | $\eta = \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | SNR linear | Upper bound; practical is 50–70% |
| 7 | Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $B$ in Hz, $C$ in bps | $B = 5\,\text{MHz}, \text{SNR}=15\,\text{dB} \Rightarrow C \approx 25.9\,\text{Mbps}$ |
| 8 | Free-Space Path Loss | $L_{fs} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz | LOS, far-field, isotropic antenna |
| 9 | Log-Distance Path Loss | $L = L_0 + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_0)$ | $n$ = path loss exponent (2–4) | $n=2$ free space; $n=4$ severe NLOS |
| 10 | Maximum Cell Radius (cost-231) | $L_{max} = 130 + 38\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | d in km, f in MHz | Okumura-Hata derived |
| 11 | Link Budget Received Power | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path} - L_{misc}$ | All in dB | $L_{misc}$ = cables, body loss |
| 12 | Noise Floor | $N = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)$ | $B$ in Hz → dBm/Hz | At 20 MHz: $N = -174 + 73 = -101$ dBm |
| 13 | SNR Required for BER $10^{-6}$ | BPSK: 10.5 dB; QPSK: 10.5 dB; 16-QAM: 14.4 dB; 64-QAM: 18.6 dB | dB | Adaptive MCS threshold |
| 14 | Carrier Frequency Reuse Factor | $K = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | $i,j$ = shift parameters | $K=3$ common; $K=7$ worst case |
| 15 | TDD Frame Efficiency | $\eta_{TDD} = T_{DL} / (T_{DL}+T_{UL}+T_{gap})$ | Fraction | Typical 0.7–0.85 |
| 16 | Delay Spread vs CP | $T_{CP} \ge \tau_{max}$ | seconds | $T_{CP} = G \times T_u$ |
| 17 | Doppler Frequency | $f_d = v \cdot f_c / c$ | $v$ m/s, $f_c$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | At 60 km/h, 2.5 GHz: $f_d \approx 139$ Hz |
| 18 | Coherence Time | $T_c \approx 0.423 / f_d$ | seconds | 60 km/h at 2.5 GHz: ~3 ms |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **WiMax in India:** BSNL deployed India's first 802.16d (fixed) network in 2009 across 16 cities. Later 2300–2400 MHz was repurposed for TD-LTE; this band is now the most contested 4G band in India.
- **Tata Indicom / Tikona:** Pioneered 802.16e fixed-wireless broadband before exiting the market.
- **Globally:** South Korea, Japan, and parts of Latin America deployed Mobile WiMax; Intel embedded WiMax chips in laptops (Intel 5150/5350).
- **Spectrum auctions:** The Indian 2.3 GHz and 3.5 GHz auctions have generated revenues in the billions of USD — spectrum allocation *is* the primary economic policy tool of modern telecom regulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** The following derivations are the most frequently asked numerical patterns in KTU University Examinations for this module. Memorising the *logical flow* is more important than memorising the final number.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Peak OFDM Symbol Rate of Mobile WiMax (10 MHz TDD)

**Problem Setup:** Compute the peak downlink physical-layer bit rate for a **10 MHz TDD Mobile WiMax** system using 64-QAM, code rate 3/4, CP ratio 1/8.

**Step 1 — Identify FFT size and subcarrier spacing.**
For 10 MHz channel in SOFDMA, the standard is $N_{FFT} = 1024$ with $\Delta f = 9.77\,\text{kHz}$ (from the table in §2.2).

**Step 2 — Compute the useful OFDM symbol duration.**

$$
T_u = \frac{N_{FFT}}{\Delta f} = \frac{1024}{9.77 \times 10^{3}} \approx 1.0486 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 104.86\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cyclic prefix duration.**
For CP ratio $G = 1/8$:

$$
T_{CP} = G \times T_u = \frac{1}{8} \times 104.86\,\mu\text{s} = 13.11\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total OFDM symbol duration.**

$$
T_s = T_u + T_{CP} = 104.86 + 13.11 = 117.97\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute OFDM symbol rate.**

$$
R_s = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{1}{117.97 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 8476.6\,\text{symbols/sec}
$$

Wait — in practice, **the 1024-FFT frame is grouped into 47 OFDM symbols per 5 ms frame**, giving:

$$
R_{s,\text{frame}} = 47 / 5\,\text{ms} = 9400\,\text{symbols/sec}
$$

For the exam we use the simpler $R_s = 1/T_s \approx 8.48\,\text{k}$ symbols/s. The 9400 figure includes a 4.7% overhead for the idle period; the methodology is the same.

**Step 6 — Compute number of useful data subcarriers.**
For 10 MHz profile, $N_{used} = 840$.

**Step 7 — Compute peak bit rate with 64-QAM and code rate 3/4.**

$$
R_b = N_{used} \times \log_2 M \times R_c \times R_s
$$

$$
R_b = 840 \times \log_2(64) \times \frac{3}{4} \times 9400
$$

$$
R_b = 840 \times 6 \times 0.75 \times 9400
$$

$$
R_b = 840 \times 6 \times 7050
$$

$$
R_b = 35{,}532{,}000\,\text{bps} \approx 35.5\,\text{Mbps}
$$

**Final Answer:** Peak DL PHY rate $\approx$ **35.5 Mbps** for a single 10 MHz carrier. With 4×4 MIMO and 64-QAM, four parallel streams bring this to **~140 Mbps**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly why the 802.16m (WiMax 2) standard quotes 100+ Mbps at the cell edge with 4×4 MIMO — the math is consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Link Budget for a 3.5 GHz Fixed WiMax Cell

**Problem Setup:** A WiMax BS at 3.5 GHz, $P_t = 35$ dBm, $G_t = 18$ dBi, $G_r = 24$ dBi (subscriber antenna), cable + connector loss $L_c = 3$ dB, body/penetration loss $L_p = 6$ dB. Find maximum cell range if the receiver sensitivity is $P_{r,\min} = -97$ dBm and the path-loss exponent $n = 3.5$ (urban NLOS).

**Step 1 — Write the link budget equation.**

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{path} - L_c - L_p
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for maximum allowable path loss.**

$$
L_{path,\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_c - L_p - P_{r,\min}
$$

$$
L_{path,\max} = 35 + 18 + 24 - 3 - 6 - (-97)
$$

$$
L_{path,\max} = 165\,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the log-distance path loss model.**

$$
L_{path} = L_0 + 10 n \log_{10}(d / d_0)
$$

We need a reference $L_0$. At $d_0 = 1$ km, free-space path loss at 3.5 GHz:

$$
L_0 = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(1) + 20\log_{10}(3500) = 32.45 + 0 + 70.88 = 103.33\,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $d_{\max}$.**

$$
165 = 103.33 + 10(3.5) \log_{10}(d_{\max})
$$

$$
165 - 103.33 = 35 \log_{10}(d_{\max})
$$

$$
61.67 = 35 \log_{10}(d_{\max})
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{\max}) = 1.762
$$

$$
d_{\max} = 10^{1.762} \approx 57.8\,\text{km}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply real-world de-rating.**
This theoretical 57.8 km is **over-optimistic** because:
- The $L_0$ reference assumes free-space, not urban NLOS.
- Rain fading, foliage loss, and shadowing typically consume **20–30 dB** extra at 3.5 GHz over such long distances.

**Final Practical Answer:** $d_{\max} \approx$ **5–8 km** in real urban deployments. **Always apply 20–30 dB shadow/fade margin** in KTU link-budget problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Spectral Efficiency & Shannon Capacity Check

**Problem Setup:** A 20 MHz WiMax channel at SNR = 20 dB.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{lin} = 10^{20/10} = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Shannon capacity.**

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 20 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(101)
$$

$$
\log_2(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.658
$$

$$
C = 20 \times 10^6 \times 6.658 = 133.16\,\text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with 64-QAM practical rate.**
For 64-QAM (6 bits/symbol) at 3/4 coding × 0.7 (TDD + CP efficiency):

$$
R_{\text{practical}} = 20\,\text{MHz} \times 6 \times 0.75 \times 0.7 \approx 63\,\text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute efficiency ratio.**

$$
\eta = \frac{R_{\text{practical}}}{C} = \frac{63}{133.16} \approx 0.47 = 47\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The system is operating at **~47% of Shannon capacity**, which is in the expected range (40–60%) for OFDM-based systems. This is a *good* result for KTU exams — the examiner expects a 50–60% efficiency benchmark.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cyclic Prefix Length vs Delay Spread

**Problem Setup:** 802.16e operating in an urban environment with maximum delay spread $\tau_{max} = 5\,\mu\text{s}$. OFDM parameters: $N_{FFT} = 1024$, $\Delta f = 10.94$ kHz. Verify which CP ratio (1/4, 1/8, 1/16, 1/32) is appropriate.

**Step 1 — Compute useful symbol duration.**

$$
T_u = \frac{1024}{10.94 \times 10^3} = 93.6\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_{CP}$ for each option.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CP}(1/4) &= 23.4\,\mu\text{s} \\
T_{CP}(1/8) &= 11.7\,\mu\text{s} \\
T_{CP}(1/16) &= 5.85\,\mu\text{s} \\
T_{CP}(1/32) &= 2.93\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the rule $T_{CP} \ge \tau_{max}$.**

For $\tau_{max} = 5\,\mu\text{s}$:
- $1/4$: $23.4 \ge 5$ ✓ (overhead 20%, conservative)
- $1/8$: $11.7 \ge 5$ ✓ (overhead 11.1%, balanced — **chosen default**)
- $1/16$: $5.85 \ge 5$ ✓ (overhead 5.9%, marginal)
- $1/32$: $2.93 < 5$ ✗ (causes ISI — unacceptable)

**Final Selection:** CP ratio **1/8** — the standard Mobile WiMax default for urban/suburban cells.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Summary Table (Common KTU Question Pattern)

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Computed Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_u$ for 1024-FFT, 10 kHz | $1024/10\,\text{kHz}$ | 102.4 μs |
| 2 | $T_{CP}$ for $G=1/8$ | $102.4/8$ | 12.8 μs |
| 3 | $T_s$ | $T_u + T_{CP}$ | 115.2 μs |
| 4 | Symbol Rate | $1/T_s$ | 8681 sym/s |
| 5 | Peak rate 64-QAM 3/4, 840 subcarriers | $840 \times 6 \times 0.75 \times 8681$ | ~32.8 Mbps |
| 6 | Shannon Capacity, 20 MHz, 20 dB SNR | $20\,\text{MHz} \times \log_2(101)$ | ~133 Mbps |
| 7 | FSPL at 3 km, 2.5 GHz | $32.45 + 20\log(3) + 20\log(2500)$ | 110.0 dB |
| 8 | Doppler at 60 km/h, 2.5 GHz | $(60/3.6)(2.5 \times 10^9)/3 \times 10^8$ | 138.9 Hz |
| 9 | Coherence time | $0.423/138.9$ | ~3.05 ms |
| 10 | Noise floor at 10 MHz | $-174 + 10\log(10^7)$ | -104 dBm |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 WiMax Network Reference Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

<!-- SECTION_4_START_DIAGRAM_1 -->
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[End User Devices<br/>Laptop, Mobile, CPE, USB Dongle] --> B[Mobile Station MS<br/>Subscriber Station SS<br/>CPE Indoor or Outdoor Antenna]
    B -->|Radio Air Interface<br/>IEEE 802.16 OFDM or OFDMA| C[Base Station BS<br/>Sectorised Antenna Tower]
    C --> D[ASN Gateway ASN-GW<br/>Mobility, QoS, Security, AAA Client]
    D --> E[Connectivity Service Network CSN<br/>IP Backbone Routers, DHCP, DNS, RADIUS]
    E --> F[Public Internet and PSTN Core]
    D --> G[Home Agent HA<br/>Mobile IP Anchor]
    G --> F
    C --> H[Operations and Management System NMS<br/>Fault, Configuration, Accounting, Performance, Security FCAPS]
    D --> H
    F --> I[Content Servers, VoIP Servers, IPTV Multicast]
    I --> A
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END_DIAGRAM_1 -->

## 4.2 OFDMA Frame Structure (TDD Mobile WiMax)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[5 ms OFDMA Frame TDD] --> B[Downlink Subframe DL]
    A --> C[TTG Transmit Receive Transition Gap]
    C --> D[Uplink Subframe UL]
    D --> E[RTG Receive Transmit Transition Gap]
    E --> F[Next 5 ms Frame]
    B --> B1[Preamble, FCH, MAP, Burst 1, Burst 2, ...]
    B1 --> B1a[Data carried in OFDM symbols allocated by MAP]
    D --> D1[Control UL-MAP, ranging, bandwidth requests, data bursts]
```

## 4.3 Wireless Spectrum Allocation Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[ITU-R International Telecommunication Union Radiocommunication Sector] --> B[World Radiocommunication Conference WRC<br/>Updates Radio Regulations every 3 to 4 years]
    B --> C[Global Frequency Allocation Table<br/>9 kHz to 275 GHz]
    C --> D[Region 1 Europe Africa<br/>Region 2 Americas<br/>Region 3 Asia Pacific]
    D --> E[National Regulators<br/>India WPC, USA FCC, Europe CEPT, Japan MIC, China MIIT]
    E --> F[Service Allocations<br/>IMT-2000 3G, IMT-Advanced 4G, IMT-2020 5G, Fixed Service, Satellite]
    F --> G[Licensed Spectrum<br/>2.3 GHz, 2.5 GHz, 3.5 GHz auctions]
    F --> H[Unlicensed Spectrum<br/>2.4 GHz, 5 GHz ISM, 5.8 GHz UNII]
    G --> I[Technology Assignment<br/>3GPP Bands for LTE 5G NR, IEEE 802.16 WiMax]
    H --> J[Wi-Fi 802.11, Bluetooth, Zigbee, WiMax Light]
    I --> K[Operators and Service Providers]
    J --> K
    K --> L[End Users]
```

## 4.4 WiMax PHY Layer Functional Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[MAC PDU from Upper Layers CS SAP] --> B[MAC Layer<br/>Connection ID, QoS UGS rtPS ErtPS nrtPS BE, ARQ HARQ, Security PKMv2]
    B --> C[Convergence Sublayer<br/>Service-Specific CS for IP, Ethernet, ATM]
    C --> D[MAC Common Part Sublayer<br/>Fragmentation, Packing, Scheduling, Bandwidth Request]
    D --> E[PHY Layer<br/>Channel Coding CC, Convolutional Turbo Code, Randomisation, Interleaving]
    E --> F[OFDM Modulator<br/>1024-FFT IFFT, Cyclic Prefix Insertion]
    F --> G[RF Front End<br/>DAC, Upconversion, Power Amplifier, Antenna]
    G --> H[Wireless Channel 2 to 66 GHz, NLOS Urban, LOS Rural]
```

## 4.5 Adaptive Modulation & Coding (AMC) Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BS Measures CINR via pilots from MS] --> B{CINR Threshold}
    B -->|CINR greater than 22 dB| C[64-QAM 3/4 4.5 bits sym Highest Throughput]
    B -->|CINR 16 to 22 dB| D[16-QAM 3/4 3.0 bits sym]
    B -->|CINR 10 to 16 dB| E[QPSK 3/4 1.5 bits sym]
    B -->|CINR 6 to 10 dB| F[QPSK 1/2 1.0 bits sym]
    B -->|CINR less than 6 dB| G[BPSK 1/2 0.5 bits sym Maximum Robustness]
    C --> H[BS sends DL-MAP UL-MAP with new MCS]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```
