# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Broadband Wireless Access (BWA). Mention any two advantages of BWA over wired broadband.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Broadband Wireless Access is a wireless networking technology that provides high-speed internet access to homes, businesses, and mobile users using radio waves in the licensed or unlicensed spectrum (above 1 GHz), without the need for last-mile copper or fibre. It uses point-to-multipoint or mesh topologies and is typically based on standards such as IEEE 802.16 (WiMax), 3GPP LTE/5G NR, or proprietary platforms.
- **Two advantages (1 mark):**
  1. **Rapid deployment** — no civil works to lay cables; a cell tower can serve thousands of users.
  2. **Reaches underserved areas** — bridges the digital divide in rural / geographically challenging regions where trenching is uneconomical.

---

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the key differences between IEEE 802.16-2004 (Fixed WiMax) and IEEE 802.16e-2005 (Mobile WiMax).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | 802.16-2004 Fixed | 802.16e-2005 Mobile |
|---|---|---|
| PHY | OFDM (256 FFT) | SOFDMA (128/512/1024/2048 FFT) |
| Handover | None (fixed) | Hard + Soft, FBSS, MDHO |
| Mobility | Stationary CPE | Vehicular 60–120 km/h |
| Duplexing | FDD or TDD | TDD (typical) |
| QoS | Four classes | Five classes (adds ErtPS) |
| Antenna | SISO | SISO, MIMO 2×2 |

*(Any 3 well-explained differences = 3 marks. Table format is acceptable in KTU answer sheets.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cyclic prefix** in OFDM rate calculations (costs 1 mark).
> 2. **Conflating Shannon capacity with practical throughput** — Shannon is the *upper bound*, not what the system delivers (costs 2 marks if not justified).
> 3. **Mixing up ITU regions** — India is **Region 3**, not Region 1 or 2 (costs 1 mark).
> 4. **Failing to convert dB ↔ linear** in SNR or path loss problems — examiners specifically check this step (costs 1 mark).
> 5. **Not stating units** in numerical answers — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 6. **Writing WiMax = Wi-Fi** — they are fundamentally different standards; examiners treat this as a serious conceptual error.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

#### (a) [7 marks] Explain the WiMax network architecture with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of ASN and CSN in detail.

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** The WiMax Forum Network Reference Model (NRM) defines the end-to-end architecture in three domains: **MS/SS, ASN (Access Service Network), and CSN (Connectivity Service Network)**.

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid architecture in §4.1. The student should draw:

- MS ↔ BS (Air Interface, IEEE 802.16) ↔ ASN-GW ↔ CSN (RADIUS, DHCP, DNS, HA) ↔ Public Internet.

**ASN (Access Service Network) – 2 marks:**
- Contains the **BS** (Base Station) and **ASN-GW** (Gateway).
- Functions: radio resource management, mobility management (handover, paging), QoS policy enforcement, security (PKMv2 key distribution), and AAA client functionality.
- Provides Layer-2 connectivity to the MS.

**CSN (Connectivity Service Network) – 2 marks:**
- Contains the AAA server (RADIUS/Diameter), Home Agent (for Mobile IP), DHCP, DNS, and inter-service provider gateways.
- Functions: IP address allocation, authentication, authorisation, accounting, inter-ASN mobility, internetwork routing, billing, and content services (VoIP, IPTV).
- Provides Layer-3 (IP) connectivity to the internet.

---

#### (b) [7 marks] With a relevant diagram, explain the OFDM/OFDMA frame structure used in Mobile WiMax. Discuss the role of the cyclic prefix and indicate why SOFDMA is preferred over fixed FFT OFDM in mobile channels.

**Model Solution:**

**Frame structure diagram (2 marks):** A 5 ms TDD frame consists of:
- **DL subframe** — Preamble (synchronisation), FCH (Frame Control Header), DL-MAP, UL-MAP, DL bursts.
- **TTG** — Transmit/Receive Transition Gap (10–100 μs).
- **UL subframe** — UL-MAP, ranging sub-channels, bandwidth-request sub-channels, UL bursts.
- **RTG** — Receive/Transmit Transition Gap.

**OFDM symbol structure (1 mark):** Data symbols = 1024-FFT inverse transform → $T_u = 102.4\,\mu\text{s}$, then cyclic prefix of $G = 1/8$ (≈12.8 μs) is prepended → total $T_s = 115.2\,\mu\text{s}$. 47 such symbols fit in a 5 ms frame.

**Role of Cyclic Prefix (2 marks):**
- Eliminates **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** caused by multipath delay spread.
- Converts linear convolution of the channel into a circular convolution, enabling **single-tap frequency-domain equalisation**.
- Trade-off: CP is **overhead** (e.g., $G=1/8$ costs 11.1% of symbol energy); for $G=1/32$ the overhead is only 3% but ISI reappears if $\tau_{max} > T_{CP}$.

**Why SOFDMA preferred in mobile (2 marks):**
- **Scalable FFT** keeps subcarrier spacing constant (~10.94 kHz) regardless of channel bandwidth.
- This is critical because **Doppler spread** and **coherence bandwidth** are functions of carrier frequency, not bandwidth. Constant $\Delta f$ → consistent tolerance to time-varying channels across all channel widths (1.25, 2.5, 5, 10, 20 MHz).
- Mobile WiMax can therefore operate across diverse spectrum allocations globally (e.g., 2.3 GHz in India, 2.5 GHz in USA, 3.5 GHz in Europe) **with the same physical-layer behaviour**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]

#### (a) [7 marks] Discuss the role of ITU-R in global spectrum allocation. With a neat diagram, describe the hierarchy from WRC to operator band assignment. List the IMT-2000 frequency bands identified in India.

**Model Solution:**

**ITU-R role (2 marks):**
- The ITU-R is the UN-affiliated body that maintains the **international Radio Regulations (RR)** — a treaty-level document binding on 193 member-states.
- Through the **World Radiocommunication Conference (WRC)** held every 3–4 years, ITU-R revises frequency allocations across three regions (India is **Region 3**).
- Allocates spectrum to **services** (Fixed, Mobile, Satellite, Broadcasting, Radiolocation) and identifies spectrum for **IMT** (International Mobile Telecommunications) generations.

**Hierarchy diagram (2 marks):** See §4.3 Mermaid diagram. Should show:
WRC → Radio Regulations → Regional Tables → National Regulator (WPC in India) → Service Allocations → Operators.

**IMT-2000 bands in India (3 marks):**
- **890–960 MHz** — 2G/3G uplink + downlink.
- **1710–1885 MHz** — 3G downlink (also 2G uplink).
- **1920–1980 MHz paired with 2110–2170 MHz** — 3G (UMTS) primary allocation in India.
- **2010–2025 MHz** — TDD 3G.
- **2300–2400 MHz** — TDD 3G / BWA (originally WiMax, now TD-LTE Band 40).
- **2500–2690 MHz** — 4G (LTE Band 38/41, 5G NR n41).

---

#### (b) [7 marks] A WiMax BS at 3.5 GHz transmits at $P_t = 40$ dBm with antenna gain $G_t = 18$ dBi. The subscriber station has $G_r = 24$ dBi, and total miscellaneous loss (cable, body, foliage) is $L_m = 8$ dB. The receiver sensitivity is $P_{r,\min} = -96$ dBm and the path-loss exponent is $n = 3$. Using the log-distance model with $L_0 = 100$ dB at $d_0 = 1$ km, find:
   (i) The maximum allowable path loss.
   (ii) The maximum cell radius.
   (iii) The spectral efficiency if a 64-QAM modulation with code rate 3/4 is selected at the cell edge, and comment on whether the system is operating near Shannon capacity if SNR at the edge is 20 dB.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum allowable path loss (2 marks):**

$$
L_{path,\max} = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_m - P_{r,\min}
$$

$$
L_{path,\max} = 40 + 18 + 24 - 8 - (-96) = 170\,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum cell radius (2 marks):**

Using $L_{path} = L_0 + 10 n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$:

$$
170 = 100 + 10 \times 3 \times \log_{10}(d_{\max})
$$

$$
70 = 30 \log_{10}(d_{\max})
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d_{\max}) = 70/30 = 2.333
$$

$$
d_{\max} = 10^{2.333} \approx 215.4\,\text{km}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply de-rating factor (1 mark):**
The theoretical 215 km is unrealistic. Applying a 30 dB shadow/fade margin reduces $L_{path,\max}$ to 140 dB:

$$
140 = 100 + 30 \log_{10}(d_{\max}) \Rightarrow d_{\max} \approx 10^{1.333} \approx 21.5\,\text{km}
$$

Realistic urban NLOS cell radius ≈ **5–8 km**.

**Step 4 — Spectral efficiency of 64-QAM 3/4 (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \log_2 M \times R_c = 6 \times 0.75 = 4.5\,\text{bits/s/Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Shannon capacity and efficiency check (1 mark):**

Shannon capacity per Hz:
$$
C/B = \log_2(1 + 100) = \log_2(101) \approx 6.66\,\text{bits/s/Hz}
$$

Efficiency ratio:
$$
\eta_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{4.5}{6.66} \approx 0.676 = 67.6\%
$$

**Comment:** The system is operating at **~68% of Shannon capacity**, which is in the typical 50–70% range for practical OFDM systems — a **good** result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam**

- [ ] **WiMax = IEEE 802.16**, not 802.11. Do not confuse.
- [ ] **OFDM** for fixed (256-FFT), **SOFDMA** for mobile (scalable 128/512/1024/2048-FFT).
- [ ] **Subcarrier spacing stays ~10.94 kHz** in SOFDMA — that's the *whole point* of "scalable".
- [ ] **TDD** is the default duplexing in Mobile WiMax; **FDD** in Fixed WiMax.
- [ ] **5 QoS classes:** UGS, rtPS, ErtPS, nrtPS, BE.
- [ ] **Cyclic prefix rule:** $T_{CP} \ge \tau_{max,\text{channel}}$.
- [ ] **Free-space path loss formula:** $L_{fs} = 32.45 + 20\log d_{km} + 20\log f_{MHz}$.
- [ ] **Doppler formula:** $f_d = v f_c / c$.
- [ ] **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — apply dB→linear conversion first.
- [ ] **Practical systems achieve 40–70% of Shannon capacity** — quote this band in the exam.
- [ ] **ITU Regions:** India = **Region 3**.
- [ ] **ITU-R** sets international allocations; **WPC** (India) / **FCC** (USA) translate into national rules.
- [ ] **IMT-2000 (3G) spectrum:** 2.1 GHz paired, 1.9 GHz TDD, 2.3 GHz BWA.
- [ ] **IMT-Advanced (4G):** 2300–2400 MHz is the Indian prime BWA band.
- [ ] **IMT-2020 (5G):** 3.5 GHz mid-band + 26/28 GHz mmWave.
- [ ] **Licensed vs unlicensed:** Licensed = protected, auctioned; Unlicensed = ISM 2.4/5 GHz, shared.
- [ ] **Always state units** in numerical answers; KTU deducts 0.5 per missing unit.
- [ ] **Always convert dB to linear** before applying Shannon; do not put dB inside the log.
- [ ] **Block diagrams in the exam:** draw clear boxes with arrows; label ASN, CSN, BS, MS, air interface.
- [ ] **India 2010–2011:** BSNL launched India's first 802.16d network; 2300 MHz band later re-farmed for TD-LTE.
- [ ] **WiMax 2 = IEEE 802.16m** — supports > 100 Mbps mobile, candidate for IMT-Advanced.
- [ ] **AMC rule of thumb:** 64-QAM needs ~22 dB SNR; 16-QAM ~16 dB; QPSK ~10 dB; BPSK ~6 dB.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Broadband Wireless Access: WiMax Technology and Wireless Spectrum Allocation Standards**
> *(Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3 of PCECL508 — Wireless Communication Systems)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cellular System Design Fundamentals: Frequency Reuse and Channel Assignment Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508 > - **Module:** Module 3: Wireless Communication Systems > - **Topic:** Cellular System Design Fundamentals: Frequency Reuse and Channel Assignment Strategies ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Cellular System Design Fundamentals** constitute the foundational engineering framework that enables the reuse of scarce radio spectrum across a wide geographic service area. The principle is rooted in dividing the service region into small geographic units called **cells**, each served by a low-power **Base Station (BS)**, and reusing the same radio frequencies in geographically separated cells to multiply system capacity without increasing the allocated bandwidth. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frequency Reuse** is defined as the systematic, repeated use of the same set of carrier frequencies (channels) in non-adjacent cells of a cellular network, subject to a minimum spatial separation called the **co-channel reuse distance (D)**, such that the resulting **co-channel interference (CCI)** remains below a tolerable threshold (typically $C/I \geq 18 \text{ dB}$ for narrowband digital systems). **Channel Assignment Strategy** is the rule-based algorithm that determines how the available pool of frequency channels is allocated to individual cells (or sectors), in order to maximize spectral efficiency, minimize blocking probability, and control co-channel and adjacent-channel interference. > [!NOTE] > The three canonical channel assignment strategies mandated in the KTU 2024 syllabus are: **Fixed Channel Assignment (FCA)**, **Dynamic Channel Assignment (DCA)**, and **Hybrid Channel Assignment (HCA)**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a vast agricultural region with **N** farmers (the *cluster*) and thousands of wells (the *frequencies*) in a nearby river. If every farmer pumped independently, the river would dry out. So, the farmers **form clusters of 7** (like the GSM system) and **schedule pumping in time slots** — each cluster uses its own 1/7 of the well, but clusters far away from each other (say, the 7th cluster downstream) can pump **simultaneously without affecting the upstream cluster's water table** because of the natural physical separation. In wireless systems: - The **river** = the finite RF spectrum (~900 MHz / 1800 MHz / 2.1 GHz bands). - The **cluster of N cells** = one full geometric "tile" that exhausts the licensed bandwidth. - The **faraway cluster** = a co-channel cell group; the geographic distance **D** ensures interference drops below noise. - The **pumping schedule** = the channel assignment policy (who gets which channel, when). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Cell Radius | $R$ | **1 – 10 km** (macrocell) | | Co-channel Reuse Distance | $D$ | km | | Co-channel Reuse Ratio | $q$ | dimensionless, typically **4.6** for $N=7$ | | Cluster Size | $N$ | $1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21...$ | | Minimum $C/I$ (narrowband) | $C/I_{\min}$ | **18 dB** (GSM-class) | | Minimum $C/I$ (AMPS analog) | $C/I_{\min}$ | **9 dB** (≈ 7.94 linear) | | Path Loss Exponent | $n$ | $3.0$ – $4.0$ (urban) | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of Frequency Reuse The cellular concept operates on four core principles that you must internalize for KTU valuation: - **Spectrum Scarcity Principle:** The usable radio spectrum is finite (e.g., **25 MHz** in the uplink band of 900 MHz GSM). Without spatial reuse, a single operator could serve only a tiny number of users. - **Spatial Reuse Principle:** Identical frequencies can be reused in cells separated by a distance **D**, provided the path loss between co-channel cells is high enough. - **Hexagonal Cell Model:** A regular hexagon is the standard abstraction because it approximates a circular coverage area with **no overlap and no gap**, and it has convenient geometric symmetry with 6 nearest neighbors. - **Cluster Principle:** A group of **N** cells exhausts the entire available frequency pool exactly once. Repeating this tile across the plane gives the full coverage. ### 2.2 The Shift Parameters (i, j) and Cluster Geometry For a hexagonal lattice, every cell has integer coordinates along two axes separated by **120°**. A co-channel cell is reached by shifting the reference cell by **$i$** cells along one axis and **$j$** cells along the other. The **cluster size N** is uniquely determined by: $$N = i^2 + ij + j^2$$ where $i$ and $j$ are non-negative integers with $i \geq j$. The **co-channel reuse distance** $D$ relates to cell radius $R$ by: $$D = R\sqrt{3N}$$ The **co-channel reuse ratio** $q$ is the dimensionless form: $$q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$?** > The hexagonal lattice is built on basis vectors of length $\sqrt{3}R$ separated by 60°. The squared distance from origin to the cell at $(i,j)$ in this skewed coordinate system yields the law of cosines form: $D^2/R^2 = 3(i^2 + ij + j^2)$. ### 2.3 Co-Channel Interference (CCI) and the S/I Formula In a hexagonal system, every cell has exactly **6 nearest co-channel interferers** (the first tier). The Carrier-to-Interference ratio at the reference mobile, using a **4th-power path loss** model (typical urban macrocell), is: $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^4 = \frac{q^4}{6} = \frac{(3N)^2}{6} = \frac{3N^2}{2}$$ For sectoring (e.g., 120° sectors), the first-tier interferers reduce to **2**, giving: $$\frac{C}{I}\bigg\vert_{\text{sectored}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^4 = \frac{q^4}{2} = \frac{3N^2}{1}$$ > [!NOTE] > The KTU examiner expects the 6-interferer (omni) form unless the question explicitly mentions sectoring. Always state your assumption. ### 2.4 Minimum Cluster Size Calculation To guarantee voice quality, the system must satisfy $C/I \geq C/I_{\min}$. Substituting the S/I equation: $$\frac{3N^2}{2} \geq (C/I)_{\min}$$ Solving for the **minimum cluster size**: $$N_{\min} = \left\lceil \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot (C/I)_{\min}}{3}} \right\rceil$$ The **ceiling function** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ ensures the next allowable value of $N$ from the set $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, \ldots\}$. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **The following table contains ALL the formulas you must memorize for the KTU ESE. No vertical pipes ($\vert$) are used inside math expressions; absolute value is rendered using `\vert`.** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Domain | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cluster Size (Fundamental) | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | integer, $i \geq j \geq 0$ | | 2 | Co-channel Reuse Distance | $D = R\sqrt{3N}$ | km | | 3 | Co-channel Reuse Ratio | $q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Carrier-to-Interference (Omni) | $C/I = q^4 / 6 = 3N^2 / 2$ | linear (convert to dB) | | 5 | Carrier-to-Interference (Sectored, 120°) | $C/I = q^4 / 2$ | linear | | 6 | C/I in dB | $C/I \text{ [dB]} = 10 \log_{10}(C/I)$ | dB | | 7 | Minimum Cluster Size | $N_{\min} = \lceil \sqrt{2 (C/I)_{\min} / 3} \rceil$ | integer | | 8 | Capacity Multiplier (FCA) | $M = M_c \cdot K / N$ | channels / cell | | 9 | Blocking Probability (Erlang B) | $P_b = B(C, A)$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Cell Splitting Capacity Gain | $C_{\text{new}} = 4 \cdot C_{\text{old}}$ | channels / area | ### 2.6 Engineering Utility of These Concepts These formulas directly drive real-world **4G LTE / 5G NR network planning**: - **Frequency Reuse Factor 1** in LTE means $N = 1$ — all cells use all frequencies — which is enabled by aggressive **ICIC (Inter-Cell Interference Coordination)** instead of physical separation. - **Cluster size 7** is the GSM default because it provides the required 18 dB C/I margin for narrowband voice. - **Cell splitting** by halving the radius roughly **quadruples** the channel density per unit area, which is the dominant capacity-expansion lever in dense urban deployments (5G pico/femtocells). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cluster Size $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ **Starting Setup:** Consider a hexagonal lattice where the cell centers are placed at the vertices of a regular triangular grid. Let the center-to-center distance between adjacent cells be $\sqrt{3}R$ (this is the standard hexagonal packing distance, NOT $R$, because the radius of a hexagon's circumcircle is $\sqrt{3}R/2$ from its center, and adjacent centers are at $\sqrt{3}R$ apart). **Step 1 — Choose shift vectors:** In the hexagonal coordinate system, basis vectors are: - $\vec{u}_1 = (\sqrt{3}R, \, 0)$ - $\vec{u}_2 = (\sqrt{3}R \cos 60°, \, \sqrt{3}R \sin 60°) = (1.5R, \, 1.5\sqrt{3}R)$ A co-channel cell is displaced by $i$ units along $\vec{u}_1$ and $j$ units along $\vec{u}_2$. **Step 2 — Displacement vector:** $$\vec{D} = i \vec{u}_1 + j \vec{u}_2$$ **Step 3 — Squared magnitude using Law of Cosines:** $$\vert \vec{D} \vert^2 = i^2 \vert \vec{u}_1 \vert^2 + j^2 \vert \vec{u}_2 \vert^2 + 2ij \vec{u}_1 \cdot \vec{u}_2$$ Since $\vert \vec{u}_1 \vert^2 = \vert \vec{u}_2 \vert^2 = 3R^2$ and $\vec{u}_1 \cdot \vec{u}_2 = 3R^2 \cos 60° = 1.5 R^2$: $$\vert \vec{D} \vert^2 = 3R^2 (i^2 + j^2) + 2ij (1.5 R^2) = 3R^2(i^2 + ij + j^2)$$ **Step 4 — Recognize $\vert \vec{D} \vert^2 = D^2$:** $$D^2 = 3R^2 (i^2 + ij + j^2)$$ $$D = R \sqrt{3(i^2 + ij + j^2)}$$ **Step 5 — Identify the term in the radical as $N$:** $$\boxed{N = i^2 + ij + j^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = R \sqrt{3N}$$ The derivation is complete. The cluster size is a **fundamental property of the hexagonal lattice topology** and has nothing to do with frequency; it is a pure geometry result. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: $C/I$ Ratio from First Principles **Assumption:** The received signal power at the mobile from its serving BS at distance $d_0$ follows the path-loss model: $$P_r = P_t \cdot K \cdot d^{-\gamma}$$ where $\gamma = 4$ for typical urban macrocells (this is the **4th-power law** used universally in KTU problems). The constant $K$ absorbs antenna gains and wavelength terms, and is **identical for all BSs** (we consider a homogeneous deployment). **Step 1 — Carrier Power at the mobile:** The mobile is at the cell boundary, so $d_0 = R$: $$C = K \cdot P_t \cdot R^{-4}$$ **Step 2 — Interference Power from one co-channel BS at distance $D$:** $$I_k = K \cdot P_t \cdot D^{-4}$$ **Step 3 — Sum the 6 first-tier interferers:** In a hexagonal grid, there are exactly **6 co-channel cells in the first tier** (verify: 6 vertices of the surrounding hexagon at distance $D$ from the origin). $$I_{\text{total}} = 6 \cdot K \cdot P_t \cdot D^{-4}$$ **Step 4 — Form the ratio:** $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{K P_t R^{-4}}{6 K P_t D^{-4}} = \frac{1}{6} \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^4$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $q = D/R$:** $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{q^4}{6}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute $q^2 = 3N$:** $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(3N)^2}{6} = \frac{3N^2}{2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The conversion $q^2 = 3N$ comes directly from $q = \sqrt{3N}$, which was derived in Section 3.1. This is a chained derivation — be ready to defend each step. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Standard KTU Pattern > **Question:** A cellular system uses a cluster size of $N = 7$. If the cell radius is $R = 4 \text{ km}$, find (a) the co-channel reuse distance, (b) the co-channel reuse ratio, and (c) the $C/I$ ratio in dB assuming omnidirectional antennas and a 4th-power path loss law. **Solution:** **(a) Co-channel reuse distance:** $$D = R\sqrt{3N} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{3 \cdot 7} = 4 \cdot \sqrt{21} = 4 \cdot 4.5826 = 18.33 \text{ km}$$ **(b) Co-channel reuse ratio:** $$q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583$$ **(c) C/I in dB (omni case, 6 interferers):** $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{q^4}{6} = \frac{(4.583)^4}{6} = \frac{441.7}{6} = 73.61$$ $$C/I \text{ [dB]} = 10 \log_{10}(73.61) = 18.67 \text{ dB}$$ > This exceeds the GSM 18 dB threshold ✓ — the choice of $N=7$ is verified as sufficient. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Minimum Cluster Size Determination > **Question:** A digital cellular system requires a minimum $C/I$ of **18 dB** to maintain acceptable voice quality. Determine the minimum cluster size for (a) omnidirectional antennas, (b) 120° sectored antennas. State the cluster size in standard allowable form. **Solution:** **(a) Omni case:** Convert 18 dB to linear: $$(C/I)_{\min} = 10^{18/10} = 10^{1.8} = 63.10$$ From $C/I = 3N^2/2 \geq 63.10$: $$N^2 \geq \frac{2 \cdot 63.10}{3} = 42.07$$ $$N \geq 6.49$$ From the set $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, \ldots\}$, the smallest $N \geq 6.49$ is $\mathbf{N = 7}$. ✓ **(b) 120° Sectored case:** Sectoring reduces first-tier interferers from 6 to 2, so: $$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{q^4}{2} = \frac{(3N)^2}{2} = \frac{9N^2}{2} \geq 63.10$$ $$N^2 \geq \frac{2 \cdot 63.10}{9} = 14.02$$ $$N \geq 3.74$$ The smallest allowable $N$ is $\mathbf{N = 4}$. ✓ > **Engineering insight:** Sectoring reduces $N_{\min}$ from 7 to 4, **increasing system capacity by 75%** (channels per cell scale as $1/N$). This is why real-world macrocells universally deploy 3-sector antennas. --- ### 3.5 Channel Assignment Strategies — Comparative Analysis | Strategy | Mechanism | Pros | Cons | Best Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **FCA** (Fixed) | Each cell permanently owns a fixed subset of channels (typically $K/N$ per cell) | Simple, low signaling, predictable interference | Poor adaptability to traffic variations; high blocking in hot-spots | Uniform traffic (rural, suburban) | | **DCA** (Dynamic) | Channels allocated to cells in real-time by an MSC based on demand and interference sensing | Maximizes trunking efficiency, adapts to hot-spots | High signaling overhead, slower handoff, complex algorithm | Non-uniform / bursty traffic (urban) | | **HCA** (Hybrid) | Each cell is assigned a permanent set of *nominal* channels; a shared pool supplements peak demand | Balances efficiency and stability | More complex than FCA, but simpler than full DCA | Mid-to-large cellular networks (typical 2G/3G) | > [!NOTE] > **DCA Sub-types** (also examinable in KTU Module 3): > 1. **Centralized DCA** — MSC makes all decisions (high latency, optimal). > 2. **Distributed DCA** — Each BS decides locally (fast, suboptimal). > 3. **Hybrid / Borrowing DCA** — A cell "borrows" a channel from a neighbor when its own pool is exhausted; this is the dominant algorithm in deployed 2G networks. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Frequency Reuse Architecture The following Mermaid diagram shows the hierarchical flow of the cellular frequency reuse system, from spectrum allocation down to per-cell channel ownership. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Spectrum Allocation
Total K Channels] --> B[Cluster of N Cells
N = i squared + ij + j squared] B --> C1[Cell 1
K over N Channels] B --> C2[Cell 2
K over N Channels] B --> C3[Cell N
K over N Channels] B --> D[Co channel Cell Group
Distance D apart] D --> C1 D --> C2 D --> C3 C1 --> E[Mobiles in Cell 1
SIR check] E --> F{Is SIR above 18 dB?} F -- Yes --> G[Call Accepted] F -- No --> H[Call Blocked or Handoff] C2 --> I[Interference Contribution
1 over D power 4] C3 --> I I --> E C1 --> J[Reuse Factor q
q equals D over R] C2 --> J C3 --> J J --> K[Capacity M equals K times K clusters over N] ``` ### 4.2 Cluster Layout — N = 7 Pattern The diagram below illustrates the canonical N = 7 cluster geometry used in GSM. Each number from 1 to 7 represents a unique channel group, and the same number reappears at co-channel cells separated by distance D. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Cluster1 A1[1] --- A2[2] A2 --- A3[3] A3 --- A4[4] A4 --- A5[5] A5 --- A6[6] A6 --- A7[7] A7 --- A1 end subgraph Cluster2 B1[1] --- B2[2] B2 --- B3[3] B3 --- B4[4] B4 --- B5[5] B5 --- B6[6] B6 --- B7[7] B7 --- B1 end A1 -. D .-> B1 A2 -. D .-> B2 A3 -. D .-> B3 A4 -. D .-> B4 A5 -. D .-> B5 A6 -. D .-> B6 A7 -. D .-> B7 ``` ### 4.3 Channel Assignment Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Incoming Call Request] --> Q1{Cell Permanent
Channels Free?} Q1 -- Yes --> Accept[Assign Permanent Channel] Q1 -- No --> Q2{HCA Mode and
Borrowable?} Q2 -- Yes --> Borrow[Borrow from Neighbor] Q2 -- No --> Q3{DCA Mode and
MSC Free Channel?} Q3 -- Yes --> Dynamic[MSC Allocates Dynamic Channel] Q3 -- No --> Block[Call Blocked
Increase Blocking Probability] Accept --> End[Call Proceeds] Borrow --> End Dynamic --> End ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model Paper)]** *(3 Marks)* **Define the term *co-channel reuse ratio* $q$. What is its significance in cellular system design?** **Model Answer:** The **co-channel reuse ratio** $q$ is defined as the ratio of the co-channel reuse distance $D$ (the distance between the centers of two co-channel cells) to the cell radius $R$: $$q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$ where $N$ is the cluster size. **Significance:** 1. It is a **dimensionless measure of spatial isolation** between co-channel cells — independent of absolute scale. 2. It directly determines the **$C/I$ ratio** through $C/I = q^4 / 6$ (omni case), which is the principal quality-of-service metric. 3. A **larger $q$** means greater interference protection but **smaller system capacity** (channels per cell = total/$\sqrt{3N^2}$); design always seeks the minimum $q$ that meets the $C/I$ threshold. > **Examiner's Note:** A complete answer must state the formula, the geometric meaning (D/R), AND the trade-off with capacity. Mentioning only the formula yields partial credit only. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model Paper)]** *(3 Marks)* **Compare Fixed Channel Assignment (FCA) and Dynamic Channel Assignment (DCA) in cellular systems.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | **FCA** | **DCA** | |---|---|---| | Channel ownership | Permanent per cell | Temporary, allocated by MSC | | Adaptability to traffic | Poor — hot-spots suffer | Excellent — adapts dynamically | | Signaling load | Low | High | | Implementation complexity | Low | High | | Blocking probability under non-uniform load | High | Low | | Stability of interference pattern | Predictable | Time-varying | **Conclusion:** FCA is preferred for uniform traffic (rural) due to its simplicity; DCA is preferred for bursty, non-uniform urban traffic where it yields **20–30% lower blocking probability** at the cost of higher MSC processing load. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (Choose Either A or B) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks) --- #### **Question A — Frequency Reuse Geometry and C/I Analysis** *(14 Marks)* **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]** **(a)** Derive the expression for the **co-channel reuse distance $D$** in terms of the cell radius $R$ and the cluster size $N$, starting from the hexagonal cell geometry and the shift parameters $(i, j)$. Show that $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A cellular operator uses a cluster of $N = 7$ with cell radius $R = 5 \text{ km}$. The minimum acceptable $C/I$ is **18 dB**. Determine: (i) The co-channel reuse distance $D$. (ii) The co-channel reuse ratio $q$. (iii) Whether the system meets the $C/I$ requirement with omnidirectional antennas. (iv) The new minimum $C/I$ in dB if 120° sectoring is introduced. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of $D$ and $N$ (7 marks):** See **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation. Brief key points the examiner expects: 1. State basis vectors: $\vec{u}_1 = (\sqrt{3}R, 0)$, $\vec{u}_2 = (1.5R, 1.5\sqrt{3}R)$. **(1 mark)** 2. Form displacement $\vec{D} = i\vec{u}_1 + j\vec{u}_2$. **(1 mark)** 3. Compute $\vert \vec{D} \vert^2$ using dot products and $\cos 60° = 0.5$. **(2 marks)** 4. Arrive at $D^2 = 3R^2(i^2 + ij + j^2)$. **(1 mark)** 5. Identify $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$, hence $D = R\sqrt{3N}$. **(2 marks)** **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i)** $D = R\sqrt{3N} = 5 \cdot \sqrt{21} = 5 \cdot 4.5826 = \mathbf{22.913 \text{ km}}$ **(1.5 marks)** **(ii)** $q = D/R = \sqrt{21} = \mathbf{4.583}$ **(1.5 marks)** **(iii)** $C/I = q^4 / 6 = (4.583)^4 / 6 = 441.65 / 6 = 73.61$ $= 10 \log_{10}(73.61) = \mathbf{18.67 \text{ dB}}$ **(2 marks)** Since $18.67 \text{ dB} \geq 18 \text{ dB}$, the system **meets the requirement with a 0.67 dB margin**. **(1 mark)** **(iv)** With 120° sectoring, only 2 first-tier interferers remain: $$C/I = q^4 / 2 = 441.65 / 2 = 220.82 = 10 \log_{10}(220.82) = \mathbf{23.44 \text{ dB}}$$ **(1 mark)** > **Capacity bonus insight:** The 23.44 dB C/I now allows a smaller cluster. Solving $C/I = 3N^2/2 \geq 63.10$ gives $N_{\min} = 4$, so capacity per cell **increases by 75%** with sectoring. --- #### **Question B — Channel Assignment Strategies** *(14 Marks)* **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]** **(a)** Explain the operation of **Fixed Channel Assignment (FCA)**, **Dynamic Channel Assignment (DCA)**, and **Hybrid Channel Assignment (HCA)** in cellular networks. Compare their relative performance in terms of blocking probability under uniform and non-uniform traffic. *(7 Marks)* **(b)** A cellular system has a total of **300 channels**. The cluster size is $N = 7$. Each user generates traffic of **0.02 Erlangs** and the desired Grade of Service (GoS) is **2% blocking** (Erlang B). Find: (i) The number of channels per cell. (ii) The number of users supported per cell at the specified GoS. (iii) The total system capacity (in users) across a service area containing **20 clusters**. (iv) Comment on how **cell splitting** would affect this capacity. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Channel Assignment Strategies (7 marks):** - **FCA (2 marks):** Each cell is permanently assigned a fixed set of $K/N$ channels (here $300/7 \approx 43$). A call within the cell is served only by these channels. If all are busy, the call is **blocked**, even if neighboring cells have free channels. Simple but inefficient under non-uniform load. - **DCA (2 marks):** No permanent assignment exists. The **MSC (Mobile Switching Center)** keeps a central pool of all 300 channels and assigns a free one to a cell upon request, provided the assignment does not violate co-channel or adjacent-channel interference constraints. **Centralized DCA** optimizes globally but adds signaling delay; **Distributed DCA** is faster but locally suboptimal. - **HCA (1.5 marks):** A hybrid of the two — each cell has a permanent **nominal channel set**, plus access to a **shared pool** for overflow. Balances stability (FCA) and adaptability (DCA). Most practical 2G/3G networks use this. - **Comparison (1.5 marks):** Under uniform traffic, FCA ≈ DCA ≈ HCA in blocking probability. Under non-uniform (hot-spot) traffic, DCA and HCA outperform FCA by **20–30%** in blocking probability because they re-route idle channels to congested cells. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **(i) Channels per cell:** $$C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{300}{7} \approx \mathbf{43 \text{ channels/cell}}$$ **(1 mark)** **(ii) Traffic supported per cell at 2% GoS (Erlang B):** From the **Erlang B table** for $P_b = 0.02$ and 43 channels, the carried traffic is $A \approx \mathbf{31.0 \text{ Erlangs}}$. $$\text{Users per cell} = \frac{A}{\text{traffic per user}} = \frac{31.0}{0.02} = \mathbf{1550 \text{ users/cell}}$$ **(2 marks)** **(iii) Total system capacity (20 clusters):** Total cells = $20 \times 7 = 140$ cells. $$\text{Total users} = 140 \times 1550 = \mathbf{217{,}000 \text{ users}}$$ **(2 marks)** **(iv) Cell splitting comment (2 marks):** **Cell splitting** divides each existing cell of radius $R$ into **4 smaller cells** of radius $R/2$ (preserving area by halving linear dimension). This **quadruples the number of cells** in the area, and because each new cell still gets 43 channels, the **total system capacity is multiplied by 4**, giving approximately **868,000 users** — at the cost of more base stations and increased handoff frequency. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Frequency Reuse Problems (Read Before Writing Your ESE!)** > > 1. **The $6$ vs $2$ interferer trap:** By default, the examiner assumes **omnidirectional** antennas with **6** first-tier co-channel interferers. If you accidentally use the sectored formula ($C/I = q^4/2$) without stating the sectoring assumption, you will lose 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Linear vs dB confusion:** Always convert $C/I$ from linear to dB using $10 \log_{10}$, NOT $20 \log_{10}$. The latter is reserved for voltage ratios. Using the wrong factor costs full marks on the final numerical answer. > > 3. **Forgetting the ceiling function on $N$:** When asked for *minimum* cluster size, you must apply $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ and verify the result is in the allowable set $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, \ldots\}$. Reporting $N = 6.49$ instead of $N = 7$ is a frequent error. > > 4. **Unit errors on $D$ and $R$:** If $R$ is given in km, $D$ must be in km, and $q$ remains dimensionless. Mixing km with miles in a problem statement is an instant **zero on that subpart** as per KTU valuation key. > > 5. **DCA explained without naming the MSC's role:** A definition of DCA that omits the central controller (MSC) is considered incomplete. The signal-vs-load trade-off must be mentioned. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Read this 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.** - ✅ **Cellular Concept:** Divide service area into small cells, reuse frequencies to multiply capacity. - ✅ **Hexagonal Model:** Standard abstraction; 6 equidistant neighbors per cell. - ✅ **Cluster $N$:** Group of $N$ cells that uses the entire frequency pool once. Computed via $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$. - ✅ **Co-channel Reuse Distance $D$:** Distance between centers of co-channel cells. $D = R\sqrt{3N}$. - ✅ **Co-channel Reuse Ratio $q$:** Dimensionless form, $q = \sqrt{3N}$. Governs $C/I$. - ✅ **Path-Loss Law:** 4th-power ($n=4$) for urban macrocells; assume unless told otherwise. - ✅ **C/I (Omni, 6 interferers):** $C/I = q^4/6 = 3N^2/2$. - ✅ **C/I (120° Sector, 2 interferers):** $C/I = q^4/2 = 9N^2/2$. - ✅ **dB Conversion:** $C/I \text{[dB]} = 10 \log_{10}(C/I_{\text{linear}})$. - ✅ **Minimum N:** $N_{\min} = \lceil \sqrt{2 (C/I)_{\min} / 3} \rceil$ (omni case). - ✅ **Standard C/I Thresholds:** 9 dB (AMPS analog), 18 dB (GSM narrowband). - ✅ **Allowable N values:** Only $N = 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, \ldots$ - ✅ **FCA:** Permanent per-cell assignment; simple; suffers hot-spot blocking. - ✅ **DCA:** MSC allocates centrally; high efficiency, high signaling load. - ✅ **HCA:** Fixed nominal set + dynamic overflow pool; the deployed 2G/3G default. - ✅ **Cell Splitting:** Halve radius → quadruple cell count → quadruple capacity. - ✅ **Sectoring:** 3 × 120° sectors → $C/I$ improves by ~5 dB → smaller $N$ allowed. ---

topics.title: Handoff Strategies, Interference, and System Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module Module 3: Wireless Communication Systems
> - **Topic:** Handoff Strategies, Interference, and System Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Handoff (Handover) — The Fundamental Cellular Operation

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> **Handoff (or Handover)** is the mechanism by which an ongoing call or data session of a Mobile Station (MS) is transferred from one Base Station (BS) or radio channel to another *without* interrupting the active communication, as the MS moves across cell boundaries or experiences degrading signal quality.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Lane Change"

Imagine you are driving on a **6-lane National Highway** at **100 km/h** in **Lane 3**. After 30 minutes, **Lane 3** has a massive pothole and heavy traffic — you cannot stop. So you smoothly steer into **Lane 4** without crashing, maintaining your speed. Your passengers (voice/data packets) feel nothing.

In wireless:
- **You** = Mobile Station (phone)
- **Lanes** = Frequencies / Cells / Base Stations
- **Steering action** = Handoff
- **Highway system** = Cellular Network

Without handoff, your call would *drop* the moment you cross a cell boundary — exactly like crashing into a divider.

---

## 1.2 Why Handoff is Needed — The "Trigger Conditions"

Handoff is triggered when **one or more** of the following situations occur:

1. **Signal Strength Degradation:** Received signal strength from the **serving BS** falls below a usable threshold.
2. **Co-channel Interference Rise:** Carrier-to-Interference (C/I) ratio drops below acceptable limits.
3. **Cell Load Balancing:** Current cell is **congested**; neighboring cell has spare capacity.
4. **Distance Threshold:** MS moves more than a preset distance (e.g., `D/2`) from the serving BS.
5. **Traffic-Driven Handoff:** Reserved channels exhausted in the current cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A handoff is initiated **only** after a *hysteresis margin* is crossed — this prevents the **"ping-pong effect"** (rapid back-and-forth handoffs between two BSs at the cell border).

---

## 1.3 Types of Handoff Strategies

| Type | Description | Used In | Break-Before-Make? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hard Handoff** | Old connection breaks *before* new one forms | GSM, TDMA, FDMA | **Yes** |
| **Soft Handoff** | New connection forms *before* old breaks; MS connects to multiple BSs simultaneously | CDMA (IS-95, 3G) | **No (Make-before-break)** |
| **Softer Handoff** | Occurs between *sectors* of the **same** BS | CDMA with sectored cells | No |

---

## 1.4 Interference — The Unwanted Guest

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **Interference** in wireless communication refers to any unwanted radio frequency energy that corrupts the desired signal, degrading the Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) and hence the Bit Error Rate (BER).

### Two Dominant Forms:

- **Co-Channel Interference (CCI):** Same frequency reused in non-adjacent cells. Controlled by the **co-channel reuse ratio** $Q = D/R$.
- **Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI):** Caused by imperfect filters; power leakage from frequencies in the *adjacent* band.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition — CCI Analogy:** Imagine two classrooms next to each other teaching the **same lesson in the same language at the same volume** — students get confused. **Solution:** Place the second class far enough away (increase cluster size $N$) so the sound fades out.

---

## 1.5 System Capacity — How Many Users Can the Network Serve?

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> **System Capacity (Cellular Capacity)** is the maximum number of simultaneous users (voice channels) or maximum achievable data throughput that a cellular system can support within a given geographic area while maintaining an acceptable **Grade of Service (GoS)** and signal quality.

Two fundamental limits govern capacity:
1. **Spectral Limit** — total available bandwidth $B_T$.
2. **Interference Limit** — minimum acceptable C/I ratio $(C/I)_{min}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Capacity is **not infinite** even if bandwidth is reused. It is bounded by the **co-channel interference constraint**, governed by $N$ (cluster size) and $Q$ (co-channel reuse ratio).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Handoff Process — Step-by-Step Logic

The handoff procedure consists of **three distinct phases**:

### Phase 1: Measurement (Monitoring)
- MS continuously measures **Received Signal Strength (RSS)** from the serving BS.
- MS also monitors **neighboring BSs** (typically the **6 closest** in a hexagonal layout).
- Reported to the Mobile Switching Center (MSC) via the **SACCH** (Slow Associated Control Channel) in GSM.

### Phase 2: Decision (Trigger)
- MSC evaluates the **handover decision algorithm**:
  - **RSS-based:** Trigger when $RSS_{serving} < RSS_{threshold}$ AND $RSS_{neighbor} > RSS_{serving} + \Delta H$, where $\Delta H$ = **hysteresis margin (3–6 dB typically)**.
  - **C/I-based:** Trigger when $(C/I)_{serving} < (C/I)_{min}$.
  - **Distance-based:** Trigger when distance to serving BS exceeds a threshold.

### Phase 3: Execution (Transfer)
- MSC reserves a channel in the new cell.
- Old channel is released (hard handoff) or retained (soft handoff).
- MS is informed via the **handover command** message.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **hysteresis margin $\Delta H$** is the *single most important parameter* in handoff design. A small $\Delta H$ causes ping-pong; a large $\Delta H$ causes poor signal quality *before* handoff.

---

## 2.2 Handoff Decision Algorithm — Mathematical Form

Let:
- $S_{old}$ = RSS from current BS (dBm)
- $S_{new}$ = RSS from candidate BS (dBm)
- $T$ = RSS threshold (dBm)
- $\Delta H$ = Hysteresis margin (dB)

**Handoff is triggered if and only if:**

$$S_{old} < T \quad \text{AND} \quad S_{new} > S_{old} + \Delta H$$

This is the **classic relative-RSS-with-hysteresis algorithm** used in AMPS, GSM, and most KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.3 Interference Analysis — The Co-Channel Reuse Ratio

### 2.3.1 Co-Channel Reuse Ratio ($Q$)

$$Q = \frac{D}{R}$$

where:
- $D$ = distance between centers of two co-channel cells
- $R$ = cell radius

### 2.3.2 Cluster Size ($N$)

For a hexagonal cellular layout, $Q$ and $N$ are related by:

$$Q = \sqrt{3N} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad N = \frac{Q^2}{3}$$

Valid values of $N$ for hexagonal cells: $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ for non-negative integers $i, j$. The first few values: $1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, \ldots$

### 2.3.3 Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR or C/I)

The signal power at distance $R$ from a BS (path-loss exponent $n$, typically $n = 4$ for urban macrocells):

$$C = P_0 \cdot R^{-n}$$

For **first-tier co-channel interferers** (6 in hexagonal layout, equally spaced at distance $D$):

$$I = 6 \cdot P_0 \cdot D^{-n}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{R^{-n}}{6 \cdot D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{-n} = \frac{Q^n}{6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical KTU formula** for SIR in a hexagonal cellular system with **6 first-tier interferers**.

---

## 2.4 Capacity of Cellular Systems

### 2.4.1 Voice Channel Capacity (Number of Users per Cell)

Let:
- $B_T$ = total spectrum allocated to the system (Hz)
- $B_c$ = bandwidth per channel (Hz, e.g., 30 kHz in AMPS, 200 kHz in GSM)
- $N$ = cluster size

Number of **channels per cell**:

$$C_{cell} = \frac{B_T / B_c}{N} = \frac{M}{N}$$

where $M = B_T / B_c$ = total number of channels in the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** As $N$ **increases** → CCI **decreases** (better quality) but $C_{cell}$ **decreases** (fewer users per cell). As $N$ **decreases** → capacity **increases** but CCI becomes severe.

### 2.4.2 Minimum $N$ from $(C/I)_{min}$ Constraint

From $C/I \geq (C/I)_{min}$:

$$\frac{Q^n}{6} \geq \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min}$$

$$Q \geq \left(6 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min}\right)^{1/n}$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{Q^2}{3} = \frac{1}{3} \left[6 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min}\right]^{2/n}$$

---

## 2.5 Trunking and Grade of Service (GoS)

### 2.5.1 Erlang B Formula (Blocked Calls Cleared)

For $C$ channels offered $A$ Erlangs of traffic, the **probability of blocking** $P_B$ is:

$$P_B = \frac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$$

This is the **Erlang B formula** — *the* standard for KTU GoS problems.

### 2.5.2 Capacity with GoS

**Total system capacity** (number of subscribers supported):

$$S_{sys} = C_{cell} \cdot \text{(number of cells)} \cdot \text{subscribers per channel}$$

More rigorously, using Erlang B inversion: given $P_B = 2\%$, $A_{per\,user}$ Erlangs, and $C$ channels/cell, supported users per cell $= C \cdot A_{per\,channel\,user}$.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = D/R$ | Co-channel reuse ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $N = Q^2/3$ | Cluster size (hexagonal) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | Valid cluster sizes | dimensionless |
| 4 | $C/I = Q^n/6$ | SIR (6 first-tier interferers, $n$ = path loss) | dB |
| 5 | $C/I = Q^n / (2(n-1))$ | SIR for $n$-tier of 6 interferers (approx) | dB |
| 6 | $N_{min} = \frac{1}{3}[6 \cdot (C/I)_{min}]^{2/n}$ | Min cluster size from interference | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{cell} = M/N = B_T/(B_c \cdot N)$ | Channels per cell | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_{sys} = C_{cell} \cdot K$ | Total system capacity (K = # cells) | channels |
| 9 | $P_B = \frac{A^C/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k/k!}$ | Erlang B blocking probability | dimensionless |
| 10 | $A = \lambda \cdot H$ | Offered traffic (calls/hour × avg holding time) | Erlangs |
| 11 | $S_{old} < T$ AND $S_{new} > S_{old} + \Delta H$ | Handoff trigger (with hysteresis) | dBm, dB |
| 12 | $\text{Capacity Increase} = N_1/N_2$ | Capacity gain from cluster size reduction | ratio |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams — "QNCB":** $Q \rightarrow N \rightarrow C_{cell} \rightarrow B$ (blocking). This is the *typical numerical chain* examiners test.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Handoff algorithms** form the heart of every 4G/5G **X2/S1 handover** procedure in LTE/5G NR.
- **Co-channel interference analysis** is what determines 5G NR's **frequency reuse factor of 1** (universal frequency reuse), made possible by advanced receivers.
- **Erlang B** is the language spoken by **telecom traffic engineers** for switch dimensioning worldwide.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Co-Channel Interference with 6 First-Tier Interferers

### Assumptions
1. Hexagonal cell layout.
2. All cells transmit equal power $P_0$.
3. Path loss follows the inverse power law: $P_r(d) = P_0 \cdot d^{-n}$, where $n$ is the path-loss exponent.
4. Only the **first tier** of 6 co-channel cells contributes dominant interference (higher tiers are negligible for typical $n \geq 3$).
5. Mobile is at the cell boundary (worst case) at distance $R$ from the **serving BS** and at distance $D$ from each of the 6 interfering BSs.

### Step 1: Received Carrier Power at the Mobile

The mobile is at the cell edge, so the carrier signal has traveled a distance $R$ from the serving BS:

$$C = P_0 \cdot R^{-n}$$

### Step 2: Received Interference Power from One Co-Channel BS

Each of the 6 co-channel BSs is at distance $D$ from the mobile (approximation — assumes mobile is equidistant from all 6, valid at cell center for hexagonal symmetry; worst case is slightly different but KTU standard uses $D$):

$$I_{one} = P_0 \cdot D^{-n}$$

### Step 3: Total Interference from 6 Co-Channel BSs

Assuming interference powers add (linear domain):

$$I = 6 \cdot P_0 \cdot D^{-n}$$

### Step 4: Compute C/I Ratio

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{P_0 \cdot R^{-n}}{6 \cdot P_0 \cdot D^{-n}}$$

Cancel $P_0$:

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{R^{-n}}{6 \cdot D^{-n}}$$

### Step 5: Rearrange Using Reuse Ratio

Recall $Q = D/R$, so $D = Q \cdot R$, which gives $D^{-n} = (Q \cdot R)^{-n} = Q^{-n} \cdot R^{-n}$.

Substituting:

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{R^{-n}}{6 \cdot Q^{-n} \cdot R^{-n}}$$

The $R^{-n}$ terms cancel:

$$\boxed{\frac{C}{I} = \frac{Q^n}{6}}$$

This is the **KPI (Key Performance Indicator)** for any cellular interference problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Cluster Size $N_{min}$

### Starting Point

For acceptable voice quality, the system requires:

$$\frac{C}{I} \geq \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min}$$

Substituting the SIR formula:

$$\frac{Q^n}{6} \geq \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min}$$

### Solve for $Q$

$$Q^n \geq 6 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min}$$

$$Q \geq \left[6 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min}\right]^{1/n}$$

### Convert to $N$

Using $Q^2 = 3N$:

$$3N \geq \left[6 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min}\right]^{2/n}$$

$$\boxed{N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{1}{3} \left[6 \cdot \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min}\right]^{2/n} \right\rceil}$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the next valid cluster size in the sequence $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, \ldots\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap:** $N$ must be a **valid cluster size** from $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$. So you cannot simply round up to the next integer. For example, $N = 5$ is **invalid**; the next valid value is $N = 7$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: System Capacity Comparison

### Setup
- System 1: cluster size $N_1$ (e.g., $N_1 = 7$, the AMPS standard).
- System 2: cluster size $N_2$ (e.g., $N_2 = 4$).

### Step 1: Channels per Cell

$$C_{cell,1} = \frac{M}{N_1}, \quad C_{cell,2} = \frac{M}{N_2}$$

### Step 2: Capacity Ratio

$$\frac{C_{sys,2}}{C_{sys,1}} = \frac{C_{cell,2}}{C_{cell,1}} = \frac{M/N_2}{M/N_1} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

So the **capacity gain factor** of System 2 over System 1 is:

$$\boxed{\text{Capacity Gain} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}}$$

### Worked Example (KTU Classic)

Suppose $N_1 = 7$ (original), and we reduce to $N_2 = 4$.

$$\text{Capacity Gain} = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75$$

→ **75% capacity increase** — but at the cost of higher interference (must verify C/I constraint!).

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 1: Interference-Based $N_{min}$ (14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A cellular operator uses a path-loss exponent $n = 4$. The minimum acceptable C/I ratio is **18 dB**. Determine the **minimum cluster size** for a hexagonal cellular system, and hence the **capacity per cell** if total spectrum is **25 MHz** and channel bandwidth is **30 kHz**.

### Step 1: Convert C/I from dB to Linear

$$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min, dB} = 18 \, \text{dB}$$

$$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min} = 10^{18/10} = 10^{1.8} = 63.10$$

### Step 2: Compute $Q_{min}$

$$Q_{min} = \left[6 \cdot 63.10\right]^{1/4} = \left[378.57\right]^{0.25}$$

Compute step by step:

$$\ln(378.57) = 5.936$$

$$\frac{5.936}{4} = 1.484$$

$$e^{1.484} = 4.41$$

So $Q_{min} = 4.41$.

### Step 3: Compute $N_{min}$

$$N_{min} = \frac{Q_{min}^2}{3} = \frac{(4.41)^2}{3} = \frac{19.45}{3} = 6.48$$

### Step 4: Snap to Valid Cluster Size

From the valid sequence $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, \ldots\}$:

$$\boxed{N = 7}$$

### Step 5: Compute Channels per Cell

$$M = \frac{B_T}{B_c} = \frac{25{,}000 \, \text{kHz}}{30 \, \text{kHz}} = 833.33 \Rightarrow 833 \text{ channels}$$

$$C_{cell} = \frac{833}{7} = 119 \text{ channels per cell}$$

### Verification of C/I for $N=7$

$$Q = \sqrt{3 \cdot 7} = \sqrt{21} = 4.583$$

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(4.583)^4}{6} = \frac{441.6}{6} = 73.6 \quad (= 18.67 \, \text{dB}) \geq 18 \, \text{dB} \, \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Marking Insight:** The verification step at the end is *not optional* — it's the step most students skip and lose 1–2 marks for.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 2: Erlang B Blocking (10-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A cell is allocated **30 channels**. Each subscriber generates **0.04 Erlangs** of traffic during the busy hour. Using the **Erlang B formula**, determine:
> (a) The blocking probability if **20 subscribers** are served.
> (b) The number of subscribers supported at **2% blocking probability**.

### Part (a): Blocking with 20 subscribers

Total offered traffic:

$$A = 20 \times 0.04 = 0.8 \text{ Erlangs}$$

For $C = 30$ channels and $A = 0.8$ Erlangs, the load is very light. Most tables and software give $P_B \approx 0$ (essentially 0% blocking).

For full credit, we can show using Erlang B:

$$P_B = \frac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$$

The numerator is negligible compared to the denominator (which is dominated by the $k=0$ term = 1). So:

$$P_B \approx 0 \quad (\text{essentially zero blocking})$$

### Part (b): Subscribers at 2% Blocking

From **Erlang B tables** (or iterative computation): for $C = 30$ channels and $P_B = 0.02$, the offered traffic $A \approx 21.9$ Erlangs.

Therefore, supported subscribers:

$$N_{sub} = \frac{A}{A_{per\,user}} = \frac{21.9}{0.04} = 547.5 \Rightarrow 547 \text{ subscribers}$$

$$\boxed{N_{sub} \approx 547 \text{ subscribers per cell}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** For numerical Erlang problems, students are expected to *show the formula* and *reference the table value*, then compute. Losing marks here is usually due to **forgetting to convert between Erlangs and subscribers**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Handoff Analysis

| Parameter | Hard Handoff | Soft Handoff |
|---|---|---|
| Connection type | Break-before-make | Make-before-break |
| Active connections | **1** (only one BS) | **2+** (multiple BSs) |
| Used in | GSM, LTE (X2) | IS-95, WCDMA |
| Latency | Lower | Slightly higher (combining) |
| Handoff failure rate | Higher (transient gap) | Lower (no gap) |
| Resource usage | Efficient | Higher (duplicate) |
| Macro-diversity gain | None | Yes (combining gain) |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (Rake receiver) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Handoff Process Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[MS in active call within cell] --> stepB[MS measures RSS from serving BS continuously]
    stepB --> stepC[MS measures RSS from 6 neighboring BSs]
    stepC --> stepD{RSS_serving less than threshold T?}
    stepD -- No --> stepE[Continue with current BS connection]
    stepE --> stepB
    stepD -- Yes --> stepF{Any neighbor BS has RSS greater than RSS_serving plus hysteresis delta H?}
    stepF -- No --> stepE
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG[MS reports candidate BSs to MSC via SACCH]
    stepG --> stepH[MSC evaluates candidate BSs and selects target]
    stepH --> stepI[Target BS allocates a new channel]
    stepI --> stepJ[MSC sends Handover Command to MS]
    stepJ --> stepK[Hard handoff: break old link then make new link]
    stepJ --> stepL[Soft handoff: make new link then break old link]
    stepK --> stepM[MS continues call on new BS]
    stepL --> stepM
    stepM --> stepN[Old channel released back to pool]
```

## 4.2 Co-Channel Interference Geometry (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph hexa[Hexagonal Cell Layout - Cluster Size N=7]
        cell1[Cell 1 - Mobile MS at edge] --> cell2[Co-channel Cell 2 distance D from cell 1]
        cell1 --> cell3[Co-channel Cell 3 distance D]
        cell1 --> cell4[Co-channel Cell 4 distance D]
        cell1 --> cell5[Co-channel Cell 5 distance D]
        cell1 --> cell6[Co-channel Cell 6 distance D]
        cell1 --> cell7[Co-channel Cell 7 distance D]
    end
    cell1 -- Signal path loss exponent n=4 --> formula[SIR equals Q raised to n divided by 6]
    cell2 --> formula
    cell3 --> formula
    cell4 --> formula
    cell5 --> formula
    cell6 --> formula
    cell7 --> formula
    formula --> result[Acceptable voice quality if SIR greater than C by I min]
```

## 4.3 System Capacity Chain (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Total Spectrum BT] --> start2[Total Channels M equals BT divided by Bc]
    start2 --> start3{Cluster Size N}
    start3 --> start4[Channels per cell equals M divided by N]
    start4 --> start5[GoS via Erlang B formula]
    start5 --> start6[Blocking Probability PB]
    start6 --> start7[Supported users per cell]
    start4 --> start8[SIR equals Q raised to n divided by 6]
    start3 --> start8
    start8 --> start9{Is SIR greater than C by I min?}
    start9 -- Yes --> start10[Design feasible]
    start9 -- No --> start11[Increase N and repeat]
```

## 4.4 Handoff Decision Algorithm State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Connected_To_Serving_BS
    Connected_To_Serving_BS --> Monitoring: Continuous RSS measurement
    Monitoring --> Connected_To_Serving_BS: RSS_serving greater than or equal to T
    Monitoring --> Candidate_Detected: Some neighbor has higher RSS
    Candidate_Detected --> Monitoring: Hysteresis not exceeded
    Candidate_Detected --> Handoff_Triggered: RSS_neighbor greater than RSS_serving plus delta H
    Handoff_Triggered --> Handoff_In_Progress: Channel reserved in target BS
    Handoff_In_Progress --> Connected_To_New_BS: Old link released
    Connected_To_New_BS --> [*]
```
