# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define handoff. List any **four** reasons why a handoff is initiated in a cellular system. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Handoff (or Handover)** is the process of transferring an ongoing call or data session of a Mobile Station (MS) from one cell/base station/channel to another without interrupting the active connection.

**Four reasons for handoff:**

1. **Signal strength degradation** — RSS from serving BS falls below threshold.
2. **Co-channel interference rise** — C/I ratio drops below the minimum acceptable limit.
3. **Cell load balancing** — current cell is congested; adjacent cell has spare capacity.
4. **Distance threshold** — MS has moved more than the maximum tolerable distance from the serving BS.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **co-channel interference** and **adjacent channel interference**. Mention the parameter that controls each. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Co-Channel Interference (CCI) | Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Interference from cells using the **same frequency** | Interference from **nearby frequencies** in the adjacent band |
| **Source** | Frequency reuse in non-adjacent cells | Imperfect filters, power leakage, sideband spillover |
| **Controlling parameter** | **Co-channel reuse ratio $Q = D/R$** (and cluster size $N$) | **Channel spacing** and **filter selectivity** |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A: Handoff Strategies and Interference (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | KTU University Exam - May 2024 Adaptive]**

**(a)** Explain the **handoff process** in a cellular system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **hysteresis margin** in preventing the ping-pong effect. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hexagonal cellular system has a path-loss exponent of **4** and a minimum acceptable C/I ratio of **15 dB**. Total spectrum allotted is **20 MHz** with a channel bandwidth of **30 kHz**. Calculate:
- (i) The minimum cluster size $N$.
- (ii) The number of channels per cell.
- (iii) The co-channel reuse ratio $Q$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Handoff Process and Hysteresis Margin (7 Marks)

The **handoff process** consists of three phases:

1. **Measurement Phase:** The Mobile Station (MS) continuously monitors the Received Signal Strength (RSS) from the **serving BS** and periodically scans the **six neighboring BSs** (in a hexagonal layout). The RSS measurements are reported to the Mobile Switching Center (MSC) via the Slow Associated Control Channel (SACCH) every **480 ms** in GSM.

2. **Decision Phase:** The MSC compares:
   - $RSS_{serving}$ with threshold $T$.
   - $RSS_{candidate}$ with $RSS_{serving} + \Delta H$, where $\Delta H$ is the **hysteresis margin** (typically 3–6 dB).
   - Handoff is triggered **only** if both conditions are met.

3. **Execution Phase:** The MSC reserves a channel in the target cell, sends a **Handover Command** to the MS, and the call is transferred.

**Role of Hysteresis Margin:**

The **ping-pong effect** occurs at cell boundaries where the MS experiences nearly equal signal strength from two BSs, causing rapid, unnecessary handoffs back and forth. This wastes network resources and causes call quality fluctuations.

**Hysteresis margin** $\Delta H$ introduces a *deliberate dead-band* — the candidate BS must exceed the serving BS by at least $\Delta H$ dB before handoff occurs. This:
- Prevents rapid oscillation.
- Slightly degrades signal quality *temporarily* (the trade-off).
- Reduces unnecessary signaling load on the MSC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — KTU Examiner's View:** Students often **forget to mention the role of the MSC** in the decision phase. Handoff is **not** decided by the MS alone; the network (MSC) makes the final call. Losing 1 mark for omitting MSC's role is extremely common.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $n = 4$
- $(C/I)_{min} = 15$ dB
- $B_T = 20$ MHz $= 20{,}000$ kHz
- $B_c = 30$ kHz

**Step 1:** Convert C/I to linear scale.

$$\left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{min} = 10^{15/10} = 10^{1.5} = 31.62$$

**Step 2:** Compute $Q_{min}$.

$$Q_{min} = \left[6 \cdot 31.62\right]^{1/4} = \left[189.74\right]^{0.25}$$

Take natural log: $\ln(189.74) = 5.246$. Divide by 4: $1.3115$. Exponential: $e^{1.3115} = 3.71$.

$$Q_{min} = 3.71$$

**Step 3:** Compute $N_{min}$.

$$N_{min} = \frac{Q_{min}^2}{3} = \frac{(3.71)^2}{3} = \frac{13.76}{3} = 4.59$$

**Step 4:** Snap to next valid cluster size.

The valid hexagonal cluster sizes are: $\{1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, \ldots\}$. The next valid value greater than 4.59 is:

$$\boxed{N = 7}$$

**Step 5:** Channels per cell.

$$M = \frac{B_T}{B_c} = \frac{20{,}000}{30} = 666.67 \approx 666 \text{ channels}$$

$$C_{cell} = \frac{M}{N} = \frac{666}{7} = 95.14 \approx 95 \text{ channels per cell}$$

**Step 6:** Reuse ratio $Q$ for $N = 7$.

$$Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} = 4.583$$

**Step 7:** Verification of C/I.

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{Q^n}{6} = \frac{(4.583)^4}{6} = \frac{441.6}{6} = 73.6 = 18.67 \, \text{dB}$$

Since $18.67 \, \text{dB} \geq 15 \, \text{dB}$ ✓, the design is **acceptable**.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $N = 7$
- (ii) $C_{cell} = 95$ channels
- (iii) $Q = 4.583$

---

### Question B: System Capacity and Erlang B (Alternative Choice) (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Adaptive]**

**(a)** With the help of the **Erlang B formula**, explain how the **Grade of Service (GoS)** is determined in a cellular system. A cell has **40 channels** and receives an offered traffic of **30 Erlangs**. Find the **blocking probability**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **co-channel interference**. Derive the **signal-to-interference ratio (SIR)** expression for a hexagonal cellular system considering the first tier of 6 co-channel cells. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Erlang B and GoS (7 Marks)

**Grade of Service (GoS)** is defined as the probability that a user's call will be **blocked** (denied a channel) because all channels are occupied. The most commonly used model is **Erlang B** (Blocked Calls Cleared — blocked calls are *not* queued; they are dropped).

**Erlang B Formula:**

$$P_B = \frac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$$

where:
- $A$ = offered traffic (in Erlangs)
- $C$ = total number of channels
- $P_B$ = blocking probability = GoS

**Numerical Computation:**

Given $C = 40$ channels, $A = 30$ Erlangs.

We compute the terms of the recursion. The Erlang B formula can be computed recursively:

$$P_B(k) = \frac{A \cdot P_B(k-1)}{k + A \cdot P_B(k-1)}$$

with $P_B(0) = 1$.

Iterating yields $P_B(40) \approx 0.0143$ (about 1.43%).

$$\boxed{P_B \approx 0.0143 \text{ (i.e., } 1.43\% \text{ blocking)}}$$

For KTU answer purposes, students typically reference the **Erlang B table**:

> "From Erlang B tables, for $C = 40$ and $A = 30$ Erlangs, the blocking probability $P_B \approx 0.014$."

#### Part (b) — SIR Derivation (7 Marks)

**Co-channel interference** is the interference caused by radio transmitters using the **same frequency channel** in cells that are spatially separated (according to the frequency reuse plan).

**Assumptions for the SIR derivation:**
1. Hexagonal cell layout with omni-directional antennas.
2. All cells transmit equal power $P_0$.
3. Path loss follows inverse power law: $P_r(d) \propto d^{-n}$.
4. Mobile is at the cell boundary (worst case), distance $R$ from serving BS.
5. Six first-tier co-channel BSs, each at distance $D$ from the mobile.

**Derivation:**

Received carrier power:

$$C = P_0 \cdot R^{-n}$$

Received interference from each of the 6 co-channel BSs:

$$I_i = P_0 \cdot D^{-n}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, 6$$

Total interference:

$$I = \sum_{i=1}^{6} I_i = 6 \cdot P_0 \cdot D^{-n}$$

Signal-to-Interference Ratio:

$$\frac{C}{I} = \frac{P_0 \cdot R^{-n}}{6 \cdot P_0 \cdot D^{-n}} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^{-n} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot Q^{-n} = \frac{Q^n}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{C}{I} = \frac{Q^n}{6}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — KTU Examiner's View:** Students frequently **omit the assumptions** and get partial credit. Also, students sometimes write $Q^n / 6$ as $Q/n$ — a clear loss of 2 marks. Show the **power-law** derivation explicitly.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP REASONS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS ON THIS TOPIC:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert dB to linear scale** when substituting $(C/I)_{min}$ — this *alone* causes 2–3 mark deductions across a problem.
> 2. **Using invalid cluster sizes** like $N = 5, 6, 8, 10, 11$ — must be from the set $\{i^2 + ij + j^2\}$.
> 3. **Omitting the verification step** at the end of a numerical — always check that the final $N$ gives $C/I \geq (C/I)_{min}$.
> 4. **Confusing hard and soft handoff** definitions — Hard = break-before-make; Soft = make-before-break. The "s" in "soft" does NOT mean "simple"!
> 5. **Erlang B numerics** — students often divide traffic incorrectly (Erlangs per user vs. total Erlangs).
> 6. **In diagrams**, students label $D$ and $R$ incorrectly — $D$ is **co-channel distance**, $R$ is **cell radius** (always $D > R$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Handoff** = transferring active call between cells/channels without interruption.
- ✅ **Hard handoff** = break-before-make; **Soft handoff** = make-before-break (CDMA).
- ✅ **Hysteresis margin $\Delta H$** prevents ping-pong effect at cell borders.
- ✅ **Co-channel reuse ratio** $Q = D/R$, **Cluster size** $N = Q^2/3$.
- ✅ Valid hexagonal cluster sizes: $N = 1, 3, 4, 7, 9, 12, 13, 16, 19, 21, \ldots$ — must satisfy $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$.
- ✅ **Co-channel SIR formula**: $C/I = Q^n/6$ (6 first-tier interferers).
- ✅ **Minimum $N$** to meet interference: $N_{min} = \frac{1}{3}[6 \cdot (C/I)_{min}]^{2/n}$.
- ✅ **Channels per cell**: $C_{cell} = B_T / (B_c \cdot N)$.
- ✅ **Capacity gain** from $N_1 \to N_2$ is $N_1/N_2$ (but verify C/I constraint).
- ✅ **Erlang B formula** gives blocking probability for offered traffic $A$ and $C$ channels: $P_B = \frac{A^C/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^C A^k/k!}$.
- ✅ **Offered traffic** $A = \lambda \cdot H$ where $\lambda$ = call arrival rate, $H$ = mean holding time.
- ✅ Typical **path-loss exponent**: $n = 2$ (free space), $n = 3$ to $4$ (urban), $n = 4$ to $5$ (heavily built-up).
- ✅ Typical $(C/I)_{min}$: **18 dB** (analog AMPS), **9–12 dB** (GSM with frequency hopping), **7 dB** (CDMA via processing gain).
- ✅ **Decision metric** for handoff: $S_{old} < T$ **AND** $S_{new} > S_{old} + \Delta H$.
- ✅ Always **convert dB ↔ linear** explicitly in numerical problems; never skip this step.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trunking and Grade of Service, Improving Coverage and Capacity: Cell Splitting, Sectoring, Microcells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module Module 3: Wireless Communication Systems
> - **Topic:** Trunking and Grade of Service, Improving Coverage and Capacity: Cell Splitting, Sectoring, Microcells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Trunking and Grade of Service (GOS)

### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trunking** is a fundamental concept in telecommunication engineering that allows a **limited number of channels** (or circuits) to be shared among a **large number of users** on a demand basis, rather than dedicating a fixed channel to each user. It is the radio resource allocation strategy that exploits the statistical behavior of users to maximize channel utilization.

**Grade of Service (GOS)** is the **probability** that a **user's call (or connection request) is blocked or delayed** because all available channels (trunks) in the system are busy at the moment of the request. Mathematically:

$$GOS = P(\text{Blocking}) = P(\text{All channels busy})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Telephony Metric — Erlang (E):**
> - **1 Erlang (E)** = the continuous use of one voice channel for **60 minutes**, OR
> - **1 Erlang** = the traffic intensity generated by a user who, on average, makes **1 call of 1 hour duration** in 1 hour, OR
> - **1 Erlang** = the average number of simultaneous calls per hour = **(Number of calls per hour × Average call duration in minutes) / 60**

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Reception Desk" Model

Imagine a small hotel reception desk with **3 staff members** (channels/trunks) handling guests. On average, **6 guests arrive every hour** and each takes **30 minutes** to be served.

- **Traffic Offered** = 6 × 0.5 = **3 Erlangs**
- If all 3 staff are busy and a 7th guest arrives, they have to **wait (or be denied service)**.
- The probability of this happening is the **Grade of Service**.

If instead of 3 staff, we have 5 staff, the chance of any guest being denied/waiting is **drastically reduced** — this is the essence of **trunking efficiency**: more shared resources → better service to more users.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:** In cellular systems, a typical macrocell might support only ~50–60 voice channels (Erlang B capacity), but serve hundreds of subscribers because **not all users are active simultaneously**. This statistical multiplexing is what makes cellular telephony economically viable.

---

## 1.2 Cell Splitting

### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cell Splitting** is the process of **sub-dividing a congested macrocell into smaller cells (called microcells)**, each with its own base station and a corresponding reduction in the transmitted power and antenna height. It is a systematic capacity-expansion technique that increases the **number of channels per unit area (traffic density)** without increasing the allocated frequency spectrum.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Franchise" Model

Picture a single, very large, overcrowded restaurant. To serve more customers without changing the recipe (spectrum), the owner **splits it into 4 smaller restaurants** — each serving the same menu but to fewer customers, with smaller kitchens (lower transmit power) and shorter walking distances. **Total customer capacity quadruples** in the same geographical region.

---

## 1.3 Sectoring

### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sectoring** is a technique used to **reduce co-channel interference (CCI)** and **increase system capacity** by replacing the single omnidirectional antenna at the base station with several **directional antennas**, each illuminating only a specific angular sector of the cell (e.g., 120°, 60°). It achieves interference reduction by concentrating the radiation pattern.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Spotlight in a Dark Room" Model

Instead of a single bulb that lights up an entire stage (omnidirectional), imagine **three spotlights (120° each)** pointing in different directions. The same total light energy is now **focused into three narrow beams**, making each beam **brighter and more focused** in its own area. In cellular terms: **less interference leaks into neighboring cells using the same frequencies**.

---

## 1.4 Microcells

### Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microcells** are **low-power, low-height (typically mounted below rooftop level) base stations** that cover a very small geographical area (radius **≤ 1 km**, often 100–500 m). They are deployed to **fill coverage holes, offload traffic from macrocells, and provide high-capacity service in dense urban zones** (streets, indoor malls, tunnels).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Local Convenience Store" Model

If a **macrocell** is like a **big supermarket** serving an entire neighborhood, a **microcell** is like a **small convenience store** on every street corner — quicker access, smaller area, lower power, but the same essential service. They relieve the burden on the bigger infrastructure.

---

## 1.5 Why These Topics Are Crucial in KTU Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern Alert:**
> Questions on **Erlang B (with table values)** are **guaranteed 7–14 mark** items in Module 3.
> The triad of **Cell Splitting + Sectoring + Microcells** is a **favourite 14-mark question** either as a full essay or a 2-part numerical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundations of Trunking Theory

### 2.1.1 Traffic Intensity and Erlang Load

A cellular system has **C channels** total. Each user generates a **mean arrival rate λ** (calls/hour) and a **mean holding time H** (minutes). The **traffic offered per user** is:

$$A_u = \lambda \cdot H \quad \text{[Erlangs]}$$

If there are **U users** in the system, the **total offered traffic** is:

$$A = U \cdot A_u = U \cdot \lambda \cdot H$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Grade of Service (GOS)** is determined by three variables: **A, C, and the queuing discipline**. Two classical models are used:
> - **Erlang B (Blocked Calls Cleared — BCC):** Blocked calls are **immediately dropped** (e.g., voice calls).
> - **Erlang C (Blocked Calls Delayed — BCD):** Blocked calls are **queued** until a channel becomes free (e.g., data packets).

### 2.1.2 The Erlang B Formula (Most Common in KTU)

For a system with **C channels** and **A Erlangs of offered traffic**, with the assumption of **infinite sources, Poisson arrivals, and blocked-calls-cleared (BCC)** behavior:

$$B(C, A) = \dfrac{A^C / C!}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$$

$$GOS = B(C, A) = \dfrac{\text{Traffic carried under blocking}}{\text{Engineering}} \cdot \text{(probability all C servers busy)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of Erlang B:**
> 1. **Recursive Nature:** $B(0, A) = 1$, and $B(C, A) = \dfrac{A \cdot B(C-1, A)}{C + A \cdot B(C-1, A)}$
> 2. **Monotonic in C:** Increasing channels always **decreases** GOS for a fixed A.
> 3. **GOS is independent of time** — it represents a long-run steady-state probability.

### 2.1.3 Trunking Efficiency

$$\eta_{trunk} = \dfrac{\text{Traffic Carried}}{\text{Number of Channels}} = \dfrac{A(1 - B(C,A))}{C}$$

This is **not the channel utilization** but rather a measure of how effectively the channels are used. A trunking efficiency > 80% is considered very high.

---

## 2.2 Improving Coverage and Capacity

The **fundamental challenge** in cellular system design is:
- **Reuse distance (D)** is **fixed** by the co-channel interference constraint.
- **Number of cells per cluster (N)** is **fixed** by the frequency reuse plan.
- As subscribers grow, **traffic per cell exceeds capacity** → we need **capacity enhancement**.

The **three classical techniques** (the heart of this module) are:

### 2.2.1 Cell Splitting

**Mechanism:** The radius of each cell is **halved** (R → R/2), which causes a **4× increase in the number of cells** in the same area (since area ∝ R²).

**Capacity Multiplication Factor:**

$$N_{\text{new}} = 4 \times N_{\text{old}}$$

If the original cluster size is N, and we maintain the same N, then **total channels per area = 4× original**.

**Transmit Power Adjustment:**

If the new cell radius is half the old, the new transmit power must be reduced to maintain the same received power at the (smaller) cell boundary:

$$P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^\gamma$$

where **γ = path loss exponent** (typically **3 to 4** in urban environments, **2 to 3** in free space).

**Co-channel Reuse Ratio (Q):** Unchanged by splitting itself, since Q depends on cluster size N.

$$Q = \dfrac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}$$

**S/I Ratio** (omnidirectional, first tier of interferers i₀ = 6):

$$\dfrac{S}{I} = \dfrac{(Q)^\gamma}{i_0} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^\gamma}{6}$$

### 2.2.2 Sectoring

**Mechanism:** Replace one omnidirectional antenna with **n directional antennas**, each covering **360°/n** of the cell.

| Sectoring Type | Sector Angle | n |
|----------------|--------------|---|
| 3-sector | **120°** | 3 |
| 4-sector | **90°** | 4 |
| 6-sector | **60°** | 6 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Point:** Sectoring does **NOT** change Q or N. It only **reduces the number of first-tier interferers (i₀)** because the directional antenna's main beam only "sees" some co-channel cells, not all 6.

**Effective Interferers:**
- **3-sector (120°):** i₀ = **2** (only 2 of the 6 co-channel cells illuminate this sector)
- **6-sector (60°):** i₀ = **1**
- **Omnidirectional:** i₀ = **6**

**New S/I ratio (3-sector case):**

$$\left(\dfrac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{3-sector}} = \dfrac{(Q)^\gamma}{2} = \dfrac{(\sqrt{3N})^\gamma}{2}$$

**Capacity gain factor (vs. omnidirectional):**

$$\text{Gain} = \dfrac{(S/I)_{\text{sectored}}}{(S/I)_{\text{omni}}} = \dfrac{6}{i_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off:** Sectoring **increases trunking efficiency** (channels are reused more often within a cell) but **decreases trunking efficiency per antenna sector** (less averaging, more variance, requires more handoffs).

### 2.2.3 Microcells

**Key characteristics:**
- **Antenna height:** Below rooftop (often **5–15 m**, lamp-post level)
- **Cell radius:** **0.1 – 1 km** (often **200–500 m**)
- **Transmit power:** **0.1 – 5 W** (vs. macrocell 5–40 W)
- **Path loss exponent:** **γ ≈ 2 to 4** (highly environment-dependent)

**Why Microcells?**
1. **Capacity Boost:** Reduces R → increases channels/unit area
2. **Coverage of Dead Spots:** Fills "holes" in macrocell coverage (tunnels, indoor)
3. **Lower Power Consumption:** Better for handset battery and EMF compliance
4. **Frequency Reuse:** Closer co-channel cells possible at lower interference

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Traffic Intensity per user | $A_u = \lambda \cdot H$ | Erlangs | λ in calls/hr, H in hours |
| 2 | Total Offered Traffic | $A = U \cdot A_u$ | Erlangs | U = number of users |
| 3 | Erlang B GOS | $B(C, A) = \frac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$ | Dimensionless | Blocked-calls-cleared |
| 4 | Erlang B Recursive | $B(C, A) = \frac{A \cdot B(C-1, A)}{C + A \cdot B(C-1, A)}$ | — | With $B(0, A) = 1$ |
| 5 | Carried Traffic | $A_c = A \cdot [1 - B(C,A)]$ | Erlangs | Useful traffic |
| 6 | Trunking Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{A(1 - B(C,A))}{C}$ | Dimensionless | Carried traffic per channel |
| 7 | Cell Splitting Capacity Factor | $N_{new} = 4 \cdot N_{old}$ | — | When R → R/2 |
| 8 | Tx Power Reduction | $P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot (R_2 / R_1)^\gamma$ | Watts | Same received power at edge |
| 9 | Co-channel Reuse Ratio | $Q = D/R = \sqrt{3N}$ | Dimensionless | Hexagonal geometry |
| 10 | S/I (Omni) | $S/I = (Q)^\gamma / 6$ | dB or ratio | 6 first-tier interferers |
| 11 | S/I (3-sector 120°) | $S/I = (Q)^\gamma / 2$ | dB or ratio | 2 effective interferers |
| 12 | S/I (6-sector 60°) | $S/I = (Q)^\gamma / 1$ | dB or ratio | 1 effective interferer |
| 13 | Minimum N from S/I req. | $N = \left[\frac{(i_0 \cdot S/I)^{1/\gamma}}{\sqrt{3}}\right]^2$ | Integer | Round up to valid N |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Erlang B Formula — Detailed Derivation

### Setup

Consider a system with **C identical channels** (servers). Call arrivals follow a **Poisson process** with mean rate **λ**, and call holding times are **exponentially distributed** with mean **1/μ** (so the mean holding time is **H = 1/μ** hours, or **60/μ** minutes).

The offered traffic load is:

$$A = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \quad \text{[Erlangs]}$$

We seek the **steady-state probability** $P(k)$ that exactly **k** of the C channels are busy at any instant.

### Birth–Death Process Analysis

In a **Markov Birth–Death process**, the steady-state probabilities satisfy the **detailed balance equation**:

$$P(k) \cdot \lambda_k = P(k+1) \cdot \mu_{k+1}$$

For our trunk:
- **Birth rate** (new call arrival) = $\lambda$ (constant, independent of state)
- **Death rate** (call completion) when $k$ channels busy = $k \cdot \mu$

Therefore:

$$P(k) \cdot \lambda = P(k+1) \cdot (k+1) \cdot \mu$$

Solving recursively starting from $P(0)$:

$$P(1) = P(0) \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = P(0) \cdot A$$

$$P(2) = P(1) \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2\mu} = P(0) \cdot \frac{A^2}{2!}$$

In general:

$$P(k) = P(0) \cdot \frac{A^k}{k!}$$

### Normalization

The sum of all probabilities must equal 1:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{C} P(k) = 1 \implies P(0) \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{C} \frac{A^k}{k!} = 1$$

$$\therefore P(0) = \frac{1}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{C} \frac{A^k}{k!}}$$

### Final Erlang B Result

The probability that **all C channels are busy** (the GOS / blocking probability) is:

$$B(C, A) = P(C) = \frac{A^C / C!}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}$$

$$\boxed{\,GOS = B(C, A) = \frac{A^C / C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k / k!}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Checks:**
> - When $A = 0$ (no traffic): $B(C, 0) = 0$ ✓ (nobody blocked)
> - When $C \to \infty$: $B(\infty, A) = 0$ ✓ (infinite channels → no blocking)
> - When $A \to \infty$ with $C$ fixed: $B \to 1$ ✓ (overload → everyone blocked)

---

## 3.2 Cell Splitting — Capacity Multiplication Derivation

### Geometric Basis

A **hexagonal cell** has area:

$$A_{\text{cell}} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot R^2$$

where R is the cell radius (vertex-to-center distance).

### Original Configuration

- **Number of cells** in the service area **A_total**: $N_1 = A_{total} / A_{cell,1}$
- **Channels per cell:** $K_1$
- **Total channels in area:** $K_{total,1} = N_1 \cdot K_1$

### After Splitting (R → R/2)

- New cell area: $A_{cell,2} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2} \cdot (R/2)^2 = \frac{1}{4} \cdot A_{cell,1}$
- New number of cells: $N_2 = A_{total} / A_{cell,2} = 4 \cdot N_1$

If each new cell is assigned **K_2 channels** (typically K_2 = K_1/2 since the cluster size N remains the same, but cluster occupies 4× more cells now, so the per-cell allocation scales):

- Total cluster channels remain $N \cdot K_1$ (fixed spectrum)
- With 4× more cells, the per-cell allocation must drop, BUT the **per-area** capacity multiplies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Cell splitting **multiplies the number of cells by 4** and **multiplies the total channels per unit area by 4**, even though the per-cell channel count may change. This is **the entire reason** splitting is used for capacity expansion.

### Transmit Power Adjustment Derivation

For a path loss model: $P_r = P_t \cdot K \cdot R^{-\gamma}$ (K = constant, $\gamma$ = path loss exponent).

At the **boundary of the original cell**:

$$P_{r1} = P_{t1} \cdot K \cdot R_1^{-\gamma}$$

At the **boundary of the split cell** (we want the same received power $P_{r1}$):

$$P_{r1} = P_{t2} \cdot K \cdot R_2^{-\gamma}$$

Equating:

$$P_{t1} \cdot R_1^{-\gamma} = P_{t2} \cdot R_2^{-\gamma}$$

$$P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^{\gamma}$$

For $R_2 = R_1 / 2$ and $\gamma = 4$ (urban macro):

$$P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot (1/2)^4 = \frac{P_{t1}}{16}$$

This is why **microcells use much lower power**.

---

## 3.3 Sectoring — S/I Improvement Derivation

### Co-channel Interferers in Hexagonal Layout

In a **7-cell reuse pattern (N = 7)**, the first tier contains **6 co-channel cells** at distance D from the central cell. For an **omnidirectional** central antenna, **all 6 interfere** equally.

The signal-to-interference ratio is:

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{omni}} = \frac{P_r}{\sum_{i=1}^{6} I_i} = \frac{R^{-\gamma}}{\sum_{i=1}^{6} D_i^{-\gamma}}$$

For a uniform first-tier distance $D_i = D$:

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{omni}} = \frac{R^{-\gamma}}{6 \cdot D^{-\gamma}} = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^\gamma = \frac{Q^\gamma}{6}$$

### With 3-Sector (120° Antennas)

Each directional antenna radiates only within a 120° sector. The **first-tier co-channel cells** that fall in this sector are **only 2** (geometrically, in a standard hexagonal layout, the 6 first-tier cells split into **2 in each of the three sectors**).

Therefore:

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{3-sector}} = \frac{R^{-\gamma}}{2 \cdot D^{-\gamma}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{R}\right)^\gamma = \frac{Q^\gamma}{2}$$

**Improvement factor:**

$$\frac{(S/I)_{\text{3-sector}}}{(S/I)_{\text{omni}}} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \quad \text{[3× improvement]}$$

In **dB terms:** $10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77$ dB improvement.

### With 6-Sector (60° Antennas)

Only **1** first-tier interferer remains:

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{6-sector}} = \frac{Q^\gamma}{1} = Q^\gamma$$

Improvement: $6/1 = 6$ → $\approx 7.78$ dB.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Pattern)

### Example 1 — Erlang B Blocking

**Problem:** A cell has **C = 10 channels** and an offered traffic of **A = 6 Erlangs**. Find the GOS using Erlang B.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the sum $\sum_{k=0}^{10} A^k / k!$ with $A = 6$:

| k | $A^k / k!$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 1.000 |
| 1 | 6.000 |
| 2 | 18.000 |
| 3 | 36.000 |
| 4 | 54.000 |
| 5 | 64.800 |
| 6 | 64.800 |
| 7 | 55.543 |
| 8 | 41.657 |
| 9 | 27.771 |
| 10 | 16.663 |

Sum ≈ **386.234**

Step 2: $B(10, 6) = 16.663 / 386.234 \approx 0.0431$

$$\boxed{GOS \approx 0.0431 \text{ (or about 4.31%)}}$$

### Example 2 — Cell Splitting Capacity

**Problem:** A service area has **100 macrocells** of radius **R = 4 km**. After splitting, new radius is **R' = 2 km**. Find new cell count and percentage capacity increase.

**Solution:**

- Area ratio: $(R'/R)^2 = (2/4)^2 = 1/4$
- New cell count: $100 \times 4 = 400$ cells
- Capacity increase: $(400 - 100)/100 = 300\%$

$$\boxed{\text{New cells} = 400, \quad \text{Capacity increase} = 300\%}$$

### Example 3 — S/I Comparison Omni vs 3-Sector

**Problem:** For N = 7, $\gamma = 4$, compute S/I for omnidirectional and 3-sector cases. Convert to dB.

**Solution:**

Step 1: $Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583$

Step 2: Omnidirectional: $S/I = (4.583)^4 / 6 \approx 441.5 / 6 \approx 73.58$
- In dB: $10 \log_{10}(73.58) \approx 18.67$ dB

Step 3: 3-sector: $S/I = (4.583)^4 / 2 \approx 441.5 / 2 \approx 220.75$
- In dB: $10 \log_{10}(220.75) \approx 23.44$ dB

$$\boxed{(S/I)_{\text{omni}} \approx 18.67 \text{ dB}, \quad (S/I)_{\text{3-sector}} \approx 23.44 \text{ dB}, \quad \text{Gain} \approx 4.77 \text{ dB}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Trunking System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["User Population U"] --> nodeB["Call Arrival Process\nPoisson lambda"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Trunk Pool\nC Channels Available?"}
    nodeC -->|"At least 1 free"| nodeD["Channel Allocated\nCall Connects"]
    nodeC -->|"All C busy"| nodeE{"GOS / Blocking Event\nErlang B Model"}
    nodeE -->|"BCC Policy"| nodeF["Call DROPPED\nBlocked Calls Cleared"]
    nodeE -->|"BCD Policy"| nodeG["Call QUEUED\nBlocked Calls Delayed"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["Service in Progress\nMean Holding Time 1/mu"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Channel Released\nBack to Trunk Pool"]
    nodeI --> nodeC
    nodeF --> nodeJ["GOS Counter\nP_block = B C A"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Performance Metrics\nCarried Traffic = A 1 minus B"]
```

## 4.2 Cell Splitting Process — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["MACROCELL\nRadius R\nPower P_t1\n1 Cell"] -->|"Split into 4"| nodeB["4 MICRO ZONES\nRadius R by 2\nPower P_t1 by 16"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Install BS in each\nAssign new frequencies\nUse same N"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["4 TIMES CAPACITY\nin same area"]
    nodeB -.->|"Boundary issue:\nold BS overlap"| nodeE["Handoff regions\ndefined"]
    nodeE --> nodeC
```

## 4.3 Sectoring — Interference Reduction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Omnidirectional BS\nAll 360 degrees\n6 cochannel interferers"] -->|"3 Sectors"| nodeB["3 x 120 degree\ndirectional antennas"]
    nodeA -->|"6 Sectors"| nodeC["6 x 60 degree\ndirectional antennas"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Effective interferers = 2\nS/I improvement 4.77 dB"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Effective interferers = 1\nS/I improvement 7.78 dB"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Capacity boost\nless handoff overhead"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

## 4.4 Microcell Deployment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Urban Service Area"] --> nodeB["MACROCELL LAYER\nHigh power BS\nR = 1 to 35 km\nRoof top antennas"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["MICROCELL LAYER\nLow power BS\nR less than 1 km\nStreet level antennas"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Coverage backbone\nhigh mobility users"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Hot spot capacity\npedestrian users"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Indoor coverage\nshopping malls"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["Coverage hole fill\ntunnels and subways"]
    nodeB -.->|"Handoff"| nodeH["Seamless mobility\nmacro to micro"]
    nodeC -.->|"Handoff"| nodeH
```

## 4.5 Cell Splitting — Before / After Topological View

```mermaid

        nodeA2["Cell B\nR = 4 km"]

    end

        nodeB5["B1 R=2km"]
        nodeB6["B2 R=2km"]
        nodeB7["B3 R=2km"]
        nodeB8["B4 R=2km"]
        nodeB9["C1 R=2km"]
        nodeB10["C2 R=2km"]
        nodeB11["C3 R=2km"]
        nodeB12["C4 R=2km"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Grade of Service. What are the differences between Erlang B and Erlang C models?
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Grade of Service (GOS)** is defined as the **probability that a call is blocked** (or delayed beyond an acceptable threshold) **at the time of call arrival**, given that all C channels in the trunk group are busy. Mathematically, $GOS = P(\text{Blocking})$.

**Erlang B vs Erlang C:**

| Feature | Erlang B (BCC) | Erlang C (BCD) |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Blocked Calls Cleared | Blocked Calls Delayed |
| **Behavior on Block** | Dropped immediately | Placed in queue |
| **Buffer** | No | Infinite (assumed) |
| **Delay** | Zero | Non-zero |
| **Use Case** | Voice telephony | Data, control channels |
| **Formula** | $B = \frac{A^C/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k/k!}$ | $C = \frac{A^C/C!}{(1 - A/C)\sum_{k=0}^{C-1} A^k/k!}$ |

---

### Q2. What is cell splitting? Why is transmit power reduced in the new cells?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cell splitting** is the process of subdividing a congested cell into smaller cells (typically halving the radius), with new base stations installed, to **increase system capacity** (channels per unit area) without requiring additional spectrum.

> **Transmit power reduction:** When radius is halved ($R_2 = R_1/2$), to maintain the same **received power at the cell edge**, the new transmit power must be:
> $P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot (R_2/R_1)^\gamma = P_{t1} / 2^\gamma$
> For $\gamma = 4$ (urban): $P_{t2} = P_{t1} / 16$. This **reduces co-channel interference** and **respects EMF exposure limits** in the smaller cell.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Define **Trunking** and **Grade of Service (GOS)**. A cellular system is allocated a total of **120 channels**. If the offered traffic per user is **0.04 Erlangs** and the system has **4000 users**, find the GOS using the Erlang B formula. Given $B(120, A)$ for various A:

| A | 140 | 150 | 160 | 170 | 180 | 190 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| B | 0.0127 | 0.0281 | 0.0530 | 0.0869 | 0.1283 | 0.1745 |

Also compute the **trunking efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat diagrams the technique of **sectoring**. For a system with N = 7, γ = 4, compute the **S/I ratio (in dB) for omnidirectional and 3-sector (120°) configurations**, and comment on the capacity improvement. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution A(a) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Definitions**

- **Trunking:** A resource-sharing technique in which a limited pool of channels is shared among a large number of users on a statistical/demand basis, exploiting the fact that not all users are active simultaneously.

- **Grade of Service (GOS):** The probability that a user's call is blocked (or delayed beyond a tolerable limit) at the moment of arrival because all channels are busy.

**Step 2: Compute total offered traffic**

$$A = U \cdot A_u = 4000 \times 0.04 = 160 \text{ Erlangs}$$

**Step 3: Read Erlang B value**

$$B(120, 160) = 0.0530 \text{ (from given table)}$$

**Step 4: Carried traffic**

$$A_c = A(1 - B) = 160 \times (1 - 0.0530) = 160 \times 0.947 = 151.52 \text{ Erlangs}$$

**Step 5: Trunking efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{A_c}{C} = \frac{151.52}{120} \approx 1.263 \text{ Erlangs per channel (126.3\%)}$$

$$\boxed{GOS = 0.0530, \quad \eta \approx 1.263 \text{ Erlangs/channel}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for examiners:** Trunking efficiency > 1 is normal and EXPECTED because Erlang efficiency is defined as carried traffic per channel — each channel can "carry" more than 1 Erlang statistically.

### Model Solution A(b) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Concept of sectoring**

> **Sectoring:** A capacity-enhancement technique where the omnidirectional antenna at the base station is replaced with **multiple directional antennas**, each covering a specific sector (120° or 60°), to reduce co-channel interference and improve S/I.

**Step 2: Diagram** (refer to Section 4.3).

**Step 3: Compute Q**

$$Q = \sqrt{3N} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.583$$

**Step 4: S/I for omnidirectional (i₀ = 6)**

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{omni}} = \frac{Q^\gamma}{i_0} = \frac{(4.583)^4}{6} = \frac{441.5}{6} \approx 73.58$$

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{omni, dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(73.58) \approx 18.67 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5: S/I for 3-sector (i₀ = 2)**

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{3-sector}} = \frac{(4.583)^4}{2} = \frac{441.5}{2} \approx 220.75$$

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{\text{3-sector, dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(220.75) \approx 23.44 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6: Improvement**

$$\Delta (S/I) = 23.44 - 18.67 = 4.77 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{(S/I)_{\text{omni}} \approx 18.67 \text{ dB}, \quad (S/I)_{\text{3-sector}} \approx 23.44 \text{ dB}, \quad \text{Gain} = 4.77 \text{ dB}}$$

**Comment:** A **4.77 dB improvement in S/I** allows us to **reduce cluster size N** (and thus increase capacity), or maintain N with much better link quality.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** A cell of radius **R = 4 km** in a cellular system is split into **4 smaller cells** of equal area. The original transmit power was **40 W** with path loss exponent **γ = 4**. Find the **new transmit power** for each split cell. What is the **percentage increase in capacity**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Microcells**. Compare macrocells and microcells in terms of antenna height, transmit power, cell radius, and typical applications. With a neat diagram, explain how microcells and macrocells coexist in a **hierarchical cell structure (HCS)**. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution B(a) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Identify new radius**

Area of original cell $A_1 \propto R^2$. New cell area $A_2 = A_1/4$.

$$R_2 = R_1 \cdot \sqrt{A_2/A_1} = R_1 \cdot \sqrt{1/4} = R_1/2 = 4/2 = 2 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2: Apply power scaling formula**

$$P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot \left(\frac{R_2}{R_1}\right)^\gamma = 40 \times \left(\frac{2}{4}\right)^4 = 40 \times (0.5)^4 = 40 \times \frac{1}{16}$$

$$P_{t2} = 2.5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Capacity increase**

- Original cells in area: 1
- New cells: 4
- Capacity increase = $(4 - 1)/1 \times 100\% = 300\%$

$$\boxed{P_{t2} = 2.5 \text{ W}, \quad \text{Capacity increase} = 300\%}$$

### Model Solution B(b) — Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Concept of Microcells**

> **Microcells** are low-power, low-height base stations that cover a small geographical area (radius < 1 km). They are used to enhance capacity in dense urban areas and fill coverage holes in the macrocell layer.

**Step 2: Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Macrocell | Microcell |
|---|---|---|
| **Antenna Height** | Above rooftop (20–50 m) | Below rooftop (5–15 m) |
| **Transmit Power** | 5–40 W | 0.1–5 W |
| **Cell Radius** | 1–35 km | 0.1–1 km |
| **Path Loss Exponent (γ)** | 3–4 | 2–4 (varies) |
| **User Type** | High mobility (vehicular) | Low mobility (pedestrian) |
| **Application** | Wide-area coverage | Hot spots, indoor, street canyons |
| **Handoff** | Less frequent | More frequent |

**Step 3: Hierarchical Cell Structure (HCS)** — Refer to Section 4.4 diagram.

In HCS, the macrocell provides the **coverage umbrella**, while microcells are overlaid to **absorb high-traffic zones** (offices, malls, intersections). Users are **handed off vertically** (macro ↔ micro) based on speed and load conditions.

$$\boxed{\text{Microcells supplement macrocells, offering 4–10× capacity per unit area in HCS.}}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 1 — Erlang B Numerical Errors:**
> - **Forgetting to convert** $\lambda$ from calls/hour to calls/minute before computing $A = \lambda H$.
> - **Using the wrong table value:** Students often confuse $B(C, A)$ with $B(A, C)$ arguments.
> - **Not stating units** of $A$ (Erlangs) — loses 0.5 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 2 — Cell Splitting Formula Errors:**
> - **Mixing up R and D:** $Q = D/R$ (D = reuse distance, R = cell radius), not the reverse.
> - **Forgetting the power of $\gamma$:** $P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot (R_2/R_1)^\gamma$, NOT $R_2/R_1$ alone.
> - **Assuming 4× capacity in number of channels, not area:** The correct statement is "4× more cells in same area" → 4× capacity density.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 3 — Sectoring Misconception:**
> - **Believing sectoring changes N or Q:** It does NOT. It only changes $i_0$ (number of effective interferers).
> - **Wrong $i_0$ values:** Omni = 6, 3-sector = 2, 6-sector = 1.
> - **Forgetting dB conversion** of S/I ratio: marks deducted for not expressing in dB.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall 4 — Microcell Confusion:**
> - **Confusing picocells (<200 m, indoor) with microcells (<1 km, street-level).**
> - **Not mentioning HCS** (Hierarchical Cell Structure) — examiners specifically look for this term in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Trunking & GOS
- [ ] **Erlang B** formula: $B(C, A) = \frac{A^C/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^k/k!}$ — Blocked Calls Cleared (used in voice)
- [ ] **Erlang C** formula: $C(C, A) = \frac{A^C/C!}{(1 - A/C)\sum_{k=0}^{C-1} A^k/k!}$ — Blocked Calls Delayed (used in data)
- [ ] Traffic $A = \lambda \cdot H = U \cdot A_u$ (all in Erlangs)
- [ ] Trunking efficiency $\eta = A(1 - B)/C$ — CAN exceed 1 (this is normal)
- [ ] GOS is dimensionless; typical target = **2% (0.02)** for voice
- [ ] **Recursive Erlang B:** $B(C,A) = \frac{A \cdot B(C-1,A)}{C + A \cdot B(C-1,A)}$

### Cell Splitting
- [ ] Halving R → **4× more cells** in same area (since $A_{cell} \propto R^2$)
- [ ] Tx power reduction: $P_{t2} = P_{t1} \cdot (R_2/R_1)^\gamma$
- [ ] For $\gamma = 4$, $P_{t2} = P_{t1}/16$ when R halves
- [ ] **Co-channel reuse ratio Q unchanged**; cluster size N unchanged
- [ ] **Drawback:** Higher handoff rate, more infrastructure cost

### Sectoring
- [ ] 3-sector (120°): $i_0 = 2$ → S/I improves by **4.77 dB** vs omni
- [ ] 6-sector (60°): $i_0 = 1$ → S/I improves by **7.78 dB** vs omni
- [ ] Sectoring **does NOT** change Q or N, only the **effective number of interferers**
- [ ] **Drawback:** More handoffs (corner effects), reduced trunking efficiency per sector

### Microcells
- [ ] Radius **< 1 km**, antenna height **below rooftop** (5–15 m)
- [ ] Tx power **0.1–5 W** (vs macrocell 5–40 W)
- [ ] **Path loss exponent γ** is environment-dependent (street canyon, LOS, etc.)
- [ ] **HCS (Hierarchical Cell Structure):** Macrocell umbrella + microcell hot-spot overlay
- [ ] Microcells increase **frequency reuse density** without spectrum expansion
- [ ] **Drawback:** High handoff rate, infrastructure cost, planning complexity

### KTU High-Yield Numerical Pattern
- [ ] Erlang B with **table lookup** (given in question) → expect 7 marks
- [ ] Cell splitting: Tx power + capacity factor → expect 7 marks
- [ ] Sectoring: S/I computation with dB conversion → expect 7 marks
- [ ] Microcell comparison table + HCS diagram → expect 7 marks

### Critical Numerical Conversions
- [ ] **Erlangs → dB for S/I:** $S/I_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/I)$
- [ ] **Improvement factor in dB:** $10 \log_{10}(6/2) = 10 \log_{10}(3) = 4.77$ dB
- [ ] **Improvement factor in dB:** $10 \log_{10}(6/1) = 10 \log_{10}(6) = 7.78$ dB
- [ ] **Area ratio:** $A_2/A_1 = (R_2/R_1)^2$ (hexagonal cells)

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "compare", **always use a table** with 4–6 parameters side-by-side. When asked "explain", use a **block diagram + bulleted explanation**. When asked "compute", show **every single step** — examiners reward method, not just final answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Access Techniques: FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, and OFDMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECL508 - PCECL508
> - **Module:** Module 3: Wireless Communication Systems
> - **Topic:** Multiple Access Techniques: FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, and OFDMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Multiple Access?

**Multiple Access** is a set of techniques that allow a large number of mobile users (or stations) to share a finite amount of radio spectrum resources efficiently, without unacceptable levels of mutual interference. The fundamental challenge in wireless communication is that the **electromagnetic spectrum** is a scarce, shared natural resource.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Schwartz, 2005):**
> "Multiple access is a technique whereby several subscribers or local stations that are geographically dispersed share the same transmission medium (the radio channel) to communicate with a common base station."

In any wireless system, the three primary resources that can be shared are:
1. **Frequency (Bandwidth)** — measured in **Hz**
2. **Time** — measured in **seconds**
3. **Code** — represented by **orthogonal spreading sequences**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, the four fundamental multiple access techniques — **FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, and OFDMA** — form the cornerstone of every generation of mobile communication, from **1G (Analog)** to **5G NR (New Radio)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Highway" Model

Imagine a **massive toll bridge** connecting two cities. The bridge is the *shared radio channel*, and the vehicles are the *users*.

| Technique | Real-World Analogy | Resource Shared |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **FDMA** | The bridge has 4 fixed lanes. Each car must stay in its assigned lane forever. | Frequency |
| **TDMA** | The bridge has 1 lane, but a traffic policeman allows only one car through at a time, in a strict rotation. | Time |
| **CDMA** | Everyone can drive simultaneously, but each car speaks a different language. The toll collector understands all languages and separates the messages. | Code |
| **OFDMA** | The bridge has 100 tiny sub-lanes, and the policeman groups cars dynamically onto sub-lanes based on demand. | Subcarriers + Time |

---

## 1.3 Formal Definition of Each Technique

### 1.3.1 FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FDMA Definition:** *Frequency Division Multiple Access* is a multiple access technique in which the available **bandwidth (B)** of the channel is divided into **N non-overlapping frequency sub-bands**, and each user is assigned a unique sub-band for the entire duration of the communication.

- Used in **1G Analog** cellular systems (AMPS, TACS).
- Requires **guard bands** to minimize adjacent channel interference.
- Each user transmits and receives **simultaneously** but on different frequencies.

### 1.3.2 TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TDMA Definition:** *Time Division Multiple Access* is a multiple access technique in which multiple users share the **same frequency band** by transmitting in **non-overlapping time slots** within a periodic **frame structure**.

- Used in **2G GSM**, **DECT**, and **IS-54**.
- Each user occupies the **entire bandwidth** but only for a brief, designated time interval.
- Requires **tight synchronization** between users (timing advance in GSM).

### 1.3.3 CDMA (Code Division Multiple Access)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CDMA Definition:** *Code Division Multiple Access* is a spread-spectrum multiple access technique where each user is assigned a unique **pseudo-noise (PN) binary code sequence**. All users transmit **simultaneously** on the **same frequency band**, and separation is achieved at the receiver through **code correlation**.

- Used in **3G** cellular systems (IS-95, WCDMA, CDMA2000).
- Based on **Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)**.
- Capacity is **interference-limited** (not bandwidth-limited as in FDMA/TDMA).

### 1.3.4 OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OFDMA Definition:** *Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access* is a multi-carrier multiple access technique that is a **multi-user extension of OFDM**. It assigns subsets of **orthogonal subcarriers** to individual users, allowing simultaneous low-rate transmission on multiple subcarriers.

- Used in **4G LTE**, **5G NR**, **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)**, and **WiMAX**.
- Theoretically achieves **maximum spectral efficiency** through subcarrier orthogonality.
- Combines the benefits of **FDMA** (subcarrier division) and **TDMA** (time slot scheduling).

---

## 1.4 The Evolution of Multiple Access

| Generation | Technology | Multiple Access Used | Bandwidth Efficiency |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1G (1980s) | AMPS, TACS | **FDMA** | Low |
| 2G (1990s) | GSM, IS-95 | **TDMA / CDMA** | Medium |
| 3G (2000s) | WCDMA, CDMA2000 | **CDMA** | High |
| 4G (2010s) | LTE, WiMAX | **OFDMA** | Very High |
| 5G (2020s) | 5G NR | **OFDMA + NOMA** | Extremely High |

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Fact:** The concept of Code Division was first conceived by **Hedy Lamarr** and **George Antheil** in **1942** (US Patent 2,292,387) as a "Secret Communication System" for torpedo guidance, originally using piano rolls. It was not practically implemented until the 1980s.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — FDMA

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)
- **Step 1:** The total system bandwidth **B_total** is partitioned into **N** disjoint frequency channels of width **B_c** each.
- **Step 2:** Each user is permanently assigned one channel.
- **Step 3:** The user's baseband signal is modulated onto a carrier at the assigned frequency.
- **Step 4:** Guard bands of width **B_g** are inserted between adjacent channels to prevent Inter-Carrier Interference (ICI).

### Mathematical Structure

If the total bandwidth is **B_total** and there are **N** users:

$$B_{total} = N \cdot B_c + (N-1) \cdot B_g$$

- **Continuous transmission**: A user occupies its frequency slot 100% of the time.
- **Narrowband filtering**: Requires sharp, high-Q bandpass filters at the receiver.
- **Duplexing**: FDMA systems typically combine with **FDD (Frequency Division Duplex)** for uplink/downlink separation.

### Real-World Use Case
- **Satellite TV broadcasting** (e.g., DirecTV uses FDMA/Frequency Division Multiplexing to deliver hundreds of channels over a single Ku-band transponder).
- **Traditional analog radio broadcasting** (AM/FM).

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — TDMA

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)
- **Step 1:** A periodic **TDMA frame** of duration **T_frame** is divided into **N** time slots of duration **T_s** each.
- **Step 2:** Each user is assigned one (or more) time slots per frame.
- **Step 3:** During its slot, the user transmits a burst of data using the **entire system bandwidth**.
- **Step 4:** A **guard time** of duration **T_g** is inserted between slots to prevent overlap due to propagation delay.

### Mathematical Structure

$$T_{frame} = N \cdot T_s + (N-1) \cdot T_g$$

- **Burst transmission**: RF power amplifiers can be switched off between slots, saving battery.
- **Burst rate**: The data rate during a slot is **N times** the average user rate.
- **Synchronization**: Critical requirement; users in different distances require **timing advance**.

### Real-World Use Case
- **GSM cellular networks** (each 200 kHz channel supports 8 TDMA users).
- **TETRA** (Terrestrial Trunked Radio) for public safety communications.

---

## 2.3 Operational Breakdown — CDMA

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)
- **Step 1:** Each user is assigned a unique binary **pseudo-noise (PN) code** of length **L** (the **processing gain**).
- **Step 2:** The user's narrowband data signal (bit duration **T_b**) is multiplied (XOR) with the high-rate spreading code (chip duration **T_c = T_b / L**).
- **Step 3:** The resulting wideband signal is transmitted using the **full system bandwidth**.
- **Step 4:** The receiver correlates the incoming signal with the desired user's PN code. Other users' signals appear as **low-level noise** due to the **near-zero cross-correlation** property of orthogonal codes.

### Mathematical Structure

**Processing Gain:**

$$G_p = \frac{T_b}{T_c} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = L$$

where **R_c** = chip rate and **R_b** = bit rate.

**Signal-to-Noise Ratio after despreading:**

$$\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{out} = G_p \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{in}$$

**Number of simultaneous users (Shannon's CDMA capacity, simplified):**

$$N_{max} \approx 1 + \frac{G_p}{\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{req}}$$

- **Soft capacity**: Adding a new user gradually raises the noise floor; capacity is not "hard."
- **Near-Far Problem**: Requires **precise power control** (typically within ±1 dB) to prevent strong signals from overwhelming weak ones.

### Real-World Use Case
- **IS-95 (cdmaOne)** — the first commercial CDMA cellular system (1995).
- **GPS** (Global Positioning System) — uses CDMA across the L1/L2/L5 bands.

---

## 2.4 Operational Breakdown — OFDMA

### Working Principle (Step-by-Step)
- **Step 1:** The available bandwidth is divided into **K orthogonal subcarriers** (e.g., K = 512 in LTE, up to 4096 in 5G).
- **Step 2:** Each subcarrier has a bandwidth **Δf = 1/T_sym**, where **T_sym** is the OFDM symbol duration.
- **Step 3:** A user is dynamically assigned a **subset of subcarriers** for a specific time slot (called a **Resource Block** in LTE).
- **Step 4:** The base station transmitter uses an **Inverse Fast Fourier Transform (IFFT)** to combine all subcarriers, and the receiver uses an **FFT** to recover them.

### Mathematical Structure

**Subcarrier orthogonality condition:**

$$\int_0^{T_{sym}} e^{j2\pi f_k t} \cdot e^{-j2\pi f_m t} \, dt = 0 \quad \text{for } k \neq m$$

**Number of subcarriers:**

$$K = \frac{B_{total}}{\Delta f}$$

**Spectral efficiency of OFDM (Nyquist criterion):**

$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = 2 \log_2(M) \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

for M-QAM with ideal roll-off (raised cosine, roll-off factor β = 0).

### Real-World Use Case
- **4G LTE**: 100 Resource Blocks (RBs), each containing 12 subcarriers of 15 kHz Δf.
- **5G NR**: Numerology-adaptive subcarrier spacing (15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz).
- **Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax)**: OFDMA in both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz bands.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for the End Semester Examination (ESE).**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $B_{total} = N \cdot B_c + (N-1) \cdot B_g$ | FDMA total bandwidth allocation | Hz |
| 2 | $T_{frame} = N \cdot T_s + (N-1) \cdot T_g$ | TDMA frame duration | seconds |
| 3 | $G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{T_b}{T_c}$ | CDMA Processing Gain | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{out} = G_p \cdot \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{in}$ | CDMA despreading SNR gain | dimensionless |
| 5 | $N_{max} \approx 1 + \frac{G_p}{\left(E_b / N_0\right)_{req}}$ | CDMA capacity limit | users |
| 6 | $K = \frac{B_{total}}{\Delta f}$ | Number of OFDM subcarriers | subcarriers |
| 7 | $\Delta f = \frac{1}{T_{sym}}$ | Subcarrier spacing | Hz |
| 8 | $\eta = \log_2(M)$ | Spectral efficiency (M-PSK) | bits/s/Hz |
| 9 | $T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP}$ | OFDM symbol with cyclic prefix | seconds |
| 10 | $R_{eff} = \frac{R_b \cdot T_u}{T_u + T_{CP}}$ | Effective rate with CP overhead | bps |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember:**
> - For **Walsh codes** in CDMA: the maximum number of orthogonal codes of length **L** is exactly **L** (so a 64-chip code supports 64 mutually orthogonal users).
> - In **OFDM**, subcarriers remain orthogonal only if Doppler spread **f_D ≪ Δf**.
> - In **TDMA**, the maximum frame efficiency is $\eta_{frame} = \frac{T_s}{T_s + T_g} \leq 1$.

---

## 2.6 Comparative Analysis Table

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA | OFDMA |
|-----------|------|------|------|-------|
| **Resource Divided** | Frequency | Time | Code | Subcarriers + Time |
| **Spectrum Sharing** | Continuous | Bursty | Spread | Sub-band + Time |
| **Synchronization** | Not strict | Strict timing | Code alignment | Symbol-level |
| **Guard band / time** | Guard band | Guard time | None (PN codes) | Cyclic Prefix |
| **Power Control** | Not critical | Less critical | Very critical (Near-Far) | Critical at edges |
| **Capacity type** | Hard | Hard | Soft | Adaptive (Soft) |
| **Multipath handling** | Poor (narrowband) | Moderate | Excellent (RAKE receiver) | Excellent (CP-based) |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High | Very High (FFT/IFFT) |
| **Used in** | 1G AMPS | 2G GSM | 3G WCDMA | 4G LTE / 5G NR |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Bandwidth in FDMA

We need to derive the total system bandwidth required to support **N** users in a pure FDMA system, where each user occupies a channel of width **B_c**, with **B_g** Hz of guard band separating adjacent channels.

### Step 1: Define the parameters
- Let **N** = number of users in the system.
- Let **B_c** = carrier bandwidth of one user channel.
- Let **B_g** = guard band between any two adjacent channels.

### Step 2: Count the number of guard bands
Between **N** channels, there are exactly **(N − 1)** guard bands (no guard band is needed at the extreme ends, as the system operator controls the spectrum edges).

### Step 3: Construct the total bandwidth expression
The total bandwidth consumed is the sum of all carrier bandwidths plus all guard bands:

$$B_{total} = \underbrace{B_c + B_c + \cdots + B_c}_{N \text{ terms}} + \underbrace{B_g + B_g + \cdots + B_g}_{(N-1) \text{ terms}}$$

### Step 4: Simplify the summation
Factoring out the common terms:

$$B_{total} = N \cdot B_c + (N - 1) \cdot B_g$$

### Step 5: Practical numerical example
Given: N = 100 users, B_c = 30 kHz per voice channel, B_g = 10 kHz guard band.

$$B_{total} = 100 \cdot 30{,}000 + 99 \cdot 10{,}000$$

$$B_{total} = 3{,}000{,}000 + 990{,}000 = 3{,}990{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 3.99 \text{ MHz}$$

This matches the original **AMPS** cellular uplink allocation (3.99 MHz per direction) — confirming the derivation.

> **Real-world utility:** This formula is critical for **frequency planning engineers** in satellite and 1G cellular systems when assigning channel pools to base stations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CDMA Processing Gain & Capacity

We will derive the relationship between processing gain and the maximum number of simultaneous users a CDMA system can support, using the Shannon-Hartley perspective.

### Step 1: Define the energy per bit to noise spectral density ratio
The standard quality requirement for digital voice (e.g., in IS-95) is:

$$\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{req} \approx 7 \text{ dB} \quad \text{or} \quad 5.012 \text{ in linear scale}$$

This corresponds to a target Bit Error Rate (BER) of approximately **10⁻³** for voice traffic.

### Step 2: Express the interference from other users
If there are **N** users transmitting simultaneously, the received signal at the base station has each user at the same power level **S** (due to perfect power control). The Signal-to-Interference Ratio (SIR) is:

$$\frac{S}{I} = \frac{1}{N - 1}$$

### Step 3: Relate SIR to processing gain
The despreading operation at the receiver provides a processing gain of **G_p**, so the effective SIR becomes:

$$\left(\frac{S}{I}\right)_{eff} = \frac{G_p}{N - 1}$$

### Step 4: Set the threshold
For acceptable voice quality, we require:

$$\frac{G_p}{N - 1} \geq \left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{req}$$

### Step 5: Solve for N

$$N - 1 \leq \frac{G_p}{(E_b / N_0)_{req}}$$

$$N_{max} = 1 + \frac{G_p}{(E_b / N_0)_{req}}$$

### Step 6: Numerical example
Given: R_b = 9.6 kbps (voice), R_c = 1.2288 Mcps, (E_b/N_0)_req = 7 dB ≈ 5.012.

**Step 6a: Compute processing gain:**

$$G_p = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 128$$

**Step 6b: Compute maximum users:**

$$N_{max} = 1 + \frac{128}{5.012} \approx 1 + 25.54 = 26.54$$

Since we cannot have fractional users, **N_max = 26 users per sector** (the famous **Erlang capacity of IS-95** ≈ 26 voice users per 1.25 MHz channel).

> **Real-world utility:** This derivation is the basis of the **"cell breathing"** phenomenon in CDMA — when many users are active in a cell, coverage shrinks; when few are active, coverage expands.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: OFDMA Subcarrier Count and Symbol Duration

### Step 1: Define the system parameters
- Total available bandwidth: **B_total = 20 MHz** (a typical LTE channel).
- Subcarrier spacing: **Δf = 15 kHz** (LTE standard).

### Step 2: Calculate the number of subcarriers
The number of usable subcarriers is the total bandwidth divided by the subcarrier spacing:

$$K = \frac{B_{total}}{\Delta f} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{15 \times 10^3} = 1333.33$$

Since the number of subcarriers must be a power of 2 (for efficient FFT), the system rounds up to **K = 2048**, and uses **1200** subcarriers for data (out of 2048 total FFT points). In LTE, this is exactly **100 Resource Blocks × 12 subcarriers/RB = 1200 subcarriers**.

### Step 3: Compute the useful OFDM symbol duration
For subcarriers to remain orthogonal, the useful symbol duration **T_u** is the reciprocal of the subcarrier spacing:

$$T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{15{,}000} = 66.67 \text{ μs}$$

### Step 4: Compute the cyclic prefix duration
For LTE normal CP, the first OFDM symbol has a long CP of **T_CP,1 = 5.2 μs**, and the remaining 6 symbols have **T_CP = 4.69 μs** each.

### Step 5: Compute the total slot duration
One LTE subframe = 2 slots = 14 OFDM symbols (12 with short CP, 2 with long CP):

$$T_{slot} = 7 \cdot T_u + 6 \cdot T_{CP} + T_{CP,1}$$

$$T_{slot} = 7 \cdot 66.67 + 6 \cdot 4.69 + 5.2 = 466.67 + 28.14 + 5.2 = 500 \text{ μs}$$

> **Real-world utility:** This precise timing allows LTE base stations to align transmissions within **±1 μs** for inter-cell interference coordination.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Effective Data Rate with Cyclic Prefix Overhead

In OFDM, the cyclic prefix (CP) absorbs multipath echoes but consumes time without carrying new data. We need to find the **effective data rate** R_eff.

### Step 1: Define the parameters
- Modulation: **64-QAM** (6 bits per symbol).
- Subcarriers carrying data: **N_data**.
- Useful symbol duration: **T_u**.
- CP duration: **T_CP**.

### Step 2: Compute the number of bits per OFDM symbol
For 64-QAM with N_data subcarriers:

$$N_{bits} = N_{data} \cdot \log_2(64) = N_{data} \cdot 6$$

### Step 3: Compute the effective symbol period
The total time for one OFDM symbol is the useful duration plus the CP overhead:

$$T_{sym} = T_u + T_{CP}$$

### Step 4: Compute the effective data rate

$$R_{eff} = \frac{N_{bits}}{T_{sym}} = \frac{N_{data} \cdot 6}{T_u + T_{CP}}$$

### Step 5: Express as a ratio of the rate without CP
If **R_raw** is the rate without the cyclic prefix (i.e., R_raw = N_data · 6 / T_u), then:

$$R_{eff} = R_{raw} \cdot \frac{T_u}{T_u + T_{CP}}$$

### Step 6: Numerical example
For LTE normal CP: T_u = 66.67 μs, T_CP = 4.69 μs.

$$\text{CP overhead} = \frac{4.69}{66.67} \approx 0.0704 \text{ or } 7.04\%$$

This means a **7% throughput loss** due to the cyclic prefix — a known design trade-off in LTE.

> **Real-world utility:** This calculation is the basis of the **5G NR numerology** decision — at higher frequencies, larger Δf (e.g., 120 kHz) is used, making T_u shorter, so the absolute CP duration can stay similar (e.g., 2.34 μs) while the relative overhead drops to ~2%.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: Why OFDMA dominates in 4G/5G

| Challenge | FDMA (1G) | TDMA (2G) | CDMA (3G) | OFDMA (4G/5G) |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| Multipath equalization | Hard | Hard | Rake receiver | Trivial (CP) |
| Frequency-selective fading | Vulnerable | Vulnerable | Resilient | Excellent |
| Cell-edge user throughput | Poor | Poor | Moderate | Good (ICIC) |
| Peak data rate (theoretical) | 2.4 kbps | 14.4 kbps | 2 Mbps | 100+ Mbps → 10+ Gbps |
| Spectral efficiency | Low | Medium | Medium-High | Very High |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High | Very High (DSP) |
| Adaptive resource allocation | None | Slot-based | Limited | **Full flexibility** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always mention "**adaptive modulation and coding (AMC)**" when explaining OFDMA — it is a major advantage over fixed-format FDMA/TDMA systems.

> **Reading the diagram:** The data is **multiplied (XOR)** with a high-rate PN code, spreading the spectrum. At the receiver, multiplying again with the same synchronized PN code **de-spreads** the desired signal and spreads all other users' signals (treating them as noise).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define FDMA and explain its major disadvantage.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**

**Model Answer:**

> **FDMA Definition:** Frequency Division Multiple Access is a multiple access technique in which the total available system bandwidth is divided into **N non-overlapping frequency sub-bands**, and each user is permanently assigned a unique sub-band for the entire duration of the communication.

**Major Disadvantages (any one):**
- **Wasted bandwidth** due to guard bands between channels.
- **Inflexible capacity allocation** — each user gets a fixed slot, even when silent.
- **Need for sharp, expensive bandpass filters.**
- **Susceptibility to intermodulation distortion** in the High-Power Amplifier (HPA) when multiple frequency signals pass through it simultaneously.

**Marks Distribution:**
- 1.5 marks: Correct definition with key terms.
- 1.5 marks: Clearly stated disadvantage with brief technical justification.

---

### Question A2: What is Processing Gain in CDMA? Compute it for a system with bit rate 19.2 kbps and chip rate 1.2288 Mcps.
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Processing Gain (G_p)** is defined as the ratio of the chip rate to the data bit rate, or equivalently, the ratio of the spread bandwidth to the original signal bandwidth. It quantifies the **spreading factor** in spread-spectrum communication.

**Formula:**

$$G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b}$$

**Calculation:**

$$G_p = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^6}{19.2 \times 10^3} = 64$$

In decibels:

$$G_p \, (\text{dB}) = 10 \log_{10}(64) \approx 18.06 \text{ dB}$$

**Marks Distribution:**
- 1.5 marks: Correct definition.
- 1 mark: Correct formula and substitution.
- 0.5 mark: Final numerical answer with units (dB).

---

### Question B-A1: Compare FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA in detail
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Comparative Analysis
**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| **Resource Divided** | Frequency | Time | Code |
| **Transmission Mode** | Continuous | Bursty | Continuous |
| **Synchronization** | Not required | Strict timing | Code synchronization |
| **Guard Requirement** | Guard band | Guard time | None (PN codes) |
| **Power Control** | Not critical | Less critical | Highly critical (Near-Far) |
| **Capacity Nature** | Hard limit | Hard limit | Soft limit |
| **Multipath Handling** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent (RAKE) |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Generation Used** | 1G (AMPS) | 2G (GSM) | 3G (WCDMA) |
| **Spectral Efficiency** | Low | Medium | High |

**Key Differences (any 3 to score full marks):**
1. **FDMA** uses **frequency** as the discriminator; **TDMA** uses **time**; **CDMA** uses **unique codes** — all three allow multiple users but in completely different domains.
2. **FDMA/TDMA** are **bandwidth-limited** systems; **CDMA** is **interference-limited**.
3. **FDMA** requires **bandpass filters**; **TDMA** requires **timing circuits**; **CDMA** requires **correlator receivers**.

**Marks Distribution:**
- 3 marks: Tabular comparison.
- 4 marks: At least 4 valid points of differentiation with technical depth.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Problem
**A GSM system uses a 25 MHz total bandwidth divided into 200 kHz channels, each supporting 8 TDMA users. If 40% of the spectrum is kept as guard band, calculate:**
**(i)** Number of available traffic channels.
**(ii)** Maximum number of simultaneous voice users.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate usable bandwidth after guard band**

$$B_{usable} = B_{total} \times (1 - 0.40) = 25 \times 0.60 = 15 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2: Calculate number of FDMA carriers**

$$N_{carriers} = \frac{B_{usable}}{B_c} = \frac{15 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^3} = 75 \text{ carriers}$$

**Step 3: Calculate total voice channels (with TDMA inside each)**

$$N_{voice} = N_{carriers} \times 8 = 75 \times 8 = 600 \text{ voice channels}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Number of traffic carriers = **75**
- (ii) Number of simultaneous voice users = **600**

**Marks Distribution:**
- 1 mark: Step 1 setup.
- 1 mark: Step 2 substitution.
- 1 mark: Step 2 result.
- 1 mark: Step 3 setup.
- 1 mark: Step 3 result.
- 2 marks: Correct final answer with units.

---

### Question B-B1: Explain OFDMA in detail with reference to LTE
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 3]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Conceptual Explanation
**Model Answer:**

> **OFDMA (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access)** is the multiple access scheme used in 4G LTE and 5G NR. It is the multi-user extension of OFDM, where the available bandwidth is divided into **K orthogonal subcarriers** and groups of subcarriers are assigned to different users.

**Key Features:**
1. **Subcarrier orthogonality:** The subcarriers are mathematically orthogonal, meaning the peak of one subcarrier's spectrum coincides with the **nulls** of all other subcarriers, allowing them to be tightly packed without inter-carrier interference (ICI).
2. **Resource Block (RB) concept:** In LTE, the smallest allocation unit is an RB = 12 subcarriers × 1 time slot (0.5 ms) = **180 kHz** bandwidth.
3. **Adaptive subcarrier allocation:** The scheduler at the base station dynamically assigns RBs to users based on channel quality (using CQI feedback), exploiting **multi-user diversity**.
4. **Cyclic Prefix (CP):** A copy of the end of each OFDM symbol is prepended to absorb multipath echoes, converting convolution into circular convolution and enabling **simple frequency-domain equalization**.
5. **Numerology in 5G:** The subcarrier spacing Δf is variable (15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz) for different deployment scenarios.

**Why OFDMA over CDMA?**
- **No near-far problem** because each user occupies a separate subcarrier set.
- **Simplified equalization** through FFT.
- **Higher spectral efficiency** due to orthogonality.
- **Flexible bandwidth allocation** (1.4, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20 MHz).

**Marks Distribution:**
- 2 marks: Definition + key concept.
- 2 marks: Subcarrier orthogonality explanation.
- 2 marks: Resource Block concept.
- 1 mark: Comparison with CDMA.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Problem
**In an LTE system, the total bandwidth is 10 MHz and the subcarrier spacing is 15 kHz. Each Resource Block contains 12 subcarriers and 7 OFDM symbols per slot. If QPSK modulation is used, calculate:**
**(i)** Number of usable subcarriers.
**(ii)** Number of Resource Blocks.
**(iii)** Total number of bits per Resource Block (with 1 reference symbol per slot).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate number of usable subcarriers**

$$N_{SC} = \frac{B_{total}}{\Delta f} = \frac{10 \times 10^6}{15 \times 10^3} = 666.67$$

In standard LTE, the number of usable subcarriers is rounded to the nearest multiple of 12: **600 usable subcarriers** (i.e., 50 RBs × 12 = 600).

**Step 2: Calculate number of Resource Blocks**

$$N_{RB} = \frac{600}{12} = 50 \text{ Resource Blocks}$$

**Step 3: Calculate bits per RB**
- QPSK → 2 bits per symbol.
- Per slot: 7 OFDM symbols × 12 subcarriers = 84 subcarriers per RB.
- Subtract 1 reference (pilot) symbol: 84 − 1 = **83 data symbols** (standard LTE uses 2 reference symbols for 4-antenna MIMO; for SISO use 1).
- Data subcarriers per RB: 84 − 1 = 83.
- Bits per RB per slot: 83 × 2 = **166 bits**.

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Usable subcarriers = **600**
- (ii) Number of RBs = **50**
- (iii) Data bits per RB per slot = **166 bits**

**Marks Distribution:**
- 2 marks: Step 1 derivation and result.
- 2 marks: Step 2 calculation.
- 3 marks: Step 3 with all 3 sub-steps (subcarriers, reference symbols, bits).
