# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**  
*Why is a Zero-Forcing equalizer not preferred in practical wireless receivers, even though it perfectly cancels ISI?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The Zero-Forcing equalizer implements $C(f) = 1/H(f)$ in the frequency domain. While this exactly inverts the channel and forces ISI to zero at the sampling instants, it suffers from **noise enhancement**. When the channel exhibits a spectral null (deep fade), $\vert H(f)\vert$ is very small, so $C(f)$ becomes very large. The noise present in the null region is therefore amplified dramatically, leading to a severely degraded SNR at the equalizer output and a high bit-error-rate. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**  
*State the Wiener-Hopf equation that defines the optimum tap weights of an MMSE equalizer. Define each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
The optimum tap-weight vector is given by:

$$\mathbf{c}_{\text{MMSE}} = \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\, \mathbf{r}_{ys}$$

where $\mathbf{R}_{yy} = E[\mathbf{y}(n)\mathbf{y}^{H}(n)]$ is the $N \times N$ autocorrelation matrix of the received signal vector, and $\mathbf{r}_{ys} = E[\mathbf{y}(n)\, s^{\ast}(n-D)]$ is the $N \times 1$ cross-correlation vector between the received signal and the delayed transmitted symbol. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks — with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the optimal tap weights of a linear MMSE equalizer from the mean-square-error cost function $J = E[\vert e(n)\vert^{2}]$. Clearly show all gradient and matrix-inversion steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wireless channel has discrete impulse response $h = [0.8, 1.0, 0.5]$. The transmitted symbols are BPSK ($s = \pm 1$) with $\sigma_{s}^{2} = 1$. The AWGN variance is $\sigma_{w}^{2} = 0.2$. Design a 3-tap linear MMSE equalizer with decision delay $D = 1$. Compute the tap weights and the resulting minimum MSE. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the cost function.

$$J(\mathbf{c}) = E\bigl[\,\bigl\vert s(n-D) - \mathbf{c}^{H}\mathbf{y}(n)\bigr\vert^{2}\,\bigr]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Expand the squared magnitude.

$$J = \sigma_{s}^{2} - \mathbf{c}^{H}\mathbf{r}_{ys} - \mathbf{r}_{ys}^{H}\mathbf{c} + \mathbf{c}^{H}\mathbf{R}_{yy}\mathbf{c}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{c}^{\ast}$ (treat as independent variable).

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{c}^{\ast}} J = -\mathbf{r}_{ys} + \mathbf{R}_{yy}\mathbf{c}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Set gradient to zero for optimality.

$$\mathbf{R}_{yy}\mathbf{c}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{r}_{ys}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve by left-multiplying with $\mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}$.

$$\mathbf{c}_{\text{MMSE}} = \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{ys}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Substitute back to compute minimum MSE.

$$\text{MSE}_{\min} = \sigma_{s}^{2} - \mathbf{r}_{ys}^{H}\mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{ys}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Note the existence condition — $\mathbf{R}_{yy}$ must be positive-definite (it is, since noise ensures $\sigma_{w}^{2}\mathbf{I}$ regularisation).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Construct the channel convolution matrix $\mathbf{H}$ (size $3 \times 5$, with $N = 3$, $L = 3$).

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 1.0 & 0.5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.8 & 1.0 & 0.5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.8 & 1.0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute $\mathbf{R}_{yy} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{H}^{H}\sigma_{s}^{2} + \sigma_{w}^{2}\mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{r}_{ys} = \mathbf{H}\,\mathbf{s}_{\text{col}}\,\sigma_{s}^{2}$ where $\mathbf{s}_{\text{col}} = [0, 0, 1, 0, 0]^{T}$ (delay $D = 1$ column is the second one — i.e., index of $s(n-1)$). Adjust index carefully:

- For $D = 1$, the desired symbol is $s(n-1)$. The column of $\mathbf{H}$ corresponding to position $k = D = 1$ is $[1.0,\; 0.8,\; 0]^{T}$.

So:

$$\mathbf{r}_{ys} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 \\ 0.8 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{H}\mathbf{H}^{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.89 & 1.30 & 0.40 \\ 1.30 & 1.89 & 0.80 \\ 0.40 & 0.80 & 1.89 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{R}_{yy} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.09 & 1.30 & 0.40 \\ 1.30 & 2.09 & 0.80 \\ 0.40 & 0.80 & 2.09 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Solve $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{ys}$. Using Gaussian elimination or matrix inversion:

$$\det(\mathbf{R}_{yy}) \approx 5.087 \text{ (computed by expansion)}$$

$$\mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.779 & -0.404 & 0.040 \\ -0.404 & 0.779 & -0.241 \\ 0.040 & -0.241 & 0.591 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{c}_{\text{MMSE}} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.456 \\ 0.219 \\ -0.153 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute MSE.

$$\text{MSE}_{\min} = 1 - \mathbf{r}_{ys}^{H}\mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{ys} = 1 - (0.456 + 0.175 + 0) \approx 0.369$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to delay-align the cross-correlation vector.** Always use the column of $\mathbf{H}$ corresponding to delay $D$, not column 0. This is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> - **Forgetting the $\sigma_{w}^{2}\mathbf{I}$ term.** In Part (a), the regularization appears as a noise term. Omitting it leads to a non-invertible $\mathbf{R}_{yy}$ for noise-free problems and a wrong MSE in noisy problems.
> - **Using $h(n)$ instead of $\mathbf{H}$.** A scalar $h$ is the impulse response; an **uppercase $\mathbf{H}$** is the convolution matrix. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> - **Not writing units or dimensions in $\mathbf{R}_{yy}$.** Mentioning that $\mathbf{R}_{yy}$ is $N \times N$ and $\mathbf{r}_{ys}$ is $N \times 1$ is a 1-mark "easy mark" you should not skip.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the structure and operation of a **Decision Feedback Equalizer (DFE)** with the help of a block diagram. Derive the output expression and explain why DFE does not enhance noise the way a linear equalizer does. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Zero-Forcing**, **MMSE**, **DFE** and **MLSE** equalizers in terms of (i) ISI cancellation capability, (ii) noise enhancement, (iii) computational complexity, (iv) error propagation, and (v) suitability for time-varying channels. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Structure (2 marks):** A DFE consists of two filters:
- **Feed-Forward Filter (FFF)** with $N_f$ taps acting on the received signal $y(n), y(n-1), \dots$
- **Feedback Filter (FBF)** with $N_b$ taps acting on **past detected symbols** $\hat{s}(n-1), \hat{s}(n-2), \dots$

**Output expression (2 marks):**

$$\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N_{f}-1} c_{f}(k)\, y(n-k) \;-\; \sum_{k=1}^{N_{b}} c_{b}(k)\, \hat{s}(n-k)$$

**Operation (1 mark):** The detected symbols are subtracted from the equalized signal to cancel **post-cursor ISI** (the ISI produced by previously transmitted symbols).

**Why no noise enhancement (2 marks):** The feedback branch operates on **known detected symbols**, not on noisy received samples. Hence, no new noise is injected. The FFF still operates on noisy samples, but its noise enhancement is bounded because the post-cursor ISI is handled by the noise-free FBF.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Criterion | ZF | MMSE | DFE | MLSE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) ISI Cancellation | Perfect (noise-free) | Optimal linear | Near-optimal | Optimal (sequence-level) |
| (ii) Noise Enhancement | Severe at nulls | Low | None on FBF branch | Minimal |
| (iii) Complexity | $O(N)$ | $O(N^{2})$ | $O(N_{f}+N_{b})$ | Exponential in $L$ |
| (iv) Error Propagation | None | None | Yes (wrong decision corrupts FBF) | None (sequence search) |
| (v) Time-Varying Channels | Needs adaptive form | LMS/RLS works | LMS/RLS works | Hard to track |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** MMSE is the workhorse linear equalizer; DFE is preferred on severely frequency-selective channels; MLSE is best in slowly-varying, short-ISI channels (e.g., GSM); ZF is rarely deployed in practice due to noise enhancement.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read 5 times before exam):**

- The **received signal model** is $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} h(k) s(n-k) + w(n)$ — write this down first in *any* equalizer problem.
- **ZF equalizer** inverts the channel: $C_{\text{ZF}}(f) = 1/H(f)$; **noise enhancement** is its fatal flaw.
- **MMSE equalizer** minimises $E[\vert e(n)\vert^{2}]$ and uses **Wiener-Hopf**: $\mathbf{c}_{\text{MMSE}} = \mathbf{R}_{yy}^{-1}\mathbf{r}_{ys}$.
- The **MMSE-to-ZF reduction** is a single equation: set $N_0 \rightarrow 0$ in the MMSE formula and you obtain ZF.
- The **MMSE transfer function** is $C_{\text{MMSE}}(f) = H^{\ast}(f) / \bigl(\vert H(f)\vert^{2} + N_0/E_{s}\bigr)$.
- **DFE** uses a **feedforward filter + feedback filter**; the FBF removes post-cursor ISI without noise penalty.
- **MLSE** (Viterbi) is **optimal in AWGN**; complexity grows **exponentially** with channel length $L$, so it is restricted to $L \le 4$ in practice.
- **Adaptive equalizers** use LMS update: $\mathbf{c}(n+1) = \mathbf{c}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{y}^{\ast}(n)$; the stability condition is $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$.
- **Training sequence** is required initially; then the equalizer switches to **decision-directed mode**.
- The **decision delay $D$** must be chosen to match the equalizer length: typically $D \approx N/2$.
- **Frequency-selective fading** $\Leftrightarrow$ $T_s < \tau_{\max}$ $\Leftrightarrow$ **equalization is required**.
- For BER, ZF performs poorly at spectral nulls; MMSE and DFE maintain graceful degradation.
- **Common KTU MCQ traps:** "MMSE = ZF when $N_0 = 0$" (true), "DFE avoids error propagation" (false), "LMS converges for any $\mu$" (false — bounded by $2/\lambda_{\max}$).
